Motivation: To evaluate SAM's generalization capability in waste segmentation tasks and demonstrate that fine-tuning SAM as a foundation model is essential for downstream tasks despite its limitations in some research fields.

Method: Fine-tuned SAM-ViT-H model on three waste segmentation datasets (Zerowaste, TACO, TrashCan 1.0) captured from real scenes with challenges including occlusions, deformable objects, transparency, and background confusion.

Result: Fine-tuned SAM-ViT-H outperformed state-of-the-art on Zerowaste and TACO with +30 IoU improvement, and closely approached TrashCan 1.0 performance with only -1.44 difference.

Conclusion: Fine-tuning SAM as a foundation model is crucial for better generalization in downstream waste segmentation tasks, and SAM should not be disregarded or wasted.

Abstract: Meta AI has recently released the Segment Anything Model (SAM), which demonstrates exceptional zero-shot image segmentation performance across various tasks with remarkable accuracy. Despite its inability to provide accurate segmentation across multiple research fields, SAM still serves as a valuable starting point for supporting the segmentation pipeline process, particularly for tasks that require extensive and senior skills annotations. This study aims to evaluate the generalization of SAM and fine-tuning SAM models using three waste segmentation datasets. Although they are captured from real scenes as SAM was pretrained on, these datasets present several challenges, including occlusions, deformable objects, transparency, and objects easily confused with backgrounds. In our findings, the fine-tuned SAM-ViT-H model outperforms the state-ofthe-art Zerowaste, and TACO datasets with a significant increase of +30 in IoU, and it closely approaches performance levels of TrashCan 1.0, with only a -1.44 difference. After evaluating these popular waste datasets, it became evident that fine-tuning SAM as a foundational model is a crucial step for providing better generalization for downstream waste segmentation tasks. Therefore, SAM should not be disregarded or wasted.

</details>


### [56] [Using the YOLOv12 Model for Verifying the Correct Color Sequence of Wires in Network Cables (Patch Cords) on the Production Line](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10699)
*Amin Doroodchi,Danial Soleimany*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An intelligent system using YOLOv12 detects wire color sequences in network cable connectors with ~98% precision, replacing error-prone manual inspection to improve manufacturing efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional microscopic visual inspection of wire color sequences in network connectors is time-consuming, tedious, and prone to human error, leading to defective products and high costs.

Method: A YOLOv12 object detection model with single-stage architecture and attention mechanisms was trained on 2,500 microscopic images (70% train, 15% validation, 15% test) to identify and verify wire positions and color sequences in patch cords.

Result: ~98% precision for wire detection; overall mean accuracy ~95%, classification precision 99%, recall 98%.

Conclusion: The system can reliably and in real-time verify wire color sequencing on the production line without human intervention, reducing errors and improving manufacturing efficiency.

Abstract: In the production process of network cables, ensuring the correct color sequence of wire pairs inside the standard connector plays a critical role in the final performance of the cable, as any misplacement or color-ordering error can lead to defective products and impose significant costs. Traditional inspection methods based on visual examination through digital microscopes are typically time-consuming, tedious, and prone to human error. In this study, an intelligent system based on the twelfth version of the YOLO1 object detection model was developed to identify the position and verify the correct color sequence of wires in patch cords. The dataset used consisted of 2,500 images captured from microscopic views of network connectors, which were divided into 70% for training, 15% for validation, and 15% for testing. The proposed model, leveraging a single-stage architecture and attention mechanisms during learning, achieved highly accurate wire detection with approximately 98% precision. Additionally, the overall mean accuracy, classification precision, and recall were around 95%, 99%, and 98%, respectively. The results demonstrate that this system can reliably and in real time verify the correctness of wire color sequencing on the production line without the need for human intervention, thereby reducing human error and enhancing efficiency in the manufacturing process.

</details>


### [57] [SAFE-Pruner: Semantic Attention-Guided Future-Aware Token Pruning for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29662)
*Shilin Ma,Chubin Zhang,Changyuan Wang,Yuji Wang,Yue Wu,Zixuan Wang,Jingqi Tian,Zheng Zhu,Yansong Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAFE-Pruner accelerates VLA model inference for robotic control by forecasting token saliency in future layers, achieving up to 1.89x speedup with <1.7% success rate degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing visual token pruning methods rely on shallow-layer cues and risk discarding information needed by deep layers, leading to unstable acceleration.

Method: SAFE-Pruner uses semantic attention consistency to forecast token saliency in deep layers (forward-looking strategy) and an adaptive subtask division strategy to handle abrupt attention shifts.

Result: Up to 1.89x speedup, <1.7% success rate drop, outperforms SOTA by up to 1.9% in simulation and real-world settings.

Conclusion: Forward-looking pruning based on attention consistency enables more stable and effective acceleration for VLA models without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: Real-time inference of vision-language-action (VLA) models is essential for robotic control. While visual token pruning has shown strong potential for accelerating inference, most existing methods mainly base pruning decisions on shallow-layer cues and risk discarding visual information required by deep layers. To address this issue, we propose SAFE-Pruner, a plug-and-play pruning framework that incorporates attention cues of future layers into pruning decisions. Specifically, we identify semantic attention consistency, the tendency that VLA models concentrate their attention probability mass on the same semantic entity across execution steps. Based on this observation, we design a forward-looking strategy to forecast the token saliency in deep layers, which prevents the premature removal of critical tokens and leads to more stable acceleration. We further introduce an adaptive subtask division strategy to detect abrupt attention shifts, thereby improving forecasting accuracy and pruning reliability. Extensive experiments in simulation and real-world settings demonstrate that our method achieves up to 1.89x speedup with a minimal degradation in success rate of less than 1.7%, while outperforming state-of-the-art methods by up to 1.9%.

</details>


### [58] [Vector Map as Language: Toward Unified Remote Sensing Vector Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10701)
*Yinglong Yan,Yunkai Yang,Haoyi Wang,Wei Fu,Linshan Wu,Honghu Pan,Shaobo Xia,Shanghang Zhang,Hao Chen,Leyuan Fang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VecLang reformulates multiclass vector mapping as structured text generation using a GeoJSON-like language, enabling unified modeling across different geospatial entities (buildings, roads, water bodies) with a progressive vision-language framework and reinforcement learning optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vector mapping methods are category-specific: polygons poorly capture topological relations and graphs blur instance boundaries. A unified model is needed to handle heterogeneous entity structures (geometry, semantics, topology) across multiple map layers.

Method: VecLang encodes vector map elements into a GeoJSON-like structured text format, uses a progressive vision-language framework (first localizing vectorization units, then generating structured elements), and applies Hierarchical Vector Language Optimization via reinforcement learning for syntax validity, content fidelity, and map executability.

Result: VecLang handles both single-class and multiclass vector mapping, achieves strong cross-dataset and open-vocabulary generalization. VecMap-Bench contains 54K images and 800K instances.

Conclusion: Language is a flexible and expressive representation for heterogeneous map elements. VecLang offers a unified paradigm for diverse vector mapping needs with strong generalization capabilities.

Abstract: Remote sensing vector mapping aims to generate structured maps of geospatial entities, such as buildings, roads, and water bodies, from remote sensing imagery. In practice, vector maps usually contain multiple category layers and heterogeneous entity structures, requiring a unified model for diverse mapping needs. However, existing methods typically represent vector objects as polygons or graphs, making them suitable only for specific categories: polygons poorly capture topological relations, while graphs often blur instance boundaries. We observe that language, as a natural medium for human communication, offers a flexible and expressive representation that can accommodate heterogeneous map elements, including geometry, semantics, and topolog. Motivated by this insight, we propose Vector Map as Language (VecLang), a unified paradigm that reformulates multiclass vector mapping as structured text generation. VecLang encodes the common elements of different geospatial entities into a GeoJSON-like vector language, enabling cross-category modeling within a shared textual format. To generate this language reliably, we design a progressive vision-language mapping framework that first localizes vectorization units and then generates structured map elements. We further introduce Hierarchical Vector Language Optimization, which uses reinforcement learning to improve syntax validity, content fidelity, and map executability. We also build VecMap-Bench with 54K images and 800K instances, supporting training and evaluation across standard and generalization settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VecLang handles both single-class and multiclass vector mapping while achieving strong cross-dataset and open-vocabulary generalization. The model and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/yyyyll0ss/VecLang.

</details>


### [59] [Patient-Level Diagnosis of Acute Myeloid Leukemia via Deep Learning Analysis of Bone Marrow Smear](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10735)
*Yuqi Ma,Tianyi Wang,Weihua Meng,Hongru Chen,Fajin Tao,Qunxian Lu,Lin An,Xiaodong Mo,Gen Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A deep learning pipeline using YOLO-based segmentation and EfficientNet-B0 classification aggregates single-cell predictions from bone marrow smears into patient-level CBLC ratios for AML diagnosis, achieving strong internal and external validation (F1-scores 0.87–0.91) across six centers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bone marrow smear review is essential for AML diagnosis, but manual single-cell interpretation is labor-intensive and patient-level conclusions require aggregating many cellular observations, motivating an automated cell-to-patient pipeline.

Method: A fixed YOLO-based segmentation module detects cells, matches contours to expert annotations via IoU, and generates standardized crops. An EfficientNet-B0 classifier is trained via two-stage GT-to-YOLO and YOLO-to-YOLO strategy with class imbalance correction, center-border regularization, and morphology-assisted supervision. Cell predictions are aggregated into patient-level CBLC ratios.

Result: Ensemble weighted F1-scores of 0.9076, 0.8696, and 0.9124 on Centers 4, 5, and 6 respectively, demonstrating stable internal validation and external generalization across 258 patients from six centers.

Conclusion: The cell-to-patient deep learning pipeline effectively supports AML-assisted diagnosis from bone marrow smear images with robust generalization across multiple centers.

Abstract: Bone marrow smear review remains important for acute myeloid leukemia (AML) assessment, but manual single-cell interpretation is labor-intensive and patient-level diagnosis requires aggregation of many cellular observations. We present a cell-to-patient deep learning pipeline for AML-assisted diagnosis from bone marrow smear images. The study included 258 patients from six anonymized centers, including a main cohort of 169 patients from Centers 1-3 and an external validation cohort of 89 patients from Centers 4-6. A 16-category cell annotation vocabulary was used to describe the global cellular composition, including granulocytic, monocytic, erythroid, lymphoid, eosinophilic, and other cells. Rather than identifying strict AML blasts or leukemic blasts, the model targets an expert-defined composite category termed Composite Blast-like Cells (CBLC), comprising N, N1, M, M1, R, R1, J, and J1 according to the project-wide morphological standard. A fixed YOLO-based segmentation module detected cells, predicted contours were matched to expert polygon annotations by contour IoU, and standardized single-cell crops were generated. An EfficientNet-B0 classifier was trained through a two-stage GT-to-YOLO and YOLO-to-YOLO strategy with class-imbalance correction, center-border regularization, and morphology-assisted supervision. Cell-level predictions were aggregated into patient-level CBLC ratios for AML-oriented diagnostic support. The pipeline achieved stable internal validation and maintained external generalization, with ensemble weighted F1-scores of 0.9076, 0.8696, and 0.9124 on Centers 4, 5, and 6, respectively.

</details>


### [60] [DD-INR: Dynamics-Driven Implicit Neural Representation for Accelerated Whole-Brain Functional MRI Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10756)
*Qiaoxin Li,Caini Pan,Pierre-Antoine Comby,Chaithya Giliyar,Philippe Ciuciu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DD-INR is a Dynamics-Driven Implicit Neural Representation framework for accelerated fMRI reconstruction that separates static background from dynamic BOLD signals, outperforming traditional methods in image quality and activation pattern retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accelerated fMRI acquisition suffers from high k-space undersampling where task-evoked BOLD signals are small and traditional anatomical MRI reconstruction methods fail because they prioritize spatial accuracy over temporal fidelity.

Method: DD-INR splits fMRI data into a static background and a temporally varying dynamic component, representing only the dynamics with a dedicated Implicit Neural Representation (INR). It leverages incoherent time-varying sampling and a tailored spatiotemporal prior.

Result: DD-INR outperforms traditional methods in both simulation and in-vivo acquisition, demonstrating superior image quality and improved retrieval of activation patterns.

Conclusion: DD-INR provides a promising framework for accelerated fMRI reconstruction, with potential to improve sensitivity and robustness of fMRI studies within practical scan time limits.

Abstract: Accelerated acquisition of fMRI enables enhanced detection of neurovascular (BOLD) activity in the brain, but image reconstruction becomes challenging with high k-space undersampling: Task-evoked BOLD signals are small in magnitude, which traditional anatomical MRI reconstruction methods fail to recover, as they favor spatial accuracy over temporal fidelity. We present DD-INR, a Dynamics-Driven Implicit Neural Representation framework tailored for accelerated fMRI that benefits from incoherent time-varying sampling and a tailored spatiotemporal prior, outperforming traditional methods, demonstrated in simulation and in-vivo acquisition, both in terms of image quality and retrieval of activation patterns. DD-INR achieves this by splitting the fMRI data into a static background and a temporally varying dynamic component, representing only the dynamics with a dedicated INR, thereby focusing the model's capacity on activation-relevant changes while remaining compact. In general, DD-INR provides a promising framework for accelerated fMRI reconstruction, with the potential to improve the sensitivity and robustness of fMRI studies within practical scan time limits. The source code is available at https://github.com/JoosenLi/DD-INR.

</details>


### [61] [ZODS-RS -- Zero-training Oriented Detection & Segmentation for Remote Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10769)
*Zuan Gu,Tianhan Gao,Langxu Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ZODS-RS is a training-free, closed-form pipeline for horizontal-box detection and instance segmentation in aerial imagery, using DINOv3 features and SAM-style proposals with prototype purification, rotation-scale equivariant matching, and uncertainty-aware merging. It achieves competitive results on FAIR1M, xView, and a UAV dataset, with notable gains on small/crowded targets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Remote-sensing and UAV applications need models that generalize across platforms and viewpoints without task-specific training, but existing training-free pipelines struggle with oriented geometry, scale/rotation variation, crowded scenes, and fail to unify detection and segmentation.

Method: ZODS-RS builds on DINOv3 dense features and SAM-style proposals, chaining three modules: PP (prototype purification via Tyler covariance), R-SEM (rotation-scale equivariant matching with separable kernels and Hungarian assignment), and UAM (uncertainty-aware pixelwise merging with adaptive priors). A lightweight CWLA fuses multiple DINOv3 layers.

Result: FAIR1M (HBB): mAP 13.06, AP_S 2.93; xView (HBB): mAP 16.69; UAV dataset: mask mIoU 31.10, small-object AP +30.70 over Grounded-SAM.

Conclusion: ZODS-RS offers a unified, no-training solution for horizontal-box detection and instance segmentation in aerial imagery, with consistent gains on small/crowded targets and cross-domain shifts, while keeping deployment simple.

Abstract: Remote-sensing and UAV applications need models that generalize across platforms and viewpoints without task-specific training. Yet training-free pipelines often falter on oriented geometry, scale/rotation variation, and crowded ports or airfields, and rarely unify detection and segmentation. We introduce ZODS-RS, a training-free, closed-form pipeline that outputs horizontal boxes (HBB) and instance masks. Built on DINOv3 dense features and SAM-style proposals, ZODS-RS chains: PP (prototype purification via Tyler covariance), R-SEM (rotation-scale equivariant matching with separable kernels and global Hungarian assignment), and UAM (uncertainty-aware pixelwise merging with adaptive priors and optional negative prototypes). A lightweight CWLA fuses multiple DINOv3 layers. On FAIR1M (HBB) we obtain $\mathrm{mAP}_{0.50:0.95}=\mathbf{13.06}$ and $\mathrm{AP}_S=\mathbf{2.93}$ \emph{(class-averaged over ship/airplane)}; on xView (HBB) we report $\mathrm{mAP}=\mathbf{16.69}$. On our UAV dataset, ZODS-RS achieves mask $\mathrm{mIoU}=\mathbf{31.10}$ and improves small-object AP by $\mathbf{+30.70}$ over Grounded-SAM on a single 5090. This work offers a unified, \emph{no-training} solution for horizontal-box detection plus instance segmentation in aerial imagery; provides explicit closed-form formulations for PP/R-SEM/UAM tightly coupled with DINOv3; and demonstrates \emph{consistent} gains on small and crowded targets and under cross-domain shifts while keeping deployment simple.

</details>


### [62] [Spatially Selective Self-Training for Unsupervised Building Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10775)
*Wafaa I. M. Hussin,Zhi Lu,Anas M. I. Mohammed,Xiang Zhou,Ratiba A. H. Abubaker,Zhenming Peng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SST-CD is a spatially selective self-training framework for unsupervised building change detection that uses noisy temporal discrepancies as pseudo labels, trains only on reliable pixels filtered by local consistency, and incorporates a feature adapter and prototype-based decoder to outperform existing label-free methods on three benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing label-free building change detection methods follow a discrepancy-to-mask paradigm that directly uses temporal differences, frozen models, or prompt-based outputs as final change maps, without learning a task-specific detector. These approaches remain vulnerable to noisy, task-irrelevant discrepancies caused by appearance shifts, registration errors, and non-building modifications.

Method: SST-CD proposes a spatially selective self-training framework: (1) uses temporal discrepancies as candidate pseudo labels, (2) trains the detector only on spatially reliable pixels filtered by a local consistency criterion, (3) a lightweight feature adapter recalibrates bi-temporal features, and (4) a prototype-based decoder produces compact change/no-change representations.

Result: Achieves F1 scores of 83.08% on LEVIR-CD, 91.69% on WHU-CD, and 86.60% on DSIFN-CD, outperforming existing unsupervised and label-free baselines.

Conclusion: Reformulating fully label-free building change detection as end-to-end detector learning under noisy pseudo supervision is effective, and SST-CD demonstrates state-of-the-art performance among label-free methods.

Abstract: Unsupervised building change detection aims to learn building-change masks from unlabeled bi-temporal remote sensing images. Existing label-free methods often follow a discrepancy-to-mask paradigm, directly using temporal differences, frozen foundation-model responses, prompt-based outputs, or post-processing results as final change maps. Although these strategies provide annotation-free cues, they do not learn a task-specific building-change detector and remain vulnerable to the gap between generic temporal discrepancies and building-defined structural changes. In practice, such discrepancies are often noisy and task-irrelevant, as appearance shifts, registration errors, and non-building modifications can produce strong but misleading responses. To address this problem, we propose SST-CD, a spatially selective self-training framework that reformulates fully label-free building change detection as end-to-end detector learning under noisy pseudo supervision.
  SST-CD uses temporal discrepancies as candidate pseudo labels and trains the detector only on spatially reliable pixels, whose reliability is estimated by a local consistency criterion that filters inconsistent regions from supervision. To further stabilize noisy self-training, a lightweight feature adapter recalibrates bi-temporal features, while a prototype-based decoder produces compact change and no-change representations. Experiments on LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, and DSIFN-CD show that SST-CD achieves F1 scores of 83.08\%, 91.69\%, and 86.60\%, respectively, outperforming existing unsupervised and label-free baselines. Code will be made publicly available.

</details>


### [63] [From Patches to Patients: A study of the tile-to-slide performance transferability in Digital Pathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10778)
*Sofiène Boutaj,Leo Fillioux,Maria Vakalopoulou,Stergios Christodoulidis,Pierre Marza*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether tile-level linear probing can serve as a reliable proxy for slide-level performance in histopathology, avoiding costly full-pipeline evaluation of every foundation model. By benchmarking 19 models on 42 slide-level and 16 tile-level tasks, they find high correlation between tile and slide performance, suggesting tile benchmarks can efficiently shortlist candidate models before final slide-level validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Selecting the optimal foundation model for a specific clinical cohort requires multiple preprocessing steps, computationally expensive feature extraction, and training of MIL aggregators for each model. The authors aim to find a more efficient proxy to reduce this computational burden.

Method: Benchmarked 19 state-of-the-art foundation models on 42 slide-level and 16 tile-level tasks. Compared tile probing metrics against slide-level outcomes using ABMIL and Mean Pooling aggregations. Conducted sensitivity analyses on cohort sizes, tiles per slide, and task difficulty.

Result: High correlation observed between tile and slide performance across varying task difficulties. Transferability is stable across models and more influenced by cohort sizes and number of tiles per slide than by average task difficulty. Tile benchmarks reliably shortlist strong candidate models.

Conclusion: Tile-level benchmarking provides an efficient first step for narrowing down candidate models, but slide-level evaluation remains essential for final validation on clinical tasks.

Abstract: Foundation Models (FMs) have recently redefined the state-of-the-art in histopathology by providing robust representations for whole-slide image (WSI) analysis. However, selecting the optimal foundation model (FM) for a specific clinical cohort currently requires multiple preprocessing steps, followed by computationally expensive feature extraction and the training of a Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) aggregator for every model. In this work, we investigate whether efficient tile-level linear probing can serve as a reliable proxy for slide-level performance, reducing the need to run full slide-level pipelines for every candidate encoder. We benchmark 19 state-of-the-art FMs on 42 slide-level and 16 tile-level tasks, comparing tile probing metrics against slide-level outcomes using ABMIL and Mean Pooling aggregations. We observe a high correlation between tile and slide performance across varying task difficulties, indicating that encoder representation quality is the primary determinant of WSI success. Sensitivity analyses show that transferability is stable across models and is more influenced by cohort sizes and numbers of tiles per slide than by average task difficulty. We also measure the agreement in best performing models between tile and slide-level tasks, showing tile benchmarks reliably shortlist strong candidates. Overall, our study indicates that tile-level benchmarking provides an efficient and practical first step for narrowing down candidate models, while slide-level evaluation remains essential for final validation on clinical tasks.

</details>


### [64] [A Multimodal RGB and Events Dataset for Hand Detection in First-Person View](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10790)
*Bharghav Kota,Yulia Sandamirskaya*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a methodology for generating synthetic event-based hand detection datasets from existing RGB data (Egohands) using the v2e toolbox, enabling hand detection with event cameras for robotic systems while overcoming the limited training data bottleneck. The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance in multi-modal event+RGB hand detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hand detection algorithms on images suffer from motion blur and frame rate limitations in moving robotic systems, especially in low light. Event-based cameras offer high dynamic range and temporal resolution, but lack sufficient training data for object detection tasks.

Method: The authors synthesize an event-based hand dataset from the RGB Egohands dataset using the v2e toolbox, varying parameters to simulate different lighting conditions and scales. Ground truth labels are generated by fine-tuning YOLOv8 on RGB images and interpolating onto high-temporal-resolution events. The multi-modal dataset is used with existing object detection algorithms for event+RGB cameras.

Result: The proposed synthetic event-based hand dataset enables hand detection using multi-modal event+RGB algorithms with performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The methodology effectively addresses the data scarcity bottleneck for event-based hand detection, demonstrating that synthetic event data from existing RGB datasets can achieve competitive detection performance.

Abstract: Existing hand detection algorithms work on images and the detection rate is restricted by the frame rate of the camera. In hand detection applications for moving robotic systems, conventional cameras cause motion blur, especially in darker lighting conditions. We can leverage the use of event-based cameras which possess a high dynamic range, high temporal resolution, and low power consumption. Recent work has shown that using a stereo setup of an event-based and a frame-based camera improves detection accuracy and the bandwidth-latency tradeoff. The main bottleneck in using event-based cameras in object detection and recognition tasks is a relatively low amount of training data. In this work, we propose a methodology and an exemplary synthetic event-based hand dataset from an egocentric, first-person view perspective. The data is synthesized from the existing RGB Egohands dataset with the v2e toolbox. Parameters of the v2e toolbox are varied to provide versions of the dataset with different lighting conditions and scales. Ground truth detections are generated with a fine-tuned YOLOv8 model which is applied to the RGB images in the Egohands dataset and interpolated on the high-temporal resolution events. We use the multi-modal dataset to perform hand detection with existing object detection algorithms which use a multi-modal setup of event and RGB cameras and demonstrate performance comparable to the state-of-the-art.

</details>


### [65] [SCAIL-2: Unifying Controlled Character Animation with End-to-end In-Context Conditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10804)
*Wenhao Yan,Fengjia Guo,Zhuoyi Yang,Jie Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SCAIL-2 is an end-to-end framework for controlled character animation that bypasses intermediate representations (pose skeletons, masked backgrounds) by directly using driving videos as input. It introduces MotionPair-60K dataset, in-context mask conditioning, mode-specific RoPE, and Bias-Aware DPO to address data scarcity, unification, and synthetic artifacts, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior works rely on intermediate representations (pose skeletons, masked backgrounds) for motion transfer, causing information loss. The goal is to achieve direct end-to-end animation without such intermediates.

Method: (1) Direct concatenation of driving videos to the sequence for end-to-end visual information. (2) MotionPair-60K synthetic dataset for heterogeneous sub-tasks. (3) In-context mask conditioning and mode-specific RoPE for soft guidance. (4) Bias-Aware DPO for mitigating synthetic data errors.

Result: Substantially outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches across various character animation tasks.

Conclusion: End-to-end character animation is feasible without intermediate representations, and the proposed dataset and training techniques effectively bridge the gap between synthetic training and real-world performance.

Abstract: Controlled character animation requires transferring motion from a driving sequence to a reference character. Prior works heavily rely on intermediate representations, including pose skeletons to represent motion or masked background to represent environment, which inevitably leads to information loss. To address this, we present SCAIL-2, an framework that bypasses those intermediates and achieves \textbf{end-to-end} character animation. By directly concatenating driving videos to the sequence, the model can obtain all the required visual information from the input video. To address lack of end-to-end data, we unify sub-tasks of character animation with decoupled conditions and then curate a pipeline to synthesize MotionPair-60K, an end-to-end motion transfer dataset containing heterogeneous tasks of character animation. To archive the unification, we utilize in-context mask conditioning and mode-specific RoPE as soft guidance beyond textual instructions and raw visual information. To address synthetic discrepancy in detailed regions, we propose Bias-Aware DPO to construct preference items to mitigate the errors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method substantially outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches in various character animation tasks. A large subset of synthetic data as well as model weights will be released at our project page: https://teal024.github.io/SCAIL-2/.

</details>


### [66] [Deep learning for echo sounder data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10811)
*Ketil Malde*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper argues that current deep learning methods applied to underwater acoustic data have limited success because they simply recycle image-processing techniques, and calls for developing specialized deep learning approaches tailored to acoustic data properties, alongside creating standardized datasets and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite deep learning revolutionizing image and text processing, its application to underwater acoustics (echograms) has yielded modest results, motivating the need to identify why these methods fall short and how to advance the field.

Method: The paper presents a critical analysis arguing that acoustic data have intrinsic properties fundamentally different from images, requiring novel deep learning approaches rather than reusing existing models. It also identifies infrastructure gaps (lack of standard formats, datasets, and benchmarks) as key barriers.

Result: The paper does not present experimental results; it provides a perspective arguing that current approaches are inadequate and outlines necessary conditions for progress in this domain.

Conclusion: Significant advances in acoustic deep learning require developing domain-specific methods and establishing standardized, high-quality datasets with clear performance benchmarks.

Abstract: There is no doubt that over the last decade, techniques from the field of machine learning have revolutionized how we process and interpret data, especially images and text. For underwater observations acoustics is a primary source of information, and naturally, deep learning methods have been applied to echograms and other acoustics data, but so far with rather modest results. Here, we argue that due to intrinsic properties of acoustic data, substantial advances will likely require research into deep learning methods beyond mere recycling of models and techniques from image processing. Currently, the potential for breakthroughs in method development is hindered by the lack of standard data formats and organization, and even more by the lack of readily available, high quality data sets with established performance goals. To advance the field, these shortcomings should be remedied

</details>


### [67] [Earth-OneVision: Extending Remote Sensing Multimodal Large Language Models to More Sensor Modalities and Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10819)
*Miaoxin Cai,Guanqun Wang,Wei Zhang,Guangyao Zhou,Yin Zhuang,Tong Zhang,Hao Wang,He Chen,Jun Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Earth-OneVision is a 2B RS-MLLM that unifies six sensor modalities and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories in a single autoregressive framework, achieving SOTA results across benchmarks and outperforming models up to 72B parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RS-MLLMs support only a narrow range of sensor types and tasks, yielding a fragmented view of the earth and leaving cross-modal geoscientific knowledge largely unexploited.

Method: Proposes three mechanisms: Full-Granularity Vision-Language Alignment (FGVLA), Spatial-Linguistic Isomorphic Serialization (SLIS), and Progressive Cross-Modality Adaptation (PCMA); constructs MMRS-OneVision dataset with ~34M QA pairs across 6 modalities and 9 tasks.

Result: 87.52% P@0.5 on OPT-RSVG (optical visual grounding), 80.68% on SARLANG-Bench (SAR VQA), 75.74% recall on BigEarthNet-MS (multispectral classification), 81.94% MCQ accuracy on EarthMind-Bench (cross-modality reasoning).

Conclusion: With only 2B parameters, Earth-OneVision achieves competitive or SOTA results, consistently matching or outperforming 4B-72B RS-MLLMs.

Abstract: RS-MLLMs enable natural-language understanding and spatial reasoning over earth observation imagery. However, existing models support only a narrow range of sensor types and tasks, yielding a fragmented view of the earth and leaving cross-modal geoscientific knowledge largely unexploited. This work presents Earth-OneVision, a 2B RS-MLLM that unifies six sensor modalities (i.e., optical, SAR, infrared, multispectral, temporal, and video) and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories within a single autoregressive framework. Three dedicated mechanisms address three bottlenecks. Full-Granularity Vision-Language Alignment (FGVLA) aligns multi-level visual features with the multi-dimensional language space. Spatial-Linguistic Isomorphic Serialization (SLIS) unifies heterogeneous spatial outputs as autoregressive tokens. Progressive Cross-Modality Adaptation (PCMA) decomposes the compound domain gap into sequential stages, tackling the viewpoint and imaging physics gaps in turn. To support joint training, MMRS-OneVision is constructed with ~34M QA pairs spanning all six sensor modalities and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories, substantially exceeding existing RS multimodal instruction datasets. With only 2B parameters, Earth-OneVision achieves competitive or state-of-the-art results across extensive benchmarks, consistently matching or outperforming 4B-72B RS-MLLMs. It achieves 87.52% P@0.5 on the OPT-RSVG testset for optical visual grounding and 80.68% on the SAR VQA benchmark SARLANG-Bench, exceeding 7B models by over 7%. It further achieves 75.74% recall on the BigEarthNet-MS testset for multispectral classification, and 81.94% MCQ accuracy on EarthMind-Bench for cross-modality reasoning.

</details>


### [68] [HarmoView: Harmonizing Multi-View Constraints for Identity-Consistent Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10839)
*Cong Wang,Zhentao Yu,Hongmei Wang,Weicong Liang,Zixiang Zhou,Zilin Yang,Jiarong Ou,Rui Chen,Yuan Zhou,Qinglin Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HarmoView is a framework for identity-consistent video generation that uses multi-view inputs, featuring Multi-level Feature Injection, learnable proxy tokens, Jump-RoPE, and a Progressive View Curriculum to achieve state-of-the-art performance in preserving identity fidelity under large viewpoint changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current identity-consistent video generation methods struggle with appearance fidelity under large viewpoint changes. Multi-view reference inputs offer a natural solution but progress is limited by the lack of effective frameworks for multi-view inputs and scarcity of multi-view data.

Method: Three architectural refinements with a staged training curriculum: (1) Multi-level Feature Injection (MFI) anchors identity fidelity by injecting raw ViT features from frontal references; (2) Learnable proxy tokens unify heterogeneous reference layouts and resolve reference-view mismatch; (3) Jump-RoPE enables identity-wise feature isolation to reduce identity crosstalk. A Progressive View Curriculum with four-stage training (using view dropout) transitions from vanilla T2V to high-fidelity identity-consistent generation. A large-scale multi-view dataset was also constructed.

Result: Extensive evaluation on a multi-view benchmark (100 manually-curated cases, 52 identities) shows HarmoView significantly outperforms open-source baselines and matches leading closed-source engines, achieving state-of-the-art in identity-consistent video generation.

Conclusion: HarmoView effectively integrates multi-view cues through architectural refinements and staged training, solving identity preservation under large viewpoint changes, and sets a new state-of-the-art for identity-consistent video generation.

Abstract: Current identity-consistent video generation methods struggle to preserve appearance fidelity under large viewpoint changes. While introducing multi-view reference input offers a natural solution, progress remains constrained by the lack of effective frameworks for multi-view inputs and the scarcity of multi-view data. We address these challenges by proposing HarmoView, a robust framework for identity-consistent video generation that effectively integrates multi-view cues through three architectural refinements complemented by a staged training curriculum. Specifically, we first introduce Multi-level Feature Injection to anchor identity fidelity; by injecting raw ViT features from frontal references alongside text tokens via cross-attention, MFI provides persistent low-level appearance anchors that complement the high-level identity features within DiT blocks, leading to enhanced identity preservation. Then, we employ learnable proxy tokens to unify heterogeneous reference layouts across single-/multi-view settings while simultaneously resolving the reference-view mismatch problem. Jump-RoPE is further developed for identity-wise feature isolation to reduce identity crosstalk. To activate these structural capabilities while preserving the original generative priors, we propose the Progressive View Curriculum. This four-stage training strategy employs view dropout to facilitate a stable transition from vanilla T2V generation to high-fidelity, identity-persistent spatial reasoning. Furthermore, we construct a large-scale multi-view dataset to address the issue of data scarcity. Extensive evaluation on our multi-view benchmark, comprising 100 manually-curated cases spanning 52 unique identities, demonstrates that HarmoView significantly outperforms open-source baselines and matches leading closed-source engines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in identity-consistent video generation.

</details>


### [69] [LIBERO-Occ: Evaluating and Improving Vision-Language-Action Models under Scene-Induced Occlusion via Viewpoint Imagination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10862)
*Taishan Li,Jiwen Zhang,Siyuan Wang,Xuanjing Huang,Zhongyu Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VLA models fail under occlusion; authors propose LIBERO-Occ benchmark and Viewpoint Imagination (VIM) to generate complementary views for robust manipulation without extra cameras.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models assume full visibility, but real-world occlusion degrades performance. This work identifies scene-induced occlusion as a fundamental, under-explored challenge.

Method: Leverages LIBERO to create LIBERO-Occ (occlusion-oriented benchmark) and proposes Viewpoint Imagination (VIM) that generates a complementary view from occluded observations, conditioning action prediction on both observed and imagined evidence.

Result: State-of-the-art VLAs degrade significantly under occlusion. VIM improves robustness across tasks, occlusion types, and severity levels without additional deployment cameras.

Conclusion: Viewpoint imagination is a promising mechanism for perception completion under partial observability; VIM is effective and practical for real-world manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models achieve strong performance on standard manipulation benchmarks, but most evaluations assume that task-relevant objects are fully visible. This assumption often fails in realistic settings, where occlusion makes manipulation partially observable. In this paper, we study \textit{scene-induced occlusion} as a fundamental challenge for VLA models and introduce \textbf{LIBERO-Occ}, an occlusion-oriented extension of LIBERO. Experiments show that state-of-the-art VLAs suffer substantial performance degradation under occlusion. To address this issue, we propose \textbf{Viewpoint Imagination (VIM)}, which generates a complementary view from an occluded primary observation and conditions action prediction on both observed and imagined evidence. VIM improves robustness across task suites, occlusion types, and severity levels without requiring additional cameras at deployment time, suggesting that viewpoint imagination is an promising mechanism for perception completion in partially observable manipulation. Our benchmark and corresponding code are available at: \href{https://github.com/litsh/Libero-Occ}{https://github.com/litsh/Libero-Occ}.

</details>


### [70] [Schmidt Decomposition-Based Methods for Efficient Quantum Image Encoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10874)
*Ana-Maria Pangeva,Yassine Ferhi,Alexander Geng,Andreas Weinmann,Desislava Ivanova,Ali Moghiseh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper explores low-rank state approximation via Schmidt decomposition to reduce circuit complexity in quantum image encoding (FRQI, QPIE, NEQR), achieving up to 97% circuit depth reduction with minimal quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantum image encodings like FRQI, QPIE, and NEQR produce circuits with excessive gates, depth, and qubit usage, making them impractical for NISQ devices. The authors aim to reduce this complexity.

Method: Low-rank approximation via Schmidt decomposition is applied to three quantum image encoding techniques (FRQI, QPIE, NEQR). Only the most significant parts of the entanglement structure are retained for efficient state preparation.

Result: FRQI achieved a 97% reduction in circuit depth with near-perfect reconstruction (MSE ~0.27). Meaningful accuracy-resource trade-offs were observed across all three encoding methods.

Conclusion: Low-rank approximation is a promising technique for making quantum image processing practical on near-term NISQ hardware by significantly reducing resource requirements while preserving image quality.

Abstract: In quantum image processing, a fundamental step is encoding classical image data into quantum states. This can be achieved using methods such as Flexible Representation of Quantum Images (FRQI), Quantum Probability Image Encoding (QPIE), and Novel Enhanced Quantum Representation (NEQR). However, on real quantum hardware, these encodings can quickly lead to circuits with many gates, large circuit depth, and high qubit usage, which is a problem for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices. In this work, we investigate whether low-rank state approximation, formulated via Schmidt decomposition, can help reduce this complexity. The method keeps only the most significant parts of a quantum state's entanglement structure, making state preparation more efficient while preserving most of the image information. We compare the three encoding techniques in their original form and with low-rank approximation, evaluating metrics such as circuit depth, CNOT count, MSE, and visual quality of reconstructed images. The results reveal meaningful trade-offs between accuracy and resource efficiency, with the FRQI model achieving a 97 percent reduction in circuit depth while maintaining a near-perfect reconstruction (MSE of about 0.27). This demonstrates the potential of low-rank techniques for advancing practical quantum image processing on near-term hardware.

</details>


### [71] [Advancing Wood Identification in the Philippines: Utilizing the Xylorix Platform for Efficient AI Model Development and Deployment for Five Key Species](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10876)
*Rosalie C. Mendoza,Vivian C. Daracan,Arlene D. Romano,Ronniel D. Manalo,Xin Jie Tang,Yi Hong Wong,Yong Haur Tay*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This study demonstrates that non-programming wood scientists can successfully build high-performance AI models for macroscopic wood identification using the Xylorix platform, achieving near-perfect accuracy (AUC 0.969–1.000) across five Philippine hardwood species, though some species showed limitations due to small sample sizes and shared anatomical traits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Illegal logging and timber trade in the Philippines require accurate wood species identification for enforcement, but current methods are limited by the need for specialized equipment and expertise. The study aims to evaluate whether AI models can be developed by wood scientists without programming skills using the Xylorix platform.

Method: Binary classifiers were trained on 10,663 verified cross-section images from 260 specimens of five Philippine hardwood species using the Xylorix platform. Models were evaluated using specimen-level mean scoring to mirror operational field conditions, with AUC and Average Precision metrics.

Result: AUC values ranged from 0.969 (Ipil) to 1.000 (Mangium); AP values from 0.589 (Samanea) to 1.000 (Mangium). Four of five species achieved AA grade (AUC & AP ≥ 0.90). Error analysis revealed 9 false negatives from Ipil due to image artifacts and 4 false positives from shared tribal-level anatomical traits.

Conclusion: Xylorix enables non-programmers to construct operationally reliable wood identification models suitable for field deployment at supply chain checkpoints, offering a practical solution for combating illegal logging.

Abstract: Illegal logging and timber trade continue to pose significant challenges in the Philippines, where accurate wood species identification is essential for enforcement but limited by the need for specialised equipment and expertise. This study aims to evaluate whether AI models for macroscopic wood identification can be developed and deployed by wood scientists without programming expertise using the Xylorix platform, focusing on five Philippine hardwood species: Mangium (Acacia mangium Willd.), Rain Tree [Samanea saman (Jacq.) Merr.], Banuyo (Wallaceodendron celebicum Koord.), Tindalo [Afzelia rhomboidea (Blanco) Vidal], and Ipil [Intsia bijuga (Colebr.) O. Kuntze]. Binary classifiers were trained on 10,663 verified cross-section images from 260 specimens and evaluated using specimen-level mean scoring to mirror operational field conditions. Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) values ranged from 0.969 (Ipil) to 1.000 (Mangium), and Average Precision (AP) values ranged from 0.589 (Samanea) to 1.000 (Mangium). Four of five species achieved AA grade (AUC and AP both \geq 0.90); Rain Tree received AE (AUC \geq 0.90, AP < 0.60) due to AP compression from its small positive test set (3 specimens). All five classifiers rank their target specimens above non-target specimens with near-perfect fidelity. Specimen-level error analysis revealed 9 false negatives from Ipil, primarily stemming from localized image artifacts and 3 false positives for Rain Tree and 1 false positive for Tindalo caused by shared tribal-level anatomical traits. These findings demonstrate that Xylorix non-programmers can leverage the Xylorix platform to construct operationally reliable wood identification models suitable for field deployment at supply chain checkpoints.

</details>


### [72] [Listen, Look, and Learn: Learning Without Forgetting through SAM-Audio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10887)
*Avi Gupta,Nilotpal Sinha,Vishnu Raj,Sambuddha Saha,Pratik Joshi,Koteswar Rao Jerripothula,Tammam Tillo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method for Audio-Visual Class-Incremental Learning by integrating SAM-Audio's multimodal priors with guided attention and dual-level distillation to overcome catastrophic forgetting, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Audio-visual class-incremental learning is underexplored, and foundational multimodal models like SAM-Audio struggle in incremental settings despite having rich static priors.

Method: The method leverages SAM-Audio's dense audio and visual representations with a guided attention mechanism (audio contextually guides visual features) plus dual-level distillation objectives at both feature and logit levels to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.

Result: Extensive evaluations on audio-visual CIL benchmarks show the approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Integrating SAM-Audio's priors with guided attention and dual-level distillation effectively addresses the audio-visual CIL problem.

Abstract: Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) aims to continuously learn new classes without forgetting previously acquired knowledge. While recent CIL advances have spurred significant interest across various modalities, the audio-visual setting remains underexplored. Furthermore, although foundational multimodal models like SAM-Audio encapsulate rich static priors, our empirical analysis reveals that these representations struggle in incremental settings. This work bridges this gap by integrating SAM-Audio's audio-visual priors into the CIL setting. Specifically, we leverage its dense audio and visual representations and employ a novel guided attention strategy where the audio features contextually guide the visual representations. To further mitigate catastrophic forgetting, we introduce dual-level distillation objectives at both the feature and logit levels. Extensive evaluations on audio-visual CIL benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [73] [Improving Text-Instance Alignment Of Foreground Conditioned Out-Painting Via Customized Concept Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10892)
*Yihao Zhao,Xuan Han,Bin He,Mingyu You*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CCE-Diffusion is a framework that reduces artifacts in foreground-conditioned image outpainting by customizing concept embeddings to align text-derived concepts with specific visual instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing text-driven Foreground Conditioned Outpainting (FCO) methods produce artifacts—regions in synthesized backgrounds that share semantics with the foreground instance—which diminish object prominence and degrade image quality, caused by misalignment between the given instance and text-derived concept embeddings.

Method: Proposes CCE-Diffusion with three key components: (1) CCE-Module to customize concept embeddings bridging generic noun semantics and specific visual instances, (2) Instance-Aware Loss to guide module optimization, and (3) Semantic-Preserving Prompt Template to prevent embedding distortions to other prompt words.

Result: Qualitative and quantitative evaluations show CCE-Diffusion significantly reduces artifacts in outputs; the CCE-Module is plug-and-play and enhances various FCO methods.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses the artifact problem in FCO by aligning concept embeddings with specific instances, and the module can be integrated with existing methods as a plug-and-play component.

Abstract: To showcase products, merchants often incur substantial costs creating high-quality display images. Foreground Conditioned Outpainting (FCO) meets this demand, allowing users to create desired backgrounds for foreground instances at a low cost by adjusting the text prompt. However, existing text-driven FCO methods exhibit critical flaws in their outputs, most notably the presence of artifacts, which refer to regions in the synthesized background that share the same semantics as the foreground instance. Such artifacts diminish the object's prominence and degrade image quality. We attribute the issue to the misalignment between the given instance and text-derived concept embeddings. To address this, we propose the Customized Concept Embedding Diffusion (CCE-Diffusion) framework. Its core is a CCE-Module to customize concept embeddings, bridging the gap between generic noun semantics and a specific visual instance. An Instance-Aware Loss guides the module's optimization, while a Semantic-Preserving Prompt Template prevents customized embeddings from distorting other words in the prompt. Both qualitative and quantitative evaluations demonstrate that CCE-Diffusion significantly reduces artifacts in the outputs. As a plug-and-play component, the CCE-Module can integrate with various FCO methods, enhancing their performance.

</details>


### [74] [The 1st PortraitCraft Challenge: A CVPR 2026 Workshop Competition on Portrait Composition Understanding and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10894)
*Zijie Lou,Youyun Tang,Xiaochao Qu,Haoxiang Li,Ting Liu,Luoqi Liu,Xun Zhu,Zheng Zhang,Xi Chen,Miao Li,Ji Wu,Dizhe Zhang,Xian Ge,Sujia Wang,Ruiyang Zhang,Jiaming Wang,Xianshun Wang,Lu Qi,Boao Kang,Wei Zhou,Jinghui Sun,Zhenyu Yan,Jiliang Zhao,Rui Yang,Yipo Huang,Boyuan Liu,Shanglin Li,Zifan Xie,Yichen Zhang,Anlan Wang,Wenfeng Lin,Mingyu Guo,Dong Li,Xinghao Wang,Yanting Li,Shanzhao Tong,Shuai He,Qiu Zhou,Yongqi Yang,Taoyang Mu,Dianqiao Lei,Anlong Ming,Huadong Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The PortraitCraft Challenge at CVPR 2026 introduces a two-track benchmark for portrait composition understanding and generation, supported by a large-scale dataset of 50,000 curated real portrait images with multi-level supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance AI research in portrait aesthetics analysis and controllable image synthesis, moving beyond global aesthetic scoring toward structured composition understanding and generation.

Method: A unified evaluation framework with two tracks: (1) structured portrait composition understanding, and (2) portrait image generation from structured composition descriptions under explicit compositional constraints. A large-scale dataset of ~50,000 real portrait images with multi-level supervision was constructed.

Result: The paper describes the challenge setup, evaluation protocols, dataset composition, final results, and analysis of technical characteristics of submitted solutions.

Conclusion: The challenge provides a standardized and reproducible platform expected to foster progress in portrait aesthetics and controllable image generation research.

Abstract: This paper presents an overview of the inaugural PortraitCraft Challenge, held as one of the official competitions at CVPR 2026. The challenge focuses on portrait composition understanding and generation, aiming to advance AI research in portrait aesthetics analysis and controllable image synthesis. Unlike existing datasets and tasks that primarily focus on global aesthetic scoring, PortraitCraft introduces a unified evaluation framework comprising two complementary tracks. Track 1 requires models to perform structured portrait composition understanding, and Track 2 requires models to generate portrait images from structured composition descriptions under explicit compositional constraints. To support the challenge, we constructed and publicly released a large-scale portrait composition dataset consisting of approximately 50,000 curated real portrait images, providing multi-level supervision. This report describes the challenge setup, evaluation protocols, dataset composition, and final results, along with an analysis of the technical characteristics of the submitted solutions. The PortraitCraft Challenge provides a standardized and reproducible platform for research on portrait composition understanding and generation, and is expected to foster further progress in the fields of portrait aesthetics and controllable image generation.

</details>


### [75] [Pose-ICL: 3D-Aware In-Context Learning for Pose-Controllable Subject Customization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10902)
*Xuan Han,Yihao Zhao,Mingyu You*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pose-ICL is a tuning-free framework for subject customization that uses 3D-aware In-Context Learning with Surface-Anchored Position Embedding (SAPE) to achieve accurate pose control and identity consistency without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing subject customization methods struggle with accurate pose control and consistent cross-pose appearances because 2D-native backbones lack volumetric understanding of objects.

Method: Proposes Pose-ICL, a tuning-free framework using 3D-aware In-Context Learning (ICL) with multiple paired image-pose references. Core mechanism is Surface-Anchored Position Embedding (SAPE), which anchors image tokens to surface coordinates of a volumetric bounding box, providing explicit 3D awareness. Compatible with existing DiT models.

Result: Evaluated on 3D assets and real-world subjects; significantly outperforms current methods in both pose accuracy and identity consistency.

Conclusion: 3D-aware in-context learning via SAPE effectively addresses pose control challenges in subject customization, achieving better results than existing methods.

Abstract: Subject Customization is a foundational task in modern image generation. By providing a few reference images and a text prompt, users can generate images of a specific object in any desired scene. However, existing methods still struggle to achieve effective pose control for customized subjects. In practice, they often exhibit inaccurate poses or inconsistent cross-pose appearances. These limitations suggest that understanding objects in a volumetric manner remains a significant challenge for 2D-native backbones. To address this challenge, we propose Pose-ICL, a tuning-free framework that leverages 3D-aware In-Context Learning (ICL) to directly adapt to new subjects through multiple paired image-pose references. Its core mechanism,Surface-Anchored Position Embedding (SAPE), equips the model with explicit 3D awareness by anchoring image tokens to the surface coordinates of a volumetric bounding box. Dedicated refinements ensure its seamless compatibility with existing DiT models. Extensive evaluations on both 3D assets and real-world subjects demonstrate that Pose-ICL significantly outperforms current methods in both pose accuracy and identity consistency.

</details>


### [76] [Beyond Model Size: Probing the Gaps in Visual in-Context Learning by Training a Tiny Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10905)
*Sunil Khatri,Steven Landgraf,Markus Ulrich,Simon Reiß*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper stress-tests large Visual In-Context Learning (VICL) models by training a tiny 1M-parameter model on 70K images and comparing it against 7,000× larger VICL models, revealing fundamental gaps in how adaptive capabilities are measured across task encoding, pre-training tasks, and evaluation metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether model- and data-scaling are truly essential for in-context learning in vision models, by challenging large VICL models with an extreme counterexample of a tiny capacity-capped model.

Method: Train a tiny VICL model with only 1 million parameters on 70,000 images, and compare it against 7,000× larger VICL models across three adaptive settings: (1) small distribution shifts, (2) unseen task encodings, and (3) completely new tasks.

Result: The tiny model's competitive performance reveals significant gaps in current VICL evaluation, including issues with how tasks are encoded, which tasks are used in pre-training, and the choice of metrics.

Conclusion: Current VICL benchmarking has fundamental flaws in evaluating adaptive capabilities, underscoring the need for innovation in evaluation methodologies.

Abstract: Visual in-Context Learning (VICL) aims at making progress towards adaptive vision models, that can -- based on a few examples -- adapt to a new task at test-time. With the history of in-context learning in natural language processing research, where large, parameter-heavy models are in use, one pathway that current VICL methods take is model- and data-scaling as key ingredients. Yet, it is not clear, whether these ingredients are the key for in-context learning to take shape in vision models. To stress-test such large models, we challenge them with an extreme counterexample: we train a tiny visual in-context model with merely $1$ million parameters and a modest amount of $70,000$ images. We compare the results of this severely capacity capped tiny model to $7,000\times$ larger VICL models in different adaptive settings, (1) on image data with small distribution shifts, (2) on unseen task encodings and (3) on a completely new task, i.e., the setting VICL envisions. With the chasm of training resources between the tiny- and large models, our experiments showcase a lack in how adaptive capabilities are measured, with respect to how tasks are encoded, which tasks were used in pre-training and the choice of metrics. These gaps in current VICL benchmarking underscore a need for innovation in evaluation of adaptive capabilities.

</details>


### [77] [PENet+: A Lightweight Residual Transformer Framework for Efficient Image Steganalysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10939)
*Jincheol AN,Dongsu Kim,Haneol Jang,YoungJoon Yoo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PENet+ is a lightweight image steganalysis framework that retains PENet's core structure while achieving up to 45.5% fewer parameters and ~97% fewer FLOPs via channel narrowing, an activation-aware HPF stem with a balanced SRM-Gabor filter subset, MobileNetV2-style backbone replacement, and PReLU activation, with minimal accuracy loss on ALASKA2 JPEG benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing residual Transformer-based steganalysis (e.g., PENet) achieves high detection accuracy but suffers from excessive computational and memory demands, hindering deployment in resource-constrained environments such as edge devices or real-time forensic systems.

Method: (1) Classifier-streamlining that progressively narrows SPP-to-FC1 channels; (2) Activation-aware HPF stem with a balanced top-K subset of SRM and Gabor filters; (3) Replacing PENet's backbone with MobileNetV2-style inverted residual blocks; (4) Using PReLU to preserve negative responses for weak stego signal capture.

Result: On a disjoint ALASKA2 JPEG QF90 protocol at 512x512 resolution, PENet+ achieves up to 45.5% fewer parameters and ~97% fewer FLOPs than the re-evaluated PENet baseline, with a balanced K=31 filter configuration matching or surpassing heavier settings.

Conclusion: PENet+ offers a computationally efficient direction for resource-constrained steganalysis while maintaining discriminative performance, though device-level latency and power measurements remain to be assessed in future work.

Abstract: Image steganalysis, the detection of hidden information embedded in digital images, is a core component of modern cybersecurity and digital forensics. Recent residual Transformer architectures, such as the Pixel-Difference-Convolution and Enhanced-Transformer-Network (PENet) [1], achieve strong detection accuracy, but their computational and memory demands hinder deployment in resource-constrained settings. We present PENet+, a lightweight steganalysis framework that preserves PENet's discriminative structure while substantially improving efficiency. Rather than redesigning or compressing the attention blocks, we retain PENet's self-attention topology for reproducibility and add a classifier-streamlining stage that progressively narrows the SPP-to-FC1 input channels (SPP: spatial pyramid pooling; FC1: first fully connected layer), yielding large reductions in parameters and FLOPs with negligible accuracy loss. We further refine the high-pass-filter (HPF) stem with an activation-aware mechanism that aggregates HPF responses early and selects a balanced SRM-Gabor top-K subset, and we replace PENet's backbone with a MobileNetV2-style inverted residual network. A balanced configuration with K=31 filters (16 Gabor + 15 SRM) matches or surpasses heavier settings at lower compute. Finally, we motivate PReLU from a steganalysis standpoint, arguing that preserving negative responses helps capture weak stego cues that ReLU suppresses. On a disjoint ALASKA2 JPEG QF90 protocol at 512x512 resolution (5,000 cover images for training, validation, and internal testing; a separate 19,000-cover evaluation set), PENet+ achieves up to 45.5% fewer parameters and about 97% fewer FLOPs than the re-evaluated PENet baseline, offering a computationally efficient direction for resource-constrained steganalysis. Device-level latency and power measurements remain future work.

</details>


### [78] [Democratising Camera Trap AI: An Open-Source Model for Detecting UK Mammals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10940)
*Paul Fergus,Philip Stephens,Russell A. Hill,Lee Oliver,Katie Appleby,Sarah Beatham,Naomi Davies Walsh,Stuart Nixon,Naomi Matthews,Chris Sutherland,Kelly Hitchcock*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An open-source YOLO26x object detection model for 28 UK mammal/bird species and 3 utility classes (48,165 labeled instances) achieves 0.984 mAP@0.5 and 0.956 mAP@0.5-0.95, released under non-commercial license to provide a free alternative to commercial camera trap AI platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Camera trap AI is often locked behind commercial platforms or trained on non-UK fauna, creating barriers for British ecologists. The authors aim to remove these barriers by releasing a free, open-source model specifically for UK species.

Method: Trained a YOLO26x detector on 48,165 labeled instances of 31 classes (28 UK species + humans, calibration poles, vehicles) from multiple sites over a decade via Conservation AI and Trap Tracker, using an 80/10/10 class-stratified train/val/test split.

Result: 0.984 mAP@0.5 (0.956 mAP@0.5-0.95), precision 0.988, recall 0.965 on validation; per-species confidence 0.96-0.99 on test set; 0.17% false-negative rate concentrated in night/distant/occluded images.

Conclusion: The model provides a strong open-source alternative to commercial camera trap AI for UK biodiversity monitoring, but performance at entirely new sites requires future validation.

Abstract: Camera traps have become a cornerstone of biodiversity monitoring, but the artificial intelligence that turns vast quantities of images into usable ecological data is often locked behind commercial platforms or trained on fauna that does not match that of the British Isles. In an attempt to remove barriers and increase uptake, we release an open-source object detection model for 31 classes, 28 common UK mammal and bird species, plus utility classes for humans, calibration poles, and vehicles, drawn from a curated dataset of 48,165 labelled instances assembled from multiple sites over a decade of operational deployment through Conservation AI and its successor, Trap Tracker. The model, a YOLO26x detector trained and tested on an 80/10/10 class-stratified split, achieves a mean Average Precision of 0.984 at Intersection over Union (IoU) of 0.5 (0.956 at IoU 0.5-0.95) on the held-out validation set, with precision 0.988 and recall 0.965. On an unseen held-out test split, mean per-species confidence ranged from 0.96 to 0.99 across the 31 classes, with a 0.17% false-negative rate concentrated in difficult night-time, distant, or occluded images. These metrics are from data from the same pool of sites and cameras as training, so performance at entirely new sites is left to future work. We release the trained weights in ONNX format under a non-commercial licence, with local desktop and real-time camera support, aimed explicitly at ecologists with no machine-learning experience. This release is a deliberate counterweight to the multiple paid for models that have developed over the last decade.

</details>


### [79] [Quo Vadis, Visual In-Context Learning? A Unified Benchmark Across Domains and Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10967)
*Pradnya Halady,Jiale Wei,Zdravko Marinov,Alexander Jaus,Simon Reiß*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces VIBE, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating visual in-context learning models across diverse domains and tasks, revealing key limitations and failure modes through systematic evaluation of 6 models on 106 dataset-task combinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous evaluations of visual in-context learning models were limited to narrow setups that mirrored pre-training tasks/domains where adaptation wasn't truly needed, so the authors constructed a broader benchmark to properly assess adaptive capabilities.

Method: They built VIBE (Visual In-Context BEnchmark) covering diverse imaging domains and tasks, tested 6 models on 14 datasets and 12 tasks (106 dataset-task combinations) under a unified, reproducible one-shot evaluation protocol.

Result: The evaluation uncovered key insights on the state of visual in-context learning, including limitations, systematic failure modes, and promising directions for future research.

Conclusion: Visual in-context models still have significant limitations when faced with truly new distributions, and VIBE provides a standardized evaluation framework to drive progress in this area.

Abstract: Visual in-context learning has been proposed as a pathway towards dynamic models that can generate predictions based on a provided context and thereby can adapt to new vision tasks at test-time. Yet, the evaluation of the adaptation capabilities of these models has been limited to narrow setups that mainly mirror tasks or image domains from pre-training for which real adaptation is not required. We address this gap by constructing a broad Visual In-Context BEnchmark (VIBE) with a focus on diverse imaging domains and a wide range of tasks. With this, we are able to get a much clearer picture of the adaptive capabilities of visual in-context models when faced with new image- and task distributions. We stress test six models on $14$ datasets and $12$ tasks (in total, we explore $106$ dataset-task combinations) and compare them under a unified, reproducible evaluation protocol, in an one-shot setting. Our evaluation uncovers key insights on the state of visual in-context learning, including limitations, systematic failure modes and promising directions. To foster broader evaluation, we will openly release our VIBE toolkit.

</details>


### [80] [AnimaSpark: A Feed-Forward Method for Animating Arbitrary 3D Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10988)
*Yiming Zhao,Haoyu Sun,Aoyu Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AnimaSpark generates category-agnostic 3D animations from static 3D models by rendering mesh/skeleton into images, using video generation + keypoint tracking to capture 2D joint motions, then lifting them to 3D, achieving faster and higher-quality results than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current category-agnostic 3D animation generation suffers from slow inference, poor motion quality, and weak text-prompt adherence, leaving the field reliant on labor-intensive manual artistry.

Method: (1) Render rigged static 3D model into multi-layered image representations (mesh + skeleton). (2) Feed into a video generation model. (3) Apply keypoint tracking on generated video to capture skeletal joint motion in 2D. (4) Distill planar translations/rotations and lift them from 2D to 3D to animate the character.

Result: Superior performance over state-of-the-art methods in text-motion alignment, motion quality, and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: AnimaSpark effectively overcomes key bottlenecks in category-agnostic 3D animation generation, offering a fast, high-quality, and text-adherent alternative to manual artistry.

Abstract: While recent advancements in generative AI have substantially accelerated static 3D model creation workflows, the synthesis of category-agnostic 3D animations remains a significant bottleneck in 3D asset production. Current methods for category-agnostic animation generation exhibit critical limitations in inference speed, motion quality, and adherence to textual prompts, thereby leaving the process dependent on labor-intensive manual artistry. To address these challenges, this paper introduces AnimaSpark, a novel pipeline for category-agnostic 3D animation generation. Our approach is motivated by the key insight that for many fundamental motions in the 3D world, the corresponding joint transformations can often be effectively modeled within a two-dimensional subspace. The pipeline begins by rendering a rigged static 3D model into multi-layered image representations of its mesh and skeleton, which are subsequently fed into a video generation model. We then employ a keypoint tracking algorithm on the generated video to capture the motion of the skeletal joints projected onto the camera's viewing plane. In the final stage, we distill the planar translations and rotations from these tracked keypoints and lift them from the 2D domain into 3D space to animate the character. Comprehensive evaluations reveal that our method achieves superior performance over existing state-of-the-art techniques across key metrics, including text-motion alignment, quality of motion, and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [81] [IPSM-Bench: A New Intermediate Phase Segmentation Benchmark in Microstructure Images of Zinc-Based Absorbable Biomaterials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11001)
*Jinglin Xu,Shangyan Zhao,Jiabo Wang,Xinghong Mu,Yulong Lei,Jiacheng Zhang,Hongbo Sun,Yageng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces IPSM-Bench, the largest dataset for zinc-alloy intermediate phase segmentation, and proposes SCoP-SAM, a Spatial Context Prior-guided SAM method that leverages gradient and grayscale properties to improve segmentation. The method achieves SOTA performance and generalizes well to other alloy scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intermediate phase segmentation in zinc alloy microstructures faces challenges including scarce annotated datasets, low contrast, difficulty detecting small targets, and heterogeneous morphologies, motivating the need for better datasets and methods.

Method: The authors construct IPSM-Bench (largest zinc-alloy intermediate phase segmentation dataset) and propose SCoP-SAM, which captures spatial context priors from gradient structure and grayscale properties of intermediate phases, then integrates these priors into the entire SAM encoding-decoding process.

Result: SCoP-SAM achieves state-of-the-art performance on IPSM-Bench and demonstrates strong generalization to other public alloy benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed dataset and method effectively advance zinc alloy microstructure analysis, with SCoP-SAM showing both superior performance and broad applicability across different alloy scenarios.

Abstract: Zinc-based alloys are indispensable emerging absorbable metallic biomaterials, and their macroscopic performance is governed by microstructural characteristics. Intermediate phases-key microstructural constituents-are pivotal in regulating mechanical and functional properties. However, intermediate phase segmentation in zinc alloy microstructures faces formidable challenges: scarce annotated datasets, low contrast, difficulty detecting small targets, and heterogeneous morphologies. To this end, we construct IPSM-Bench, the largest high-quality dataset for zinc-alloy intermediate phase segmentation. Furthermore, we propose SCoP-SAM, a new Spatial Context Prior-guided SAM method that leverages the gradient structure and grayscale properties of intermediate phases to capture spatial context priors and incorporates them into the entire SAM encoding-decoding process, improving segmentation performance.
