### [159] [Fundamentals of NOMA in Low-Earth Orbit Coordinated Multi-Satellite Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10301)
*Xiangyu Li,Bodong Shang,Junchao Ma,Qingqing Wu,Jie Feng,Deshuang Huang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analyzes a CoMS-NOMA framework for satellite networks, using stochastic geometry to model coverage and spectrum efficiency under inter-/intra-satellite interference and imperfect SIC. Results show that more satellites don't always help; near-perfect SIC offers marginal gains over reasonably good SIC; CoMS-NOMA achieves up to 270% coverage improvement and 56% spectral efficiency gain over conventional schemes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To jointly enhance coverage, capacity, and spectrum efficiency for satellite networks by integrating Coordinated multi-satellite (CoMS) transmission with non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA), enabling more efficient, reliable, and energy-efficient multi-user access.

Method: Stochastic geometry-based modeling to derive intermediate angle/distance relationships, combined signal power distributions, and coverage/spectrum performance analysis under inter- and intra-satellite interference with imperfect SIC.

Result: Increasing cooperative satellites does not always improve performance; near-perfect SIC offers only slight benefits over reasonably good SIC; CoMS-NOMA achieves up to 270% coverage improvement and 56% sum spectral efficiency gain over orthogonal and single-satellite baselines.

Conclusion: CoMS-NOMA shows strong potential for green, energy-efficient satellite networking, with proper power allocation being critical for realizing gains.

Abstract: Coordinated multi-satellite (CoMS) transmission and non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) are envisioned to jointly enhance coverage, capacity, and spectrum efficiency for satellite networks. Their integration into a unified CoMS-NOMA framework will allow more efficient, reliable, and energy-efficient multi-user access. This paper investigates the downlink performance of CoMS-NOMA networks from a system-level perspective, in which multiple satellites cooperatively serve multiple users via NOMA. Leveraging tools from stochastic geometry, related angles and distances in CoMS-NOMA are first derived as intermediate results. Then, we obtain the combined signal power distributions and analyze coverage and spectrum performance under both inter- and intra-satellite interference, accounting for potential imperfect successive interference cancellation (SIC). The analytical model is validated across a range of system parameters, including the number of satellites, service region angle, error-propagation factor, and power allocation coefficients. Numerical results indicate that increasing the number of cooperative satellites does not always improve coverage and spectrum efficiency. Additionally, while a higher main-lobe gain improves coverage, a near-perfect SIC provides only slightly greater benefits than a reasonably good SIC. With properly selected power allocation coefficients, CoMS-NOMA achieves up to a 270% improvement in coverage and a 56% gain in sum spectral efficiency, compared with conventional orthogonal and single-satellite schemes, indicating potential for green, energy-efficient satellite networking.

</details>


### [160] [Simplified Temporal Convolutional-Based Channel Estimation for a WiFi Vehicular Communication Channel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10511)
*Simbarashe Aldrin Ngorima,Albert Helberg,Marelie Davel*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes DPA-TCN, a simplified Temporal Convolutional Network for channel estimation in vehicular communication. It reduces model complexity by ~65% compared to LSTM-DPA-TA while achieving comparable bit error rate performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IEEE 802.11p pilot signals are insufficient for accurate channel estimation in high-mobility vehicular scenarios. Data pilot-aided estimation suffers from demapping errors.

Method: A simplified Temporal Convolutional Network (DPA-TCN) trained on mixed SNR datasets, using data pilot-aided estimation approach.

Result: Achieves bit error rate comparable to LSTM-DPA-TA while reducing model complexity by approximately 65%.

Conclusion: DPA-TCN is an effective and computationally efficient solution for vehicular channel estimation, offering significant complexity reduction without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: Channel estimation in vehicular communication is a crucial element in the advancement of intelligent transportation systems. However, the use of pilot signals in the IEEE 802.11p standard is insufficient for accurate channel estimation in high-mobility scenarios. Data pilot-aided (DPA) estimation helps address this, but suffers from demapping errors. We propose a simplified Temporal Convolutional Network-based estimator (DPA-TCN) trained on a mixed signal-to-noise ratio dataset to improve estimation performance and reduce computational complexity. Our DPA-TCN estimator achieves a bit error rate comparable to a state-of-the-art long-short-term memory network with DPA and temporal averaging (LSTM-DPA-TA) while reducing the complexity of the model by approximately 65%.

</details>


### [161] [Complex VAE with Heavy-Tailed Likelihood for Radar Target Detection in Sea Clutter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10540)
*Ting Bai,Jun Tang,Yuxin Xu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: An unsupervised complex-valued VAE is proposed for maritime radar target detection, using Student-t likelihood and amplitude error constraints to handle heavy-tailed sea clutter, achieving CFAR-constrained detection improvements over baselines on measured data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sea clutter exhibits heavy-tailed, spike-prone characteristics, and labeled target data is scarce, making supervised detection methods challenging. An unsupervised approach that learns from clutter-only data is needed.

Method: The model uses a complex-valued VAE with in-phase/quadrature (I/Q) components as input, a Student-t negative log-likelihood for robust reconstruction of heavy-tailed errors, and a time-domain amplitude error constraint. Detection is based on reconstruction deviation with a CFAR threshold via empirical quantile from clutter validation set.

Result: On measured sea-clutter data, the proposed method consistently outperforms MF, AMF, and a real-valued β-VAE under CFAR constraints.

Conclusion: The unsupervised complex-valued VAE effectively addresses heavy-tailed clutter and data scarcity, offering improved radar target detection performance with CFAR guarantees.

Abstract: To address the heavy-tailed, spike-prone nature of sea clutter and the scarcity of labeled target data, an unsupervised complex-valued variational autoencoder (VAE) for maritime radar target detection is proposed. In implementation, each complex baseband slow-time sequence is represented by its in-phase and quadrature components, and the model learns their joint reconstruction from clutter-only data. A Student-\(t\) negative log-likelihood is adopted to capture heavy-tailed reconstruction errors while reducing sensitivity to outliers during clutter learning. In addition, a time-domain amplitude error constraint is introduced to penalize slow-time magnitude mismatch in the reconstruction. At inference, reconstruction deviation is used as the detection statistic, and the decision threshold is set via an empirical quantile estimated from a clutter-only validation set to enforce a constant false-alarm rate (CFAR). Experiments on measured sea-clutter data show that detection performance is consistently improved over MF, AMF, and a real-valued \(β\)-VAE under CFAR constraints.

</details>


### [162] [Information Bottleneck Meets Quantization: Finite Rate Analysis and Optimal Designs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10869)
*Francesco Binucci,Paolo Banelli*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analyzes quantization effects on Gaussian Information Bottleneck (GIB) representations and proposes task-oriented quantization designs that jointly optimize finite-rate latent representations, showing significant gains over heuristic approaches, with extensions to non-Gaussian settings via modified VAE cost functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The optimal GIB solution produces continuous-valued latent representations, but practical systems require finite-bit storage/representation, creating a gap between theory and practice that this paper addresses.

Method: The authors (1) theoretically analyze scalar and vector quantization effects on GIB representations, (2) reformulate GIB optimization under finite-rate constraints for task-oriented quantization, and (3) extend to non-Gaussian settings by modifying VAE cost functions for IB-inspired vector quantizers.

Result: Simulation results on MMSE regression problems confirm significant gains of the proposed task-oriented quantization designs over heuristic or separate quantization approaches.

Conclusion: Task-oriented quantization designs outperform standard separate quantization of GIB latent representations, and the approach extends effectively to non-Gaussian settings via modified VAE frameworks.

Abstract: The Information Bottleneck (IB) is a well established framework that looks for a latent compact representation of a data source, by trading rate and data-size representation, for information accuracy with respect to another target data. The Gaussian IB (GIB) is its simple closed form solution, when the target is jointly Gaussian with the source. Actually, in many practical problems the latent representation has to be stored or represented by a finite number of bits, while the optimal (G)IB solution has not. First, this manuscript theoretically analyzes the effect of scalar and vector quantization of the GIB latent representation, and its impact on the (dis)informativeness with respect to the target data. Then, task-oriented quantization designs are proposed by (jointly) reformulating the GIB optimization problem under a finite-rate constraint on the latent representation. Simulation results on MMSE regression problems confirm the effectiveness of the proposed quantization designs, which show significant gains with respect to more heuristic, or separate, quantization designs of the standard GIB latent representation. Finally, the paper extends the task-oriented philosophy to non-Gaussian settings, by properly modifying the cost function used in variational auto-encoders (VAEs) of IB-inspired vector quantizers.

</details>


### [163] [Personalized Deep Learning for Short-Term Forecasting of Impending Atrial Fibrillation from Continuous Wearable ECG Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10900)
*Jangwon Suh,Soonil Kwon,Jungmin Ko,Yun Kwan Kim,Hee Seok Song,Eue-Keun Choi,Wonjong Rhee*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Personalizing a global deep learning model via fine-tuning on individual wearable ECG data significantly improves short-term (5-minute) forecasting of impending atrial fibrillation, achieving AUROCs of 0.711 vs 0.614, with benefits scaling with more patient-specific data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous wearable ECG monitoring is increasingly used for AF surveillance, but inter-patient ECG variability makes forecasting impending AF challenging. The authors investigated whether personalizing a global model via fine-tuning on individual ECG signals could improve performance.

Method: A global model trained on ICENTIA11K was fine-tuned for personalization across three cohorts (ICENTIA11K, IRIDIA-AF, MobiCARE). Models processed 60-second ECG segments with a 5-minute forecast horizon. Impacts of adaptation data volume and ECG features (heart rate, RMSSD) were analyzed.

Result: Personalized models outperformed the global model (AUROCs 0.711 vs 0.614 in ICENTIA11K; 0.686 vs 0.585 in MobiCARE). Personalization benefits increased with more fine-tuning data. Pre-AF episodes showed elevated heart rates and RMSSD. Feature attributions highlighted PACs and short SVTs as clinically relevant precursors.

Conclusion: Adapting deep learning models with patient-specific wearable ECG data significantly enhances short-term AF forecasting, supporting timely preventive interventions and improved AF management in ambulatory monitoring environments.

Abstract: Background and Objective: Continuous wearable electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring is increasingly used for ambulatory arrhythmia surveillance, yet forecasting impending atrial fibrillation (AF) is challenged by inter-patient ECG variability. This study investigated whether personalizing a global model via fine-tuning on an individual's ECG signals improves short-term forecasting of impending AF. Methods: A global model trained on the ICENTIA11K dataset was compared against personalized models fine-tuned across three cohorts: ICENTIA11K, IRIDIA-AF, and MobiCARE. Following preprocessing, models processed 60-second ECG segments for a five-minute forecast horizon. We evaluated the impact of adaptation data volume and analyzed ECG features, such as heart rate and RMSSD. Results: Personalized models significantly outperformed the global model, achieving AUROCs of 0.711 vs. 0.614 in ICENTIA11K and 0.686 vs. 0.585 in MobiCARE. Personalization benefits increased with the amount of patient-specific fine-tuning data. While the global model's accuracy rose as AF onset approached, personalized models in the two external cohorts exhibited distinct temporal dynamics, which may indicate the capture of patient-specific characteristics less dependent on proximity to the AF event. Pre-AF episodes showed elevated heart rates and RMSSD. Feature attributions highlighted clinically relevant precursors, including frequent premature atrial complexes (PACs) and short supraventricular tachycardias (SVTs). Conclusions: Adapting deep learning models with patient-specific wearable ECG data significantly enhances short-term forecasting of impending AF. This personalized framework supports timely preventive interventions and improved AF management in ambulatory monitoring environments.

</details>


### [164] [Multi-Channel Soil Moisture Measurement: High Accuracy and Low Crosstalk Through Optical-Semiconductor Based Differential Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11020)
*Thomas Maier,Charlotte Rohleder,Lukas Kamm,Philipp Dauner,Jan Pinski,Bjoern M. Eskofier*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a compact multi-depth capacitive soil moisture probe with differential activation and optically coupled transistor bridges, achieving improved accuracy, reduced temperature sensitivity, and strong sensing volume confinement for agricultural and urban monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Soil moisture measurement remains unreliable due to heterogeneous soils, limited sensing volumes, temperature drift, and parasitic inter-channel coupling, motivating the need for a more robust and accurate sensing solution.

Method: The probe extends a parallel-plate geometry with differential activation to suppress stray capacitances, uses an equivalent-circuit model to quantify parasitic effects, employs optically coupled transistor bridges to isolate sensing layers, and converts raw capacitance to water content via established calibration models.

Result: Laboratory results show a fourfold reduction in temperature sensitivity, strong sensing volume confinement, and improved repeatability in heterogeneous soils. Field validation demonstrates high accuracy and precision comparable to widely used instruments.

Conclusion: The proposed probe offers a practical and scalable solution for multi-depth soil moisture monitoring in agricultural and urban environments.

Abstract: Soil moisture measurement plays a key role in irrigation and environmental management. Yet it remains unreliable due to heterogeneous soils, limited sensing volumes, temperature drift, and parasitic inter-channel coupling. This work presents a compact multi-depth capacitive probe that extends a parallel-plate geometry from previous work with differential activation to suppress stray capacitances and improve accuracy. An equivalent-circuit model quantifies parasitic effects, and optically coupled transistor bridges isolate each sensing layer. Raw capacitance is converted to volumetric water content and plant-available water using established calibration models. Laboratory results show a fourfold reduction in temperature sensitivity, strong confinement of the sensing volume, and improved repeatability in heterogeneous soils. Field validation against reference sensors demonstrates high accuracy and precision comparable to widely used instruments, enabling a practical and scalable solution for agricultural and urban soil-moisture monitoring.

</details>


### [165] [DMT: Demographic Conditioning, Morphology-Enhanced Transformer for Cuffless Blood Pressure Estimation from PPG Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11125)
*Yidan Shen,Neville Mathew,Maham Rahimi,Deependra Dhakal,George Zouridakis,Xin Fu,Renjie Hu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Transformer-based network for cuffless blood pressure estimation from PPG signals, using self-attention, demographic-conditioned FiLM modulation, and an auxiliary morphology head to improve accuracy and reduce reliance on shortcuts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PPG-based BP models rely on amplitude-dominated shortcuts, ignore long-range dependencies across cardiac cycles, and incorporate demographic covariates only via late fusion, limiting subject-specific representation learning.

Method: A Transformer network with self-attention for long-range dependencies, FiLM-style feature modulation conditioned on demographics applied through attention and feed-forward sublayers, and an auxiliary morphology head to guide attention to BP-relevant waveform morphology.

Result: Achieves MAE of 4.56 mmHg for systolic BP and 2.62 mmHg for diastolic BP on PulseDB, reducing errors by 47% and 50% compared to demographic-enhanced PPG baselines.

Conclusion: The lightweight, single-sensor model supports scalable and clinically grounded cuffless BP estimation in calibration-enabled deployment settings.

Abstract: Blood pressure (BP) is a key marker for cardiovascular risk assessment and therapeutic decision-making, and Photoplethysmography (PPG) enables low-cost, wearable-friendly cuffless BP estimation. However, even with recent progress, many PPG-based models are trained with BP regression alone and may rely on amplitude-dominated shortcuts. In addition, demographic covariates that systematically modulate vascular compliance are often incorporated only via late fusion, limiting subject-specific representation learning. We propose a Transformer-based network for cuffless BP estimation from PPG signal, leveraging self-attention to capture long-range dependencies across multiple cardiac cycles. To account for subject-specific vascular differences, the model is conditioned on demographics via FiLM-style feature modulation applied through the attention and feed-forward sublayers of Transformer blocks. In addition, we add an auxiliary morphology head to guide the model to attend to BP-relevant waveform morphology associated with arterial stiffness and wave reflection. Under calibration-based evaluation protocols on the large-scale PulseDB dataset, the proposed method achieves MAE of 4.56 mmHg for systolic BP and 2.62 mmHg for diastolic BP, reducing errors by 47% and 50% compared with prior demographic-enhanced PPG baselines. The resulting lightweight, single-sensor model supports scalable and clinically grounded cuffless BP estimation in calibration-enabled deployment settings.

</details>


### [166] [Pre-Fault Voltage Discrimination and Time-Domain Protection for Distribution Networks with Inverter-Based Resources](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11135)
*Junyuan Zhao,François Bouffard,Géza Joós*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a pre-fault voltage discrimination (PVD) strategy to enable fast and reliable time-domain fault detection in distribution networks with inverter-based resources, overcoming limitations of traditional overcurrent protection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional phasor-based overcurrent protection (e.g., ANSI 51) fails in distribution networks with high penetration of IBRs because IBRs lack sufficient short-circuit current sourcing capacity, making alternative protection approaches necessary.

Method: The authors propose a pre-fault voltage discrimination (PVD) strategy that detects faults by discriminating between actual faults and normal switching/transformer inrush disturbances. This is integrated with time-domain protection principles to create a simple yet effective fault detection algorithm.

Result: Offline simulation experiments and controller hardware-in-the-loop (CHIL) real-time simulation validated the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm against various fault and normal switching events. The PVD strategy enables faster fault detection without compromising security or dependability.

Conclusion: The PVD-based time-domain protection approach offers a promising alternative to traditional overcurrent protection for distribution networks containing IBRs, achieving both speed and reliability in fault detection.

Abstract: The increasing proliferation of inverter-based resources (IBRs) in distribution networks is presenting a major challenge for phasor-based overcurrent protection. This challenge stems from IBRs' lack of short-circuit current sourcing capacity. As a result, traditional overcurrent protection functions (e.g., ANSI 51) are inadequate in such scenarios, and warrant alternative approaches. Time-domain protection, for example, shows promise in overcoming this challenge. In this paper we propose a pre-fault voltage discrimination (PVD) strategy whose role is to detect faults and discriminate normal switching and transformer inrush disturbances from actual faults. The use of PVD allows for the design of a simple, yet effective fault detection algorithm by using time-domain protection principles for distribution networks containing IBRs. The introduction of PVD provides for faster fault detection without reducing security and dependability. Offline simulation experiments and controller hardware-in-the-loop real-time simulation validate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm against various fault and normal switching events.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [167] [Support sufficiency as action-sufficient compression: a single-cycle rate-regret formulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09858)
*Mark Walsh*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper formalizes robust decision-making as action-sufficient compression, where support states are compressed to only preserve distinctions needed for optimal actions under a given consequence geometry. It defines exact and approximate sufficiency via policy equivalence and regret, showing the optimal compression follows a rate-distortion Gibbs form with regret distortion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust decision-making requires compression because a full support state cannot preserve its entire structure at the point of action. The paper seeks to formalize which distinctions in a support state must be retained for effective decision-making under a given consequence geometry.

Method: The paper formalizes support sufficiency as action-sufficient compression. For exact sufficiency, it uses quotient of support space by policy equivalence. For approximate sufficiency, it defines bounded expected policy regret, yielding a rate-regret problem with source H, reproduction alphabet A, and consequence-sensitive regret distortion. The optimal stochastic action channel follows the standard rate-distortion Gibbs form.

Result: The coarsest exactly action-sufficient compression is the quotient of support space by policy equivalence, where two support states may be merged exactly when they require the same optimal action. The optimal stochastic action channel for approximate sufficiency inherits the standard rate-distortion Gibbs form applied to support states with regret distortion.

Conclusion: Action adequacy is distinguished from reconstruction fidelity, information-bottleneck prediction, and rational inattention. Robust single-cycle arbitration does not require preserving all support, but it does require preserving the distinctions that consequence geometry makes action-relevant.

Abstract: Robust decision-making requires compression. A system that forms a rich support state cannot usually preserve its full structure at the point of action. It must retain only those distinctions needed to act, verify, abstain, or defer under the current consequence geometry. This paper formalizes support sufficiency as action-sufficient compression. Let $H$ denote a full support state, $\mathcal{A}$ a finite action set, and $Z$ a consequence geometry specifying payoff structure. For fixed $Z$, the coarsest exactly action-sufficient compression is the quotient of support space by policy equivalence. Two support states may be merged exactly when they require the same optimal action. This clarifies why content-only and scalar-confidence-only arbitration fail whenever their induced partitions cross action boundaries. Approximate sufficiency is then defined by bounded expected policy regret. In the finite single-cycle setting, this yields a rate-regret problem with source $H$, reproduction alphabet $\mathcal{A}$, and distortion given by consequence-sensitive regret. The optimal stochastic action channel inherits the standard rate-distortion Gibbs form, applied here to support states with regret distortion. The contribution is interpretive: action adequacy is distinguished from reconstruction fidelity, information-bottleneck prediction, and rational inattention. Robust single-cycle arbitration does not require preserving all support, but it does require preserving the distinctions that consequence geometry makes action-relevant.

</details>


### [168] [Translation Symmetry, Fisher Information, and the Entropy Power Inequality in Blahut--Arimoto Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09895)
*Qiao Wang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Fisher information is identified as a temperature-invariant spectral quantity (Rayleigh quotient of translation mode) in Blahut-Arimoto rate-distortion geometry, with the entropy power inequality emerging as a consequence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover hidden geometric structure in the finite-temperature Blahut-Arimoto rate-distortion framework, specifically linking Fisher information, score functions, and the entropy power inequality.

Method: Derives an exact partition identity Z(x)=(π/β)^{d/2}p(x) from the BA fixed-point equation, then analyzes the BA effective score g_β and its relation to the Fisher score s, along with the BA projection of the translation mode v.

Result: Fisher information J(p) equals the Rayleigh quotient R(v) of the translation mode under the BA relaxation kernel, making it a temperature-invariant spectral quantity. The Fisher information inequality becomes a parallel-combination law for Rayleigh quotients under convolution.

Conclusion: The entropy power inequality follows from this geometric interpretation via heat-flow argument, though the main contribution is identifying Fisher information as spectral charge in BA rate-distortion geometry, not providing a new proof.

Abstract: We identify a previously unrecognised structure in the finite-temperature geometry of Blahut--Arimoto (BA) rate-distortion optimisation.
  The starting point is an exact partition identity. For every source density (p) and every inverse temperature $β>0$, the BA partition function $Z(x)=\int q^*(y)e^{-β|x-y|^2}dy$ satisfies $$ Z(x)=\left(\fracπβ\right)^{d/2}p(x). $$ This identity, obtained from the BA fixed-point equation, implies that the BA effective score $g_β=-\nabla\log Z$ coincides exactly with the classical Fisher score $s=-\nabla\log p$ for all temperatures. Moreover, if $v=-\nabla\log q^*$ denotes the translation mode generated by the quadratic-distortion symmetry, then its BA projection satisfies $\mathcal P v=-s$.
  These observations lead to the central identity $$ J(p)=\mathcal R(v):=\langle v,\mathcal G v\rangle_{L^2(q^*)}, $$ where $\mathcal G$ is the BA relaxation kernel. Thus Fisher information is exactly the Rayleigh quotient of the translation mode and is therefore a temperature-invariant spectral quantity in the BA framework.
  This yields a geometric interpretation of the Fisher information inequality: the inequality $$ J(X+Y)^{-1}\ge J(X)^{-1}+J(Y)^{-1} $$ becomes the parallel-combination law of a Rayleigh quotient under convolution. The entropy power inequality then follows through the standard heat-flow argument.
  The contribution is not a new proof of the entropy power inequality, but the identification of a hidden geometric structure: Fisher information as the spectral charge of the translation mode in BA rate-distortion geometry, with the entropy power inequality emerging as a consequence of this temperature-invariant fact.

</details>


### [169] [The Bioelectrical Information Theory: Investigating the theoretical compression limit of bioelectrical signals under artificial intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09922)
*Jiawen Zou,Bo Yan*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes reframing bioelectrical signal compression from waveform preservation to an information-theoretic, task-conditioned hierarchy (signal, physiological, semantic levels) that compresses based on physiological structure, model capacity, and downstream task requirements rather than raw signal entropy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bioelectrical signals (e.g., brain-computer interfaces) are acquired at scales challenging bandwidth limits, yet compression is still treated as waveform preservation bounded by raw signal entropy, which is insufficient for modern applications.

Method: Proposes a three-level hierarchical compression framework: (1) Signal level - noise reduction based on information about latent physiological sources; (2) Physiological level - parametric encoders produce compact, structured, quantized representations; (3) Semantic level - task-irrelevant information discarded, deep learning models exploit causal dependencies to replace marginal entropy with conditional entropy.

Result: Redefines the compression limit as a model- and task-conditioned quantity rather than a fixed waveform property; enables shifting from transmitting raw signals to transmitting only residual information needed for task-level interpretation.

Conclusion: As expressive models integrate with neural/physiological interfaces, bioelectrical compression will transition from signal transmission to task-conditioned residual information transmission.

Abstract: Bioelectrical signals are increasingly acquired at scales that challenge the bandwidth of brain-computer interfaces. However, their compression is still often framed as a problem of waveform preservation, limited by the entropy of the raw signal. Here we propose an information-theoretic framework in which the effective information of bioelectrical data is determined not only by signal fidelity, but also by physiological structure, model capacity and downstream task requirements. We formulate bioelectrical compression as a three-level hierarchy. At the signal level, noise is reduced to the information they carry about latent physiological sources. At the physiological level, parametric encoders map purified signals into compact, structured and quantized representations. At the semantic level, task-irrelevant information is discarded, while deep learning models exploit causal dependencies to replace marginal entropy with conditional entropy. This perspective reframes the compression limit of bioelectrical signals as a model- and task-conditioned quantity rather than a fixed property of the waveform. As increasingly expressive models become integrated with neural and physiological interfaces, bioelectrical compression may shift from transmitting signals to transmitting only the residual information required for task-level interpretation.

</details>


### [170] [Equation Asymmetry: An Algebraic Framework for Unifying Secrecy and Covertness in Information-Theoretic Security](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10374)
*Wang Hao,Zhang Kuang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Equation Asymmetry Degree (EAD) as a unified algebraic parameter governing both secrecy and covertness across information-theoretic security problems, unifying seven existing schemes and establishing exact capacity results for finite fields and Gaussian regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To discover a common algebraic structure underlying diverse information-theoretic security problems (secrecy and covertness) and unify them under a single parameter, the Equation Asymmetry Degree (EAD).

Method: Defines EAD Φ = (n - r)/n; derives equivocation bounds, secrecy capacities, and covertness conditions for both finite fields and continuous Gaussian channels; unifies seven existing security schemes under C_s = (n - r) log q; provides strong converse and SDoF equivalence proofs.

Result: Establishes exact equivocation lower bound and secrecy capacity on finite fields (Theorems 1-2), high-SNR secrecy capacity asymptotics and Wasserstein covertness condition for Gaussian regime (Lemma 2, Theorem 5'), EAD-SDoF equivalence d_s = n·Φ (Theorem 7), and near-perfect Pearson correlation (0.997) between EAD and security metrics in experiments.

Conclusion: The EAD (Φ) is a fundamental unifying parameter for information-theoretic security, simultaneously governing secrecy and covertness across finite-field and continuous settings, with post-quantum security implications from underdetermined linear systems.

Abstract: This paper studies the algebraic structure underlying a broad class of information-theoretic security problems. We define the equation asymmetry degree (EAD) as $Φ= (n - r)/n$, where $n$ is the signal embedding dimension and $r$ is the effective rank of the adversary's observation matrix. This single parameter is shown to simultaneously govern both secrecy (measured by equivocation $H(M|Y_E)$) and covertness (measured by detection error probability $P_e$). On finite fields $\mathbb{F}_q$, we establish the equivocation lower bound $H(M|Y_E) = \min(k, n - r_E) \log q$ with exact probabilistic conditions (Theorem~1), the secrecy capacity $C_s = (n - r_E) \log q$ with complete achievability and converse proofs (Theorem~2), and a strong converse (Theorem~8). In the continuous Gaussian regime, we derive a differential-entropy equivocation bound (Lemma~1), the high-SNR secrecy capacity asymptotics (Lemma~2), and a 2-Wasserstein distance covertness condition $W_2 \approx \sqrt{r_W} \cdot P / (2Nσ) \to 0$ (Theorem~5'). The EAD-SDoF equivalence $d_s = n \cdot Φ$ is established (Theorem~7). Both $η_s$ and $η_c$ are shown to be monotone functions of $Φ$ (Theorem~6), with a Pearson correlation of $0.997$ in continuous-domain experiments. Seven existing security schemes -- matrix embedding, MIMO wiretap, secure network coding, FRFT multi-angle transmission, traffic steganography, group-key secure summation, and MDS secure summation -- are unified under the common form $C_s = (n - r) \log q$. Post-quantum security follows from the information-theoretic hardness of underdetermined linear systems (Theorem~9). All numerical experiments are reproducible with open-source code.

</details>


### [171] [Minimum Distortion Quantization with Specified Output Distribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10458)
*Aolin Xu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Derives the optimal quantizer minimizing MMSE for a real-valued random variable while enforcing a specified discrete output distribution, with closed-form solutions for uniform cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design quantizers that not only minimize distortion (MMSE) but also allow explicit control over the output distribution, which is useful for entropy control, mutual information maximization, channel matching, and data anonymization.

Method: Derives the optimal quantizer as X = σ(F_{σ^{-1}(X)}^{-1}(F_W(W))), where σ is an optimal permutation minimizing MMSE. Uses majorization theory for the optimality proof. Simplifies to X = F_X^{-1}(F_W(W)) when either P_W or P_X is uniform.

Result: The optimal quantizer is characterized by a composition of the CDF of W, the inverse CDF of the permuted output distribution, and an optimal permutation σ. Uniformity in either input or output yields a simplified form.

Conclusion: Provides a principled framework for optimal quantization with constrained output distribution, with potential applications in communication, information theory, and privacy.

Abstract: We derive the optimal quantizer of a real-valued random variable $W$ with distribution $P_W$ such that 1) the distribution of the quantization output $X$ that can take $k$ values follows any specified distribution $P_X$ over $\{1,\ldots,k\}$, and 2) the minimum mean squared error (MMSE) of estimating $W$ from $X$ is minimized. It is shown that the optimal quantizer takes the form $X=σ\big(F_{σ^{-1}(X)}^{-1}(F_W(W))\big)$, where $σ$ is the optimal permutation of $\{1,\ldots,k\}$ among all permutations to minimize the MMSE, and $F$ is the cumulative distribution function. When $P_W$ is uniform over an interval or $P_X$ is uniform over $\{1,\ldots,k\}$, the quantizer takes a simple form $X=F_{X}^{-1}(F_W(W))$. The concept of majorization plays a key role in the optimality proof. Specifying the output distribution is useful for designing quantizers with explicitly controlled output entropy, maximized mutual information between input and output, tailored output distribution to match channel input requirements for communication, and data anonymization.

</details>


### [172] [On concatenation of matrices for reversible linear codes over a finite commutative ring and applications to DNA codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10633)
*Avanish Kumar Chaturvedi,Satyadeep Pandey*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper develops a matrix-theoretic framework using involutory matrices to construct reversible linear, reversible self-dual, and reversible DNA codes with good parameters, resolving an open problem and correcting previous results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a generalized framework for constructing reversible linear, self-dual, and DNA codes using involutory matrices, and to resolve an open problem by Oztas et al. while correcting/improving their results.

Method: Uses a matrix-theoretic approach based on involutory matrices with a concatenation scheme to generate generator matrices; employs a matrix product approach for unified analysis and proof.

Result: Produces a large class of generator matrices yielding codes with good parameters; resolves an open problem; corrects and improves results from Oztas et al.

Conclusion: The proposed framework is effective for constructing reversible and DNA codes with improved parameter quality, and advances the theoretical understanding by addressing previous gaps.

Abstract: In this paper, we develop a generalized framework for constructing reversible linear, reversible self dual and reversible DNA codes using a matrix-theoretic approach based on involutory matrices. The proposed concatenation scheme gives a large class of generator matrices and yields codes with good parameters. The construction is carried out at the level of linear codes and then extended to DNA codes. Using a matrix product approach, we provide a unified method for analysis and proof. Further, we resolve an open problem raised by Oztas et.al. and also we correct and improve some results of them.

</details>


### [173] [Secure Aggregation with Top-K Sparsification in Decentralized Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10780)
*Hengxuan Tang,Jinbao Zhu,Xiaohu Tang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a communication-efficient sparse secure aggregation scheme for decentralized federated learning that combines Top-K gradient sparsification with information-theoretic security, offloading dimension-dependent overhead to an offline phase and using random masks and permutations to protect gradients against user dropouts and collusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Secure aggregation in federated learning suffers from communication costs that scale with gradient dimension, which is prohibitive for large models and decentralized settings with limited bandwidth and unreliable nodes. Existing top-K sparsification is efficient for communication but creates challenges for sparse secure aggregation because selected entries are unpredictable and vary across users.

Method: The scheme combines top-K gradient sparsification with information-theoretic secure aggregation in a decentralized federated learning setting. It offloads dimension-dependent communication overhead to an offline phase and protects private gradients using random masks and permutations, while handling user dropouts and user collusion.

Result: The scheme preserves accuracy comparable to full-gradient aggregation even with only 1% gradient sparsification, while substantially reducing communication cost.

Conclusion: Top-K sparsification can be effectively combined with secure aggregation in decentralized federated learning to achieve both strong security (against dropouts and collusion) and high communication efficiency without sacrificing model accuracy.

Abstract: Secure aggregation is a vital component for mitigating gradient leakage in federated learning, but its communication cost conventionally scales with the gradient dimension. This becomes prohibitive for large models and even more pronounced in decentralized federated learning with limited bandwidth and unreliable nodes. Top-K gradient sparsification is an effective approach to reduce communication by transmitting only a few entries of the full gradient, while maintaining competitive model accuracy. Nevertheless, the top-K entries selected by each user are unpredictable and vary across users, which poses a challenge for efficient sparse secure aggregation. This paper studies information-theoretic secure aggregation with top-K sparsification in decentralized federated learning under user dropouts and user collusion. We propose a communication-efficient sparse secure aggregation scheme that offloads dimension-dependent overhead to an offline phase and protects private gradients using random masks and permutations. Experimental results demonstrate that our scheme preserves accuracy comparable to full-gradient aggregation even with only 1% gradient sparsification, while substantially reducing the communication cost.

</details>


### [174] [A New Invariant for Prime Alternating Knots From Error-Correcting Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10871)
*Altan B. Kilic,Ruud Pellikaan,Alberto Ravagnan*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new knot invariant derived from the Alexander-Briggs code, capable of distinguishing prime alternating knots, outperforming some traditional knot polynomials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a new invariant that can distinguish knots that are not separable by existing invariants like certain knot polynomials, specifically focusing on prime alternating knots.

Method: The approach uses the Alexander-Briggs code of a knot, relying on Tait's flyping theorem to restrict the analysis to prime alternating knots.

Result: The new invariant successfully distinguishes prime alternating knots in cases where well-known invariants (e.g., some knot polynomials) fail.

Conclusion: The Alexander-Briggs code yields a powerful new invariant for prime alternating knots with greater distinguishing power than some traditional invariants.

Abstract: This paper shows that the Alexander-Briggs code of a knot gives rise to a new invariant that distinguishes prime alternating knots. The restriction to prime alternating knots precisely follows from the fact that our approach relies on Tait s flyping theorem. We also provide examples where the new invariant succeeds in separating knots that the well known invariants, such as some knot polynomials, fail.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [175] [Mechanistic Analysis of Alignment Algorithms in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09850)
*Aarush Sinha,Ishan Garg,Veeraraju Elluru,Arth Singh,Kushal Garg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper systematically analyzes how six preference-optimization methods (PPO, DPO, SimPO, ORPO, GRPO, KTO) reshape language models' internal computations across three model families, finding that different methods produce qualitatively distinct representational shifts—KTO and GRPO enhance separability while DPO and ORPO degrade it—and that behavioral alignment does not imply uniform internal restructuring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training alignment algorithms are predominantly evaluated as black boxes, obscuring how they reshape language models' internal computations. The authors aim to open this black box to understand the internal mechanisms underlying alignment.

Method: The authors perform systematic mechanistic analysis using layer-wise linear probing, Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs), and crosscoders across six preference-optimization methods (PPO, DPO, SimPO, ORPO, GRPO, KTO) and three open-weight model families, localizing preference representations and quantifying alignment-induced geometric transformations in latent space.

Result: Preference signals consistently concentrate in early-mid or mid-late layers, but different objectives induce qualitatively distinct representational shifts. KTO and GRPO enhance linear separability through constructive feature sharing and sparse, high-salience recruitment, while DPO and ORPO degrade separability via non-constructive geometric rotation and feature attenuation. PPO and SimPO largely preserve baseline geometry. These transformations exhibit architecture-dependent variability.

Conclusion: Behavioral alignment does not imply uniform internal restructuring; alignment is a heterogeneous intervention. The findings motivate standardized feature-level auditing for safety and interpretability, and highlight the need for mechanism-aware optimization objectives.

Abstract: Post-training alignment algorithms are predominantly evaluated as black boxes, obscuring how they reshape language models' internal computations. We present a systematic mechanistic analysis of six preference-optimization methods: PPO, DPO, SimPO, ORPO, GRPO, and KTO across three open-weight model families. By integrating layer-wise linear probing, Sparse Autoencoders, and crosscoders, we localize preference representations and quantify alignment-induced geometric transformations in latent space. We find that preference signals consistently concentrate in early--mid or mid--late layers, but different objectives induce qualitatively distinct representational shifts. KTO and GRPO enhance linear separability through constructive feature sharing and sparse, high-salience recruitment. In contrast, DPO and ORPO degrade separability via non-constructive geometric rotation and feature attenuation, while PPO and SimPO largely preserve baseline geometry. These transformations exhibit architecture-dependent variability, demonstrating that behavioral alignment does not imply uniform internal restructuring. Our findings establish alignment as a heterogeneous intervention, motivate standardized feature-level auditing for safety and interpretability, and highlight the need for mechanism-aware optimization objectives.

</details>


### [176] [SynIB: Informational Bottleneck for Maximizing Synergy in Multimodal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09853)
*Konstantinos Kontras,Teodora Gagaleska,Thomas Strypsteen,Christos Chatzichristos,Matthew Blaschko,Maarten De Vos,Paul Pu Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Synergistic Information Bottleneck (SynIB), a training objective that explicitly targets multimodal synergy—information only available from combining modalities—by penalizing overconfidence when any modality is masked, improving performance on synergy-dependent examples by up to 7.8%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard multimodal learning approaches focus on architectural fusion but often fail to capture true cross-modal synergy, instead relying on unimodal or redundant information. The authors aim to directly target synergy through the training objective rather than model architecture.

Method: SynIB formalizes multimodal synergy via information theory and penalizes model confidence when any modality is masked during training, forcing the model to learn genuine cross-modal interactions rather than relying on unimodal shortcuts.

Result: SynIB recovers ground-truth synergy on synthetic XOR tasks where standard training fails. On five real-world benchmarks, it improves accuracy on synergy-dependent examples by up to 7.8% and overall accuracy by up to 3.8%.

Conclusion: Shaping the training objective is a complementary and effective axis for improving multimodal synergy beyond architectural changes, with SynIB providing a scalable and theoretically grounded solution.

Abstract: A central objective in multimodal learning is to capture synergy: task-relevant information that arises only from the joint use of multiple modalities, and is not available from any single modality alone. While most approaches operate at the architectural level through larger or more complex fusion models, we propose a complementary axis: shaping the training objective itself. Standard training often emphasizes unimodal or redundant information, falling short on examples that require cross-modal reasoning. We formalize multimodal synergy through information theory and introduce the Synergistic Information Bottleneck (SynIB), a scalable objective that targets synergy directly. To prioritize learning synergy, SynIB motivates the model to predict accurately from all modalities while penalizing confidence when information from any modality is withheld. Alongside the standard task loss, the model runs forward passes with one modality masked at a time and is penalized for remaining confident, which would indicate reliance on unimodal cues rather than cross-modal interactions. We validate SynIB in two regimes. On synthetic XOR tasks where the ground-truth synergy is known by construction, standard training fails to recover it while SynIB does. On five real-world benchmarks, including three MultiBench affective tasks, Hateful Memes with CLIP-ViT and DeBERTa backbones, and a controllable irony extension of CREMA-D we introduce, SynIB improves accuracy on synergy-dependent examples by up to 7.8% and overall accuracy by up to 3.8%.

</details>


### [177] [Uncertainty-aware Multi-fidelity Closure via Conditional Normalizing Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09857)
*Jice Zeng,Shady E. Ahmed,David Barajas-Solano,Panos Stinis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multi-fidelity learning framework using conditional normalizing flow to improve reduced-order model (ROM) accuracy by learning probabilistic mappings from low-fidelity to high-fidelity coefficients, with residual learning outperforming direct learning on a 2D vortex merging problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ROMs suffer from truncation errors due to inadequate representation of unresolved scales (closure problem), limiting their predictive accuracy for complex multiscale systems.

Method: Formulate ROM closure as multi-fidelity learning; use conditional normalizing flow to learn probabilistic mapping from low-fidelity to high-fidelity coefficients. Two strategies: direct learning and residual learning.

Result: Both strategies improve ROM accuracy; residual learning consistently outperforms direct learning; the framework provides uncertainty quantification for corrected coefficients.

Conclusion: The proposed uncertainty-aware MF framework enhances ROM predictive fidelity and reliability, with residual learning as the superior correction strategy.

Abstract: Reduced-order models (ROMs) provide an efficient surrogate for complex multiscale systems, but their predictive accuracy is often compromised by truncation errors and the inadequate representation of interactions between resolved and unresolved scales. The missing effect of truncated (unresolved) scales on ROM (resolved) scales is often denoted as the closure problem. In this work, we formulate ROM closure modeling as a multi-fidelity (MF) learning problem and propose an uncertainty-aware MF framework based on conditional normalizing flow to enhance ROM predictive accuracy. The proposed approach learns a probabilistic mapping from low-fidelity (LF) ROM coefficients to high-fidelity (HF) coefficients, thereby improving predictive fidelity while quantifying the uncertainty associated with the learned closure. Two correction strategies are investigated: direct learning, in which HF coefficients are predicted directly from LF inputs, and residual learning, which learns the discrepancy between LF and HF coefficients and uses it to recover the corrected HF solution. The framework is demonstrated on a vortex merging problem governed by the two-dimensional Navier Stokes equations. Results show that both correction strategies improve ROM accuracy over uncorrected ROM, with residual learning achieving consistently better performance than direct learning. Moreover, the two proposed deep generative model-based strategies provide uncertainty quantification for the corrected ROM coefficients, which is critical for assessing prediction confidence and supporting the reliable use of ROMs in practical applications.

</details>


### [178] [Mitigating Manifold Departure: Uncertainty-Aware Subspace Rectification for Trustworthy MLLM Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09859)
*Yingxuan Zhuang,Jingxiao Yang,Miao Pan,Cheng Tan,Yuxiang Cai,Siwei Tan,Chen Zhi,Xuhong Zhang,Jianwei Yin,Jintao Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MGAP is a training-free decoding method that selectively suppresses harmful language priors in MLLMs by using SVD-based subspace projection and a consistency-aware gate, reducing hallucinations without degrading semantic coherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs hallucinate objects inconsistent with visual inputs due to over-reliance on language priors. Prior training-free methods blindly suppress all language priors, causing "Manifold Departure" — disruption of the semantic manifold and performance degradation.

Method: MGAP constructs a language-prior subspace from blind hidden states via SVD, then projects multimodal hidden states onto this subspace during decoding, using a consistency-aware gate to adaptively attenuate only the projected prior component while preserving orthogonal semantic components.

Result: MGAP outperforms prior decoding baselines on POPE and CHAIR benchmarks, achieving stronger hallucination suppression without sacrificing coherence.

Conclusion: MGAP effectively balances language priors — suppressing harmful ones while retaining helpful ones — offering a geometry-aware, training-free solution to hallucination in MLLMs.

Abstract: MLLMs frequently hallucinate objects inconsistent with visual inputs. This issue is typically attributed to the over-reliance on language priors, which can override the visual context. Recent training-free decoding strategies address this by penalizing language priors. However, these methods overlook the dual nature of language priors, where they can be both helpful and harmful depending on the alignment with visual evidence. In particular, blindly suppressing language priors often disrupts the model's semantic manifold, leading to performance degradation, a phenomenon we term Manifold Departure. To address this, we propose Manifold-Guided Adaptive Projection (MGAP), a geometry-aware, training-free decoding method that mitigates hallucinations while preserving representation structure. MGAP first constructs a language-prior subspace from blind hidden states via SVD. During decoding, MGAP projects each multimodal hidden state onto this subspace and applies a consistency-aware gate to adaptively attenuate only the projected prior component, yielding a subspace-selective update that largely preserves the orthogonal semantic components. Extensive experiments on POPE and CHAIR show that MGAP outperforms prior decoding baselines, achieving stronger hallucination suppression without sacrificing coherence.

</details>


### [179] [Conformal Risk Prediction for Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease Using Gradient Boosting with Distribution-Free Coverages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09860)
*Xinze Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A machine-learning framework (Method) combining gradient-boosted trees with conformal prediction for NAFLD risk prediction, achieving high accuracy (AUROC 0.912) and calibrated coverage guarantees on a multicenter Chinese cohort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NAFLD affects ~25% of global adults but population-level screening tools remain inadequate, motivating the need for a reliable, clinically interpretable risk prediction framework.

Method: Gradient-boosted decision trees + conformal prediction for calibrated, distribution-free coverage guarantees; mutual-information-based stability selection for compact feature subset via bootstrap resampling.

Result: AUROC 0.912 (internal) and 0.891 (external); 91.3% empirical coverage at 90% nominal level; high-risk subgroup shows 4.7x progression rate vs. low-risk; selected features (waist circumference, ALT, GGT, triglycerides, fasting glucose, BMI) align with known metabolic risk factors.

Conclusion: Method provides an effective, interpretable NAFLD risk prediction tool with statistical coverage guarantees, outperforming existing models and enabling clinically actionable three-tier risk stratification.

Abstract: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) affects roughly 25% of global adults, posing substantial hepatic and cardiovascular risks. Yet, population-level screening tools remain inadequate. We present Method, a machine-learning framework for NAFLD risk prediction coupling gradient-boosted decision trees with conformal prediction to yield calibrated, distribution-free coverage guarantees on individual risk estimates. It integrates a mutual-information-based stability selection procedure to identify a compact, clinically interpretable feature subset via bootstrap resampling, constructing prediction sets whose marginal coverage provably exceeds a user-specified confidence level. We evaluated Method on a multicenter cohort from Guangzhou, China (primary n=2,187; external validation n=412) using 78 candidate features across demographics, metabolic biomarkers, and lifestyle factors. Method achieves an AUROC of 0.912 internally and 0.891 externally, outperforming deep neural networks, TabNet, support vector machines, and logistic regression. Conformal prediction sets achieve 91.3% empirical coverage at the 90% nominal level. A three-tier risk stratification derived from these scores separates the population into distinct groups, with the high-risk subgroup showing a 12-month progression rate 4.7 times that of the low-risk tier. The selected features -- notably waist circumference, ALT, GGT, triglycerides, fasting glucose, and BMI -- align with established metabolic risk factors, providing biological plausibility.

</details>


### [180] [Time Series as Language: A Universal Tokenizer for General-Purpose Time Series Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09861)
*Yunhao Zhang,Ruiying Qi,Jiale Zheng,Jianfeng Zhang,Lujia Pan,Junchi Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UniTok-FM is a foundation model that tokenizes continuous time series into discrete tokens using UniTok, enabling zero-shot/prompt-boosted forecasting, few-shot generation, and classification via training-free in-context inference, outperforming statistical and supervised baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between Next-Token Prediction (NTP) success in LLMs and its adaptation to unbounded, continuous time series data, which remains an open problem.

Method: UniTok (vector-quantized autoencoder with prefix normalization, progressive-resolution causal architecture, structure-preserving loss) tokenizes TS; UniTok-FM uses off-the-shelf LLM architecture to perform NTP on context windows of multiple series with similar patterns.

Result: UniTok-FM outperforms statistical and supervised baselines, matches task-specific foundation models, and uniquely enables training-free in-context inference across forecasting, generation, and classification tasks.

Conclusion: A single unified foundation model can handle multiple TS tasks via NTP without task-specific modifications, achieving strong zero-shot and in-context learning capabilities.

Abstract: While Next-Token Prediction (NTP) has unified LLM pretraining, its adaptation to unbounded, continuous time series (TS) remains open. To bridge the gap, we introduce UniTok, a universal tokenizer that transforms TS into discrete tokens, and UniTok-FM, a foundation model pretrained via NTP on these tokens. UniTok-FM is a general-purpose foundation model that supports zero-shot and prompt-boosted forecasting, as well as few-shot generation and classification via training-free in-context inference--a capability not achieved by prior works. Technically, UniTok is a vector-quantized autoencoder incorporating prefix normalization for scale stabilization, a progressive-resolution causal architecture for encoding and decoding, and a structure-preserving reconstruction loss for training. UniTok-FM adopts an off-the-shelf LLM architecture without TS-specific modifications. Instead of pretraining on isolated TS, it performs NTP on context windows formed by multiple series with similar patterns, aiming to capture their shared dynamics. Experiments on forecasting, generation, and classification show that a single unified UniTok-FM consistently outperforms statistical and supervised baselines, achieves competitive performance with task-specific foundation models, and uniquely enables training-free in-context inference across tasks.

</details>


### [181] [Blurry Window Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09862)
*Axel Laborieux,Christos Sourmpis,Juan Gabriel Kostelec,Qinghai Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BLA introduces a novel ABC method using Dirichlet kernels to interpolate blurry KV history, achieving 8× better state efficiency than SWA on MQAR while maintaining competitive performance with linear attention models on recall tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Softmax Attention's quadratic complexity and growing KV cache size become bottlenecks in long contexts; linear models still lag behind Transformers in retrieval/recall tasks.

Method: Blurry Window Attention (BLA) stores a frequency window and reconstructs blurry KV history via Dirichlet kernel interpolation; it generalizes SWA and is a special case of Gated Slot Attention.

Result: BLA achieves 8× better state efficiency than SWA on MQAR; competitive with linear attention models; only BLA and SWA improve with larger state size on RegBench among tested linear models.

Conclusion: BLA bridges the gap between linear-complexity models and Transformers on recall tasks, offering better state efficiency than SWA while maintaining linear complexity.

Abstract: The Softmax Attention operation in Transformer language models has a quadratic complexity in the sequence length and a growing state size in the form of KV cache, which becomes a bottleneck in long context scenarios. To overcome this limitation, alternative architectures with linear complexity and finite state size have been introduced, such as State-Space Models (SSMs), Linear Attention (LA), and Attention with Bounded-memory Control (ABC). Though linear models achieve similar language perplexity as Transformers, they are still behind in tasks which require retrieval or recall of specific information. In this work, we introduce Blurry Window Attention (BLA) a novel ABC method inspired by SSMs. BLA stores a frequency window from which a blurry KV history is reconstructed via interpolation using Dirichlet kernels. BLA can be understood as a generalization of Sliding Window Attention (SWA) depending on the Dirichlet kernels resolution or as a special case of the Gated Slot Attention (GSA), where the decay factor is implemented with Dirichlet kernels. We describe in details the theory and efficient implementation of BLA. On the Multi-Query Associate Recall (MQAR) synthetic task, we show that the state efficiency of BLA is 8$\times$ better than SWA and is competitive with popular linear attention models, and in the RegBench synthetic task, only BLA and SWA improve their performance as the state size grows among the linear models we tested.

</details>


### [182] [From Confident Closing to Silent Failure: Characterizing False Success in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09863)
*Laksh Advani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM agents frequently claim task completion despite failing (false success). LLM judges are poor at detecting this (AUROC ≤0.65), while lightweight TF-IDF detectors outperform them significantly (AUROC up to 0.95) with 3,300x lower latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents can silently fail by asserting task completion when the environment state shows otherwise, and this false success failure mode is understudied.

Method: Analyzed 9,876 tau2-bench and 1,879 AppWorld trajectories with text-independent ground truth; tested 5 LLM judges with 5 prompt strategies; compared against lightweight TF-IDF detectors.

Result: False success varies (3-75.8% of failures). LLM judges max out at AUROC 0.65 (tau2-bench) and 0.54 (AppWorld). TF-IDF achieves AUROC 0.83 (tau2-bench) and 0.95 (AppWorld), recovering 4-8x more false successes with 3,300x lower latency.

Conclusion: Production monitoring should use lightweight, domain-calibrated detectors as triage signals instead of relying on LLM judges as the primary monitor for false success.

Abstract: LLM agents can fail silently by asserting task completion when the environment state shows otherwise. We study this failure mode, false success, across two agent benchmarks: 9,876 tau2-bench trajectories from 8 model families and 1,879 AppWorld trajectories from 4 model families with text-independent ground truth. False success is common but varies by setting: 45--48% of failures in single-control tau2-bench domains, 3% in dual-control telecom, and 75.8% among AppWorld self-assessing coding-agent trajectories with explicit status claims. LLM judges fail reliably: no configuration across 5 judges, 5 prompt strategies, and full task specifications exceeds AUROC 0.65 on tau2-bench, and the same judges reach only 0.54 AUROC on AppWorld API-call traces. Judges rely on surface completion proxies -- confident closing language in tau2-bench and coarse action-sequence volume in AppWorld -- rather than verified state changes. Lightweight TF-IDF detectors achieve task-disjoint AUROC 0.83 on tau2-bench and 0.95 on AppWorld, recovering 4--8x more false successes than the best judge at the same flag rate with 3,300x lower latency. These results suggest that production monitoring should use lightweight, domain-calibrated detectors as triage signals rather than relying on LLM judges as the primary monitor for false success.

</details>


### [183] [Algorithmic and Minimax Complexities in Kernel Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11171)
*Yunbei Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper unifies GP-UCB and DEC methods within a common algorithmic-information framework for frequentist RKHS bandits. It shows that algorithmic complexity and class-wide minimax coefficients answer different questions, with kernel bandits revealing mathematically visible gaps between the two.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reconcile two seemingly separate theoretical perspectives (GP-UCB and DEC) in RKHS bandits, and to demonstrate that algorithmic information and minimax certificates provide fundamentally different insights, especially in overparameterized models.

Method: The authors propose a unified MAIR framework with heterogeneous positive-semidefinite algorithmic priors, generalizing both GP-UCB analysis and the MAMS algorithm. They introduce a safeguarded master that combines advantages from both approaches.

Result: (1) A common algorithmic-information language unifying GP-UCB and DEC perspectives. (2) A safeguarded hybrid algorithm combining both approaches' strengths. (3) A kernel-bandit construction showing algorithmic complexity can be more informative than class-wide minimax or DEC certificates in overparameterized models.

Conclusion: Algorithmic information and class-wide minimax coefficients answer different questions and can yield different gaps; kernel bandits cleanly expose this mathematical distinction.

Abstract: Gaussian-process upper confidence bound (GP-UCB) and decision-estimation-coefficient (DEC) methods may appear, at first sight, to belong to different theories. This paper places the two viewpoints in a common algorithmic-information language for frequentist RKHS bandits. GP-UCB fixes an algorithmic, rather than true, Gaussian-process prior and exploits realized-trajectory complexity together with computational tractability, whereas MAMS optimizes a robust class-wide MAIR/DEC envelope. Through the unified MAIR framework and heterogeneous positive-semidefinite algorithmic priors, we generalize both the GP-UCB analysis and the MAMS algorithm, propose a safeguarded master that combines their advantages, and provide a kernel-bandit construction showing that algorithmic complexity can be more informative than class-wide minimax or DEC certificates in overparameterized models. The resulting message is that algorithmic information and class-wide minimax coefficients answer different questions and can lead to different gaps; kernel bandits provide a clean setting in which this distinction becomes mathematically visible.

</details>


### [184] [Alignment Collapse Under KV Cache Quantization: Diagnosis and Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09864)
*Bruce Changlong Xu,Adarsh Kumarappan,Mu Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KV cache quantization can silently destroy LLM safety alignment without affecting perplexity. The paper identifies geometric root causes and proposes Per-Channel Reduction (PCR) to diagnose failure modes and recover alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of KV cache quantization focus only on perplexity and accuracy, completely overlooking safety alignment impacts. The authors aim to uncover how quantization silently compromises alignment and develop a diagnostic to detect and mitigate these failures.

Method: Analyzed 11 instruction-tuned models (3.8B-72B) across 5 benchmarks (1,894 prompts). Identified geometric root cause via activation subspace analysis. Proposed Per-Channel Reduction (PCR) to classify models into three mechanistic failure modes: outlier-crushes-safety, outlier-as-safety, and multi-layer dilution. PCR uses 20 calibration prompts and runs ~35 GPU-minutes.

Result: Mistral-7B loses 15.2% refusals at only 1.03x perplexity change. No universal safe bit-width exists. PCR correctly predicts mitigation for 9 primary models and 1 held-out model, recovers up to 97.2% alignment via KIVI, and generalizes across unseen prompts, models, and quantizers.

Conclusion: KV cache quantization poses a hidden safety risk. The proposed training-free PCR protocol effectively diagnoses failure modes and recovers alignment (up to 97%) with minimal overhead, outperforming attention-based methods. Vulnerabilities confirmed in production vLLM serving with FP8 on NVIDIA GPUs.

Abstract: Key-value (KV) cache quantization is widely used to reduce Large Language Model (LLM) inference memory, yet existing evaluations solely focus on measuring perplexity and accuracy without assessing the safety impact. In this study, we explore alignment preservation under KV cache quantization. Across eleven instruction-tuned models (3.8B-72B) and five benchmarks (1,894 prompts), we find that low-bit quantization can silently destroy safety alignment: Mistral-7B loses 15.2% of its refusals at only 1.03x perplexity, and no universal safe bit-width exists, with sharp model-specific phase transitions invisible to standard metrics. We identify that the root cause is geometric: safety features occupy a low-dimensional activation subspace 10^2-10^3x more vulnerable to quantization noise than the full representation space perplexity averages over. Inspired by this observation, we propose Per-Channel Reduction (PCR), a diagnostic that classifies each model into one of three mechanistic failure modes: outlier-crushes-safety, where safety lives in non-outlier channels collaterally damaged by outlier-driven scale factors; outlier-as-safety, where safety overlaps outlier channels and finer granularity cannot rescue it; and multi-layer dilution, where safety is distributed across many layers and per-layer fixes fail. PCR predicts the correct mitigation direction on all nine primary models and one held-out model from an independent family using 20 calibration prompts. PCR generalizes across unseen prompts, models, and production quantizers, including KIVI with up to 97.2% recovery, succeeding where attention-based allocation methods fail. The resulting training-free protocol, requiring approximately 35 GPU-minutes, recovers up to 97% of lost alignment at minimal memory overhead, addressing vulnerabilities confirmed in production vLLM serving with FP8 KV cache on NVIDIA GPUs.

</details>


### [185] [LLM-as-a-Discriminator: When Synthetic Tables Still Look Real](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09865)
*Manel Slokom,Malek Slokom,Thierno Kante*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes using LLMs (LLaMA, Gemini) as discriminators to audit whether tabular data is real or synthetic, testing on CTGAN, TVAE, and Gaussian Copula across Adult and Census datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy and data sharing are often in tension; synthetic data is used to reduce privacy risk, but auditing privacy for tabular data remains difficult, and even humans struggle to distinguish real from synthetic tables.

Method: The authors propose an LLM discrimination method where an LLM classifies each table sample as REAL or SYNTHETIC. Two settings are tested: C1 (table only) and C2 (table plus distributional metadata). They use LLaMA and Gemini on data generated by CTGAN, TVAE, and Gaussian Copula from UCI Adult and ACS Census datasets, with 451 valid trials.

Result: Clear differences between models: On Adult, LLaMA achieves DRS=0% while Gemini reaches DRS=100% for CTGAN and TVAE. On Census, LLaMA predicts SYNTHETIC for most samples; Gemini stays high in C1 but drops for CTGAN and TVAE in C2. Results are compared with C2ST, record linkage baselines, and a human pilot study.

Conclusion: LLM discrimination is a practical privacy audit signal, provided that model choice, per-provider reporting, and data encoding are carefully handled. Code and scripts are released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Privacy and data sharing are often in tension. Many organizations use synthetic data to reduce privacy risk and still share useful data. For tabular data, auditing privacy remains hard. In many cases, even humans cannot easily tell if a table is real or synthetic. In this paper, we propose a method based on LLM discrimination. We ask an LLM to classify each table sample as REAL or SYNTHETIC. We test two settings: C1 with table only, and C2 with table plus distributional metadata. We use LLaMA as an open model and Gemini as a reference model. In our experiments, we run three synthesis models, CTGAN, TVAE, and Gaussian Copula, on two public datasets, UCI Adult and ACS Census. We collect 451 valid trials. Our results show clear differences between models. On Adult, LLaMA reaches DRS=0% in reported cells, while Gemini reaches DRS=100% for CTGAN and TVAE. On Census, LLaMA predicts SYNTHETIC for most samples, while Gemini stays high in C1 but drops for CTGAN and TVAE in C2. We also compare with a classifier two-sample test (C2ST) and record linkage as distributional baselines, and with a human pilot of 2 annotators and 240 trials. Our results show that LLM discrimination is a practical privacy audit signal when model choice, per provider reporting, and data encoding are handled with care. For reproducibility, code and experiment scripts are available at https://github.com/SlokomManel/LLM-as-a-Discriminator.

</details>


### [186] [Two to Tango: Coupled Task-Reference Selection for Safe LLM Fine-tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09866)
*Xinrui Chen,Jianhao Zhang,Ou Wu,Di Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DualSelect is a coupled framework for selecting safety references and task samples jointly to preserve safety alignment in fine-tuned LLMs without sacrificing task utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning safety-aligned LLMs on downstream data often erodes their learned safety behavior, and existing methods using fixed safety examples or global constraints fail to adapt to varying safety constraints exposed by different task updates.

Method: DualSelect jointly selects task-conditioned safety references and compatible task samples via entropy-regularized scoring, lazy reference refresh, and gradient correction under a minimax optimization view.

Result: On 1B-8B LLMs, DualSelect improves Safety Avg. by at least 5.10 points over the strongest baseline (REDORCA judge) and maintains top safety performance across multiple judges with moderate overhead.

Conclusion: DualSelect effectively preserves safety without losing task utility, and the minimax view can be extended to retention-focused continual learning.

Abstract: Fine-tuning safety aligned large language models (LLMs) on downstream data improves adaptation but may erode learned safety behavior. Existing methods use fixed safety examples, global constraints, or one-sided task filtering. Our diagnostics show task updates expose different safety constraints, motivating joint selection of relevant references and compatible task samples. We propose DualSelect, a coupled framework for task and reference selection that refreshes task conditioned safety references before filtering whole task samples compatible with the induced reference direction. Under a minimax view, DualSelect selects safety references with high preservation loss and task conflict, together with compatible task samples, through entropy-regularized scoring surrogates, lazy reference refresh, and gradient correction. On 1B-8B LLMs, DualSelect preserves safety without losing task utility; using the REDORCA judge, it improves Safety Avg. over the strongest baseline by at least 5.10 points and remains highest in Safety Avg. across judges with moderate overhead. This view extends to retention focused continual learning.

</details>
