### [187] [SPACE: Source-free Proxy Anchor Concept Erasure for MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09868)
*Zhijing Zhang,Jiaqi Ding,Qianshan Wei,Nan Zhou,Jiaqi Li,Yongliang Wu,Tongxin Zhu,Xiaolin Fang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPACE is the first source-free unlearning framework for MLLMs that erases target concepts without requiring visual data by using proxy anchors and dual-constraint optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MU methods rely on visual data of target concepts, which is often unavailable due to strict data retention policies. There is a need for source-free unlearning approaches that operate without access to target data.

Method: SPACE has two stages: (1) Text-Guided Proxy Anchor Selection (TPAS) for retrieving semantically aligned proxy anchors, and (2) Dual-Constraint Semantic Isolation (DCSI) for optimizing anchors to erase target concepts while confining updates to the null space of retained knowledge.

Result: Experiments across six datasets show SPACE achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art data-dependent methods in source-free MU scenarios.

Conclusion: SPACE is an effective source-free unlearning framework that bounds perturbation on retained knowledge and maximizes feature spectral entropy to maintain model performance.

Abstract: As Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face growing privacy risks and regulatory constraints, machine unlearning (MU) has emerged as a crucial solution for removing sensitive data while preserving model performance. However, existing MU methods typically rely on visual data of the target concepts, which is often unavailable due to strict data retention policies, thus creating a demand for source-free unlearning approaches that operate without access to the target data. In this work, we propose Source-free Proxy Anchor Concept Erasure (SPACE), the first source-free unlearning framework specialized for MLLMs. SPACE consists of two stages: (1) Text-Guided Proxy Anchor Selection (TPAS), which retrieves semantically aligned proxy anchors from the shared feature space. (2) Dual-Constraint Semantic Isolation (DCSI), which optimizes these anchors to indirectly erase target concepts. DCSI confines updates to the null space of retained knowledge, ensuring structural integrity. We theoretically prove that SPACE strictly bounds the perturbation on retained knowledge and maximizes feature spectral entropy, thereby maintaining the model's performance. Furthermore, extensive experiments across six datasets show that SPACE achieves performance comparable to that of state-of-the-art data-dependent methods, validating its effectiveness in source-free MU scenarios. The source code will be released.

</details>


### [188] [Structured Adaptive Tensor Prediction for Streaming Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10085)
*Zhen Qin,Yang Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops adaptive tensor regression frameworks (MoM and ToM) with SGD algorithms for streaming matrix-valued time series prediction, showing ToM achieves better performance with theoretical guarantees under various low-dimensional structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for matrix-valued time series are designed for static settings and lack adaptability to streaming environments. Adaptive filtering techniques are limited to scalar/vector data, leaving adaptive forecasting for matrix-valued time series inadequately understood.

Method: Proposes two formulations: Matrix-on-Matrix (MoM) and Tensor-on-Matrix (ToM) regression, with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) algorithms for online learning. ToM stacks multiple responses across time into higher-order tensors to exploit temporal structure.

Result: ToM achieves lower steady-state error and stronger denoising capability than MoM. Establishes fixed-time recovery guarantees for ToM under sparsity, low-rankness, and joint sparse-low-rank models. Characterizes tracking behavior of SGD under time-varying dynamics.

Conclusion: Stacking multiple responses across time into higher-order tensors improves performance in streaming matrix-valued prediction, with ToM framework offering theoretical guarantees for online tensor regression.

Abstract: Matrix-valued time series arise in a wide range of applications, such as spatio-temporal data from medical imaging and geophysics. Existing methods are mainly designed for static settings and lack adaptability to streaming and time-varying environments. Adaptive filtering techniques have also been largely limited to data with scalar or vector values, leaving adaptive forecasting for matrix-valued time series inadequately understood. To bridge these gaps, we develop an adaptive tensor regression framework that includes Matrix-on-Matrix (MoM) and Tensor-on-Matrix (ToM) formulations for streaming matrix-valued prediction. The two formulations differ in whether to directly model matrix-valued outputs or to exploit temporal structure via higher-order tensor representations. For the proposed tensor regression framework, we develop stochastic gradient descent (SGD) algorithms for online learning. We show that stacking multiple responses across time into higher-order tensors improves performance; in particular, the ToM achieves lower steady-state error and stronger denoising capability than MoM, motivating our focus on the ToM model. We further characterize the tracking behavior of SGD under time-varying dynamics. From a statistical perspective, we establish fixed-time recovery guarantees for ToM under general low-dimensional structures, including sparsity, low-rankness, and their joint sparselow-rank models.

</details>


### [189] [QSplitFL: Capability Aware Deep Q-Learning for Optimal Split Point Selection in Split Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09869)
*Nazmus Shakib Shadin,Xinyue Zhang,Jingyi Wang,Miao Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QSplitFL uses a DQN-based framework with lightweight hardware metrics (CPU, memory, battery, latency) and a committee voting mechanism to dynamically select optimal split points in Split Federated Learning, achieving better convergence and accuracy across heterogeneous devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed split points in SFL overload weak devices, increase communication/server load, slow convergence, and reduce stability when clients have heterogeneous hardware capabilities.

Method: A capability-aware DQN framework using lightweight state from client hardware metrics (CPU, memory, battery, latency), a decayed loss-drop reward function for early convergence, and a committee-based DQN with majority voting to prevent reward hacking.

Result: Outperforms existing methods in convergence and accuracy across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 datasets using CNN, ResNet50, MobileNetV4, and ConvNeXt architectures.

Conclusion: QSplitFL effectively adapts split point selection to heterogeneous device resources, improving FL training efficiency and stability in SFL environments.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) combined with Split Learning (SL) is a privacy preserving paradigm that enables training deep neural networks (DNNs) on resource constrained devices while reducing overall training cost. However, determining the optimal split point, meaning the layer where the model is divided still remains a critical challenge, especially when clients have heterogeneous hardware capabilities. Fixed split points can overload weak devices and increase the communication and server load, which slows convergence and reduces stability. This paper introduces QSplitFL, a novel capability-aware Deep Q-Network (DQN) framework for optimal split point selection in Split learning based Federated Learning (SFL) environments. Unlike existing approaches that rely on high-dimensional model weight representations, QSplitFL employs a lightweight state representation derived directly from client hardware metrics, including CPU utilization, memory, battery level, and network latency. The proposed framework incorporates a decayed loss-drop reward function that prioritizes early convergence, and a committee-based DQN architecture with majority voting to mitigate reward hacking. Extensive experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 datasets using CNN, ResNet50, MobileNetV4, and ConvNeXt architectures demonstrate that our approach achieves better convergence and higher accuracy compared to existing methods, while effectively adapting to heterogeneous device resources. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/AIPO-Lab/QSplitFL.

</details>


### [190] [A Comprehensive Inference-Time Augmentation Framework in Physiological Signals: Application to PPG-Based AF Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10410)
*Davood Fattahi,Runze Yan,Saurabh Kataria,Zhaoliang Chen,Xiao Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified Inference-Time Augmentation (ITA) framework with 13 augmentation methods and Bayesian hyperparameter optimization, demonstrating improved PPG-based atrial fibrillation detection on multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physiological signal classification faces challenges from sensor noise, motion artifacts, and distribution shifts. Existing ITA methods are limited in scope, using few augmentations with fixed, unoptimized parameters.

Method: A unified ITA framework with 13 augmentation methods (time, amplitude, frequency, artifact-injection), hyperparameters optimized via Bayesian optimization, evaluated on AF detection using GPT-PPG and ResNet across 5 datasets.

Result: Standard ITA improved AUROC up to 8.5% (GPT-PPG) and 0.7% (ResNet), AUPRC up to 10.6% (GPT-PPG) and 0.8% (ResNet). Selective ITA reduced FPR up to 4.4% (GPT-PPG) and 1.3% (ResNet) on non-AF datasets.

Conclusion: ITA is a practical, model-agnostic approach for improving PPG-based AF classification reliability in deployment scenarios where retraining is infeasible, with broader applicability to physiological signal analysis.

Abstract: Objective: Accurate classification of physiological signals in real-world deployments is challenged by sensor noise, motion artifacts, and distribution shifts between training and deployment data. Inference-time augmentation (ITA), which applies augmentations during inference rather than retraining, offers a simple, model-agnostic mechanism to improve robustness. However, ITA application to physiological signals has remained narrow in scope, relying on limited augmentation methods with fixed, unoptimized parameters. This work proposes a unified ITA framework to address that gap.
  Approach: The framework incorporates 13 augmentation methods spanning time-domain, amplitude-domain, frequency-domain, and artifact-injection transformations, with hyperparameters optimized via Bayesian optimization. We evaluate on atrial fibrillation (AF) detection from 30-second PPG signals using GPT-PPG and ResNet across five datasets comprising more than 400 patients and ${\sim}$9,800 hours of recording.
  Main results: Standard ITA consistently improved AUROC (up to 8.5% for GPT-PPG and 0.7% for ResNet) and AUPRC (up to 10.6% for GPT-PPG and 0.8% for ResNet). Selective ITA further reduced average FPR by up to 4.4% (GPT-PPG) and 1.3% (ResNet) on non-AF datasets.
  Significance: These findings establish ITA as a practical, model-agnostic approach for improving PPG-based AF classification reliability in deployment settings where retraining is not feasible, with broader applicability to physiological signal analysis.

</details>


### [191] [PatchSTG: Scalable Spatiotemporal Graph Transformers for Traffic Forecasting on Irregular Sensor Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09872)
*Jichao Li,Xuanming Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PatchSTG introduces a patch-based hierarchical spatial representation and dual attention mechanism for efficient traffic forecasting on irregular sensor networks, achieving near-linear complexity and competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traffic forecasting is challenged by irregular sensor distributions and the high computational cost of modeling large-scale spatiotemporal dependencies; existing graph/attention-based models lack effectiveness and scalability under non-uniform spatial structures.

Method: PatchSTG partitions sensors into balanced, locality-preserving patches using geographic information, then applies a dual attention encoder with intra-patch (local) and inter-patch (global) attention, reducing complexity from quadratic to near-linear.

Result: Evaluated on Rhode Island and large-scale datasets; achieves stable, competitive forecasting across multiple horizons with significantly improved efficiency. Ablations confirm the value of spatial partitioning and dual attention.

Conclusion: Patch-based spatiotemporal modeling offers a scalable and effective framework for traffic forecasting under irregular spatial settings.

Abstract: Traffic forecasting is a fundamental component of intelligent transportation systems, yet remains challenging in real-world settings due to irregular sensor distributions and the high computational cost of modeling large-scale spatiotemporal dependencies. In practical traffic networks, sensors are unevenly distributed across regions, leading to non-uniform spatial structures that limit the effectiveness and scalability of existing graph-based and attention-based models. To address these challenges, we propose PatchSTG, a patch-based spatiotemporal graph Transformer designed for efficient forecasting on irregular sensor networks. The key idea is to introduce a hierarchical spatial representation that partitions sensors into balanced, locality-preserving patches based on geographic information. On top of this structure, a dual attention encoder alternates between intra-patch attention for capturing local interactions and inter-patch attention for modeling global dependencies, reducing computational complexity from quadratic to near-linear scaling. We evaluate PatchSTG on real-world traffic data from Rhode Island and additional large-scale datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves stable and competitive forecasting performance across multiple horizons, while significantly improving computational efficiency. Ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of spatial partitioning and dual attention in capturing both local and long-range traffic dynamics. These results suggest that patch-based spatiotemporal modeling provides a scalable and effective framework for traffic forecasting under irregular spatial settings.

</details>


### [192] [Learning Doubly Sparse Explicitly Conditioned Transforms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10975)
*Tudor Pistol*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a novel structured transform learning method that combines a fixed canonical matrix with a data-adaptive sparse component, achieving state-of-the-art results in sparse representation with lower computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing analytical transforms (DFT, DCT) are efficient but assume fixed priors, failing to capture specific signal structures. Data-adaptive transforms improve approximation but may lack generalization. The paper aims to balance adaptivity with stability and efficiency.

Method: The transform is formulated as a product of a fixed canonical matrix and a refining data-adaptive sparse component. The algorithm is derived using inexact proximal methods with a newly developed closed-form projection operator, explicitly conditioning the transform via condition number metrics.

Result: State-of-the-art results on doubly sparse transform learning problems, with comparable performance to dense variants but at significantly lower computational cost, faster convergence, and better avoidance of bad local minima.

Conclusion: The proposed structured, explicitly conditioned transform learning approach successfully bridges analytical and adaptive transforms, offering a novel formulation with practical advantages in efficiency and optimization behavior.

Abstract: Finding convenient spaces in which certain hypotheses regarding an assumed sparse structure of natural signals hold true has become a desirable result in recent research, its implications being reflected in areas such as data compression, noise reduction and feature extraction. While the extensively used analytical transforms, such as DFT or DCT, already provide efficient algorithms and robust sparse representations, they assume a fixed prior about the data, failing to accurately capture the specific structure of more restrictive classes of signals. To address this, the concept of a data-adaptive, learnt transform has been introduced in the literature, allowing for the reduction of a residual term in the transform domain. More recent studies have shown that the condition number serves as a good metric in this context, where the desired outcome alternates between a generalizing tendency and one that achieves minimal approximation error. Motivated by these considerations, we introduce the learning of a structured, explicitly conditioned transform formulated as the product of a fixed canonical matrix and a refining data-adaptive sparse component. This approach seeks to preserve the advantages of fast and stable analytical transforms, while introducing controllable adaptivity to the data. No references that concern this specific formulation have been identified so far, indicating its novelty. The proposed algorithm is motivated within the framework of inexact proximal methods, leveraging a newly derived closed-form projection operator. Empirical observations demonstrate state-of-the-art results on the doubly sparse transform learning problem and comparable performance with its dense variant at significantly lower computational costs and sometimes faster convergence and better avoidance of bad local minima.

</details>


### [193] [Rotate2Think: Geometric Priming via Orthogonal Rotation to Improve Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09873)
*Aditya Sharma,Christopher J. Pal,Amal Zouaq*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shows that reasoning models' hidden representations during thinking are geometrically distinct from input embeddings (high conicity, non-collinear), and proposes Rotate2Think—a training-free method that rotates input embeddings toward thinking embeddings via Procrustes analysis and injects synthetic thinking vectors to improve reasoning accuracy across math, science, and code tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how hidden representations differ between input prompts and reasoning (thinking) traces, and to exploit this geometric structure to elicit stronger reasoning without additional training.

Method: Measure conicity of input and thinking embeddings (mean-pooled last-layer hidden states); observe they occupy distinct non-collinear regions; cast input-to-thinking transition as a rotation problem solved via orthogonal Procrustes analysis; propose Rotate2Think which estimates this rotation from few correct examples and injects synthetic thinking vectors at inference time.

Result: Rotate2Think improves accuracy in 30 of 32 model-benchmark configurations across mathematics, science, and code tasks, and generalizes zero-shot to multimodal reasoning on MATH-Vision.

Conclusion: The geometric distinction between input and thinking embeddings can be exploited via a simple rotation-based method to improve reasoning performance across diverse tasks and model families, without any training.

Abstract: Reasoning models achieve strong performance on challenging tasks by generating explicit intermediate reasoning traces before producing a final answer. Yet the internal structure of representation space when reasoning remains poorly understood: how do a model's hidden representations differ during thinking versus the embeddings of the input prompt, and can this structure be exploited to elicit stronger reasoning at inference time? We show that both input embeddings and thinking embeddings (mean-pooled last-layer hidden states over the prompt and reasoning trace, respectively) exhibit extremely high conicity, with all vectors clustering tightly around a single mean direction. Crucially, these mean input and thinking directions are non-collinear, with thinking embeddings occupying a geometrically distinct region of embedding space across many different models and benchmark tasks. This observation motivates casting the input-to-thinking transition as a rotation problem admitting a closed-form solution via orthogonal Procrustes analysis. We propose Rotate2Think, a training-free method that estimates this rotation from a small set of correctly solved examples and injects the resulting synthetic thinking vector between thinking delimiters at inference time, providing a geometric primer at the onset of the reasoning trace. Evaluated across multiple benchmarks and model families, Rotate2Think improves accuracy in 30 of 32 model-benchmark configurations across mathematics, science, and code tasks, and generalizes zero-shot to multimodal reasoning on MATH-Vision.

</details>


### [194] [Disjoint or Overlapping? Inference Windowing for Reconstruction-Based Time Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09874)
*Guillaume Coulaud,Reza Akbarinia,Florent Masseglia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper systematically studies how inference stride (overlap vs. disjoint windows) affects reconstruction-based univariate time series anomaly detection, finding that overlapping windows consistently improve performance by up to +28% across multiple model architectures, and proposing a unified evaluation protocol.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reconstruction-based anomaly detection methods suffer from heterogeneous evaluation practices and underspecified inference procedures, making results hard to compare. The role of inference stride—whether subsequences are processed as disjoint windows or with overlap—has been largely overlooked.

Method: The authors propose a unified training, tuning, and multi-seed evaluation protocol on the TSB-AD benchmark, systematically studying overlapping vs. disjoint inference across multiple models (PCA, DLinear, AutoEncoder, TimesNet, Transformer variants). They also evaluate on the full UCR archive with localization criteria.

Result: Overlapping windows consistently improve performance across all models with average relative gain up to +28%. The choice of inference stride can alter method rankings. Reconstruction-based baselines achieve strong performance on both TSB-AD and UCR benchmarks.

Conclusion: Reconstruction-based anomaly detection performance depends not only on model architecture and training but also on inference choices like stride, motivating clear and reproducible protocols. Reconstruction-based baselines remain competitive and practical for univariate time series anomaly detection.

Abstract: Reconstruction-based methods are widely used for time series anomaly detection, where models are trained to reconstruct subsequences, and anomalies are identified through reconstruction errors. However, reported results are often hard to compare due to heterogeneous evaluation practices and underspecified inference procedures. In this paper, we revisit reconstruction-based anomaly detection in the univariate offline setting and study the role of the inference stride, which controls whether subsequences are processed as disjoint windows or with overlap. We propose a unified training, tuning, and multi-seed evaluation protocol on the curated TSB-AD benchmark, and study how overlapping inference affects anomaly detection performance for a range of reconstruction models, including PCA-based baselines, DLinear, an AutoEncoder, TimesNet, and Transformer variants. The results show that across all models, overlapping windows yield consistent improvements, with average relative gain up to +28%, and can alter method rankings. We further analyze variability across datasets, random seeds, and hyperparameter configurations. Finally, we complement the benchmark study with an evaluation on the full UCR archive using localization criteria aligned with sliding-window reconstruction. Overall, our results highlight that reconstruction-based anomaly detection performance depends not only on model architecture and training, but also on inference choices, motivating a clear and reproducible protocol. Our results show that reconstructionbased baselines achieve strong performance on both TSB-AD and UCR benchmarks, supporting them as competitive and practical approaches for univariate time series anomaly detection.

</details>


### [195] [Integrating Local and Global Entropy for Uncertainty Quantification in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09875)
*Johanne Medina,Tianyi Zhou,Keivin Isufaj,Aristides Gionis,Sanjay Chawla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces GLU, an unsupervised uncertainty quantification method that combines geometric entropy from hidden states (global) with token-level entropy (local), capturing confident-yet-wrong failures that local-only methods miss, while being efficient and architecture-agnostic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs hallucinate confidently, and existing UQ methods rely on token-level signals, ignoring geometric structure of hidden states, which leaves certain failure modes (confident-but-wrong) undetected.

Method: Proposes GLU (Global-Local Uncertainty): fuses global uncertainty (geometric entropy of hidden-state matrices) with local uncertainty (token-level entropy) via a multiplicative gate, in a single unsupervised forward pass.

Result: Across 3 model families and 6 benchmarks, GLU matches or outperforms all unsupervised baselines, while being length-normalized and architecture-agnostic. Global and local signals are statistically near-orthogonal, capturing complementary failure regimes.

Conclusion: Hidden-state geometric structure is a powerful and complementary signal for UQ; fusing global and local uncertainty yields superior reliability prediction without additional computational overhead.

Abstract: Large language models hallucinate confidently, making uncertainty quantification (UQ) essential for reliable deployment. Existing methods rely predominantly on token-level signals, leaving the geometric structure of intermediate hidden states underused. In this paper, we take the geometric complexity of hidden-state matrices as a measure of the global uncertainty of LLMs, while treating token-level uncertainty estimation as a local metric. We show that hidden-state geometric entropy (global uncertainty) and token-level entropy (local uncertainty) are statistically near-orthogonal, capturing distinct failure regimes for reliability prediction. In particular, global geometry recovers the confident-but-wrong failure mode that local signals systematically miss. Building on this, we propose Global-Local Uncertainty (GLU), an unsupervised, single-pass score that fuses the two signals via a multiplicative gate. Across three model families and six benchmarks, GLU matches or outperforms all unsupervised baselines while requiring only a single forward pass and remaining length-normalized and architecture-agnostic.

</details>


### [196] [Calibrating Overconfidence Without Sacrificing Confidence: Probe-Conditioned Head Intervention for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09876)
*Ke Li,Chongzhe Zhang,Zifan Zeng,Feng Liu,Qunli Zhang,Zheng Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PCHI is an inference-time method that detects likely wrong-but-confident LLM responses and selectively rescales attention-head outputs to reduce overconfidence without significantly damaging warranted confidence, achieving ECE reduction from 21.9% to 9.2% on Qwen3-4B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often express high confidence in wrong answers, and existing calibration methods reduce both unwarranted and warranted confidence indiscriminately. The authors aim to selectively suppress overconfidence while preserving correct high-confidence expressions.

Method: PCHI (Probe-Conditioned Head Intervention) uses a frozen probe to detect likely wrong-but-confident responses, then conditionally rescales downstream attention-head outputs during confidence generation at inference time.

Result: On Qwen3-4B-Instruct: readout-token PCHI converts 82.2% of wrong-yes confidence to no; joint intervention reduces ECE from 21.9% to 9.2% while damaging only 5.1% of correct-yes readouts. Similar readout effect on Gemma3-4B but upstream interventions are weaker.

Conclusion: Verbalized overconfidence can be selectively reduced through conditional internal intervention, partially decoupling suppression of unwarranted confidence from loss of warranted confidence.

Abstract: Large language models often express high confidence in answers that are wrong. Standard calibration remedies typically act globally or at the score level, reducing unwarranted confidence but also risking erosion of warranted confidence on correct answers. We introduce Probe-Conditioned Head Intervention (PCHI), an inference-time method that uses a frozen probe to detect likely wrong-but-confident responses and conditionally rescales downstream attention-head outputs during confidence generation. On Qwen3-4B-Instruct solving OpenMathInstruct problems with a structured binary confidence field, readout-token PCHI converts 82.2% of originally wrong-yes confidence readouts to $\texttt{no}$, while a joint intervention across upstream confidence-template tokens reduces ECE from 21.9% to 9.2% and damages only 5.1% of originally correct-yes readouts. The readout-token effect also appears on Gemma3-4B, though upstream interventions are weaker and more mask-dependent. These results show that verbalized overconfidence can be selectively reduced through conditionally applied internal intervention, partially decoupling the suppression of unwarranted confidence from the loss of warranted confidence.

</details>


### [197] [Streaming Knowledge Compilation: Proactive Materiality-Scored Pinning for Time-Evolving LLM Wikis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09877)
*Juan M. Huerta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper formalizes Streaming Knowledge Compilation for LLM wiki systems that maintain compiled knowledge under evolving document streams. The authors propose a materiality signal φ_t(k,n) to proactively pin important documents before queries arrive, proving an O(√T log K) regret bound. They instantiate in finance (using abnormal stock volatility) and Wikipedia (using Abnormal Edit Ratio), demonstrating the same algorithm generalizes across domains. They also identify an LLM-as-judge confound on post-training knowledge, establishing regret analysis as a more reliable evaluation metric than absolute QA scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM wiki systems compile knowledge into pre-filled KV caches but assume a static corpus, which fails when the information landscape evolves. The paper aims to address the challenge of maintaining a compiled wiki under a document stream with a fixed token budget and unknown future queries, minimizing cumulative regret against an offline oracle.

Method: The paper formalizes Streaming Knowledge Compilation with a materiality signal φ_t(k,n) ∈ [0,1] that scores document importance as a query-relevance surrogate. They prove an O(√T log K) regret bound. They instantiate in finance using abnormal stock volatility predicted by a frozen Llama 3.1 8B classification head (AUROC=0.728), and in Wikipedia using Abnormal Edit Ratio (AER), a cross-sectionally normalized edit velocity.

Result: Finance: AUROC=0.728 on 76K articles with strict temporal split; 1.49× higher realized forward volatility for predicted-material articles. End-to-end QA on 173 matched pairs (finance) and 119 (Wikipedia). Finance cumulative regret converges to -20.0 (-0.12/step); Wikipedia to +16.0 (+0.13/step). Oracle outperforms No Wiki baseline (4.74 vs 3.80).

Conclusion: The paper establishes that regret analysis, not absolute QA scores, is the reliable evaluation metric for compiled knowledge systems due to an LLM-as-judge confound on post-training knowledge. The O(√T log K) guarantee applies to any domain where knowledge gaps can be predicted from streaming signals.

Abstract: LLM wiki systems compile knowledge into pre-filled KV caches for efficient inference, but assume a static corpus -- an assumption that fails whenever the underlying information landscape evolves. We formalize Streaming Knowledge Compilation: given a document stream, a fixed token budget, and future queries unknown at ingestion time, maintain a compiled wiki that minimizes cumulative regret against an offline oracle with perfect foresight. The enabling insight is a materiality signal $φ_t(k,n)\in[0,1]$ that scores document importance for entity $k$ at time $t$, acting as a query-relevance surrogate for proactive pinning before queries arrive; we prove an $O(\sqrt{T\log K})$ regret bound where $\varepsilon=\mathbb{E}[|φ_t-\hatφ_t|]$ is the only domain-specific quantity. We instantiate in two domains: finance, where $φ_t$ is abnormal stock volatility predicted by frozen Llama 3.1 8B classification head (AUROC = 0.728 on 76K articles, strict temporal split; $1.49\times$ higher realized forward volatility for predicted-material articles); and Wikipedia, where $φ_t$ is the Abnormal Edit Ratio (AER), a cross-sectionally normalized edit velocity -- showing the same algorithm generalizes beyond the finance domain. End-to-end QA evaluation on 173 matched pairs (finance) and 119 (Wikipedia) reveals a pervasive LLM-as-judge confound on post-training knowledge, establishing that regret analysis -- not absolute QA scores -- is the reliable evaluation metric for compiled knowledge systems. Finance cumulative regret converges to -20.0 (-0.12/step); Wikipedia to +16.0 (+0.13/step), with the positive sign confirming that Wikipedia edit content is genuinely post-training -- richer context consistently improves scores (No Wiki 3.80 vs. Oracle 4.74) -- and eliminates this confound. The $O(\sqrt{T\log K})$ guarantee applies to any domain where knowledge gaps can be predicted from streaming signals.

</details>


### [198] [FailureScope: Cross-Regime Behavioral Diagnosis of Language Model Weaknesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09878)
*Nicholas Saban*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FailureScope clusters evaluation probes by cross-model pass/fail patterns to produce interpretable failure taxonomies across single-turn benchmarks, multi-turn dialogue, and adversarial agent attacks, achieving strong predictive accuracy and cluster cohesion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard benchmarks report only aggregate accuracy, failing to tell practitioners which specific capabilities a model lacks. There is a need for a diagnostic method that reveals fine-grained behavioral failure patterns.

Method: Uses leave-one-model-out (LOMO) clustering of evaluation probes by cross-model pass/fail patterns. Applied across three regimes: single-turn benchmarks (2,664 tasks, 18 models), multi-turn dialogue (363 tasks), and adversarial agent attacks (630 traces).

Result: Taxonomy-conditioned sampling achieves Kendall's tau=0.81 at 50 tasks (vs 0.34 random); cross-model failure prediction reaches AUC 0.88. Reveals a 73-100 percentage-point gap between LLM-judge ASR and real execution in adversarial traces.

Conclusion: Behavioral clustering is a portable diagnosis primitive that generalizes beyond single benchmarks, with strong cluster cohesion across all three regimes. Pipeline and corpora are released publicly.

Abstract: Standard benchmarks report aggregate accuracy, but practitioners need to know which specific capabilities a model lacks. We introduce FailureScope, a behavioral-diagnosis method that clusters evaluation probes by their cross-model pass/fail patterns (leave-one-model-out, LOMO), and show it yields stable, interpretable failure taxonomies across three regimes usually studied separately: single-turn benchmarks, multi-turn dialogue, and adversarial agent attacks. On 2,664 single-turn tasks across 18 models, taxonomy-conditioned sampling reaches Kendall's tau = 0.81 at 50 tasks (versus 0.34 for random selection), and cross-model failure prediction reaches AUC 0.88. The same primitive recovers interpretable clusters on a 363-task multi-turn corpus and on 630 adversarial agent traces, where it exposes a meta-failure mode: a 73-100 percentage-point gap between LLM-judge ASR and real execution. Cluster cohesion remains strong across all three regimes, which we take as evidence that behavioral clustering is a portable diagnosis primitive that generalizes beyond any single benchmark. We release the pipeline, three annotated corpora, and the cross-regime taxonomies.

</details>


### [199] [Operator Fusion for LLM Inference on the Tensix Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09879)
*Qingbo Wu,Ke Li,Wenzhu Wang,Jie Yu,Ruian Zhang,Lili Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes an operator fusion strategy for Transformer models on Tenstorrent's Tensix architecture, fusing RMSNorm with matrix multiplication and using NoC-based multicast for multi-core parallelism, achieving up to 37.44% latency reduction on attention and 15.89% on MLP while maintaining high numerical accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address on-device inference bottlenecks of Transformer models on Tenstorrent's Tensix architecture, particularly reducing DRAM reads/writes of intermediate results and scheduling overhead.

Method: Operator fusion strategy fusing RMSNorm with matrix multiplication in self-attention and FFN, enabling back-to-back execution of memory-bound and compute-bound operators in on-chip SRAM; NoC-based multicast mechanism for multi-core parallelism using row/column master nodes.

Result: Up to 37.44% latency reduction for attention, 15.89% for MLP, up to 7.91% reduction per decoder layer; PCC above 98.75%.

Conclusion: The proposed fusion strategy achieves significant end-to-end efficiency gains under numerical consistency on Tenstorrent's Wormhole platform.

Abstract: This study addresses on-device inference bottlenecks of Transformer models on Tenstorrent's Tensix architecture and proposes an operator fusion strategy that enhances data locality. RMSNorm is fused with matrix multiplication in self-attention and in the FFN, enabling back-to-back execution of memory-bound and compute-bound operators in on-chip SRAM to significantly reduce DRAM reads/writes of intermediate results and scheduling overhead. To support multi-core parallelism, a NoC-based multicast mechanism is leveraged in which row/column master nodes efficiently distribute inputs and weights across the core mesh, alleviating DRAM bandwidth contention. Experiments on the Wormhole platform with Qwen2.5-0.5B, Qwen3-0.6B, and Qwen3-4B show up to 37.44% latency reduction for attention and 15.89% for MLP, with up to 7.91% reduction per decoder layer, while Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) remains above 98.75%, confirming significant end-to-end efficiency gains under numerical consistency.

</details>


### [200] [Hyperparameter Learning for Latent Factorization of Tensors for Representation Learning to Large-scale Dynamic Weighted Directed Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09880)
*Yaqian Zhan,Jialan He,Tianzhu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes DE-LFT, an automated hyperparameter optimization framework using Differential Evolution for Latent Factorization of Tensors in dynamic weighted directed networks, achieving better accuracy than manual tuning on real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The performance of Latent Factorization of Tensors (LFT) heavily depends on hyperparameter selection, which is often done manually or via grid search—requiring significant computational resources and human effort.

Method: Integrates Differential Evolution (DE) into the LFT training process to automatically learn optimal regularization parameters λ₁, λ₂, and λ₃.

Result: On four real-world datasets, DE-LFT achieves lower MAE and RMSE compared to manually tuned baselines, while reducing the need for extensive parameter tuning.

Conclusion: Automated hyperparameter optimization via DE effectively improves LFT prediction accuracy and reduces human effort in parameter tuning.

Abstract: Large-scale dynamic weighted directed networks (DWDNs) are widely used to model time-varying interactions among nodes. Latent factorization of tensors (LFT) extracts target knowledge from DWDNs via low-rank embedding. However, similar to many machine learning models, the performance of LFT heavily depends on the selection of hyperparameters. In practice, these parameters are often tuned manually or through grid search, which requires significant computational resources and human effort. Motivated by this challenge, this paper proposes an automated hyperparameter optimization framework based on Differential Evolution (DE) for LFT (DE-LFT). The proposed method integrates DE into the training process of the LFT model to automatically learn optimal regularization parameters $λ_1$, $λ_2$ and $λ_3$. As a result, the model can adaptively search the hyperparameter space and improve prediction accuracy. Experimental results on four real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves lower MAE and RMSE compared with manually tuned baselines while reducing the need for extensive parameter tuning.

</details>


### [201] [Toward Calibrated, Fair, and accurate Deepfake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09881)
*Ryan Brown,Chris Russell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Face-Fairness (FF) is a plug-and-play framework for deepfake detection bias mitigation that includes Face-Feature Tuning (FFT), the first demographic label-free fairness method, along with variants FF-Max and FF-Discover, which reduce performance gaps across demographic groups without retraining or sacrificing accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deepfake detectors exhibit significant performance disparities across demographic groups, and existing fairness approaches require demographic labels, model retraining, or sacrifice overall accuracy.

Method: The authors propose Face-Fairness (FF) framework with three variants: (1) Face-Feature Tuning (FFT) - a lightweight calibrator that remaps logits conditioned on frozen face embeddings without demographic labels; (2) FF-Max - maximizes worst-group accuracy when demographics are available; (3) FF-Discover - achieves similar results using embedding-discovered groups.

Result: FF consistently reduces FPR/TPR gaps and improves minimum group accuracy while maintaining or improving overall accuracy across in-domain and cross-dataset settings.

Conclusion: The approach is detector-agnostic, adds negligible runtime overhead, requires no identity attributes, and can be applied as a plug-and-play solution without retraining the base detector.

Abstract: Deepfake detectors show large performance gaps across demographic groups. Existing fairness approaches require demographic labels, retraining, or sacrifice accuracy. We introduce Face-Fairness (FF), a plug-and-play framework for bias mitigation. Our primary contribution, Face-Feature Tuning (FFT), is the first demographic label-free fairness method demonstrated for deepfake detection: a lightweight calibrator that performs a logit remapping conditioned on frozen face embeddings. We complement FFT with two variants: FF-Max, which maximizes worst-group accuracy when demographics are available, and FF-Discover, which does the same with embedding-discovered groups. Across in-domain and cross-dataset test settings, FF consistently reduces FPR/TPR gaps and improves minimum group accuracy while maintaining (often improving) overall accuracy. The approach is detector-agnostic, adds negligible runtime overhead, and requires no access to identity attributes.

</details>


### [202] [TD-Grokking: Learning from Zero-Reward Problems by Training-Time Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09883)
*Ningyuan Xi,Hao Xu,Hongsheng Xin,Ning Miao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TD-Grokking is a training-time decomposition framework that recursively breaks down intractable zero-reward problems into solvable subproblems with verifiable rewards, enabling RLVR to improve LLMs on problems where all sampled trajectories fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR (reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards) fails on highly challenging zero-reward problems because all sampled reasoning trajectories yield uniformly failed outcomes, providing no optimization signal. Prior solutions like dense process supervision or prefix-guided exploration have inherent limitations.

Method: TD-Grokking recursively decomposes intractable root problems into self-contained, verifiable subproblems, forming hierarchical trees where solvable leaves provide non-zero rewards for training.

Result: Outperforms vanilla GRPO and all baseline approaches on mathematical and medical tasks; converts zero-reward examples into usable training signals, yielding consistent performance gains.

Conclusion: Training-time decomposition effectively transforms zero-reward problems into learnable signals, enabling consistent performance improvements for LLMs on previously intractable problems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have made remarkable progress in reasoning tasks, largely driven by post-training paradigms, especially reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). However, a critical bottleneck persists: RLVR fails on highly challenging zero-reward problems, where all sampled reasoning trajectories yield uniformly failed outcomes, providing no optimization signal to drive model improvement. Prior efforts to address this limitation, such as dense process supervision, partial reward assignment, or prefix-guided exploration, suffer from inherent task constraints or do not fully equip the policy model with the capabilities necessary to solve the original intractable problems. To address this, we propose TD-Grokking, a training-time decomposition framework for zero-reward problems. It recursively decomposes intractable root problems into self-contained, verifiable subproblems, forming hierarchical trees where solvable leaves provide non-zero rewards. Evaluations on mathematical and medical tasks show that TD-Grokking outperforms vanilla GRPO as well as all baseline approaches. Together with detailed analysis, these results confirm that training-time decomposition effectively converts zero-reward examples into usable training signals, enabling consistent performance gains. Our code and datasets are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TD-Grokking-6567/.

</details>


### [203] [TENP: Trapezoidal Expert Neuron Pruning For Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09885)
*Jiangyang He,Shaolin Zhu,Deyi Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TENP is a structured pruning framework for MoE LLMs that selectively prunes less important experts and neurons in a trapezoidal pattern, achieving high sparsity with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE LLMs are constrained by large static parameter footprints, and existing compression methods either disrupt routing topology or rely on impractical unstructured pruning.

Method: TENP identifies important experts using both output magnitude and input direction change, applies Expert Neuron Pruning (ENP) to less important experts, and reserves parameters in a trapezoidal pattern across layers.

Result: At 40% routing expert sparsity and 63.76% activated params, DeepSeek drops only 1 point in accuracy, and outperforms full model by 10% on code generation.

Conclusion: Structured trapezoidal pruning effectively reduces MoE LLM footprint while maintaining or even improving performance on certain tasks.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts large language models (LLMs) scale efficiently through sparse activation, yet their deployment is fundamentally constrained by the large static parameter footprint of experts. Existing compression approaches either remove entire experts, disrupting routing topology and harming performance, or rely on unstructured weight pruning with limited practical efficiency. To address the limitations, we propose TENP, a structured Trapezoidal ExpertNeuron Pruning framework. Using a few samples, we identify and retain important experts, while applying expert neuron pruning (ENP) to less important experts, reserving model parameters in a trapezoidal pattern from shallow to deep layers. When evaluating expert importance, we jointly consider both the magnitude of the expert output and its ability to change the direction of the input vector. For ENP, we measure each neuron's projected contribution to the expert output to identify and retain important neurons. We conduct extensive experiments on the Qwen and DeepSeek models. Under a routing expert sparsity of 40% and an average of 63.76% activated expert parameters, the DeepSeek model suffers only a 1-point drop in accuracy compared to the full-parameter model. Moreover, it outperforms the full-parameter model by 10% on code generation tasks.

</details>


### [204] [SHAPE: Coalition-Aware Expert Pruning for Sparse Mixture-of-Experts LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09886)
*Yuhao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHAPE is a Shapley-based pruning framework for MoE LLMs that models expert cooperation within layers using cooperative game theory, preserving accuracy while reducing memory footprint by 20-40% expert pruning without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing expert pruning methods score experts independently and ignore that MoE inference is inherently coalitional, where outputs depend on top-k routed expert combinations. This oversight limits pruning effectiveness and memory efficiency.

Method: SHAPE formulates routing traces on a calibration set as an empirical cooperative game, assigns interaction-aware expert values via Shapley-style attribution over observed top-k coalitions, and uses a quality-coverage selection rule with bisection to retain minimal expert subsets covering an α fraction of non-negative Shapley mass under a global pruning budget.

Result: Experiments on Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-OSS-20B, and DeepSeek-V2-Lite show SHAPE consistently outperforms global and layer-wise pruning variants, maintaining competitive accuracy under 20% and 40% expert pruning without training, with clear reductions in peak GPU memory.

Conclusion: Modeling intra-layer expert cooperation via Shapley values is an effective, training-free pruning strategy for MoE LLMs, enabling significant memory savings while preserving model quality.

Abstract: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) large language models achieve strong quality with low per-token compute, yet their deployment is often limited by the memory wall: the full expert pool must remain resident to support token-dependent routing. Expert pruning is a direct remedy, but prior criteria typically score experts independently and overlook that MoE inference is inherently \emph{coalitional}, where outputs arise from routed top-$k$ expert combinations. We propose \textbf{SHAPE}, a task-driven pruning framework that explicitly models \emph{intra-layer} expert cooperation. SHAPE formulates routing traces on a small calibration set as an empirical cooperative game and assigns interaction-aware expert values via a Shapley-style attribution over observed top-$k$ coalitions, enabling the identification of experts that are essential for high-utility collaborations rather than merely frequent. To preserve MoE topology under a global pruning budget, SHAPE further introduces a \emph{quality-coverage} selection rule that retains, in each layer, the minimal expert subset covering an $α$ fraction of non-negative Shapley mass, while using bisection to match a target keep rate. Experiments on three modern MoE backbones (Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-OSS-20B, and DeepSeek-V2-Lite) across diverse benchmarks show that SHAPE consistently improves robustness over global and layer-wise pruning variants, maintaining competitive accuracy under 20\% and 40\% expert pruning without additional training and delivering clear reductions in peak GPU memory footprint. The open-source code is available at https://github.com/Alizen-1009/Shapley-Moe.

</details>


### [205] [SocraticPO: Policy Optimization via Interactive Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09887)
*Zirui Liu,Jie Ouyang,Qi Liu,Xianquan Wang,Jiayu Liu,Tingyue Pan,Qingchuan Li,Jing Sha,Zhenya Huang,Shijin Wang,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SocraticPO augments RL for LLMs with Socratic-style natural-language guidance from a teacher model during rollouts, paired with reward decay, improving scientific reasoning without requiring access to teacher logits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalar outcome rewards (e.g., binary correctness) provide direction but lack explanation of how to fix mistakes, leading to shortcut learning and brittle policies.

Method: During rollout: (1) student answers independently; (2) if incorrect, teacher provides corrective text guidance; (3) student continues under expanded context. Correct answers after intervention receive decayed rewards. Compatible with existing policy-gradient backends (e.g., Reinforce++).

Result: Outperforms strong RL and self-distillation baselines on SciKnowEval benchmarks. Ablations confirm both targeted guidance and reward decay are essential.

Conclusion: SocraticPO effectively integrates teacher guidance into policy optimization without logit access, and reward decay prevents over-reliance on assisted correction.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models usually supervises reasoning with scalar outcome rewards, such as binary correctness. Such rewards provide an optimization direction but rarely explain how a model should revise its mistaken reasoning, which can encourage shortcut learning and brittle policies. We propose \textbf{SocraticPO} (Socratic Policy Optimization), a policy-optimization framework that augments RL rollouts with Socratic-style natural-language guidance. During rollout, the student first answers independently; if the answer is incorrect, a teacher diagnoses the attempt and provides concise corrective guidance, after which the student continues under the expanded context. Crucially, this guidance is paired with reward decay: correct answers obtained after teacher intervention only receive decayed rewards, preventing the policy from treating teacher help as a free path to reward. Since SocraticPO only modifies the rollout process while leaving the standard expected-reward objective intact, it can be plugged into existing policy-gradient backends such as Reinforce++. Moreover, because the teacher provides only text-level guidance, SocraticPO can leverage stronger black-box teacher models without requiring access to logits or distribution matching. On undergraduate-level scientific reasoning benchmarks from SciKnowEval, SocraticPO improves over strong RL and self-distillation baselines. Ablations show that both targeted guidance and reward decay are necessary, with reward decay mitigating reliance on assisted correction.

</details>


### [206] [SinkRec: Mitigating Semantic State Sink in Long Sequence Recommendation with Memory-Conditioned Gated Delta Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09888)
*Zhuang Zhuang,Zhipeng Wei,Ji Dai,Jie Chen,Fei Pan,Peng Jiang,Kun Gai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SinkRec introduces a hybrid memory-transition architecture with residual vector quantization and TDGD to mitigate semantic state sink in linear attention for long-sequence recommendation, achieving efficient dynamic modeling while externalizing repetitive patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Linear attention for long-sequence recommendation suffers from "semantic state sink" — compressed recurrent states are over-occupied by repetitive behavior patterns, biasing subsequent readouts and degrading performance.

Method: SinkRec uses a hybrid memory-transition looped architecture that (1) externalizes recurring local patterns into a learnable conditional memory via residual vector quantization, (2) reinjects retrieved codes and exposes memory key-value pairs to attention, and (3) introduces Temporal-Aware State-Relation Differential Gated DeltaNet (TDGD) to purify recurrent writing/reading by suppressing memory-covered updates and removing memory-aligned readout responses.

Result: Experiments on public and industrial datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of SinkRec for long-sequence recommendation.

Conclusion: Turning recurring semantics from state-competing signals into memory-retrievable patterns allows the recurrent state to focus on dynamic transitions, alleviating semantic state sink while maintaining linear-time efficiency.

Abstract: Linear attention provides an efficient backbone for long-sequence recommendation by avoiding the quadratic cost of standard Transformers, but its compressed recurrent state can be dominated by repetitive behavior patterns. We identify this phenomenon as semantic state sink, where recurring semantics over-occupy the recurrent state and bias subsequent readouts. To mitigate semantic state sink, we propose SinkRec, a hybrid memory-transition looped architecture that decouples collaborative behavioral pattern storage from dynamic transition modeling. SinkRec externalizes recurring local patterns into a learnable conditional memory through residual vector quantization, reinjects the retrieved codes, and exposes memory key-value pairs to the attention block. It further introduces Temporal-Aware State-Relation Differential Gated DeltaNet (TDGD), which uses memory to purify recurrent writing and reading by suppressing memory-covered updates and removing memory-aligned readout responses. This design turns recurring semantics from state-competing signals into memory-retrievable patterns, allowing the recurrent state to focus on dynamic transitions and alleviating semantic state sink with linear-time efficiency. Experiments on public and industrial datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of SinkRec.

</details>


### [207] [Optuna Constrained Tree-Structured Parzen Estimator Is a Joint Density Generalization of c-TPE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09889)
*Shuhei Watanabe,Kaichi Irie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes Optuna's constrained TPE hyperparameter optimization algorithm, showing it uses a joint density approach (joint c-TPE) that is equivalent to the ECI acquisition function, and compares it to independent c-TPE, demonstrating that joint c-TPE is invariant to constraint duplication while independent c-TPE degrades.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Constrained hyperparameter optimization is common in practice, but Optuna's widely used constrained TPE algorithm lacked formal algorithmic analysis, creating a gap between practical usage and theoretical understanding.

Method: The authors mathematically analyze Optuna's constrained TPE, proving it is equivalent to joint c-TPE (using a single joint density over objective and constraints with ECI acquisition function), and compare it analytically against independent c-TPE which assumes independence between objective and constraints.

Result: Joint c-TPE is invariant to constraint duplication, while independent c-TPE degrades as the product accumulates duplicated constraint factors. The paper outlines practical tradeoffs between the two formulations.

Conclusion: The paper provides the first algorithmic analysis of Optuna's constrained TPE, clarifies its theoretical foundations, and suggests directions for future study on constrained HPO.

Abstract: Constrained hyperparameter optimization (HPO) is common in practice, yet Optuna's widely used constrained TPE lacks algorithmic analysis. While c-TPE proposes an expected constrained improvement (ECI) approach assuming independence between the objective and constraints, Optuna uses a single joint density over both. We show that Optuna's constrained TPE is joint c-TPE -- the same ECI acquisition function using a joint likelihood. We demonstrate joint c-TPE is invariant to constraint duplication whereas independent c-TPE degrades as the product accumulates duplicated factors. We outline practical tradeoffs between the formulations and directions for future study.

</details>


### [208] [PreAct-Bench: Benchmarking Predictive Monitoring in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09890)
*Hainiu Xu,Italo Luis da Silva,Jiangnan Ye,Yuhao Wang,Wei Liu,Linyi Yang,Jonathan Richard Schwarz,Nicola Paoletti,Yulan He,Hanqi Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Predictive Monitoring - a task to predict unethical actions from partial action trajectories before they happen - along with PreActBench (1,000 paired trajectories across 5 domains) and finds that even strong LLMs struggle at this task compared to humans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety research detects unethical behavior only after complete trajectories (retrospective), but harm may already be done. The authors argue for a future-oriented, predictive approach to intervene before unethical actions occur.

Method: Created PreActBench with 1,000 paired ethical/unethical action trajectories across 5 domains. Evaluated LLMs, safety guardrails, and latent probing methods on partial trajectories using a Prefix Foresight F1 metric.

Result: Humans achieve promising performance on predictive monitoring, but even strong LLM-based models struggle with this task, indicating significant room for improvement.

Conclusion: Predictive monitoring is a challenging but important safety task that current models cannot handle well. Future work should focus on developing future-oriented risk reasoning capabilities for LLM safety.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents capable of executing multi-step action trajectories toward a given objective. While existing safety research has focused on detecting unethical behavior from complete trajectories, this paradigm is fundamentally retrospective: it identifies harm only after it has already occurred. In this work, we study a critical yet overlooked safety task, which we term Predictive Monitoring: given only a partial action trajectory, can a model infer whether it will culminate in an unethical action before the overt action is executed? To support this task, we present PreActBench, a benchmark of 1,000 paired ethical and unethical action trajectories spanning five domains. We evaluate a range of LLMs, safety guardrail models, and latent probing methods across varying fractions of the action trajectory using our Prefix Foresight F1 metric. Results show that while humans achieve promising performance, predictive monitoring remains challenging even for strong models, highlighting the need for future-oriented risk reasoning in LLM safety.

</details>


### [209] [Representation Curriculum: Stagewise Training for Robust Ranking and Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09891)
*Ehsan Ebrahimzadeh,Sina Baharlouei,Abraham Bagherjeiran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Representation Curriculum (RC), a training-time intervention that temporally stages feature utilization to reduce over-reliance on exposure-confounded signals (like popularity and CTR) and promote content-based merit signals in digital marketplace ranking systems, improving cold-start generalization and robustness with controlled trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern ranking systems rely heavily on exposure-confounded signals (popularity, CTR/CVR aggregates, ID-based representations) which are predictive under stationary demand but create learning shortcuts. This over-reliance entrenchs incumbents, degrades cold-start generalization, and reduces robustness under distribution shift, motivating the need for methods that promote exposure-independent merit signals.

Method: Representation Curriculum (RC) is a training-time intervention that temporally stages feature utilization. It first foregrounds content-based merit signals, then introduces exposure-dependent belief signals while anchoring the content pathway near the learned merit representation, curbing shortcut reliance and mitigating gradient starvation on content signals. It is formalized independently of task and hypothesis class with ranking-specific instantiations, and provides closed-form solutions in a Gaussian linear ridge setting.

Result: In Gaussian linear ridge settings, RC strictly reduces population risk on cold-start target distributions with quantified Pareto tradeoffs against source performance. Experiments on public learning-to-rank and recommendation benchmarks, plus randomized online experiments in large-scale e-commerce search, show RC shifts reliance from historical belief signals toward content-based merit signals and yields consistent gains on cold populations with controlled trade-off in head performance.

Conclusion: Representation Curriculum effectively addresses the shortcut learning problem in ranking systems by promoting content-based merit signals over exposure-confounded ones, improving cold-start generalization and robustness while maintaining a manageable trade-off in head performance. The approach is theoretically grounded and empirically validated across multiple settings.

Abstract: Ranking in digital marketplaces is a dynamic exposure-allocation mechanism: displayed items shape discovery trajectories and success events logged by the platform to update future allocation policies. Modern ranking systems rely heavily on exposure-confounded signals (e.g. popularity estimates, CTR/CVR aggregates, and ID-based representation), because they are highly predictive under stationary demand. Yet this predictive power can become a learning shortcut: early access to exposure-dependent belief signals steers optimization toward over-reliance on them and away from exposure-independent merit signals (e.g., content-based competitiveness and semantic affinity). Consequently, the learned policy tends to entrench incumbents and degrade cold-start generalization and robustness under distribution shift. We propose Representation Curriculum (RC), a training-time intervention that temporally stages feature utilization. RC foregrounds content-based merit signals initially, then introduces exposure-dependent belief signals while anchoring the content pathway near the learned merit representation, curbing shortcut reliance on historical signals and mitigating gradient starvation on content signals. We formalize RC independently of task and hypothesis class and provide ranking-specific instantiations. In a Gaussian linear ridge setting, we derive closed-form solutions and sufficient conditions under which RC strictly reduces population risk on a cold-start target distribution, with a quantified Pareto tradeoff against source performance. Experiments on public learning-to-rank and recommendation benchmarks, and randomized online experiments in a large-scale e-commerce search system, show that RC measurably shifts reliance from historical belief signals toward content-based merit signals and yields consistent gains on cold populations with a controlled trade-off in head performance.

</details>


### [210] [LMT: A Bayesian Framework for Causal Discovery from Textual Alarm Records in Manufacturing Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09892)
*Xiaofeng Xiao,Jianhong Chen,Qiuzhuang Sun,Naichen Shi,Xubo Yue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LMT is a Bayesian causal discovery framework that combines LLM-extracted semantic signals from event descriptions with timestamp-based Poisson process likelihood to produce interpretable, data-supported causal graphs for engineering event data, particularly effective in small-sample alarm scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Engineers need to understand causal relationships in textual event records (e.g., alarm logs), but existing methods either only find correlations/patterns or fail because semantic clues alone cannot reliably distinguish causation from correlation or sequential patterns.

Method: LMT uses LLMs to extract semantic causal signals from event descriptions to construct a Bayesian prior over causal graphs, then refines this prior using a Poisson-process-based likelihood on timestamp data, jointly integrating textual and temporal evidence.

Result: Simulation studies demonstrate LMT's effectiveness across various settings, with particular advantages in small-sample alarm-event scenarios where pure data-driven or pure LLM-based approaches fall short.

Conclusion: Integrating LLM-informed priors with temporal statistical evidence yields more accurate and interpretable causal discovery for engineering event data than relying on either modality alone.

Abstract: Textual event records, such as alarm logs, have become an increasingly common data source in engineering and manufacturing systems. Beyond identifying correlations or recurring patterns, engineers are often interested in understanding which types of events causally trigger or influence other events during system operation. Textual event descriptions may contain semantic clues about such causal relationships, and recent large language models (LLMs) provide a promising tool for extracting these signals. However, relying solely on LLM-encoded textual information is insufficient for accurate causal discovery, since semantic patterns do not directly reveal causal mechanisms and may confuse causation with correlation or frequent sequential patterns. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{LMT}, a Bayesian causal discovery framework for engineering event data that jointly leverages textual descriptions and timestamps. Specifically, LMT first uses LLMs to extract semantic causal signals from event descriptions and constructs a prior distribution over causal graphs among event types or event clusters. It then incorporates temporal evidence through a Poisson-process-based likelihood, allowing the LLM-informed prior to be refined by timestamp-based statistical evidence. By integrating the textual and temporal information, LMT produces a causal graph that is both interpretable and data-supported. Simulation studies show that the proposed framework is effective across different settings and is especially advantageous in small-sample alarm-event scenarios.

</details>


### [211] [A Navigable Manifold of Hypothesized Consciousness-Spectrum States in Language Model Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09894)
*Sophie Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that transformer embedding spaces encode a structured, navigable geometric organization aligned with a consciousness spectrum (from reactive to integrative states), where similar states cluster locally, intermediate regions form transition corridors, and trajectories naturally traverse from lower to higher levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether language models encode a human-interpretable consciousness spectrum in their representation space, which is important for model guidance, evaluation, and alignment.

Method: The authors analyze the geometric structure and dynamics of embedding patterns along the consciousness spectrum in transformer embedding spaces, studying clustering properties, convexity-like stability of regions, and trajectory dynamics using both utility-guided and geometry-only greedy approaches.

Result: Embeddings exhibit globally organized geometry aligned with the consciousness spectrum; similar states cluster into locally coherent regions forming a structured manifold; higher/lower-level regions show convexity-like stability while intermediate regions form transition corridors; trajectories consistently traverse from lower to higher regions.

Conclusion: Embedding spaces encode structured and navigable geometry aligned with a hypothesized consciousness-spectrum taxonomy, providing a representation-level perspective for analyzing and guiding model behavior.

Abstract: Across contemplative, philosophical, and psychological accounts, human consciousness is often described along a similar spectrum, ranging from reactive and self-focused patterns to more integrative and coherent ones. Understanding whether language models encode such a structured, human-interpretable consciousness spectrum in representation space is important for model guidance, evaluation and alignment. In this work, we study the geometric structure and dynamics of patterns along this spectrum in transformer embedding spaces. We show that embeddings exhibit a globally organized geometry aligned with this spectrum: sentences associated with similar states cluster into locally coherent regions, forming a structured manifold. In particular, higher-level and lower-level regions exhibit convexity-like stability, while intermediate regions form a transition corridor. Dynamically, both utility-guided and geometry-only greedy trajectories consistently traverse from lower- to higher-level regions, passing through intermediate tiers, indicating that navigability is an intrinsic property of the representation space, guided but not dictated by a global directional signal. These results suggest that embedding spaces encode structured and navigable geometry aligned with a hypothesized consciousness-spectrum taxonomy, broadly inspired by recurring structural descriptions of human consciousness across contemplative traditions, philosophy, and modern psychology, providing a representation-level perspective for analyzing and guiding model behavior.

</details>


### [212] [TRAPS: Therapeutic Response Analysis via Pathway-informed Stratification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09898)
*Sujoy Banik,Sayantan Chakraborty,Boishakhi Das Toma,Zainab Ghafoor,Ushashi Bhattacharjee,Koushik Howlader,Tirtho Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First unified benchmark comparing three pathway-informed deep learning models (BINN, GraphPath, PATH) across five cancer cohorts for joint therapy response and survival prediction, finding no single winner: PATH excels at targeted therapy prediction, BINN at survival prediction, and GraphPath achieves 0.92 AUROC on prostate targeted therapy despite 11% class imbalance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pathway-informed models are developed and tested in isolation, making fair comparison impossible. Clinicians need multi-dimensional decisions (targeted therapy, radiation therapy, survival prognosis), yet no unified benchmark exists for combined therapy and survival prediction.

Method: Benchmarked three architectures (BINN, GraphPath, PATH) on 2,622 patients from TCGA using Reactome pathway activity scores. All models were trained jointly on three clinical outcomes (targeted therapy prediction, radiation therapy prediction, survival prediction) under identical data splits and evaluation protocols.

Result: No single architecture dominates: PATH best for targeted therapy, BINN best for survival, all models fail for radiation therapy. GraphPath achieves 0.92 AUROC on prostate targeted therapy despite 11% positive class prevalence.

Conclusion: Pathway-structured models show promise but no universal winner exists; radiation therapy prediction requires clinical variables beyond gene expression; GraphPath's lateral co-regulation structure excels when matched to cohorts with narrow targetable driver programmes.

Abstract: Cancer treatment planning requires decisions across multiple clinical dimensions at once. Clinicians must determine whether a patient should receive targeted molecular therapy, radiation therapy, and whether they are likely to survive beyond six months. Existing pathway-informed deep learning models have been developed and tested in isolation, making fair comparison across architectures impossible. We present the first unified benchmark for pathway-guided therapy response modeling, evaluating three biologically informed architectures, BINN, GraphPath, and PATH, across five cancer cohorts drawn from The Cancer Genome Atlas, representing 2,622 patients encoded using Reactome pathway activity scores. Each model is trained jointly on all three clinical outcomes under identical data and evaluation conditions, the first study to treat pathway-structured deep learning as a combined therapy and survival prediction problem. Our results show that no single architecture wins across all tasks: PATH performs best for targeted molecular therapy prediction overall, BINN is most reliable for survival prediction, and no model produces useful predictions for radiation therapy, as the key drivers of that decision are clinical variables not captured in gene expression data. Most strikingly, GraphPath achieves an AUROC of 0.92 on prostate targeted molecular therapy prediction, the highest score in the entire benchmark, demonstrating that lateral co-regulation structure produces exceptional discriminative power when matched to a cohort with a narrow targetable driver programme, even under conditions of extreme class imbalance at only 11\% positive prevalence.

</details>


### [213] [When Attribution Patching Lies: Diagnosis and a Second-Order Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09899)
*Luyang Zhang,Jialu Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies that attribution patching errors in mechanistic interpretability stem from downstream non-linearities (not local curvature), and proposes three practical solutions: a reliability score, error bounds, and a computationally efficient HVP correction that outperforms Integrated Gradients at scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activation patching is the gold standard for causal circuit identification but is computationally prohibitive. Attribution patching (gradient-based approximation) is widely used but its reliability is poorly understood, risking misidentification of underlying mechanisms.

Method: The authors characterize the source of attribution patching error (downstream non-linearities > local curvature), then develop: (i) a reliability score, (ii) error bounds, and (iii) an HVP correction requiring one additional backward pass. Evaluated across five model families (124M-9B) on random-token and name-swap perturbations.

Result: HVP correction is the only feasible second-order method at large scale; multi-step HVP matches/exceeds Integrated Gradients accuracy at lower compute. Improves circuit recovery fidelity on benchmarks. Enables a Screen-Flag-Fix workflow targeting computation only toward unreliable components.

Conclusion: Downstream non-linearities dominate attribution patching errors. The proposed tools (reliability score, error bounds, HVP correction) enable higher-fidelity circuit recovery with practical computational efficiency, supporting scalable mechanistic interpretability.

Abstract: A central goal of mechanistic interpretability is to identify which internal components causally drive a language model's behavior.
