This baseline introduces a structural vulnerability: on harder instances, a poor baseline produces noisy gradient estimates that can destabilize training. We evaluate Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), an algorithm from large language model alignment that eliminates the baseline entirely by normalizing advantages within groups of sampled trajectories. In a controlled comparison of five RL algorithms on TSP and CVRP benchmarks within the RL4CO framework, we find that: (i) GRPO avoids the training collapse observed with REINFORCE on TSP-100, where performance degrades from cost 9.8 to 52.1 immediately after the warmup phase and does not recover under extended training; (ii) at matched gradient updates, GRPO achieves solution quality within 2% of POMO, a strong AM-based multi-start baseline, while requiring no external baseline; and (iii) P3O, a pairwise preference algorithm also from the alignment literature, is competitive on TSP but shows higher variability on CVRP. These results identify GRPO as a promising baseline-free alternative for NCO, particularly in settings where baseline-dependent training becomes fragile.

</details>


### [241] [Bittensor Agent Arenas as a Trajectory Primitive: Distilling a Shopping Agent from ShoppingBench Subnet Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10064)
*Shardul Bansal,Seth Schilbe,Jarrod Barnes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that small-model agentic post-training is bottlenecked by trajectory data quality, not algorithms. It introduces ORO Subnet 15 (SN15), a Bittensor-based arena that produces incentive-aligned, judged, anti-memorized trajectories. Post-training Qwen3-4B with this data raises ASR from 18.0% to 42.7%, near the synthetic-data SFT baseline (43.6%), and identifies sub-task firehose as key to closing the gap to the 48.7% SFT+GRPO bar.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing trajectory data sources are flawed: frontier-synthesized data inherits biases and collapses the long tail, while production logs lack judgment and contain shortcut behaviors. The authors aim to engineer an incentive-aligned agent arena to manufacture high-quality training trajectories.

Method: The authors deploy ORO Subnet 15 (SN15) on Bittensor using the ShoppingBench agentic-commerce benchmark. The arena includes a race mechanism, LLM reasoning judge, and rotating leak-cluster-guarded problem suite. They apply a structural-quality filter to distinguish agentic trajectories (model emits tool calls) from sub-task trajectories (model classifies over a deterministic loop), then post-train Qwen3-4B with SFT-then-GRPO pipeline. They also introduce Dr. GRPO for per-step process improvement.

Result: Qwen3-4B ASR lifts from 18.0% to 42.7%, within single-problem noise of the synthetic-data SFT-only baseline (43.6%), trained on a fraction of a single day of subnet output. Pass@8 is 53.3% vs pass@1 34.8%, showing a large gap. Dr. GRPO converts this headroom into process improvement.

Conclusion: Incentive-aligned agent arenas can manufacture high-quality trajectory data for small-model post-training. The sub-task firehose is identified as the primary lever for closing the gap to the SFT+GRPO baseline (48.7%). The filter, corpus splits, and arena mechanics are released.

Abstract: Small-model agentic post-training is bottlenecked less by the algorithm than by the trajectory substrate it consumes. Leading recipes (RLVR, group-relative RL, rejection-sampled re-SFT) all need multi-turn traces carrying per-trajectory supervision, and the two existing sources fall short: frontier-synthesised data inherits the synthesizer's biases and collapses the long tail, while unfiltered production logs are unjudged and contaminated by shortcut behaviour. We argue that an incentive-aligned agent arena can be engineered to manufacture such trajectories, and demonstrate this on ORO Subnet 15 (SN15), a Bittensor deployment of the ShoppingBench agentic-commerce benchmark. SN15's race mechanism, LLM reasoning judge, and rotating leak-cluster-guarded problem suite yield a corpus with three properties: incentive-aligned diversity, per-trajectory judging, and anti-memorised held-out evaluation. We introduce a structural-quality filter that converts the raw firehose into a trainable corpus by keeping agentic trajectories (the model itself emits the tool calls) and rejecting sub-task trajectories (the model only classifies or narrates over a deterministic search loop), then post-train Qwen3-4B with a recipe matched to the published ShoppingBench SFT-then-GRPO pipeline. On a leak-cluster-guarded held-out partition scored production-strict, the model lifts from the published Qwen3-4B base of 18.0% ASR to 42.7%, within single-problem noise of the synthetic-data SFT-only baseline (43.6%), while training on a fraction of a single day of subnet output. The supervised stack leaves a large pass@8 to pass@1 gap (53.3% vs 34.8%); a per-step teacher-grounded Dr. GRPO reward converts that headroom into process improvement, and we identify the sub-task firehose as the primary lever for closing the gap to the 48.7% SFT+GRPO bar. We release the filter, the corpus splits, and the arena mechanics.

</details>


### [242] [Importance-Aware Scheduling for High-Dimensional Hyperparameter Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10068)
*Ruinan Wang,Ian Nabney,Mohammad Golbabaee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GIF is an importance-aware scheduling strategy for high-dimensional hyperparameter optimization that estimates parameter importance via warm-start, groups trials proportionally, and outperforms conventional methods like TPE and Random Search on high-dimensional benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional HPO optimizers struggle in high-dimensional spaces where evaluations are costly and progress is diluted across many low-impact variables.

Method: GIF uses a small-sample warm start to estimate hyperparameter importance, forms importance-based groups, allocates trials proportionally, and retains a full-space fallback.

Result: On high-dimensional benchmarks, GIF reaches better incumbents with faster convergence than TPE, BOHB, Random Search, and Sequential Grouping. On lower-dimensional Bayesmark, GIF remains competitive but with smaller margins.

Conclusion: GIF is a simple and plug-compatible way to improve sample efficiency in high-dimensional HPO.

Abstract: Hyperparameter Optimization (HPO) is essential for building high-performing ML/DL models, yet conventional optimizers often struggle in high-dimensional spaces where evaluations are costly and progress is diluted across many low-impact variables. We propose Greedy Importance First (GIF), an importance-aware scheduling strategy that uses a small-sample warm start to estimate hyperparameter importance, forms importance-based groups, allocates trials proportionally, and retains a full-space fallback. We evaluate GIF under fixed evaluation budgets on five anisotropic analytic functions, Bayesmark, and NAS-Bench-301. On the higher-dimensional benchmarks, GIF reaches better incumbents with faster convergence than TPE, BOHB, Random Search, and Sequential Grouping. On Bayesmark, where the effective dimensionality is smaller, GIF remains competitive but the margins are smaller. Ablation studies show that importance estimation, proportional allocation, and the fallback step all contribute to the gains. We also verify that the HIA component recovers the intended anisotropy on the analytic benchmarks. These results suggest that GIF is a simple and plug-compatible way to improve sample efficiency in high-dimensional HPO.

</details>


### [243] [Spatiotemporal Seismic Hazard Assessment Using VQ-VAE and Seismic Statistical Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10069)
*Wei Quan,Denise Gorse*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends previous earthquake prediction research by (1) moving from whole-region to localized (24km radius) predictions using seismic statistical features (SSFs), and (2) introducing a novel VQ-VAE-derived spatial feature from 2D seismic maps that enhances prediction performance and can replace the traditional b-value feature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve earthquake prediction by moving from coarse whole-region predictions to localized predictions, and to enhance predictive capability by incorporating spatial (2D map) information beyond traditional 1D catalogue-based features.

Method: Extended prior XGBoost-based work by (1) restricting feature computation and prediction to 24km-radius circles around candidate events, and (2) training a VQ-VAE model on 2D seismic maps to generate a new spatial feature indicating crustal stress buildup, then combining this with 60 SSFs and evaluating via SHAP analysis.

Result: Localized predictions using SSFs alone achieved test AUC values comparable to previous whole-region results. The VQ-VAE-derived spatial feature was top-ranked by SHAP analysis and enhanced performance, while also near-fully replacing the traditionally-computed b-value.

Conclusion: Localized earthquake prediction is feasible using SSFs, and incorporating spatial features from deep learning (VQ-VAE) further improves performance, with the new feature serving as an effective replacement for the conventional b-value.

Abstract: In this paper we build upon a previous study in which we demonstrated, using XGBoost and earthquake catalogue data from Japan and Chile, that a set of 60 seismic statistical features (SSFs) had much greater predictive value than a set of 428 generic time series features from the tsfresh package. We here extend this previous work in two key ways, focusing on data from Japan as a large dataset is necessary in order to allow for the training of a deep learning (autoencoder) model. First, we move from whole-region prediction (considering, for each candidate event, the likelihood of an event M $\geq$ 5.0 anywhere in the region in the next 15 days) to localised predictions in which both the region of feature computation and the region of prediction are restricted to a circle of radius 24 km around the candidate event, and we show that performance remains excellent, similar to our previous whole-region study for the same area. Second, we here couple this proven set of SSFs, based on one-dimensional (catalogue) data, with a novel feature based on two-dimensional seismic maps, obtained by training a VQ-VAE model to reproduce such maps as output and identifying a measure of its error in doing so with a localised build-up of crustal stress. We show that while localised prediction based on SSFs can be effective alone, with test AUC values as high as those obtained in the case of Japan in our previous whole-region study, the inclusion of the new natively-spatial VQ-VAE-derived feature, top-ranked by SHAP analysis, can enhance performance and additionally appears to near-wholly replace the traditionally-computed $b$-value in terms of feature usage.

</details>


### [244] [Temporal Sheaf Neural Networks with Dynamic Orthogonal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10071)
*Md Sadek Hossain Asif,Tanzila Khan,Md. Mosaddek Khan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TSNN is a temporal link prediction framework using time-varying local coordinate frames with explicit geometric transport, outperforming prior methods especially on heterogeneous graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing continuous-time graph models operate in a shared global embedding space, failing to capture node-specific and evolving interaction semantics.

Method: Each node gets a time-varying orthogonal frame via low-rank Householder products; node states are compared only after explicit geometric transport between local frames. A geometric-residual decoder anchors predictions on transported distances with learned residuals. All computations are strictly causal.

Result: Matches or surpasses strongest prior methods on TGB v2 and DGB benchmarks, with largest gains on graphs with strong node-role heterogeneity. Ablations confirm benefits of dynamic frames, orthogonal transport, and geometric-residual decoding.

Conclusion: Geometric transport between dynamic local frames is a principled and effective alternative to shared global embeddings for temporal link prediction.

Abstract: We introduce Temporal Sheaf Neural Networks (TSNN), a temporal link prediction framework that equips each node with a time-varying orthogonal frame and compares node states only after explicit transport between local coordinate systems. In contrast to existing continuous-time graph models that operate in a shared global embedding space, TSNN models node-specific and evolving interaction semantics through dynamic local frames. The model parameterizes per-node frames via efficient low-rank Householder products, preserves stored hidden states exactly under frame updates, and uses a geometric-residual decoder that anchors predictions on transported distances while learning residual corrections. All computations are strictly causal and use only the pre-event history. We show that the symmetric degree-normalized sheaf Laplacian is orthogonally similar to the symmetric normalized graph Laplacian, with the random-walk normalized form similar in the corresponding degree metric; the full-active, feature-scaled diffusion used by TSNN is exactly a metric-gradient step on the combinatorial sheaf Dirichlet energy, with a degree-free monotone-descent and non-expansiveness guarantee. Frame drift perturbs updates only linearly. Across TGB v2 link-prediction and temporal-heterogeneous leaderboards, together with the DGB benchmark suite, TSNN matches or surpasses the strongest prior methods on most benchmarks, with the largest improvements on graphs exhibiting strong node-role heterogeneity. Ablations confirm the distinct benefit of dynamic frames, orthogonal transport, and geometric-residual decoding.

</details>


### [245] [VFUSE: Virulent Feature Understanding with Sparse autoEncoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10080)
*Michael Yu,Matthew L. Olson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VFUSE applies sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to diffusion-transformer activations in protein models (RoseTTAFold3, RFDiffusion3) to detect hazardous protein designs, achieving interpretable features with AUROC up to 0.84 for virulence detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models for protein design risk producing hazardous proteins opaquely, necessitating interpretability methods to audit and detect dangerous designs without sacrificing model performance.

Method: Train sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on diffusion-transformer activations from RoseTTAFold3 and RFDiffusion3, then use linear probes in SAE latent space to detect virulent features.

Result: Linear probes in SAE latent space outperform original representations for hazard detection; monosemantic features achieve AUROC 0.84 (q < 10^{-13}) specifically for hazardous designs.

Conclusion: First SAE trained on an all-atom diffusion model and first feature-level virulence audit of a protein design model, enabling safer and more interpretable protein design.

Abstract: Generative models have shown remarkable progress in a variety of domains such as protein design, but such power enables the opaque generation of hazardous proteins. In this work, we introduce VFUSE (Virulent Feature Understanding with Sparse autoEncoders), a mechanistic interpretability approach that trains SAEs on diffusion-transformer activations to audit protein models for hazard-aware features. We apply VFUSE to RoseTTAFold3 and RFDiffusion3, popular open-weight models for protein folding and synthesis. We find that for certain blocks, linear probes detect hazardous designs significantly better when fit in the SAE latent space over the original model's representations: improving interpretability without sacrificing model performance. Furthermore, we identify monosemantic features from the SAE that fire only on hazardous designs at up to AUROC $0.84$ ($q < 10^{-13}$). To our knowledge this is the first SAE trained on an all-atom diffusion model and the first feature-level virulence audit of a protein design model, paving the way towards safe and interpretable protein design.

</details>


### [246] [Divide-and-Conquer Modeling for the CTF-4-Science Lorenz Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10084)
*Shundong Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A divide-and-conquer strategy using multiple specialized models (smoothing, NG-RC/NVAR, Lorenz correction, parametric blend) achieves 79.63 score on the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark by tailoring each model to specific task scenarios rather than using one unified model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chaotic-system prediction involves diverse regimes (noisy, clean, few-shot, parametric) where a single model class struggles. The authors aim to show that bounded, scenario-specific updates outperform broad model replacement.

Method: Divide-and-conquer: smoothing for noisy reconstruction; NG-RC/NVAR for noisy long-time forecasting; fitted Lorenz correction for clean short-time prefix; parametric prefix blend for interpolation. Each model block is matched to its task group's evaluation behavior.

Result: Final public score of 79.63 on the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark across twelve hidden scores and five scenario families.

Conclusion: Bounded, scenario-specific updates can outperform broad model replacement on mixed chaotic forecasting benchmarks.

Abstract: This work presents a divide-and-conquer modeling strategy for the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark, which evaluates chaotic-system prediction across twelve hidden scores and five scenario families: clean forecasting, noisy reconstruction, noisy-input forecasting, few-shot learning, and parametric generalization. Rather than forcing one model class to handle all regimes, the final system matched each prediction block to the evaluation behavior of its task group. The main contributions are: smoothing-based reconstruction for noisy full-trajectory denoising; NG-RC/NVAR models tuned for noisy long-time attractor forecasting; a fitted Lorenz transition correction restricted to the sensitive clean short-time prefix; and a parametric prefix blend for the interpolation task. The resulting system with final public score of 79.63 shows that bounded, scenario-specific updates can outperform broad model replacement on mixed chaotic forecasting benchmarks.

</details>


### [247] [A Theory on Flow Matching with Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10089)
*Yihan He,Qishuo Yin,Yuan Cao,Jianqing Fan,Han Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides theoretical foundations for flow matching with neural-network-parameterized conditional velocity fields, including convergence guarantees, generalization bounds, and Wasserstein-distance guarantees for generated samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish rigorous theoretical foundations for flow matching with neural-network-parameterized velocity fields, which lack comprehensive theoretical understanding despite empirical success.

Method: Developed theoretical analysis using over-parameterized 2-layered ReLU neural networks, generalization bounds for multi-task representation learning with unbounded losses, and validated through synthetic and real-world image benchmarks.

Result: Convergence guarantees for gradient descent, generalization bounds for conditional velocity-field matching, and Wasserstein-distance guarantees for generated samples, validated through experiments.

Conclusion: The theoretical framework establishes convergence and generalization guarantees for flow matching, with potential broader applicability beyond flow-based generative modeling.

Abstract: In this work, we develop theoretical foundation for flow matching with neural-network-parameterized conditional velocity fields. We establish convergence guarantees for gradient descent in the over-parameterized 2-layered ReLU neural network regime. We derive generalization bounds for the conditional velocity-field matching objective. Building on these results, we provide Wasserstein-distance guarantees for the samples generated by the induced flow. Our analysis is based on generalization bound for multi-task representation learning with unbounded losses, which may be of independent interest beyond flow-based generative modeling. These theoretical results are validated through extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world image benchmarks.

</details>


### [248] [Decision-Making under Combinatorial Risk](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10092)
*Yifan Hong,Hongmiao Fan,Chen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an investment-allocation task to study decision-making under combinatorial risk, finding that people rely on core features like probability increments and initial probabilities rather than fully evaluating induced outcome distributions, and that revealing the probability mass function shifts behavior toward more standard lottery valuation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world decisions often involve combinatorial risk from multiple interacting components, yet existing research focuses on single-shot lottery choices. The authors aim to understand how people navigate complex risk structures where the outcome distribution is induced rather than given.

Method: The authors designed an investment-allocation task where investing in a component increases its success probability. They compared behavior with and without displaying the induced probability mass function (PMF), and used symbolic regression to discover compact descriptive models beyond standard benchmarks.

Result: Participants favor options with larger probability increments and, when increments are equal, higher initial success probabilities. Revealing the PMF significantly changes behavior, reducing responsiveness to combinatorial-risk features and lowering choice variance. Discovered models rely on combinatorial-risk features rather than full distribution evaluation.

Conclusion: People navigate combinatorial risk primarily through its core features rather than exact lottery valuation, and shift toward prospect-theoretic valuation only when the induced PMF is explicitly displayed.

Abstract: Decision-making under risk is typically studied through single-shot lottery choices. Yet many real decisions involve combinatorial risk, where risk arises from multiple risky components, so the lottery over outcomes is induced rather than given outright and can be costly to evaluate exactly. We introduce an investment-allocation task to study decision under combinatorial risk, where investing in a component raises its success probability and thereby reshapes the outcome distribution. Participants favor the option with the larger probability increment, and, when increments are equal, the option with the higher initial success probability. Revealing the induced probability mass function (PMF) substantially changes behavior, making participants less responsive to combinatorial-risk features and reducing choice variance. To explain these patterns, we move beyond standard benchmarks and hand-crafted hypotheses with symbolic regression to discover compact descriptive models. The discovered models rely mainly on combinatorial-risk features, such as the after-investment success probability, rather than exact evaluation of the full induced distribution. Behavior under the displayed PMF is then well explained by augmenting this model with a prospect-theoretic residual model. The results show that people navigate combinatorial risk primarily through its core features, shifting toward lottery valuation only when the induced PMF is displayed.

</details>


### [249] [Unsupervised Style Representation Learning for AI-Text Detection via Paraphrase Inversion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10099)
*Rafael Rivera Soto,Barry Chen,Nicholas Andrews*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes learning discriminative style features for LLM text detection without authorship labels, using a style encoder trained to reconstruct human text from machine-generated paraphrases. The method achieves competitive few-shot and zero-shot detection, generalizing well to unseen LLMs and tasks like authorship verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM misuse (plagiarism, misinformation, influence operations) demands robust detectors. Style-based detectors are effective and robust to adversarial attacks, but current methods rely on authorship labels and are limited to few-shot inference requiring in-distribution samples that may not be available.

Method: Train a style encoder to reconstruct human-authored text from its machine-generated paraphrase, while freezing a semantic encoder during training to bias the style encoder toward capturing only non-semantic (style) features. Evaluation uses few-shot detectors and zero-shot DeepSVDD-based detectors.

Result: Matches or outperforms all baselines in few-shot setting. In zero-shot regime, competitive with fully supervised classifiers on in-distribution data and generalizes better to unseen LLMs. Representations also generalize to authorship verification and fine-grained style discrimination without training on those tasks.

Conclusion: Style representations learned without authorship labels can effectively detect LLM-generated text in both few-shot and zero-shot settings, with strong generalization to unseen LLMs and auxiliary tasks like authorship verification and style discrimination.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has raised concerns about misuse such as plagiarism, misinformation, and automated influence operations, motivating the need for robust detectors. Recent work has shown that neural representations of writing style are effective for detection and, crucially, robust to adversarial attacks that defeat most existing detectors. However, current style-based detectors rely on authorship labels for training, and are limited to few-shot inference for detection, requiring in-distribution samples that may not always be available. We learn discriminative style features without authorship labels by training a style encoder to reconstruct human-authored text from its machine-generated paraphrase; freezing a semantic encoder during training biases the style encoder to capture only the non-semantic features needed for reconstruction. We evaluate the learned representations via two detection strategies: a few-shot detector and a zero-shot DeepSVDD-based detector. Across benchmarks, our method matches or outperforms all baselines in the few-shot setting and, in the zero-shot regime, is competitive with fully supervised classifiers on in-distribution test data while generalizing better to unseen LLMs. Beyond detection, the learned representations generalize to unseen tasks, achieving competitive performance on authorship verification and fine-grained style discrimination despite never being trained on either objective.

</details>


### [250] [Nonlinear Estimator: Dual Bayesian Affine Estimators for Parameter Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10111)
*Sasan Vakili,Daniël Woonings,Pradyumna Paruchuri,Peyman Mohajerin Esfahani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes nonlinear parameter estimators for Wiener-type state-space models using a fixed-point architecture that couples two affine MMSE estimators. Two frameworks are developed: a dual basis-parameter estimator and a dual state-parameter estimator, with the latter achieving the best performance in Monte Carlo experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a nonlinear parameter estimator for Wiener-type state-space models that improves upon purely affine MMSE estimators by incorporating Dynamic Basis Statistics (DBS) estimates.

Method: Two dual estimator frameworks are constructed using a fixed-point architecture coupling affine MMSE estimators for parameters and latent variables. The dual basis-parameter estimator combines an affine basis estimator with the affine parameter estimator, while the dual state-parameter estimator computes affine state estimates and maps them through a Gaussian DBS operator. Both alternate between components using updated priors.

Result: The dual basis-parameter estimator achieves parameter MSE comparable to purely affine estimators. The dual state-parameter estimator achieves the lowest parameter MSE, outperforming both the dual basis-parameter estimator, purely affine estimators, and sequential Monte Carlo variants of Particle Gibbs and Expectation-Maximization.

Conclusion: The proposed dual state-parameter estimator is the most effective nonlinear estimator for Wiener-type state-space models among compared methods.

Abstract: This paper presents a nonlinear parameter estimator for Wiener-type state-space models obtained as a fixed-point architecture that couples two affine minimum mean-squared error (MMSE) estimators: one for the unknown parameters and one for latent variables. The architecture retains the functional structure of the optimal affine MMSE parameter estimator while incorporating Dynamic Basis Statistics (DBS) estimates that summarize nonlinear basis-function evaluations. Two DBS construction strategies are developed, leading to two nonlinear estimator frameworks. The dual basis-parameter estimator combines an affine basis estimator with the affine parameter estimator, whereas the dual state-parameter estimator first computes affine state estimates and their covariances, then maps these state-estimate statistics through a Gaussian DBS operator to obtain DBS estimates. Both dual estimators admit fixed-point characterizations that alternate between estimating each component using the updated prior of the other, obtained from that component's plug-in estimate statistics from the previous iteration. The efficacy of the proposed methods is examined via extensive Monte Carlo experiments, showing that the dual basis-parameter estimator attains parameter mean-squared errors comparable to those of the purely affine parameter estimator, while the dual state-parameter estimator achieves the lowest parameter mean-squared error, outperforming both the dual basis-parameter and purely affine parameter estimators, as well as sequential Monte Carlo variants of classical Particle Gibbs and Expectation-Maximization schemes.

</details>


### [251] [FedSteer: Taming Extreme Gradient Staleness in Federated Learning with Corrective Projections and Caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10124)
*Haoran Zhang,Cainã Figueiredo Pereira,Marie Siew,Xutong Liu,Carlee Joe-Wong,Rachid El-Azouzi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedSteer reduces aggregation variance in federated learning by constructing a gradient subspace from cached client gradients, projecting active gradients and steering stale gradients toward the current objective, outperforming baselines by over 7% in accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning suffers from aggregation variance due to inconsistent client participation. Reusing stale model updates can cause severe update staleness under skewed participation, destabilizing training.

Method: FedSteer builds a gradient subspace from a cache of recent client gradients as a low-dimensional representation of the optimization landscape. Active client gradients are projected onto this subspace; for inactive clients, stale gradients are "steered" using evolved subspace coordinates. A selective caching strategy identifies representative clients to reduce memory.

Result: FedSteer significantly outperforms baselines, prevents performance collapse in challenging scenarios, and delivers accuracy gains of over 7%.

Conclusion: FedSteer effectively mitigates aggregation variance from inconsistent participation by steering stale gradients toward the current objective, achieving robust performance gains.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is often subject to aggregation variance if clients do not consistently participate in training rounds. While reusing stale model updates from inactive clients is a common technique to reduce this variance, we find that with skewed client participation, the resulting update staleness can become severe enough to destabilize training. To remedy this, we propose FedSteer, a novel method that constructs a gradient subspace from a cache of recent client gradients to serve as a low-dimensional representation of the current optimization landscape. FedSteer projects an active client's true gradient onto this subspace to find a set of optimal coordinates. For an inactive client, FedSteer reuses these coordinates with the now-evolved subspace drifted by other active clients. This process effectively "steers" outdated gradients toward the current global objective. This is complemented by a selective caching strategy that identifies a representative client subset to form the subspace, reducing server memory. Experiments demonstrate that FedSteer significantly outperforms baselines, preventing performance collapse in challenging scenarios while delivering accuracy gains of over 7% in others.

</details>


### [252] [Discovering Interpretable Multi-Parameter Control Policies for Evolutionary Algorithms Using Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10129)
*Tai Nguyen,Phong Le,Carola Doerr,Nguyen Dang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper uses deep reinforcement learning to derive interpretable multi-parameter control policies for the (1+(λ,λ))-genetic algorithm on OneMax, overcoming theoretical barriers through action-space decomposition, reward shifting, and long-horizon discounting, ultimately distilling a symbolic policy that outperforms baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rigorous theoretical analysis of parameter control in evolutionary algorithms is largely restricted to single-parameter settings due to the difficulty of deriving effective, interpretable multi-parameter policies amenable to formal study. The authors aim to overcome this barrier using deep-RL.

Method: The authors apply deep-RL to the (1+(λ,λ))-GA optimizing OneMax. They enhance standard RL approaches with algorithm-agnostic techniques: action-space decomposition, reward shifting, and long-horizon discounting. They compare common deep-RL methods (DDQN vs PPO) and distill learned neural policies into symbolic control policies.

Result: Double Deep Q-Networks (DDQN) avoid the policy collapse observed in Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). The distilled symbolic policy is interpretable, suitable for theoretical analysis, and consistently outperforms existing baselines across a wide range of problem sizes.

Conclusion: Deep-RL can effectively generate interpretable multi-parameter control policies for evolutionary algorithms, bridging the gap between data-driven policy learning and formal theoretical analysis.

Abstract: While deep Reinforcement Learning (deep-RL) has been increasingly applied to parameter control in evolutionary algorithms, rigorous theoretical analysis of parameter control remains largely restricted to single-parameter settings, owing to the difficulty of deriving effective, interpretable multi-parameter policies amenable to formal study. We demonstrate how deep-RL can be leveraged to overcome this barrier, using the (1+($λ$,$λ$))-genetic algorithm optimizing OneMax, one of the few problems where a super-constant speedup of dynamic control has been formally proven, as a representative case study. We first show that standard approaches struggle to converge in this multi-parameter setting, and introduce algorithm-agnostic enhancements targeting action-space decomposition, reward shifting, and long-horizon discounting. With these in place, we compare common deep-RL methods and find that Double Deep Q-Networks uniquely avoid the policy collapse observed in Proximal Policy Optimization, yielding trajectories suitable for downstream analysis. Crucially, we move beyond the ``black-box'' nature of neural networks by distilling the learned behaviors into a transparent, symbolic control policy. This resulting policy does not only offer interpretability for future theoretical analysis but also yields exceptional performance, consistently outperforming existing baselines across a wide range of problem sizes.

</details>


### [253] [Ambiguous Strategic Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10137)
*Ivri Hikri,Nir Rosenfeld*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies strategic classification where the system only partially discloses its classifier (due to regulation), using ambiguity (a set/range of possible classifiers) to jointly optimize the classifier and the uncertainty around it, proposing efficient algorithms and empirical analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether and why a system would commit to full disclosure of its classifier in strategic classification, especially when regulation only requires partial disclosure - challenging the common assumption that the classifier is public knowledge.

Method: Adopts the concept of ambiguity from robust mechanism design, where the learner reveals a set or range of possible classifiers but privately chooses which one to ultimately realize. Develops efficient algorithms for computing best-responses and training in this partially-disclosed setting.

Result: The paper provides insights into how ambiguity affects the learning task and empirically explores strategic learning outcomes under this novel partial disclosure setting.

Conclusion: Partial disclosure (via ambiguity) can be beneficial or at least viable in strategic classification, challenging the necessity of full disclosure; efficient algorithms exist for this setting.

Abstract: A common assumption in strategic classification is that the classifier is public knowledge. However, it remains unclear whether, and why, a system would choose to commit to full disclosure. We study a setting in which regulation requires the system to disclose some, but not all, of the information. This induces a learning task in which the learner must jointly optimize the classifier and the uncertainty surrounding it. To this end, we adopt from robust mechanism design the notion of ambiguity, which in our setting allows the learner to reveal a set or range of possible classifiers, while privately choosing which of them to ultimately realize. We investigate how ambiguity affects the learning task, develop efficient algorithms for computing best-responses and training, and empirically explore strategic learning and its outcomes in this novel setting and using our approach.

</details>


### [254] [Compositional Generative Modeling from Decentralized Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10153)
*Mashrur M. Morshed,Vishnu Naresh Boddeti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DCFM enables compositional generation across decentralized data silos without sharing raw data, outperforming federated learning baselines on image, robotics, and medical tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practical physical world data is decentralized, with generative factors fragmented across isolated silos. Existing decentralized approaches only model the union of siloed data and overlook novel combinations implied by the collective whole.

Method: Decentralized Compositional Flow Matching (DCFM) enforces structural constraints across the global set of generative factors via peer interactions, without exchanging raw data, enabling novel compositions to emerge even when no single source supports them independently.

Result: DCFM substantially outperforms federated learning and mixture-of-experts baselines on conditional image generation, robotic spatial planning, and medical attribute co-occurrence modeling tasks.

Conclusion: DCFM successfully bridges the gap between decentralized data and compositional reasoning, enabling novel factor combinations to emerge from isolated silos through structured peer interactions without raw data exchange.

Abstract: Learning the compositional nature of the physical world requires joint observation of interacting factors. However, because practical data is often decentralized, these factors are fragmented across isolated silos. Existing decentralized generative approaches focus only on modeling the union of siloed data, overlooking novel combinations implied by the collective whole. To bridge this gap, we introduce Decentralized Compositional Flow Matching (DCFM), a framework that enforces structural constraints across the global set of generative factors, without exchanging any raw data. DCFM enables novel combinations to emerge through peer interactions, even when no single data source can independently support the composition. Empirically, DCFM substantially outperforms federated learning and mixture-of-experts baselines across conditional image generation, robotic spatial planning, and medical attribute co-occurrence modeling.

</details>


### [255] [Quality Is Not a Safety Proxy Under Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10154)
*Sahil Kadadekar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Quality metrics cannot replace direct safety testing for quantized LLM checkpoints: all 36 quality-safety pairings split across models, and 9/51 rows show "hidden danger" where quality improves while refusal drops 12-68 percentage points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To audit whether quality metrics (commonly used as a screening shortcut) can adequately detect safety degradation in quantized models, since practitioners often screen with quality first and test safety later or not at all.

Method: Constructed a 51-row matrix across 6 models, 4 families, with GGUF (7 levels) and AWQ/GPTQ INT4 quantizations. Measured quality-safety correlation, applied mechanistic probes (entropy, refusal-direction, calibration), and developed the Refusal Template Stability Index (RTSI) from four refusal-template drift features.

Result: All 36 quality-safety pairings disconfirmed; 9 hidden-danger rows plus 1 near-hidden-danger found. Mechanistic probes failed to reliably separate dangerous rows. RTSI successfully routed 10/10 hidden/near-hidden-danger rows to direct safety testing (Wilson 95% CI lower bound 0.72) while clearing 23/45 non-baseline rows as low-risk.

Conclusion: Retained quality cannot waive direct safety evaluation for quantized checkpoints. Quality-safety shortcuts fail across the studied models, families, and safety outcomes.

Abstract: Quantized checkpoints are often screened first with quality metrics and only later, if at all, with direct safety tests. This paper audits that shortcut on a matched 51-row matrix spanning 6 models, 4 families, a 7-level GGUF ladder, and AWQ/GPTQ INT4 checkpoints. In this matrix the shortcut fails: all 36 quality-safety pairings split direction across models, and 9 hidden-danger rows plus 1 near-hidden-danger row show quality stable or improved while refusal falls by 12-68 percentage points. Seven of the 11 AWQ/GPTQ rows are hidden-danger. A four-probe mechanistic follow-up over the 17 Hugging Face-backed FP16/AWQ/GPTQ cells does not rescue it: entropy, refusal-direction, and calibration probes are weak or null separators of dangerous rows, and although probe-identified safety-associated neurons absorb 1.39$\times$ more quantization error overall ($p < 5 \times 10^{-7}$), the effect is not regime-specific. Claude Sonnet 4 relabels 11,470 items in a predefined stratified set, agrees with the primary gemma3:12b judge on 89.9\% of rows ($κ= 0.873$, 95\% CI [0.866, 0.881]), and changes 0/10 hidden-danger cells. A calibrated study-internal behavioral screen -- the Refusal Template Stability Index (RTSI), built from four refusal-template drift features and calibrated on this matrix -- routes 10/10 hidden- or near-hidden-danger rows to direct safety testing (Wilson 95\% CI lower bound 0.72) while leaving 23 of 45 non-baseline rows in a low-risk bucket under both in-sample scoring and row-level leave-one-out validation; on the same matrix, the best single-feature baselines (unique-prefix-rate-delta, raw refusal-rate delta) recover 9/10 and 8/10 respectively at matched bucket size, and cross-stack transfer requires recalibration. For the quantized checkpoints, model families, and safety outcomes studied here, retained quality cannot waive direct safety evaluation.

</details>


### [256] [Learning Entropy and Spatial Adaptation Dynamics of Multilayer Perceptrons for Structural Point Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10170)
*Jan Glaser,Ivo Bukovsky,Marcel Jirina*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends Learning Entropy to spatial learning in MLPs for image data, creating Spatial Learning Entropy Maps (SLEM) that identify unusual image regions based on their impact on neural network learning rather than local structural properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods evaluate image structure using gradients or covariance operators (local neighborhood methods). The authors aim to provide an alternative perspective by analyzing the learning process itself through Learning Entropy, identifying image points based on their learning impact rather than local structural properties.

Method: An MLP is trained to predict the intensity of a center pixel from its surrounding spatial context. Learning Entropy is evaluated from the incremental adaptation of neural weights during learning across image-derived samples, producing Spatial Learning Entropy Maps (SLEM).

Result: SLEM identifies unusual image points and regions that induce strong neural network adaptation. Spatial Learning Entropy provides a complementary perspective to conventional feature extraction and explainability methods.

Conclusion: The framework may open new directions for learning-driven image or scene analysis in computer vision, manufacturing, and robotics by highlighting spatial locations that are particularly informative for network learning.

Abstract: This paper extends the concept of Learning Entropy (LE) from temporal adaptive systems to spatial learning in multilayer perceptron networks (MLPs) applied to image data. Instead of evaluating image structure directly from gradients or covariance operators, as local neighborhood methods do, the proposed approach analyzes the learning process itself through Learning Entropy. An MLP is trained to predict the intensity of a center pixel from its surrounding spatial context, while LE is evaluated from the incremental adaptation of neural weights during learning across image-derived samples. The resulting Spatial Learning Entropy Maps (SLEM) identify unusual image points and regions that induce strong adaptation of the neural network and therefore have an important role in the learning process. The results indicate that spatial Learning Entropy provides a complementary perspective to conventional feature extraction and explainability methods by highlighting spatial locations that are particularly informative for network learning. Spatial Learning Entropy provides a complementary perspective to conventional feature extraction and explainability methods by identifying image points and regions according to their learning impact rather than their local structural properties. The proposed framework may open new directions for learning-driven image or scene analysis in computer vision, manufacturing, and robotics.

</details>


### [257] [Dropout-GRPO: Variational Stochasticity for Continuous Latent Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10184)
*Wooil Jung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRPO fails on latent-reasoning models (e.g., Coconut) due to deterministic rollouts; this paper fixes it with rollout-level dropout masks that inject stochasticity, enabling GRPO training with theoretical guarantees and ~2% GSM8K gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO's learning signal collapses for latent-reasoning models (e.g., Coconut) because their continuous hidden states produce identical trajectories across rollouts, making the group-mean advantage zero and stalling optimization.

Method: Apply a single Bernoulli dropout mask per rollout, held constant across all latent recurrence steps, to inject structured stochasticity. This treats each rollout as a posterior sample from a variational distribution over parameters, enabling Bayesian model-average policy optimization via GRPO.

Result: On GSM8K, dropout-GRPO improves Coconut from 27.29% to 29.01% pass@1, demonstrating successful GRPO training for latent-reasoning models.

Conclusion: Dropout-GRPO is a practical, theoretically grounded approach for post-training latent-reasoning LLMs, with unbiasedness and variance reduction guarantees.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) relies on the diversity of $K$ rollouts within each group; otherwise, the group-mean advantage $A^{(k)} = r^{(k)} - μ_r$ collapses to zero. This presents a structural challenge for latent-reasoning models like Coconut, which feed continuous hidden states recurrently in place of discrete chain-of-thought tokens. Because the latent phase is inherently deterministic given the parameters and prompt, multiple rollouts produce identical trajectories, stalling GRPO's progress. Consequently, applying group-relative reinforcement learning to continuous latent reasoning has proven difficult.
  To address this, we propose sourcing the necessary stochasticity through structured dropout. By applying a single Bernoulli mask held constant across all latent recurrence steps for a given rollout, we generate essential trajectory variance. This shared mask effectively treats each rollout as a posterior sample from a variational distribution over parameters, allowing GRPO to optimize the expected reward of a Bayesian model-average policy. We provide both theoretical justification for this method -- including unbiasedness, variance reduction, and the well-definedness of the latent gradient -- and empirical validation. On GSM8K, dropout-GRPO improves a Coconut baseline from $27.29\%$ to $29.01\%$ pass@1, demonstrating the viability of GRPO learning for latent-reasoning models. Our work positions this as a practical, theoretically grounded approach for post-training latent-reasoning LLMs.

</details>


### [258] [MMClima: A Framework for Multimodal Climate Science Data and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10194)
*Muhammad Umer Sheikh,Hassan Abid,Khawar Shehzad,Ufaq Khan,Muhammad Haris Khan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MMClima is a large-scale multimodal climate QA dataset (104k+ expert-validated pairs) spanning text, video, and figures across five climate domains, with benchmark results showing domain-adapted models outperforming strong baselines on textual QA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing climate QA benchmarks are small, mostly textual, and cover a narrow range of models, failing to support AI systems that need to reason across text, dynamic visual content, and scientific figures for climate change research.

Method: The authors construct MMClima via automated claim extraction and QA synthesis with human-in-the-loop validation, covering articles, video transcriptions, and figures across five core climate science domains. They benchmark multimodal language models and fine-tune on the textual split to create mmclima-70b-txt.

Result: MMClima contains 104k+ expert-validated QA pairs. The fine-tuned mmclima-70b-txt outperforms strong open- and closed-source models on textual QA tasks.

Conclusion: The dataset, evaluation pipeline, fine-tuned model weights, and data creation framework are publicly released to support standardized multimodal evaluation for climate science.

Abstract: Climate change research increasingly requires AI systems that reason across text, dynamic visual content, and scientific figures, yet existing climate QA benchmarks are small, mostly textual, and cover a narrow range of models. We introduce MMClima, a large-scale multimodal climate question answering framework with 104k+ expert-validated question-answer pairs spanning articles, video transcriptions, and figures across five core climate science domains. MMClima is constructed via automated claim extraction and QA synthesis with human-in-the-loop validation to ensure both scale and reliability. Using MMClima, we benchmark state-of-the-art multimodal language models on tasks requiring factual recall, visual interpretation, and cross-modal synthesis. We additionally fine-tune on the textual split to produce mmclima-70b-txt, a domain-adapted baseline that outperforms strong open- and closed-source models on textual QA. We release the dataset, evaluation pipeline, fine-tuned model weights, and data creation framework to support standardized multimodal evaluation for climate science.

</details>


### [259] [Density Ridge Selective Prediction for LLM and VLM Hallucination Detection under Calibration Label Scarcity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10198)
*Nina I. Shamsi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel unsupervised hallucination detection method using density ridges on a 6D kinematic feature map of LLM hidden states, achieving 5-20 AUROC points gain over existing methods under label-scarce conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hallucination detectors face a trade-off: unsupervised sampling detectors (Semantic Entropy, EigenScore) plateau in quality, while supervised probes (SAPLMA) degrade sharply when calibration labels are scarce. The authors aim to design a detector that is both unsupervised and robust under label scarcity.

Method: The method recovers the response manifold of an LLM as the density ridge of a kernel density estimate built on a 6D kinematic feature map of hidden state generation trajectories. Test generations are scored by negated Euclidean distance from their projected feature point to the nearest ridge vertex, forming a low-dimensional geometric skeleton of the stochastic output distribution.

Result: Evaluated on 7 QA benchmarks (HaluEval-QA, TriviaQA, GSM8K, POPE, ScienceQA, A-OKVQA) using 9 text and vision LLMs with a label-scarce protocol (200 calibration queries, 5 generations). The ridge-based score achieves 5-20 AUROC points gain over baselines (Semantic Entropy, SAR, EigenScore, SAPLMA, log-probability) while showing tempered degradation under calibration-label scarcity.

Conclusion: The proposed ridge-based method outperforms existing unsupervised and supervised detectors, especially under label-scarce conditions, offering a promising geometric approach to hallucination detection.

Abstract: Hallucination detection in large language and vision-language models is increasingly framed as selective prediction, where a detector assigns a confidence score and abstains when confidence is low. Unsupervised sampling detectors (Semantic Entropy, EigenScore) avoid labels but plateau in quality, while supervised probes (SAPLMA) attain stronger in-distribution scores yet degrade sharply when calibration labels are scarce. We recover the response manifold of an LLM as the density ridge of a kernel density estimate built on a six-dimensional kinematic feature map of hidden state generation trajectories. A test generation is scored by the negated Euclidean distance from its projected feature point to the nearest ridge vertex, yielding a low-dimensional geometric skeleton of the stochastic output distribution. We evaluate against Semantic Entropy, SAR, EigenScore, SAPLMA, and log-probability on seven QA benchmarks (HaluEval-QA, TriviaQA, GSM8K, POPE, ScienceQA, A-OKVQA) using nine text and vision LLMs in a deliberately label-scarce protocol ($n_{\text{cal}}{=}200$ queries, $N{=}5$ generations). Our ridge-based score beats on AUROC with 5-20 points gain, while demonstrating tempered degradation under calibration-label scarcity.

</details>


### [260] [A Continuous-Time Markov Chain Framework for Insertion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10199)
*Dhruvesh Patel,Benjamin Rozonoyer,Soumitra Das,Tahira Naseem,Tim G. J. Rudner,Andrew McCallum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper derives a diffusion-style denoising objective for Insertion Language Models (ILMs) from first principles using continuous-time Markov chains on variable-length sequences, showing previous ILM formulations are special cases of this framework, and demonstrates competitive performance with added sampling flexibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing formulations of insertion-based generation have been largely ad-hoc, lacking a principled theoretical foundation. The authors aim to derive ILMs from first principles to unify and improve upon previous approaches.

Method: The noising process is formulated as a continuous-time Markov chain on the space of variable-length sequences, from which a diffusion-style denoising objective for ILMs is derived. The framework generalizes previous ILM formulations as special cases.

Result: On a synthetic planning task, the approach retains benefits of insertion-based generation over left-to-right and masked diffusion models. In language modeling, it is competitive with both left-to-right generation and masked diffusion models while offering additional sampling flexibility.

Conclusion: The proposed diffusion-based ILM framework provides a principled theoretical grounding for insertion-based generation, unifies prior ad-hoc methods, and achieves competitive performance with greater sampling flexibility.

Abstract: Insertion Language Models (ILMs) offer several advantages over left-to-right generation and mask-based generation. However, existing formulations of insertion-based generation have largely been ad-hoc. In this paper, we derive a diffusion-style denoising objective for ILMs from first principles by formulating the noising process as a continuous-time Markov chain on the space of variable-length sequences. We show that previous formulations of ILMs can be viewed as special cases of this denoising framework. Through empirical evaluation on a synthetic planning task, we show that the proposed approach retains the benefits of insertion-based generation over left-to-right generation and masked diffusion models. In language modeling, our diffusion-based approach is competitive with left-to-right generation and masked diffusion models, while offering additional flexibility in sampling compared to existing insertion language models.

</details>


### [261] [A Source Domain is All You Need: Source-Only Cross-OS Transfer Learning for APT Anomaly Detection via Semantic Alignment and Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10216)
*Sidahmed Benabderrahmanea,Petko Valtchev,James Cheney,Talal Rahwan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a transport-based framework for cross-OS APT detection using provenance traces, combining semantic, structural, and geometric anomaly scoring without target-domain labels, achieving strong results on DARPA data across Linux, Windows, BSD, and Android.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: APTs are hard to detect due to scarce labels, class imbalance, and cross-OS transfer challenges; existing methods require target-domain supervision which is often unavailable.

Method: Process behaviors are abstracted into NL descriptions, embedded via pretrained LMs, and scored using three channels: semantic deviation from prototypes, structural deviation via graph autoencoding, and geometric deviation via OT-based barycentric anomaly scoring with entropy-weighted, angle-aware, and density-aware variants.

Result: Evaluated on DARPA Transparent Computing data (Linux, Windows, BSD, Android) across two APT scenarios and twelve cross-OS transfer pairs, the framework outperforms source-only baselines in ROC-AUC and nDCG.

Conclusion: Source-only provenance modeling with semantic abstraction and OT-based anomaly scoring enables practical cross-platform APT detection without needing target-domain labels.

Abstract: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) are stealthy, multi-stage cyberattacks whose detection is difficult due to scarce labeled traces, severe class imbalance, and the challenge of generating realistic malicious behavior. These challenges are amplified in cross-operating-system (cross-OS) settings, where a detector trained on one source platform must be deployed on an unlabeled target platform without access to target-domain labels. We study this source-only cross-OS APT detection problem using system-level provenance traces and propose a transport-based framework for ranking anomalous target processes under zero target supervision. The framework abstracts process behavior into structured natural-language descriptions, embeds them using pretrained language models, and constructs a source-normal reference for target scoring. It combines three evidence channels: semantic deviation from source-normal prototypes, structural deviation captured by graph autoencoding, and geometric deviation measured through Optimal Transport (OT). The main contribution is an OT-based barycentric anomaly score that projects target embeddings onto the source-normal manifold and quantifies residual transport mismatch. We further introduce entropy-weighted, angle-aware, and density-aware OT variants to capture uncertainty, directional drift, and sparse-support behavior. Evaluation on DARPA Transparent Computing data spanning Linux, Windows, BSD, and Android, across two APT scenarios and twelve cross-OS transfer pairs, shows that the proposed framework improves ROC-AUC and nDCG over source-only anomaly-detection baselines. The results demonstrate that source-only provenance modeling, combined with semantic abstraction and OT-based anomaly scoring, can support practical cross-platform APT detection without target-domain supervision.

</details>


### [262] [Alignment Defends LLMs from Property Inference Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10217)
*Pengrun Huang,Chhavi Yadav,Ruihan Wu,Kamalika Chaudhuri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes alignment-based defenses (using DPO and GRPO) to mitigate property inference attacks on LLMs by reshaping the model's output distribution towards a target property ratio via post-training alignment, without modifying training data or requiring retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing defenses against property inference attacks require modifying training data distribution and retraining the model, which limits applicability when data is unavailable or models are already deployed.

Method: The authors adapt RLHF frameworks (DPO and GRPO) as defenses by constructing preference pairs and defining a reward function to reshape the model's output distribution toward a target property ratio via post-training alignment, without altering training data.

Result: Comprehensive experiments show that the alignment-based defenses effectively mitigate property inference attacks while maintaining a strong utility-confidentiality tradeoff.

Conclusion: Post-training alignment (DPO/GRPO) is a viable defense against property inference attacks on LLMs, offering a practical alternative that does not require access to original data or model retraining.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly fine-tuned on domain-specific datasets that may contain sensitive, dataset-level properties. Recent work has shown that such dataset-level information can be effectively extracted through property inference attacks, posing a confidentiality risk. Existing defenses against these attacks primarily operate by modifying the training data distribution and hence require access to the original data and retraining the model, limiting their applicability to settings where data is unavailable or models are already deployed. In this work, we propose alignment-based defenses for mitigating property inference attacks in LLMs. Our approach reshapes the model's output distribution towards a target property ratio via post-training alignment, without modifying the training data. In particular, we adapt two widely used RLHF frameworks--Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)--as our defenses by constructing preference pairs and defining a specific reward function respectively. Through comprehensive experiments, we show that our alignment based defenses effectively mitigate property inference attacks while maintaining a strong utility confidentiality tradeoff.

</details>


### [263] [Fast Exact Nearest-Neighbor Learning for High-Frequency Financial Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10219)
*Henry Han,Diane Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a Mojo-based SIMD k-d tree for exact nearest-neighbor learning in high-frequency financial time series, achieving 17.5–43.5× speedup over scikit-learn baselines while preserving exact outputs, and demonstrates scalability in options pricing models with 10× more training data and 8% RMSE reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Financial AI systems face a scalability challenge: models must learn from growing historical data volumes while meeting real-time latency constraints in trading, risk management, and derivative pricing. Existing Python-based ML libraries cannot efficiently handle this tension.

Method: The authors introduce a Mojo SIMD k-d tree with variance-based splitting, contiguous flat-buffer storage, and compile-time vectorized distance computation. They also apply Mojo's compiled execution to an Extra Trees-based implied-volatility pricing model.

Result: On eight financial datasets (x86 and ARM64, up to 277K samples), the method achieves 17.5–21.6× speedup over scikit-learn's k-d tree on x86 and 28.1–43.5× over scikit-learn brute force on ARM64 equity/ETF datasets. The options pricing model trains on 10× more data with 8% put-IV RMSE reduction.

Conclusion: Mojo is positioned as a scalable, production-ready stack for financial AI and a promising foundation for efficient AI in other data-intensive fields.

Abstract: AI efficiency at scale is becoming critical in finance as market data volumes surge across equities, ETFs, FX, options, and high-frequency trading streams. This growth creates a core challenge for mature financial AI systems: models must learn from larger historical corpora while still meeting real-time latency constraints in trading, risk management, and derivative pricing. We use exact nearest-neighbor learning for high-frequency financial time series as a concrete case study to show that Mojo-based financial AI can address this challenge. We introduce a Mojo SIMD k-d tree with variance-based splitting, contiguous flat-buffer storage, and compile-time vectorized distance computation. We also provide a runtime result showing that, under standard pruning and implementation-cost assumptions, the Mojo SIMD k-d tree asymptotically dominates Mojo SIMD brute force and scikit-learn's k-d tree in the fixed-stock, large-$n$, moderate-dimensional regime. Empirically, across eight financial datasets on x86 and ARM64 with up to 277K training samples, the method achieves 17.5--21.6$\times$ speedup over scikit-learn's k-d tree on x86 and 28.1--43.5$\times$ over scikit-learn brute force on ARM64 equity/ETF datasets, while preserving exact outputs. Beyond nearest-neighbor inference, Mojo's compiled execution enables an Extra Trees-based implied-volatility pricing model to train on $10\times$ more options data, reducing put-IV RMSE by 8.0\%. These results position Mojo as a scalable, production-ready stack for financial AI and a promising foundation for efficient AI in other data-intensive fields. \keywords{Financial AI \and AI Efficiency \and Mojo \and SIMD \and K-D Trees \and KNN \and High-Frequency Trading \and Financial Time Series \and Scaling}

</details>


### [264] [Spatiotemporal Graph Transformer for 3D Neighborhood Interaction and Quality Prediction in Metal Additive Manufacturing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10227)
*Joyce Karen Pelaez,Siqi Zhang,Hoo Sang Ko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a spatiotemporal graph transformer that models 3D neighborhood interactions in metal additive manufacturing, using a weighted network representation to integrate multimodal data and a dual-attention mechanism for quality prediction, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Achieving consistent build quality in metal additive manufacturing is challenging due to complex multi-layer interactions (melting, solidification, reheating). Existing methods lack the ability to represent and quantify these multi-layer interactions and their contributions to quality.

Method: (1) A weighted network representation where fusing locations are nodes, and spatial/process-dependent relationships are edges. (2) Integration of multimodal data (geometric design, process settings, in-situ sensing) into a unified structure. (3) A dual-attention graph transformer capturing within-node feature dependencies and cross-node neighborhood interactions for quality representation learning.

Result: The proposed framework significantly outperforms image-based, sequence-based, and graph-based models in characterizing process-quality relationships. Cross-layer interactions are critical for improving quality prediction performance.

Conclusion: The spatiotemporal graph transformer effectively models 3D neighborhood interactions for quality monitoring in metal additive manufacturing, and is broadly applicable to other network modeling and graph-based representation learning tasks.

Abstract: Metal additive manufacturing enables the fabrication of complex parts, but achieving consistent build quality remains challenging due to interactions induced by repeated layer-wise melting, solidification, and reheating across the 3D build. Advanced sensing provide a great opportunity to collect rich observations of the actual manufacturing process for real-time quality monitoring and control. Yet, existing methods often have limited ability to represent multi-layer interactions and quantify their contributions to quality. In this paper, we develop a novel spatiotemporal graph transformer for modeling 3D neighborhood interactions and learn their effects on build quality in metal additive manufacturing. Specifically, we first introduce a weighted network representation of the manufacturing process, where fusing locations are modeled as nodes, and their spatial- and process-dependent relationships are encoded as edge weights. This representation also enables the integration of multimodal data (e.g., geometric design, process settings, and in-situ sensing data) into a unified structure for downstream learning tasks. Building on this network, we further design a dual-attention graph transformer that captures both within-node feature dependencies and cross-node neighborhood interactions for quality representation learning. Experimental results show that the proposed framework significantly outperforms image-based, sequence-based, and graph-based models in characterizing process-quality relationships. More importantly, the incorporation of cross-layer interactions is critical for improving quality prediction performance. This framework is broadly applicable to other tasks involving network modeling and graph-based representation learning.

</details>


### [265] [DUET -- Dual User Embedding Transformers for Offsite Conversion Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10243)
*Reazul Hasan Russel,Mingwei Tang,Rostam Shirani,Xinlong Liu,Navid Madani,Leo Ding,Yawen He,Xiangyu Wang,Mustafa Acar,Ashish Katiyar,Yuhai Li,Alan Yang,Metarya Ruparel,Derek Qiang Xu,Rupert Wu,Rui Yang,Liang Tao,Xinyi Zhao,Larry Zhang,Sri Reddy,Rob Malkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DUET proposes dual transformer encoders (self-attention for clicks, interleaved cross/self-attention for conversions) to separately model click and conversion streams for offsite conversion rate prediction, achieving up to 0.38% NE reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Click and conversion signals have fundamentally different statistical properties (dense vs. sparse, short vs. long delay), but prior methods use a single undifferentiated encoder for both, failing to address this heterogeneity.

Method: DUET partitions user behavior into click and conversion streams, pre-trains a multi-layer self-attention transformer for dense clicks and an interleaved cross- and self-attention transformer for sparse conversions, then jointly feeds the complementary embeddings to a downstream ranker.

Result: Up to 0.38% normalized entropy (NE) reduction over strongest baseline; A/B test shows consistent OCVR prediction accuracy improvements.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling click and conversion streams with dedicated architectures tailored to each signal's statistical characteristics improves OCVR prediction under serving-latency constraints.

Abstract: Offsite conversion rate (OCVR) prediction is an important ranking problem in computational recommendation systems. This task presents a modeling challenge: click signals are abundant and exhibit short temporal horizons, whereas conversion signals are inherently sparse, long-delayed, and frequently unattributed. Despite these statistical disparities, both signal types must inform models that operate within strict serving-latency constraints. Prior pre-training approaches address this heterogeneity with a single, undifferentiated encoder applied uniformly across both data streams. We propose DUET (Dual User Embedding Transformers), a framework that explicitly partitions user behavioral data into two domain-coherent streams -- clicks and conversions -- and pre-trains dedicated transformer encoders with architectures tailored to each stream's statistical characteristics: multi-layer self-attention for the dense click stream and interleaved cross- and self-attention for the sparse conversion stream. The resulting complementary embeddings are jointly consumed by a downstream ranker without exceeding serving-latency budgets. Evaluation demonstrates up to 0.38% normalized entropy (NE) reduction relative to the strongest baseline, and A/B test shows consistent improvements in OCVR prediction accuracy.

</details>


### [266] [When Design Rules Break: Benchmark Composition Determines Whether Label Informativeness Predicts GNN Aggregator Choice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10249)
*Neha Sharma,Ritesh Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GNN aggregator design rules (sum, mean, max) do not generalize across benchmark families. While label informativeness predicts performance on legacy benchmarks, it fails on dense Facebook-100 graphs where near-zero label informativeness coexists with strong sum aggregation preference. The spectral gap uniquely identifies these graphs, and PNA can underperform single-aggregator GIN on standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To examine whether GNN design rules (specifically aggregator selection) generalize across different benchmark families, and to understand why certain graph properties fail to predict performance when benchmark composition changes.

Method: Studied aggregator selection (sum, mean, max) on 24 node-classification datasets spanning citation, heterophilic, LINKX Facebook-100, co-purchase, and co-authorship graphs. Used label informativeness, edge homophily, spectral gap, and stochastic block model ablations to analyze performance gaps.

Result: Edge homophily weakly predicts GIN-Sum vs GIN-Mean gap. Label informativeness works on legacy benchmarks but fails on Facebook-100 graphs where near-zero label informativeness coexists with strong sum preference (7-10% gains, up to 13% with extended training). Spectral gap uniquely distinguishes Facebook-100 graphs. PNA can underperform best single-aggregator GIN on citation benchmarks.

Conclusion: Benchmark composition determines whether design rules appear to generalize. The Facebook-100 regime provides a concrete target for future adaptive aggregation methods.

Abstract: We examine whether graph neural network (GNN) design rules generalize across benchmark families by studying aggregator selection (sum, mean, max) on 24 node-classification datasets spanning citation, heterophilic, LINKX Facebook-100, co-purchase, and co-authorship graphs. Edge homophily is only weakly predictive of the GIN-Sum versus GIN-Mean performance gap. Label informativeness predicts this gap well on legacy benchmarks but degrades substantially when Facebook-100 graphs are included. In these dense friendship networks, near-zero label informativeness coexists with a strong preference for sum aggregation, producing gains of 7-10% and up to 13% under extended training. Stochastic block model ablations, including degree-corrected variants matching Facebook-100 degree scales, fail to reproduce this behavior, indicating that mean degree alone does not explain the effect. Among several label-independent graph statistics, the spectral gap uniquely distinguishes these graphs from other low-informativeness datasets, with the effect localized to one-hop neighborhoods and replicated across architectures. We further identify training regimes that interact with aggregator choice and show that PNA can underperform the best single-aggregator GIN on standard citation benchmarks. Our results suggest that benchmark composition, rather than numerical insufficiency, determines whether design rules appear to generalize, and that the Facebook-100 regime provides a concrete target for future adaptive aggregation methods.

</details>


### [267] [Multi-Level Analyzation of Imbalance to Resolve Non-IID-Ness in Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10250)
*Haengbok Chung,Jae Sung Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedBB addresses three levels of imbalance in federated learning (inter-case, inter-class, inter-client) using a PNB loss function for local training and Client Balanced Reweighting for aggregation, outperforming other algorithms on X-ray and natural image datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Class imbalance severely degrades deep learning performance and is a key factor causing non-IID data in federated learning. Prior work has not systematically addressed imbalance at multiple levels simultaneously.

Method: FedBB has two components: (1) PNB loss that handles inter-case and inter-class imbalances by weighting minority cases/classes higher during local training; (2) CBR that reweights clients based on inter-client imbalance during aggregation, favoring models from less skewed datasets.

Result: Outperforms other algorithms on X-ray and natural image datasets in both performance and efficiency; ablation studies confirm both components independently contribute; requires limited statistical information for better privacy.

Conclusion: FedBB can serve as a baseline for both generic and personalized FL due to its ability to build a global model that accurately classifies all classes.

Abstract: Class imbalance is a common problem in deep learning that severely degrades performance. In federated learning (FL), it is a critical factor contributing to non-identically distributed data (non-IID). Building on several previous attempts, we define and analyze imbalance issues in FL at three levels: inter-case, inter-class, and inter-client. Inter-case imbalance addresses the imbalance in every single class; inter-class imbalance compares the number of data between different classes. Inter-client imbalance represents different skewness of local data between clients.
