Based on these concepts, we propose FedBB, which consists of two main components: (1) Positive Negative Balanced (PNB) loss function addresses the inter-case and inter-class imbalances in local training, enhancing generalization on highly skewed local client datasets. It optimizes both multi-label and multi-class classifications by assigning higher weights to minority cases or classes. (2) Client Balanced Reweighting (CBR) reweights clients based on inter-client imbalance during model aggregation, giving greater weight to models trained on less skewed datasets. Various experiments on X-ray and natural image datasets demonstrate that FedBB outperforms other algorithms in both performance and efficiency. Additionally, it requires limited statistical information, which is beneficial for privacy protection. Through ablation studies, we proved that PNB loss and CBR independently contribute to performance. As FedBB aims to build a global model that accurately classifies all classes, it can serve as a baseline for the generic and personalized FL.

</details>


### [268] [A Unified Adaptive Feature Composition Framework for Multi-Task Generalization in Wireless Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10277)
*Yuxuan Shi,Tingting Yang,Kangning Ma,Liwen Jing,Yuwei Wang,Mengfan Zheng,Li Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes RAFC, a lightweight routing adapter that adaptively composes multi-level hidden features from different Transformer layers of wireless foundation models, enabling efficient multitask generalization with fewer than 50K additional parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adaptation paradigms (fine-tuning and frozen feature extraction) for WFMs suffer from high computational/storage costs or sub-optimal performance across diverse downstream tasks.

Method: RAFC treats hidden states from different Transformer depths as a reusable pool, uses a lightweight task-driven feature composition network to generate layer-wise aggregation weights, and adaptively combines hierarchical representations via weighted summation.

Result: RAFC consistently outperforms conventional adaptation baselines on four wireless tasks with fewer than 50K additional parameters, while learned routing weights provide interpretable evidence of task-specific layer preferences.

Conclusion: RAFC is a low-complexity, scalable, and explainable interface for adapting WFMs to diverse downstream scenarios.

Abstract: Though wireless foundation models (WFMs) have shown strong potential in learning universal channel representations, their adaptation to various downstream tasks remains constrained by existing paradigms. Fine-tuning strategies introduces substantial computational and storage overhead, while frozen feature extraction leads to sub-optimal performance across diverse downstream tasks. To address this issue, we propose a unified adaptive feature composition framework for multitask generalization in WFMs, where the key component is the Routing Adapter for Feature Composition (RAFC). Instead of extracting only the final-layer output, this router treats the hidden states from different Transformer depths as a reusable pool of multi-level hidden features, and employs a lightweight task-driven feature composition network to generate layer-wise aggregation weights, then adaptively combine hierarchical representations through weighted summation. This design enables each downstream task to access suitable mixture of low-, mid-, and high-level wireless features without modifying the pretrained backbone. Extensive experiments on four representative wireless tasks demonstrate that RAFC consistently outperforms conventional adaptation baselines while introducing fewer than 50K additional parameters. Moreover, the learned routing weights provide interpretable evidence of task-specific layer preferences, making the proposed framework a low-complexity, scalable, and explainable interface for adapting WFMs to diverse downstream scenarios.

</details>


### [269] [Revisiting Positive Samples in Graph Contrastive Learning: From the Perspective of Message Passing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10284)
*Lianze Shan,Ningchong Wang,Jitao Zhao,Di Jin,Dongxiao He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper discovers that Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL) can work without positive samples, and reveals that message passing trivializes positive sample learning due to Dirichlet energy effects. The authors propose SPGCL, which separates high-energy features for learning and low-energy features for sampling, achieving strong performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors discover a surprising phenomenon: GCLs achieve competitive performance even without positive samples. This motivates them to revisit the fundamental mechanism of positive samples and understand why they fail to provide effective learning signals.

Method: SPGCL separates features by Dirichlet energy: high-energy features are propagated for effective positive learning signals, while low-energy features are used to construct a probability matrix for reliable positive sampling.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that SPGCL effectively restores positive sample learning efficacy and achieves competitive performance.

Conclusion: Positive samples in GCLs are theoretically hindered by message passing due to Dirichlet energy trivialization. The proposed SPGCL mitigates this by strategically separating high and low energy features for different roles.

Abstract: Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL), which trains graph encoders by maximizing similarity between positive samples and minimizing it between negative ones, has emerged as a mainstream graph pre-training paradigm. It is widely recognized that positive samples are essential in GCLs. Ideally, maximizing the similarity of positive samples enables graph encoders to capture intrinsic semantic and patterns of graph data. However, we discover an interesting phenomenon: GCLs can achieve competitive performance even without positive samples. This motivates us to revisit the fundamental mechanism of positive samples in GCLs. From the perspective of Dirichlet energy, we theoretically finds that message passing, a key mechanism in graph encoders, trivializes the maximization of positive samples, preventing GCLs from effectively learning from positive samples. To address this, we propose SPGCL to mitigate the trivialization caused by message passing and restore the learning efficacy of positive samples. Specifically, we find that high Dirichlet energy features help positive samples provide effective learning signals while low Dirichlet energy features contribute little to positive learning signal but is useful for positive sampling. Based on this, SPGCL propagates only high Dirichlet energy features and uses low energy features to construct a probability matrix for reliable positive sampling. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of SPGCL.

</details>


### [270] [When Metrics Disagree: A Meta-Analysis of Knowledge-Graph-Completion Model Benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10287)
*Haji Gul,Ajaz Ahmad Bhat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reframes KGC evaluation as an MCDM problem, testing seven aggregators across five criteria, finding Z-score as the most balanced aggregator and revealing removal-invariant vs. sensitive tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard KGC metrics (MRR, Hits@k, Mean Rank) often produce conflicting model orderings across datasets, hindering comparison, enabling selective reporting, and obscuring real progress.

Method: The authors treat KGC evaluation as a Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) problem, conducting a meta-analysis of seven aggregators across five tests (consistency, cross-dataset stability, metric independence, robustness, generalizability) using leave-one-model-out (LOMO) and leave-one-group-out (LOGO) removals.

Result: Pareto-optimal analysis identifies Z-score as the most balanced aggregator. DualE ranks highest for tail prediction and FMS highest for relation prediction. Consistency and stability are largely removal-invariant, while generalizability and independence are the most sensitive tests.

Conclusion: The proposed framework resolves evaluation inconsistencies in KGC and provides evidence-based guidance for aggregator selection and model benchmarking.

Abstract: Evaluating Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) models remains challenging because standard assessment relies on isolated rank-based metrics such as MRR, Hits$@$k, and Mean Rank, which often produce conflicting model orderings across datasets. A model that leads on MRR may trail on Hits@1, and strong performance on one dataset may not generalize to another. This fragmentation hinders comparison, enables selective reporting, and obscures real progress. We reframe KGC evaluation as a Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) problem and present a meta-analysis of seven aggregators across five tests: consistency, cross-dataset stability, metric independence, robustness under noise, and generalizability. Each test is averaged over leave-one-model-out (LOMO) and leave-one-group-out (LOGO) removals so that reliability reflects aggregator behavior across diverse model subsets. Across tail $(h,r,?)$ and relation $(h,?,t)$ prediction, Pareto-optimal analysis identifies Z-score as the most balanced aggregator, which ranks DualE highest for tail prediction and FMS (Flow-Modulated Scoring) highest for relation prediction. A test-sensitivity analysis using the same removals shows that consistency and stability are largely removal-invariant, while generalizability and independence are the most sensitive. The framework resolves evaluation inconsistencies and offers evidence-based guidance for aggregator selection and model benchmarking in KGC.

</details>


### [271] [LLM-Guided Neural Architecture Search for Robust Co-Design of Physical Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10294)
*Tyler King,Timothee Leleu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UH-NAS is a hardware-agnostic, LLM-guided neural architecture search framework that co-optimizes task accuracy and inference energy across unconventional hardware platforms (e.g., optical MZI), enabling fair cross-platform comparison and discovering more diverse, robust architectures than conventional baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NAS methods are tailored to single hardware families, limiting cross-platform comparison and generalization. Unconventional hardware demands architectures that co-optimize task accuracy with platform-specific constraints like energy cost, physical non-idealities, and numerical precision.

Method: UH-NAS integrates language models as evolutionary operators in a hardware-agnostic framework. It exposes hardware as a swappable backend with per-platform energy models, physical constraints, and non-ideality simulators, enabling fair comparisons across backends without modifying the search algorithm.

Result: Tested on optical MZI hardware, UH-NAS discovers more diverse and robust architectures than conventional baselines, and outperforms existing LLM-to-NAS approaches. Ablations highlight the importance of architecture-hardware co-design for emerging computing platforms.

Conclusion: UH-NAS demonstrates that LLM-guided, hardware-agnostic NAS can effectively co-optimize accuracy and energy across diverse unconventional hardware platforms, underscoring the necessity of architecture-hardware co-design for next-generation computing.

Abstract: Deploying neural networks on unconventional hardware demands architectures that co-optimize task accuracy and platform-specific constraints such as energy cost, physical non-idealities, and numerical precision. Existing neural architecture search (NAS) methods are typically tailored to a single hardware family, limiting cross-platform comparison and generalization. We introduce Unconventional Hardware Neural Architecture Search (UH-NAS), a hardware-agnostic, LLM-guided NAS framework that integrates language models as evolutionary operators to co-optimize accuracy and inference energy. By exposing hardware as a swappable backend with per-platform energy models, physical constraints, and non-ideality simulators, UH-NAS enables fair system-level comparisons across various backends without modifying the search algorithm. Tested on optical MZI hardware, UH-NAS discovers more diverse, robust architectures than conventional baselines while outperforming existing LLM-to-NAS approaches. Additional ablations on architecture robustness under non-idealities and the role of system prompts highlight the importance of architecture-hardware co-design for emerging computing platforms.

</details>


### [272] [Rank Collapse, Fixed Points, and the Renormalization Group Structure of MLP Residual Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10324)
*Parviz Haggi-Mani,Irina Rish*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MLP residual stacks implement a selective coarse-graining (RG-like) procedure: depth reduces the effective rank of representations for short-correlation inputs but preserves it for long-correlation inputs, with most layers near a fixed point and only one or two transitions showing significant kernel drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The deep neural network / RG analogy has been repeatedly noted but remains purely qualitative—no measurable RG order parameter, controlled input variation, or quantitative empirical verification exists.

Method: Study a pure MLP residual stack trained on masked token prediction over synthetic Markov chains with known spectral properties; measure effective rank, rank collapse, and inter-layer kernel drift.

Result: (i) Effective residual stream rank decreases monotonically with depth after training. (ii) Rank collapse is selective: occurs for short correlation length (~1) but absent for long correlation (~7), preserving task-relevant degrees of freedom. (iii) Kernel drift concentrates at 1-2 transitions; rest of network near fixed point.

Conclusion: This provides the first quantitative, position-level evidence that MLP residual networks implement selective coarse-graining governed by input spectral structure.

Abstract: The analogy between deep neural network forward passes and renormalization group (RG) flows has been repeatedly noted in the literature, but existing treatments remain qualitative: depth is described as a coarse-graining scale, attention is likened to a partition function, and representations are said to flow toward fixed points. No existing work has defined a measurable RG order parameter, tested it under controlled variation of the input distribution, or made quantitative predictions that are empirically verified. We study the simplest architecture for which the analogy is tractable: a pure MLP residual stack trained on masked token prediction over synthetic Markov chain sequences with known spectral properties. We report three findings. (i) The effective rank of the residual stream decreases monotonically with depth after training, consistent with progressive integration of irrelevant degrees of freedom. (ii) This rank collapse is selective: it occurs for chains with short correlation length approximately 1 but is absent for chains with long correlation length approximately 7, measured at the position level to control for mean-pooling artifacts. The network preserves exactly the degrees of freedom relevant to the prediction task, the content of the RG relevance criterion. (iii) Inter-layer kernel drift is concentrated at one or two specific transitions, with the remainder of the network near a fixed point, consistent with a discrete fixed-point plateau. Together these findings constitute the first quantitative, position-level evidence that MLP residual networks implement a selective coarse-graining procedure governed by the spectral structure of the input distribution.

</details>


### [273] [Privacy-Preserving Credit Risk Prediction with Alternative Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10333)
*Hongzhe Zhang,Jiarong Xu,Jing He,Xiao Fang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes PrivacyCredit, a privacy-preserving machine learning method for credit risk prediction that securely incorporates alternative data (e.g., mobile phone data) without sharing raw data with financial institutions, achieving the same predictive performance as insecure plaintext models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Alternative data (e.g., mobile phone communication data) improves credit risk prediction, but it is held by external entities and directly sharing it with financial institutions infringes on consumer privacy. Existing studies largely overlook this privacy issue.

Method: The authors define a new problem with three constraints: privacy-preserving (protects consumer privacy), model-confidentiality (model stored centrally at the financial institution), and lossless (maintains predictive performance). They develop PrivacyCredit, a privacy-preserving machine learning method, and theoretically prove its properties.

Result: Experiments on a real-world credit dataset with alternative data show PrivacyCredit achieves the same predictive performance as the insecure plaintext combination of traditional and alternative data. The method also preserves model confidentiality and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: PrivacyCredit effectively enables secure incorporation of alternative data into credit risk prediction while satisfying all three practical constraints, bridging an important gap in privacy protection for consumer credit.

Abstract: Credit risk prediction is a critical problem in the consumer credit industry. Traditionally, financial institutions construct credit risk prediction models using borrowers' demographic, financial, and credit history data, collectively referred to as traditional data. Recent studies have demonstrated that alternative data, such as borrowers' mobile phone communication data, enable lenders to acquire fuller and more accurate profiles of borrowers' creditworthiness, thereby improving credit risk prediction performance. Nevertheless, alternative data are held by external entities independent of financial institutions. Directly sharing alternative data with financial institutions infringe on consumer privacy, yet existing credit risk prediction studies largely overlook this issue. To address this gap, we define a new problem, namely privacy-preserving credit risk prediction with alternative data, which simultaneously considers three practical constraints: the privacy-preserving constraint that protects consumer privacy, the model-confidentiality constraint that learns and stores the model centrally at the financial institution, and the lossless constraint that maintains the performance of the learned model. To solve this problem, we develop PrivacyCredit, a novel privacy-preserving machine learning method. We then theoretically demonstrate the privacy-preserving, model-confidential, and lossless properties of PrivacyCredit. Through extensive experiments using a real-world credit dataset linked with alternative data, we demonstrate the predictive value of securely incorporating alternative data into credit risk prediction and show that PrivacyCredit achieves the same predictive performance as the model learned from the insecure plaintext combination of traditional and alternative data. We further evaluate its model-confidentiality property and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [274] [Beyond Explaining Predictions: Logic-Based Explanations for Confidence in Machine Learning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10347)
*Vinícius Peixoto Chagas,Carlos Henrique Leitão Cavalcante,Thiago Alves Rocha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces confidence-aware abductive explanations that preserve both predicted class and a user-specified confidence threshold, improving upon traditional abductive explanations which may provide weak confidence guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional abductive explanations only focus on classification behavior and may produce explanations that cover instances with low predictive confidence, which is problematic when confidence influences critical decisions.

Method: The authors introduce the concept of Minimum Confidence Threshold (MCT) to quantify the weakest confidence guarantee of an abductive explanation, formulate MCT computation as an optimization problem, and propose an algorithm for generating minimal explanations that satisfy a desired confidence threshold.

Result: Experiments on boosted trees show traditional abductive explanations often provide substantially weaker confidence guarantees than the explained instance's own confidence, while confidence-aware explanations improve minimum confidence with only modest increases in explanation length.

Conclusion: The proposed approach is particularly suitable for applications where both predictive correctness and confidence are essential for trustworthy decision making.

Abstract: Machine learning is increasingly used in critical domains, where both predictions and their associated confidence levels influence important decisions. To enhance transparency in such scenarios, it is important to understand why a model is confident or uncertain about its predictions. Recent logic-based approaches provide abductive explanations, minimal subsets of features sufficient to preserve the predicted class, with correctness guarantees. However, these methods focus solely on classification behavior and may produce explanations that cover instances with low predictive confidence. In this work, we introduce the concept of Minimum Confidence Threshold (MCT), which quantifies the weakest confidence guarantee provided by an abductive explanation. Building upon this concept, we propose confidence-aware abductive explanations, which preserve not only the predicted class but also a user-specified confidence guarantee. We formulate MCT computation as an optimization problem and introduce an algorithm for generating minimal explanations that satisfy a desired confidence threshold. We evaluate the proposed framework on boosted trees for binary classification, although the approach is applicable to other machine learning models that provide confidence scores. Experimental results show that traditional abductive explanations often provide substantially weaker confidence guarantees than the confidence associated with the explained instance itself. In contrast, confidence-aware explanations consistently improve the minimum confidence guaranteed by an explanation while requiring only a modest increase in explanation length. These properties make the proposed approach particularly suitable for applications where both predictive correctness and confidence are essential for trustworthy decision making.

</details>


### [275] [KG-SoftMAP: Soft Knowledge-Graph Priors for Bayesian Network Structure Learning from Sparse Discrete Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10358)
*Guoliang Xu,James E. Corter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KG-SoftMAP learns Bayesian network structure from sparse discrete data by combining BDeu scoring with a soft knowledge graph prior, outperforming baselines in structure recovery (especially when data is very sparse, ρ=0.05) and providing KG-consistent, calibrated diagnostic models, though discriminative logistic regression may be preferred when no meaningful knowledge graph exists.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning BN structure from sparse discrete data is fundamentally difficult because few variable pairs have sufficient joint observations for reliable scoring. The authors aim to leverage imperfect domain knowledge (from experts or LLMs) as a weighted knowledge graph prior to overcome data sparsity limitations.

Method: KG-SoftMAP encodes a weighted directed knowledge graph as a soft, confidence-weighted, data-overridable edge prior, then maximizes a MAP objective that combines the BDeu score with a logit-form prior.

Result: On synthetic benchmarks, KG-SoftMAP recovers partial directed structure at ρ=0.05 (DF1 0.14-0.29 vs near-zero baselines) and more at ρ≥0.2 (DF1 0.46-0.96); degradation is graceful with lower KG quality. On real sparse educational data, it trails logistic regression by 0.03 F1_FAIL but offers KG-consistent edges and calibrated probabilities.

Conclusion: KG-SoftMAP is effective for sparse data when an informative but imperfect KG is available, best used as a diagnostic model. Without a meaningful KG, discriminative logistic regression is preferable.

Abstract: Learning Bayesian network (BN) structure from sparse discrete data is hard: when each instance records only a few variables, most variable pairs lack the joint observations needed for reliable scoring, and data-only methods recover little structure. Imperfect domain knowledge, expressible as a weighted directed knowledge graph (KG), is often available. We propose KG-SoftMAP, which encodes such a KG as a soft, confidence-weighted, data-overridable edge prior and maximizes a MAP objective combining the BDeu score with a logit-form prior; the KG may be expert-curated or LLM-extracted. On controlled synthetic benchmarks, the only setting with ground-truth DAGs, KG-SoftMAP recovers partial directed structure at $ρ=0.05$ (DF1 $0.14$ to $0.29$, versus near-zero baselines) and substantially more once $ρ\geq0.2$ (DF1 $0.46$ to $0.96$), when paired with an informative but imperfect KG; recovery degrades gracefully as KG quality drops. On real sparse educational data, which has no ground-truth DAG, we evaluate deployment-facing measures only: prediction, calibration, and KG-consistency. The learned BN is best read as a diagnostic model: on SAF it trails logistic regression by $0.03$ F1_FAIL while providing KG-consistent edges, calibrated joint probabilities, and inference from arbitrary observed concept subsets; when no meaningful KG exists, discriminative logistic regression is preferable.

</details>


### [276] [Beyond Absolute Imitation: Anchored Residual Guidance for Privileged On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10385)
*Wenhao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AR-OPD introduces a dual-view framework for on-policy distillation that disentangles privileged information (oracle traces) into a locally compatible anchor and a controlled residual, preventing the student from learning hindsight-biased shortcuts. It outperforms full privileged OPD by 2.3 points and reduces hindsight leakage by 21.7%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current privileged OPD methods treat privileged information as a monolithic imitation target, failing to separate locally reachable reasoning steps from future-conditioned oracle signals. This causes a reachability mismatch where students are encouraged to match hindsight-biased distributions outside their local predictive support, leading them to skip valid intermediate reasoning and favor unsupported shortcuts.

Method: AR-OPD (Anchored Residual On-Policy Distillation) is a dual-view framework that disentangles privileged supervision into two components: (1) a locally compatible anchor from a partially privileged teacher, and (2) a controlled residual that injects oracle foresight as destination-directed guidance rather than forcing strict full-view imitation.

Result: AR-OPD outperforms full privileged OPD by 2.3 points and SFT by 7.9 points across diverse reasoning tasks. It reduces hindsight leakage by 21.7%, mitigates late-stage drift, and achieves up to a 7.2-point advantage on challenging long-horizon trajectories exceeding 768 tokens.

Conclusion: Disentangling privileged information through an anchored residual mechanism is an effective way to resolve the reachability mismatch in OPD, reducing shortcut learning and improving performance especially on long-horizon reasoning tasks.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) has demonstrated strong empirical gains in enhancing complex reasoning in LLMs by aligning a student model with a teacher's predictive distribution over the student's own trajectories. An emerging variant, Privileged OPD, further strengthens this paradigm by employing a self-teacher model augmented with privileged information, such as oracle traces, to mitigate teacher-student capacity gaps while providing dense, answer-directed supervision. However, current methods treat privileged information as a monolithic imitation target, failing to disentangle locally reachable reasoning steps from future-conditioned oracle signals. Consequently, the student is encouraged to match a hindsight-biased distribution that often falls outside its local predictive support. This reachability mismatch incentivizes the student model to skip valid intermediate reasoning in favor of locally unsupported shortcuts. To resolve this, we introduce Anchored Residual On-Policy Distillation (AR-OPD), a dual-view framework that disentangles privileged supervision. Rather than enforcing strict full-view imitation, AR-OPD establishes a locally compatible anchor using a partially privileged teacher, isolating and injecting oracle foresight as a controlled residual to provide destination-directed guidance. Across diverse reasoning tasks, AR-OPD outperforms full privileged OPD by 2.3 points and SFT by 7.9 points. Crucially, this anchored residual mechanism reduces hindsight leakage by 21.7% and mitigates late-stage drift, yielding up to a 7.2-point advantage on challenging long-horizon trajectories exceeding 768 tokens.

</details>


### [277] [Validation-Stage Combinatorial Fusion Analysis for Imbalanced Credit-Card Fraud Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10393)
*Xiao Han,Chenyu Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows Combinatorial Fusion Analysis (CFA)—searching over model subsets and rank-score fusion rules—improves fraud detection over single models (AUC-ROC=0.9405, AUPRC=0.6699, F1=0.6373) by selecting complementary subsets of Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM with diversity-weighted scores, while CTGAN-based data augmentation actually hurts performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Credit-card fraud detection is challenging due to rarity, cost, and skewed distribution of fraudulent transactions. Although gradient-boosted trees already perform well on structured data, it's unclear whether additional fusion methods like CFA can still add meaningful value.

Method: Using a leakage-free 60/20/20 split on IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection data, 480 fusion configurations from seven base classifiers were evaluated. CFA searches over model subsets and applies rank-score fusion rules, including diversity-weighted score fusion (DEF WtScore). A CTGAN augmentation experiment was also conducted.

Result: Best result: DEF WtScore (RF+XGB+LightGBM) achieves AUC-ROC=0.9405, AUPRC=0.6699, F1=0.6373. Bootstrap confidence intervals confirm significant gains over the best single model. CFA matches soft voting on AUC-ROC but improves AUPRC/F1, and outperforms stacking. CTGAN augmentation degrades performance.

Conclusion: CFA is most valuable as a validation-stage method for selecting a small, complementary subset of classifiers and assigning diversity-aware weights, rather than combining all available classifiers indiscriminately.

Abstract: Credit-card fraud detection is difficult because fraudulent transactions are rare, costly, and unevenly distributed. Strong gradient-boosted tree models already perform well on structured transaction data, so the value of another fusion method is not obvious. This paper examines whether Combinatorial Fusion Analysis (CFA), which searches over model subsets and rank-score fusion rules, can still add value on the IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection benchmark. Using a leakage-free 60/20/20 train/validation/test protocol, we evaluate 480 fusion configurations built from seven base classifiers. The best test-set result comes from diversity-weighted score fusion of Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM (DEF WtScore), with AUC-ROC = 0.9405, AUPRC = 0.6699, and F1 = 0.6373. Bootstrap confidence intervals from 1,000 resamples show that the gains over the strongest single model exclude zero for all three metrics. CFA matches soft voting on AUC-ROC, improves AUPRC and F1, and outperforms stacking in this setting. A CTGAN augmentation experiment gives a negative result: synthetic fraud samples degrade both individual models and CFA. Overall, CFA is most useful here not as a way to combine every classifier, but as a validation-stage method for choosing a small, complementary subset and assigning diversity-aware weights.

</details>


### [278] [FOGO: Forgetting-aware Orthogonalization Optimizer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10406)
*Toan Nguyen,Yang Liu,Trung Le,Celso de Melo,Flora D. Salim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FOGO is a scalable optimizer that prevents gradient interference in both standard training and continual learning by spectrally orthogonalizing momentum updates and resolving conflicts using a compact codebook memory, outperforming Adam and Muon across various settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors argue that forgetting is not unique to continual learning but is a general optimization problem where dominant mini-batch gradients suppress rare useful directions, causing short-term forgetting that compounds into long-term forgetting.

Method: FOGO spectrally orthogonalizes momentum updates to prevent dominant directions from monopolizing optimization, stores representative past directions in a compact codebook memory via random projection (preserving pairwise distances), and resolves conflicts through lightweight orthogonal correction with a proximal step.

Result: FOGO consistently improves convergence and knowledge retention across class-imbalanced classification, continual visual learning (domain/class shifts), continual fine-tuning of LLaVA-7B, and GPT-2 pretraining, outperforming Adam and Muon.

Conclusion: Forgetting is a general optimization phenomenon, and gradient interference can be effectively addressed by the proposed FOGO optimizer with minimal overhead and no data storage requirements.

Abstract: We argue that forgetting is not confined to continual learning but is a general optimization phenomenon: during standard training, dominant mini-batch gradients suppress rare but useful update directions, causing short-term forgetting at every step. When such knowledge is never revisited, these losses compound into long-term forgetting-the classical failure mode of continual learning. We introduce FOGO, a scalable optimizer that continuously detects and resolves gradient interference across both regimes. FOGO spectrally orthogonalizes momentum updates to prevent dominant directions from monopolizing optimization, then stores representative past directions in a compact codebook memory built on random projection, where pairwise distances are provably preserved in low-dimensional space. At each step, conflicts between the current update and stored directions are resolved via lightweight orthogonal correction and lifted back through a proximal step, with minimal overhead and no data storage. Across class-imbalanced classification, continual visual learning under domain and class shifts, continual fine-tuning of LLaVA-7B, and GPT-2 pretraining, FOGO consistently improves convergence and knowledge retention, outperforming Adam and Muon.

</details>


### [279] [Geometry-Aware Reinforcement Learning for 2D Irregular Nesting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10611)
*Auguste Lehuger,Guillaume Henon-Just*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Polygons Transformer (PoT) — a geometry-aware neural encoder combined with Reinforcement Learning to solve 2D irregular nesting problems, achieving competitive performance with the state-of-the-art heuristic solver Sparrow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional heuristic solvers for 2D irregular nesting are blind to polygon geometry, relying on brute-force search with minimal geometrical guidance. The authors argue RL can overcome this bottleneck by learning geometric priors from data.

Method: They propose the Polygons Transformer (PoT), a novel architecture that encodes 2D continuous vector geometries with cross-polygons attention, paired with a Combinatorial Optimization Reinforcement Learning (CORL) training framework.

Result: The trained RL agent achieves area utilization performance highly competitive with Sparrow, the state-of-the-art heuristic solver, demonstrating that RL can discover and exploit geometric awareness for spatial tasks.

Conclusion: Reinforcement learning, when paired with a geometry-aware neural encoder, can successfully overcome the blindness of traditional heuristics for 2D irregular nesting, offering a data-driven alternative that learns geometric priors.

Abstract: Traditional heuristic solvers for the 2D irregular nesting problem share a fundamental limitation: they are blind to polygon geometry, relying on guided brute-force to navigate the continuous placement space with minimal geometrical guidance. In this paper, we argue that Reinforcement Learning is uniquely positioned to overcome this bottleneck. By pairing an optimization policy with a geometry-aware neural encoder, an agent can automatically discover rich geometric priors directly from data, utilizing these learned intuitions to strategically guide exploration. To realize this, we introduce the Polygons Transformer (PoT), a novel architecture that encodes 2D continuous vector geometries while allowing cross-polygons attention. We couple this novel architecture with a Combinatorial Optimization Reinforcement Learning (CORL) training framework to find optimal solutions. To support this paradigm, we release an open-source training dataset derived from complex geographic contours alongside a dedicated evaluation benchmark. Our empirical validation demonstrates that our trained agent achieves area utilization performance highly competitive with Sparrow, the state-of-the-art heuristic solver, proving that reinforcement learning can successfully discover and exploit geometric awareness for precise spatial tasks.

</details>


### [280] [Parallel Causal Associative Fields: Gated Sparse Memory for Long-Context Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10435)
*Muhammad Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Parallel Causal Associative Field (PCAF), a new language modeling primitive that uses parallel content-addressed memory over causal successor records to achieve sparse long-context access without quadratic scaling or fixed recurrent state bottlenecks. PCAF outperforms dense Transformers in both perplexity and throughput at 303M parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers have quadratic scaling with context length, while recurrent/state-space models compress history into fixed-size states. The authors seek a third primitive that avoids both bottlenecks.

Method: PCAF writes local records from a context window into hash buckets, retrieves a bounded candidate set for the current query, forms a sparse cache distribution over successor tokens, and mixes that cache with a parametric local language model via a learned gate.

Result: At 303M parameters with T=2048, PCAF achieves 36.31 perplexity on WikiText-103 and 52.45 on PG-19 (vs. 47.49 and 53.84 for dense Transformer). Throughput: 0.61-0.62M tokens/s vs. 0.43M tokens/s for baselines.

Conclusion: The associative cache, retrieval capacity, and learned gate are critical components affecting the speed-quality trade-off. PCAF offers an effective alternative to dense attention and recurrent compression.

Abstract: Transformers achieve strong language modeling performance by providing direct token-to-token communication paths, but causal self-attention scales quadratically with context length. Recurrent and state-space models reduce this cost, yet compress history into sequentially updated fixed-size states. This paper studies a third primitive: a parallel content-addressed memory over causal successor records. The proposed Parallel Causal Associative Field (PCAF) writes local records from a context window into hash buckets, retrieves a bounded candidate set for the current query, forms a sparse cache distribution over successor tokens, and mixes that cache with a parametric local language model through a learned gate. The resulting model maintains sparse long-context access while avoiding a single fixed recurrent state bottleneck. We evaluate PCAF under full autoregressive pretraining on WikiText-103 and PG-19 using a distributed Google Cloud TPU v4-32 pod. At 303M parameters and context length T = 2048, PCAF-semantic reaches 36.31 perplexity on WikiText-103 and 52.45 perplexity on PG-19, compared with 47.49 and 53.84 for a matched dense Transformer. PCAF-semantic simultaneously processes 0.61-0.62M tokens/s across the TPU pod, versus 0.43M tokens/s for dense and local attention baselines. Supporting 41M-parameter multi-seed sweeps and single-GPU component ablations show that the associative cache, retrieval capacity, and learned gate materially affect the speed-quality trade-off.

</details>


### [281] [SpenseGPT: Practical One-shot Pruning Enabling Sparse and Dense GEMMs for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10445)
*Jaeseong Lee,Seung-won Hwang,Samyam Rajbhandari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spense proposes a hybrid sparse-dense format (2:4 sparse + dense regions) for one-shot LLM post-training pruning, achieving up to 1.2x end-to-end decoding speedup on B200 GPUs with FP8 while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 2:4 sparsity formats cause accuracy degradation due to strict 50% sparsity constraint in post-training pruning, while relaxed formats require specialized compiler support or introduce runtime overhead limiting speedup.

Method: Spense splits each weight matrix into a 2:4 sparse region and a dense region (hybrid format), compatible with existing sparse/dense GEMM libraries. SpenseGPT is a one-shot post-training pruning method with two strategies for selecting dense regions.

Result: Achieves up to 1.2x end-to-end decoding speedup on B200 GPUs with FP8 precision on Qwen3-32B and Seed-OSS-36B models while preserving accuracy.

Conclusion: First one-shot pruning demonstration of real-world end-to-end LLM decoding speedup from semi-structured sparse tensor cores on recent GPUs (B200s) while maintaining model quality.

Abstract: Semi-structured 2:4 sparsity is widely supported by modern accelerators, providing up to a 2x theoretical speedup. However, its strict 50% sparsity constraint often causes non-negligible accuracy degradation under post-training pruning. Meanwhile, existing relaxed sparsity formats either require specialized compiler support or introduce runtime overheads that limit end-to-end speedup. We propose Spense, a practical hybrid sparse-dense format that splits each weight matrix into a 2:4 sparse region and a dense region. This design relaxes the effective sparsity constraint while remaining compatible with existing high-performance sparse and dense GEMM libraries, avoiding both custom compiler support and input activation expansion. Building on this format, we introduce SpenseGPT, a one-shot post-training pruning method that produces sparse and dense regions. Notably, we show that selecting the right dense regions is important, and we devise two different strategies to choose them. Experiments on Qwen3-32B and Seed-OSS-36B demonstrate that our method achieves up to 1.2x end-to-end decoding speedup on B200 GPUs with FP8 precision, while preserving accuracy. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first one-shot pruning demonstration of real-world end-to-end LLM decoding speedup from semi-structured sparse tensor cores on recent GPUs such as B200s, while maintaining model quality.

</details>


### [282] [Mitigating Bias in Low-SNR Financial Reinforcement Learning via Quantum Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10448)
*Zeyu Liu,Xuanzhi Feng,Sing Kwong Lai,Yuanchen Gao,Xiaoyi Pang,Hualei Zhang,Jingcai Guo,Jie Zhang,Song Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FPQC-SAC is a plug-and-play SAC variant using Parameterized Quantum Circuits to constrain feature propagation in financial markets, improving stability and returns by 66.89% over SAC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Financial markets have low SNR, causing off-policy maximum-entropy methods like SAC to fail due to noisy state representations and bootstrapping error amplification, termed the "Financial Entropy Trap".

Method: FPQC-SAC places a compact, bounded Parameterized Quantum Circuit (PQC) before actor and critic networks to constrain feature propagation at the representation level, reducing extreme market fluctuation impact on Bellman targets while maintaining cross-asset interactions via trainable quantum entanglement.

Result: Achieves 66.89% relative gain in cumulative return over standard SAC and ~27% improvement over the best continuous-control deep RL baseline on real-world portfolio management tasks.

Conclusion: FPQC-SAC effectively mitigates the Financial Entropy Trap by constraining representations with quantum circuits, demonstrating superior out-of-sample stability and returns.

Abstract: The financial market is a typical low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) setting, which often destabilizes off-policy maximum-entropy methods like Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). Specifically, noisy state representations may produce unreliable Q-value estimates, and bootstrapping amplifies these errors, forming a failure mode we call the "Financial Entropy Trap". In this paper, we propose FPQC-SAC, an efficient and plug-and-play SAC variant that places a compact and bounded Parameterized Quantum Circuit (PQC) before the actor and critic networks to constrain feature propagation at the representation level, rather than filtering raw inputs or regularizing Q-values after bootstrapping. Notably, FPQC-SAC reduces the impact of extreme market fluctuations on Bellman target estimation, while trainable quantum entanglement preserves flexible cross-asset interactions. Empirical evaluations on real-world portfolio management tasks demonstrate that FPQC-SAC substantially enhances out-of-sample stability and cumulative returns by achieving a 66.89% relative gain in cumulative return over standard unconstrained SAC and outperforms the best continuous-control deep reinforcement learning baseline by approximately 27%. Open-source code is available at https://github.com/ZeyuLIU-UST/FPQC-SAC-main.

</details>


### [283] [ERAlign: Energy-based Representation Alignment of GNNs and LLMs on Text-attributed Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10461)
*Xianlin Zeng,Fan Xia,Xiangyu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ERAlign is an Energy-based Representation Alignment framework that aligns GNN and LLM representations on text-attributed graphs using an energy-based objective with Energy Discrepancy for efficient training, achieving SOTA results on eight TAG datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior methods for integrating GNNs and LLMs on TAGs rely on heuristics with coarse-grained matching, lacking sufficient constraints and ignoring distributional alignment, which leads to representation drift and limited generalization.

Method: Projects GNN-encoded graph structure and LLM-derived text embeddings into a shared latent space using layer-wise distance metrics optimized via an EBM objective. Introduces Energy Discrepancy (ED) to avoid intractable normalization costs, with theoretical guarantees for training efficiency.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across eight TAG datasets under varying supervision levels and cross-task transfer scenarios.

Conclusion: Energy-based alignment with ED provides better distribution consistency, higher training efficiency, and reduced energy landscape distortion for TAG representation learning.

Abstract: Text-attributed Graphs (TAGs) incorporate textual node attributes with graph structures to describe rich relational semantics. Recent efforts to integrate Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise for learning on TAGs, yet achieving well-aligned representations remains challenging. Prior studies largely rely on heuristics that perform coarse-grained matching. They lack sufficient constraints and ignore distributional alignment, leading to representation drift and limited generalization. Building on Energy-based Models (EBMs), we propose an Energy-based Representation Alignment (ERAlign) framework that projects GNN-encoded graph structure and LLM-derived text embeddings in a shared latent space to achieve distribution consistency. Concretely, layer-wise alignment is quantified by a distance metric and optimized via an EBM objective. By decreasing energy values, our framework yields well-aligned representations for downstream tasks. During training, we introduce Energy Discrepancy (ED) to avoid high sampling costs associated with intractable normalization. ED also carries theoretical guarantees of higher training efficiency and reduced energy landscape distortion. Empirical evaluations on eight TAG datasets demonstrate that ERAlign obtains state-of-the-art performance across varying levels of supervision and cross-task transfer scenarios.

</details>


### [284] [XtrAIn: Training-Guided Occlusion for Feature Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10877)
*Thodoris Lymperopoulos,Ioannis Kakogeorgiou,Denia Kanellopoulou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: XtrAIn addresses key issues in occlusion-based attribution by shifting feature removal from input space to parameter space, following model training trajectories to produce cleaner, more interpretable explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Occlusion-based attribution methods suffer from bias, out-of-distribution issues, unstable explanations, and "attribution shift" where occluding features alters non-occluded feature contributions in nonlinear models.

Method: Introduces XtrAIn (training-guided attribution transferring occlusion from input to parameter space), Xstep (lightweight approximation), and XtrAIn+ (target-focused variant emphasizing class-aligned updates).

Result: Produces cleaner, more interpretable attribution patterns than standard baselines on controlled image datasets and PAM50 breast-cancer subtype classification.

Conclusion: XtrAIn offers a training-aware perspective on feature attribution, providing a diagnostic tool for studying how feature-level evidence forms during training.

Abstract: Occlusion-based attribution methods provide an intuitive way to estimate feature importance by perturbing input features and measuring the resulting change in model output. However, their reliability is strongly affected by how feature removal is implemented: externally selected baselines can introduce bias, out-of-distribution samples, and unstable explanations, while in nonlinear models the occlusion of a set of features can also alter the contribution of non-occluded features. We refer to this effect as attribution shift, as the attribution scores of the non-occluded features drift from their initial values. To challenge these major issues that render explanations unstable, we introduce XtrAIn, a training-guided attribution method that transfers the occlusion operation from the input space to the parameter space. Instead of replacing input values with hand-crafted baselines, XtrAIn follows the model's training trajectory and measures how feature-associated parameter updates affect the output logits. We further introduce Xstep, a lightweight approximation for reducing computational cost, and XtrAIn+, a target-focused variant that emphasizes updates aligned with the target class. Experiments on controlled image datasets and PAM50 breast-cancer subtype classification show that the proposed methods produce cleaner and more interpretable attribution patterns than standard attribution baselines. Overall, XtrAIn provides a training-aware perspective on feature attribution and offers a useful diagnostic tool for studying how feature-level evidence is formed during training.

</details>


### [285] [UPLOTS: A Unified Pretrained Language Model for Constrained Time-series Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10466)
*Du Yin,Hao Xue,Jinliang Deng,Yang Yang,Shuang Ao,Arian Prabowo,Flora Salim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UPLOTS is a single transformer-based language model that uses learned constraint prompts and dynamic multi-dataset re-weighting to generate time-series data across diverse domains, outperforming task-specific models on multiple pattern-constrained benchmarks and downstream forecasting tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing time-series generation approaches require separate models per dataset, limiting scalability and failing to leverage shared temporal structures across domains.

Method: UPLOTS uses a single pre-trained transformer backbone with learned constraint prompts, dynamic multi-dataset loss re-weighting, and prompt-to-pattern mapping to internalize and conditionally generate diverse temporal structures.

Result: Evaluated on four real-world benchmarks with multiple constraint settings (peak-period, calendar, load-level, volatility); generalizes to held-out constraint combinations and improves data augmentation under scarce data regimes.

Conclusion: UPLOTS demonstrates that a unified, prompt-guided framework can effectively replace task-specific models for constrained time-series generation across domains, with strong generalization and data augmentation benefits.

Abstract: In time-series generation, existing approaches typically handcraft ortrain a separate model for each dataset, which hinders their scalability and fails to leverage shared temporal structures across domains. To address this fragmentation, we propose UPLOTS, a Unified, Prompt-guided Language model framework fOr constrained Time-Series Generation across diverse domains. Instead of building task-specific models, UPLOTS leverages a single pre-trained transformer backbone guided by learned constraint prompts, enabling on-demand generation with precise pattern control. One key innovation is our dynamic multi-dataset loss re-weighting and prompt-to-pattern mapping, which allows UPLOTS to internalize diverse temporal structures during training and conditionally generate them at inference. We evaluate UPLOTS on four real-world benchmarks and multiple constraint settings, including peak-period, calendar, load-level, and volatility patterns. Additional held-out constraint-combination and downstream forecasting experiments further demonstrate that UPLOTS generalizes beyond the original peak-pattern setting and improves data augmentation under scarce real-data regimes. Our code and baselines are available at anonymous github repo: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/UPLOTS-6C36.

</details>


### [286] [Advancing the State-of-the-Art in Empirical Privacy Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10481)
*Nicole Mitchell,Galen Andrew,Arun Ganesh,Brendan McMahan,Peter Kairouz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes generating synthetic canaries via high-temperature sampling from LLMs to improve privacy auditing of parameter-efficient fine-tuning, and introduces a synthetic data audit method by fine-tuning an auxiliary model, while systematically investigating the effects of model capacity and canary entropy on memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of LLMs can exhibit problematic memorization of training examples, and empirical privacy auditing faces a key challenge in designing effective "canary" examples that are mixed with privacy-sensitive training data.

Method: The authors propose generating synthetic canaries via high-temperature sampling (T ≥ 0.8) from LLMs with tailored prompts, and introduce a synthetic data audit based on fine-tuning an auxiliary model on synthetic data to estimate privacy leakage.

Result: Synthetic canaries act as high-influence outliers ensuring high identifiability and strong audits; the auxiliary model audit provides a strong estimate of privacy leakage through synthetic data.

Conclusion: The proposed methodologies enable systematic investigation into the interacting effects of model capacity and canary entropy on memorization, providing stronger privacy auditing for LLMs fine-tuned on privacy-sensitive data.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) can exhibit problematic memorization of individual training examples. Empirical privacy auditing (EPA) quantifies this risk by measuring realistic data leakage on membership inference (MI) or reconstruction attacks. A key challenge in EPA is designing ``canary'' examples that are mixed with the privacy-sensitive training data. We propose generating synthetic canaries via high-temperature sampling ($T \geq 0.8$) from LLMs, using prompts tailored to the privacy-sensitive training data. These canaries act as high-influence outliers, ensuring high identifiability and hence strong audits. Further, since the canaries are themselves non-private, they are inspectable and can be inserted with repetition without jeopardizing the privacy of the real data. An important use of models fine-tuned on privacy-sensitive data is the generation of synthetic data. This also comes with privacy risk. We introduce a powerful synthetic data audit based on fine-tuning an auxiliary model on the synthetic data. Auditing the auxiliary model for the original canaries then provides a strong estimate of the privacy leakage through the synthetic data. Finally, leveraging our strong auditing methodologies, we perform a systematic investigation into the interacting effects of model capacity and canary entropy on memorization.

</details>


### [287] [Stop Early, Spend Less: Hidden-State Probes as a Practical Recipe for Streaming Moderation of LLM Outputs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10487)
*Huizhen Shu,Xuying Li,Piao Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes lightweight token-level probes trained on internal model activations to perform safety filtering during generation, eliminating the need for a separate moderation model and enabling sub-millisecond per-token checks within the decoding loop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety moderation approaches require a separate model after generation, doubling inference cost and only detecting violations post-hoc. The authors aim to reduce overhead and enable real-time intervention during generation.

Method: Train lightweight linear probes on internal hidden states of a single mid-layer to produce per-token safety scores. These probes reuse generator activations (no extra forward pass), operate inside the decoding loop, and can also be used for activation steering.

Result: The single-layer probe recovers most decisions of a strong guard model, achieves orders-of-magnitude lower compute overhead with minimal latency, and enables streaming halting/modification of unsafe outputs before full generation.

Conclusion: Token-level probing is a highly efficient, drop-in replacement for post-hoc moderation, offering continuous monitoring and intervention at negligible cost, with practical deployment guidelines provided.

Abstract: Deploying large language models in user-facing systems requires efficient output safety filtering. Existing approaches typically rely on a separate moderation model applied after generation, which doubles inference cost and only detects violations after generation completes. We observe that the signal needed for moderation is already present in the model hidden states. Based on this, we train lightweight token-level probes that operate directly on internal activations, producing per-token safety scores that can be aggregated for both offline evaluation and online intervention. The probe reuses activations from the generator and requires no additional forward pass, enabling sub millisecond per-token safety checks inside the decoding loop. A probe applied to a single mid layer recovers most decisions of a strong guard model, acting as a low cost surrogate optimized for latency rather than accuracy. In streaming settings, it can halt or modify unsafe outputs before they are fully generated, replacing end of sequence moderation with continuous token level monitoring. Compared to post hoc and streaming guard models, our method achieves orders of magnitude lower compute overhead with minimal latency cost. We also provide a practical deployment recipe, including layer selection, aggregation strategy, probing frequency, and triggering thresholds. Finally, we show that the probe linear component corresponds to a direction in residual space, enabling both detection and activation steering at negligible cost.

</details>


### [288] [MoE Enhanced Federated Learning for Spatiotemporal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10499)
*Zhehao Dai,Xiao Han,Zhaolin Deng,Zijian Zhang,Xiangyu Zhao,Guojiang Shen,Xiangjie Kong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoE-FedTP is a personalized federated learning framework using Mixture-of-Experts networks to enable privacy-preserving cross-city traffic prediction, effectively handling spatiotemporal heterogeneity while improving prediction accuracy for data-scarce cities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many cities suffer from traffic data scarcity due to limited sensors and uneven development. Cross-city knowledge transfer helps but centralized approaches raise privacy concerns, and existing federated methods fail to handle spatiotemporal heterogeneity across cities effectively.

Method: MoE-FedTP uses spatiotemporal neural networks to extract features from source and target cities, introduces expert networks from different source cities via partial parameter sharing, and employs a gating mechanism to dynamically fuse experts for fine-grained modeling of urban heterogeneity while preserving privacy.

Result: Experiments on four real-world traffic datasets show MoE-FedTP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art cross-city and federated learning baselines, improving prediction accuracy for data-scarce cities.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses privacy concerns and spatiotemporal heterogeneity in cross-city traffic prediction, demonstrating strong performance gains for data-scarce cities through personalized federated learning with Mixture-of-Experts.

Abstract: Traffic prediction is fundamental to intelligent transportation systems and urban computing, yet many cities continue to suffer from traffic data scarcity due to limited sensor deployment and uneven urban development. Cross-city knowledge transfer has thus attracted increasing attention, enabling data-rich cities to assist data-scarce ones. However, centralized approaches raise privacy concerns, while existing federated methods struggle with pronounced spatiotemporal heterogeneity across cities. To address these challenges, we propose MoE-FedTP, a personalized federated cross-city spatiotemporal prediction framework based on lightweight Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) networks. MoE-FedTP first employs spatiotemporal neural networks to extract features from both source and target cities, then introduces a set of expert networks derived from different source cities through partial parameter sharing. A gating mechanism dynamically fuses the experts to capture diverse traffic dynamics, achieving fine-grained modeling of urban heterogeneity while preserving privacy. Experiments on four real-world traffic datasets show that MoE-FedTP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art cross-city and federated learning baselines, demonstrating its effectiveness in enhancing prediction accuracy for data-scarce cities.

</details>


### [289] [Representation-Aware Advantage Estimation: Your Reward Model Provides More Than A Scalar Output](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10528)
*Guozheng Li,Xiyan Fu,Yiwen Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphAE improves RLHF by using RM hidden states as auxiliary signals via graph-based advantage estimation, achieving gains of +6.3 on Arena-Hard, +8.27 on AlpacaEval 2.0, and +0.22 on MT-Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalar rewards from trained RMs are noisy and miss fine-grained preference differences, while RM hidden states contain richer semantic and preference information that remains underutilized.

Method: GraphAE treats sampled response groups as graphs (nodes=responses, edges=similarity in RM hidden space), then computes advantages via graph propagation to incorporate neighbor context. Applied to GRPO, GSPO, and RLOO.

Result: Consistent improvements across three benchmarks: +6.3 on Arena-Hard-v0.1, +8.27 on AlpacaEval 2.0, +0.22 on MT-Bench, demonstrating more sample-efficient and robust RLHF.

Conclusion: Leveraging RM representations as auxiliary signals improves RLHF quality and robustness, and GraphAE is a lightweight plug-in for group-based RL algorithms.

Abstract: Current reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) methods primarily rely on scalar rewards from a trained reward model (RM). While effective, scalar rewards are often noisy and fail to capture fine-grained preference differences, whereas RM hidden states encode richer semantic and preference information. We introduce the representation-aware advantage estimation, which leverages RM hidden states and models them as auxiliary signals for better advantage estimation. Specifically, we propose the Graph-based Advantage Estimation (GraphAE), treat each sampled group as a graph, where nodes correspond to responses and edges capture their similarity in the RM hidden space. Then advantages are computed via graph propagation, enabling each sample to incorporate contextual information from its neighbors. GraphAE is lightweight and can be seamlessly integrated into existing group-based RL algorithms. We apply GraphAE to GRPO, GSPO and RLOO, and conduct extensive experiments on different models and benchmarks. Empirical results show consistent improvements across three benchmarks, with gains of up to + 6.3 on Arena-Hard-v0.1, + 8.27 on AlpacaEval 2.0, and + 0.22 on MT-Bench. These results demonstrate that leveraging RM representations leads to more sample efficient and robust RLHF.

</details>


### [290] [Machine Learning Methods for Studying Latent Neural Activity Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10530)
*Shufeng Kong,Fumei Deng,Xinyi Dong,Caihua Liu,Weiwei Chen,Yingheng Wang,Daniel Cao,Azahara Oliva,Antonio Fernandez-Ruiz,Carla Gomes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper surveys Latent Variable Models (LVMs) for neural decoding, covering single-region dynamics, multi-region communication, behavior-aligned modeling, and foundation models, concluding with benchmarks and open challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent advances in brain recording techniques demand machine learning tools that can decode latent structure from large populations of neurons.

Method: The authors organize the literature into three domains: (1) Single-Region Latent Dynamics (state-space models, LDS, RNNs, Neural ODEs); (2) Multi-Region Communication (probabilistic & subspace methods for inter-area information transfer); (3) Behavior-Aligned Modeling (supervised/contrastive learning to disentangle task-related from internal neural activity). Also covers large-scale foundation models (Transformers, diffusion models).

Result: A comprehensive survey tracing the evolution of LVMs from early state-space models to modern deep generative and foundation models, structured across three interrelated domains.

Conclusion: Key open challenges remain: identifying causal links and directionality of communication, establishing better benchmarks and evaluation criteria for bridging interpretable brain dynamics with reliable neural decoding.

Abstract: Recent developments in brain recording are driving a demand for machine learning tools capable of decoding the latent structure of large populations of neurons. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive survey that outlines the trajectory of Latent Variable Models (LVMs) from early state-space models to more recent deep generative models. We organize the literature into three closely related domains: (1) Single-Region Latent Dynamics, which includes models such as linear dynamical systems to more complex dynamics represented by Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs); (2) Multi-Region Communication, which employs probabilistic as well as subspace methods to study how information is transferred across different brain areas considering synaptic propagation delays and network connectivity; and (3) Behavior-Aligned Modeling, which seeks to disentangle neural activity related to task performance from other internal states via supervised or contrastive learning. This survey also includes large-scale neural foundation models, such as Transformers and diffusion models, that rely on large-scale pre-training for optimal performance across subjects. Finally, we conclude and discuss benchmarks, evaluation criteria, and open challenges, such as the ability to identify causal links or directionality of communication, to facilitate future research for bridging interpretable brain dynamics with reliable neural decoding.

</details>


### [291] [Flexible Flows for Biological Sequence Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10543)
*Yogesh Verma,Dani Korpela,Harri Lähdesmäki,Vikas Garg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A structured coupling with latent edit-based parameterization and latent classifier-free guidance improves Discrete Flow Matching for biological sequence generation, achieving state-of-the-art results across DNA and peptide tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Discrete Flow Matching (DFM) approaches rely on biologically uninformative couplings and lack flexibility for variable-length sequence generation and fine-grained control, motivating the need for more structured, domain-informed generative frameworks.

Method: The paper proposes (1) a structured coupling encoding domain-specific preferences, (2) a latent edit-based rate parameterization for variable-length generation, (3) latent classifier-free guidance in continuous latent space, and (4) Dirichlet-prior temperature scaling for test-time control.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across density estimation, unconditional and conditional DNA sequence generation, and peptide sequence generation tasks.

Conclusion: Structured, biologically informed couplings and latent-space control mechanisms significantly enhance Discrete Flow Matching for biological sequence design, offering improved flexibility and performance.

Abstract: Designing functional biological sequences requires navigating vast discrete spaces under strict evolutionary and biophysical constraints. Discrete Flow Matching (DFM) offers a generative framework over such spaces, but existing approaches rely on biologically uninformative couplings and offer limited flexibility for variable-length sequence generation and fine-grained control. We propose a structured coupling that encodes domain-specific preferences among sequence elements, biasing the source distribution toward plausible regions without modifying the flow objective or training procedure. Building on this, we introduce a latent edit-based rate parameterization that models variable-length generation via edit operations conditioned on a shared global latent, akin to a latent variable model, while remaining tractable. We further introduce a latent classifier-free guidance mechanism that steers generation coherently in continuous latent space, along with Dirichlet-prior temperature scaling for test-time control over edit operations. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse biological sequence tasks, including density estimation, unconditional and conditional DNA sequence generation, and peptide sequence generation.

</details>


### [292] [Convergence of Monte Carlo Optimistic Policy Iteration: Beyond Uniform State-Action Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10580)
*Octave Oliviers,Glenn Vinnicombe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that Monte Carlo optimistic policy iteration (MC-O-PI) converges to optimality under relaxed conditions—specifically, when updates are uniform only over actions within each state, rather than over the entire state-action space. The proof uses monotonic improvement in mean-field dynamics and extends the lock-in argument of the stability-ODE method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The asymptotic behaviour of MC-O-PI has been a long-standing open question. The only known condition guaranteeing convergence to optimality requires impractical uniform initialization over the entire state-action space, which is infeasible when the model is unknown or the state space is large.

Method: First, the authors show that the mean-field dynamics of MC-O-PI generate monotonically improving policies when updates are uniform over actions within each state. Then, they prove that noise cannot consistently prevent this improvement by extending the lock-in argument of the combined stability-ODE method.

Result: MC-O-PI converges to optimality even when updates are uniform only over actions within each state, allowing episodes to start in different states at arbitrary frequencies. This is a strict relaxation of previous conditions.

Conclusion: The proof departs from Tsitsiklis' classical commutativity argument and suggests a new general approach for studying optimistic policy-iteration algorithms.

Abstract: The asymptotic behaviour of Monte Carlo optimistic policy iteration (MC-O-PI) is a long-standing open question. When the model of the environment is unknown, as is common in practice, the only known condition that guarantees convergence to optimality is impractical. In its canonical form, this condition requires that the episodes used for policy evaluation be initialised uniformly over the entire state-action space. This paper strictly relaxes that requirement. Specifically, we prove that initial-visit MC-O-PI converges to optimality even when updates are uniform only over the actions within each state. This allows episodes to start in different states at arbitrary frequencies; a realistic implementation when the state space is large or unknown but the action space in each state is manageable. The proof departs from the classical analysis of Tsitsiklis whose central commutativity argument no longer applies when states are updated at different frequencies. Instead, we first show that the mean-field dynamics of MC-O-PI generate monotonically improving policies when updates are uniform over the actions in each state, and then prove that noise cannot consistently prevent this improvement by extending the lock-in argument of the combined stability-ODE method. This approach suggests a new way to study optimistic policy-iteration algorithms in general.

</details>


### [293] [Drawing with Strangers: Population Scaling Drives Zero-Shot Mutual Intelligibility in Emergent Sketching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10582)
*Jooyeon Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces zero-shot mutual intelligibility (ZMI) for emergent communication between agents from disjoint communities, showing that scaling training populations improves ZMI through increased in-group variation and cross-group convergence via perceptual grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While generalization in emergent communication has focused on novel inputs and linguistic structures, the ability for agents from strictly disjoint communities to communicate without prior exposure remains unexplored. The authors formalize this gap as zero-shot mutual intelligibility (ZMI).

Method: The authors formalize ZMI and use emergent sketching (agents communicating through drawn strokes) as a visually grounded modality. They scale the training population size and measure in-group variation, cross-group variation, and perceptual grounding of emergent sketches.

Result: Scaling training population substantially improves ZMI. Larger populations show increased in-group communicative variation (preventing co-adaptation into homogeneity) while cross-group variation decreases (structural convergence toward universality). This convergence is achieved through perceptual grounding.

Conclusion: ZMI represents a distinct axis of generalization in emergent communication. Scaling populations toward perceptual grounding offers a route toward socially interoperable artificial agents.

Abstract: Generalization in emergent communication has largely focused on novel inputs or linguistic structures, yet the capacity for agents to communicate with strangers from strictly disjoint communities remains relatively unexplored. In this work, we formalize this capability as \textit{zero-shot mutual intelligibility (ZMI)}: successful communication between independently trained populations without prior exposure. Leveraging emergent sketching -- in which agents communicate through sets of drawn strokes -- as a visually grounded modality, we find that scaling the training population substantially improves ZMI across independent groups. Crucially, as we scale the population size, in-group communicative variation increases, preventing co-adaptation into homogeneity. Simultaneously, cross-group variation decreases, indicating a structural convergence toward a certain type of universality. Further analysis reveals that this universality is achieved through perceptual grounding: scaled populations increasingly anchor their emergent sketches on the objective visual resemblance of the target images. Together, these results position ZMI as a distinct axis of generalization in emergent communication and suggest a route toward socially interoperable artificial agents.

</details>


### [294] [NOVA: Symbolic Regression Discovery of Interpretable Car-Following and Lane-Change Models with Driver Heterogeneity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10583)
*Ishak Abassi,Nassim Ali Bouazzouni,Farah Ibelaiden,Nadir Farhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NOVA is an autonomous symbolic regression framework that discovers interpretable driving behavior models (car-following & lane-changing) from raw trajectory data, outperforming baselines while uncovering a dominant nonlinear term linked to psychophysical collision avoidance theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify interpretable car-following and lane-change structures from raw trajectory data with minimal behavioral priors, addressing the need for transparent, theory-grounded driving models rather than black-box approaches.

Method: An autonomous symbolic regression framework with a deterministic Rust-powered search engine that evaluates over 10,000 candidate algebraic structures, using a forward-shifted rolling-mean prediction target and two complementary preprocessing pipelines.

Result: Achieves RMSE=1.376 m/s² (R²=15.57%) on intent-forecasting, outperforming SR-LLM baseline by 0.135 m/s²; 67.4% balanced accuracy on lane-change (surpassing baselines by +29.8 pp); feature operators transfer zero-shot between freeway sites with under 3 pp R² loss.
