Conclusion: NOVA discovers a compact two-term acceleration model with a single dominant nonlinear term as a robust backbone of human car-following, linked to psychophysical collision avoidance theory, demonstrating strong cross-site generalizability and superior lane-change modeling performance.

Abstract: We present NOVA, an autonomous symbolic regression framework that identifies interpretable car-following and lane-change structures from raw trajectory data with minimal behavioral priors. Applied to 4,765,788 active driving observations from the NGSIM I-80 and US-101 datasets, NOVA's deterministic Rust-powered search engine evaluates over 10,000 candidate algebraic structures and identifies a compact two-term acceleration model under a forward-shifted rolling-mean prediction target. Evaluated under two complementary preprocessing pipelines, NOVA achieves $RMSE = 1.376 m/s^2$ ($R^2 = 15.57\%$) on the intent-forecasting benchmark, outperforming the best recalibrated symbolic-regression baseline (SR-LLM, PNAS~2025) by 0.135 m/s$^2$ in RMSE under an identical evaluation protocol. Across eight independent experiments, a single dominant nonlinear term emerges as a robust backbone of human car-following; a residual-guided extension further links the selected structure to an established psychophysical theory of collision avoidance. The discovered feature operators transfer zero-shot between freeway sites with under 3 pp $R^2$ loss. Extended to lane-change modelling within a multinomial logit framework, NOVA achieves 67.4\% balanced accuracy under strict vehicle-ID holdout on 502 unseen drivers, surpassing existing lane-changing baselines by +29.8 percentage points on a three-class problem.

</details>


### [295] [Towards Diverse Scientific Hypothesis Search with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10587)
*Haorui Wang,Parshin Shojaee,Kazem Meidani,Kunyang Sun,José Miguel Hernández-Lobato,Teresa Head-Gordon,Jiajun He,Chandan K. Reddy,Chao Zhang,Yuanqi Du*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a parallel tempering evolutionary framework for hypothesis generation that improves diversity and quality under limited validation budgets, outperforming standard evolutionary search methods across molecular, equation, and algorithm discovery tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard evolutionary search for hypothesis generation suffers from diversity collapse due to optimization pressure, yet scientists need diverse, high-quality alternatives to hedge against noisy and expensive validation.

Method: The authors reformulate hypothesis search as a sampling problem and propose an evolutionary framework inspired by parallel tempering, which searches at multiple temperature levels with principled information exchange across temperatures to balance exploration and convergence.

Result: The approach consistently improves both hypothesis quality and diversity under the same validation budget across molecular discovery, equation discovery, and algorithm discovery domains.

Conclusion: The parallel tempering evolutionary framework effectively addresses diversity collapse in hypothesis generation, producing candidates that remain robust under more expensive downstream computational validations.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are on the rise for accelerating scientific discovery, most recently in advanced tasks such as generating valid scientific hypotheses. Yet in many discovery settings, the goal is not to identify a single best hypothesis since validation can be noisy and expensive, and scientists benefit from a set of high-quality alternative hypotheses that hedge against downstream uncertainty for the best solutions. Nevertheless, commonly used evolutionary search recipes tend to prioritize optimization over exploration in hypothesis generation, and the resulting selection pressure during the search process leads to diversity collapse. Motivated by these limitations, we formulate hypothesis search as a sampling problem, where the objective is to efficiently produce diverse, high-quality hypotheses under a fixed validation budget. Building on this perspective, we propose \ours, an evolutionary framework inspired by the classical parallel tempering algorithm that searches hypotheses at multiple temperature levels and enables principled information exchange across temperatures to improve exploration without disrupting convergence. Across domains including molecular discovery, equation discovery, and algorithm discovery, our approach consistently improves both hypothesis quality and diversity under the same validation budget, and produces candidates that remain robust under more expensive downstream computational validations.

</details>


### [296] [Dirichlet-Guided Group Forecasting for Alleviating Over-smoothing in Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10592)
*Xingyu Zhang,Jingyao Wang,Xin Yu,Zeen Song,Jianqi Zhang,Changwen Zheng,Wenwen Qiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies over-smoothing in time series forecasting as a problem of latent dynamical mode compression, and proposes Dirichlet-Guided Group Forecasting (DGF), a framework that models multiple mode-conditioned distributions using Dirichlet-guided hierarchical sampling and reward-based optimization to produce accurate, diverse, and dynamically consistent forecasts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time series forecasting suffers from over-smoothing, where forecasts capture coarse trends but fail to preserve sharp changes, oscillations, turning points, and regime transitions. The authors motivate this as a problem of latent dynamical mode compression under partial observation and single-realization supervision.

Method: DGF (Dirichlet-Guided Group Forecasting) uses a Dirichlet-guided hierarchical sampling mechanism and reward-based optimization to explicitly model multiple mode-conditioned predictive distributions and uncertainty over selection probabilities.

Result: Experiments on real-world forecasting benchmarks show that DGF reduces over-smoothing while improving forecasting accuracy, diversity, and dynamical consistency.

Conclusion: DGF is an effective framework for mode-preserving forecasting that addresses over-smoothing by preserving multiple plausible future dynamics.

Abstract: Time series forecasting often suffers from over-smoothing, especially when future dynamics are multi-modal. Forecasts may follow the coarse trend of the observed future, but fail to preserve sharp changes, oscillations, turning points, and regime transitions that define plausible dynamic evolution. In this work, we revisit over-smoothing from the perspective of latent dynamical mode compression: under partial observation and single-realization supervision, multiple plausible future modes can be weakened, merged, or averaged during forecasting. Based on this view, we propose Dirichlet-Guided Group Forecasting (DGF), a mode-preserving forecasting framework that explicitly models multiple mode-conditioned predictive distributions and uncertainty over their selection probabilities. DGF uses a Dirichlet-guided hierarchical sampling mechanism and reward-based optimization to encourage forecasts that are accurate, dynamically consistent, and mode-distinct. Extensive experiments on real-world forecasting benchmarks show that DGF reduces over-smoothing while improving forecasting accuracy, diversity, and dynamical consistency.

</details>


### [297] [Embedding Hybrid Systems into Continuous Latent Vector Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10596)
*Sangli Teng,Hang Liu,Koushil Sreenath*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid systems (n-dimensional) can be embedded into continuous vector fields in higher dimensions (m>2n), enabling a Neural ODE approach with consistency loss to learn hybrid system dynamics from time series data, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hybrid systems are intrinsically discontinuous, making them difficult for differentiable optimization. The paper seeks to find a continuous extrinsic representation that is well-posed for such optimization.

Method: (1) Proves an existence theorem that n-dimensional hybrid systems can be embedded into m>2n Euclidean space with a continuous vector field. (2) Builds a latent Neural ODE with consistency loss in both latent and state space to recover hybrid system flow.

Result: Extensive experiments show the proposed method outperforms existing methods in learning hybrid systems with varying geometries from only time series data.

Conclusion: Hybrid systems, despite their discontinuity, can be represented continuously in higher dimensions, and this theoretical insight leads to a practical Neural ODE method that learns hybrid dynamics effectively from data.

Abstract: This work proves that an $n$-dimensional hybrid system can be embedded into an $m$-dimensional Euclidean space equipped with a continuous vector field on its embedded image whenever $m>2n$. This result suggests that an intrinsically discontinuous hybrid system generically admits a continuous extrinsic representation that is well-posed for differentiable optimization. Building on this existence theorem, we show that a latent Neural ODE with consistency loss in both the latent and state space can accurately recover the flow of hybrid systems. Extensive experiments suggest the proposed method outperforms the existing method in learning hybrid systems with varying geometries from only time series data.

</details>


### [298] [Causal Ensemble Agent: Hierarchical Causal Discovery with LLM-guided Expert Reweighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10607)
*Xinyu Li,Yuanyuan Wang,Haoxuan Li,Chuan Zhou,Erdun Gao,Bo Han,Tongliang Liu,Kun Zhang,Howard Bondell,Mingming Gong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CEA is a framework combining statistical causal discovery methods with LLM-based meta-analysis via linear opinion pooling and dynamic reweighting to produce more accurate and robust causal graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Different causal discovery algorithms often produce conflicting results, and existing methods overlook rich domain-specific information (e.g., feature descriptions). LLM-based direct queries are unreliable due to misalignment with data. A more robust aggregation approach is needed.

Method: CEA aggregates structural insights from multiple statistical causal discovery experts via linear opinion pooling across graph levels, and employs an LLM as a meta-referee to dynamically reweight experts when confidence is near decision boundaries.

Result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show CEA achieves the strongest overall performance across a wide range of causal discovery methods.

Conclusion: Using LLMs for meta-analysis in causal discovery is effective, and CEA provides a robust framework for composing improved and more complete causal graphs.

Abstract: Causal discovery aims to uncover causal structures from observational data, which is crucial for real-world decision-making. However, different causal discovery algorithms can produce divergent results that conflict with each other, complicating the identification of accurate causal graphs. Traditional approaches rely on numerical values and statistical assumptions, often ignoring rich domain-specific information, such as feature descriptions, which could also help structure learning. While recent works explore using Large Language Models (LLMs) to infer causal relations via direct queries, such methods can be unreliable due to a lack of alignment with the actual data. To address these limitations, we propose Causal Ensemble Agent (CEA), a novel framework that aggregates structural insights from statistical discovery experts across different graph levels via linear opinion pooling, and uses an LLM as a meta-referee to dynamically reweight experts when the aggregated confidence is close to the decision boundary, thereby composing an improved and more complete causal graph. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that CEA achieves the strongest overall performance across a wide range of causal discovery methods, highlighting the effectiveness of using LLMs for meta-analysis in causal discovery.

</details>


### [299] [Fast and Highly Expressive Policy Learning for Offline Reinforcement Learning via Bootstrapped Flow Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10613)
*Thanh Nguyen,Tri Ton,Hongbin Choe,Tung M. Luu,Chang D. Yoo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BFQ replaces multi-step diffusion denoising with single-step action generation via bootstrapped flow matching, achieving faster, simpler, and more robust offline RL without auxiliary networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based Q-learning is computationally expensive and brittle due to multi-step denoising. Existing acceleration methods introduce auxiliary networks or multi-phase training, compromising simplicity and stability.

Method: BFQ adopts a divide-and-conquer view of displacement vectors along the flow path: learns short-range displacements from Flow Matching marginal velocity, then bootstraps these components to learn a direct noise-to-action mapping in a single step.

Result: Extensive D4RL evaluations show BFQ improves performance while significantly reducing computational cost compared to multi-step diffusion baselines.

Conclusion: Single-step action generation suffices for high-performance offline RL, eliminating the need for multi-step denoising or auxiliary components.

Abstract: Diffusion-based Q-learning has emerged as a powerful paradigm for offline reinforcement learning, but its reliance on multi-step denoising makes both training and inference computationally expensive and brittle. Recent efforts to accelerate diffusion Q-learning toward single-step action generation typically introduce auxiliary networks, policy distillation, or multi-phase training, which frequently compromise simplicity, stability, or performance. To address these limitations, we introduce Bootstrapped Flow Q-Learning (BFQ), a novel framework that enables accurate single-step action generation during both training and inference, without auxiliary networks or distillation procedures. BFQ adopts a divide-and-conquer view of the displacement vector along the flow path: it begins by learning short-range displacements that can be accurately estimated from the Flow Matching marginal velocity, and bootstraps these components to directly learn a noise-to-action mapping in a single step. This formulation eliminates multi-step denoising, resulting in a learning procedure that is substantially faster, simpler, and more robust. Extensive D4RL evaluations show that BFQ improves performance while significantly reducing computational cost compared to multi-step diffusion baselines, demonstrating that single-step action generation suffices for high-performance offline Reinforcement Learning.

</details>


### [300] [Is Fairness Truly Fair? Towards Reliable Lipschitz Fairness in Multi-Task Learning via Fixed-\texorpdfstring{$δ$}{delta} Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10632)
*Junbo Ding,Xin Zang,Chenchen Pan,Donghao Song,Jiaxin Zhu,Danhuai Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies threshold confounding in evaluating Lipschitz-style individual fairness in multi-task learning, proposes ReLiF—a reliability-aware framework with fixed-δ auditing and controlled regularization—and shows through experiments that fixed-δ auditing reveals genuine utility-fairness trade-offs obscured by method-dependent thresholds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation of Lipschitz individual fairness in MTL suffers from threshold confounding: different algorithms are compared under different semantic thresholds because auditing tolerances are derived from each model's own representation distances, making fairness comparisons unreliable.

Method: Proposes ReLiF, which separates evaluation-time fixed-δ auditing from training-time controlled regularization. It uses a shared reference tolerance for comparable auditing and a violation-rate feedback controller to balance Lipschitz regularization. Includes supporting analysis for threshold drift, reference-tolerance selection, and the relationship between huberized and unsmoothed surrogates.

Result: On NYUv2 (ResNet50), ReLiF achieves competitive utility with substantially reduced aligned bias under shared fixed thresholds. On clinical benchmarks, ReLiF yields controlled fairness-regularized trade-offs, while fixed-δ auditing reveals task-balancing baselines can achieve lower bias and that genuine utility-fairness trade-offs persist.

Conclusion: Fixed-δ auditing is a semantically consistent protocol for evaluating Lipschitz fairness in MTL, and threshold-dependent evaluations can obscure real trade-offs that fixed-threshold auditing exposes.

Abstract: Lipschitz-style individual fairness formalizes the idea that semantically similar examples should receive similar predictions, but its evaluation in multi-task learning (MTL) can be confounded by method-induced representation scales. This paper identifies threshold confounding: when the auditing tolerance is derived from each model's own representation distances, different algorithms are compared under different semantic thresholds. A threshold-drift analysis further shows how Bias rankings can change and identifies sufficient conditions for ranking preservation.
  We propose \textbf{ReLiF}, a reliability-aware framework that separates evaluation-time fixed-$δ$ auditing from training-time controlled regularization. ReLiF uses a shared reference tolerance for comparable auditing and a violation-rate feedback controller to keep the Lipschitz surrogate active without letting it dominate stochastic training. This work also develops supporting analysis for threshold drift, reference-tolerance selection, and the relationship between the huberized training surrogate and its unsmoothed positive-margin counterpart.
  Experiments on clinical time-series benchmarks and NYUv2 (NYU Depth V2) dense prediction show that fixed-$δ$ auditing exposes utility--fairness trade-offs that method-dependent thresholds can obscure. On NYUv2 with a ResNet50 backbone, ReLiF achieves competitive utility while substantially reducing aligned bias under shared fixed thresholds. On clinical benchmarks, ReLiF yields controlled fairness-regularized trade-offs, while fixed-$δ$ auditing reveals that task-balancing baselines can sometimes achieve lower bias and that genuine utility--fairness trade-offs persist. These results support fixed-$δ$ auditing as a semantically consistent protocol for evaluating Lipschitz fairness in MTL.

</details>


### [301] [PhysMetrics.Weather: An Evaluation Framework for Physical Consistency in ML Weather Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10642)
*Emma Kasteleyn,Timo Maier,Axel Lauer,Veronika Eyring,Pierre Gentine,Ana Lucic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PhysMetrics.Weather is an evaluation framework that assesses physical realism of MLWP models using conservation, spectral, and dynamical metrics, beyond standard pixel-wise error metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLWP models lack guarantees of physical law consistency because they are purely data-driven and evaluated only with pixel-wise error metrics like RMSE.

Method: The framework evaluates physical realism across three metric types: conservation (physical law adherence), spectral (frequency-domain patterns), and dynamical (temporal evolution consistency).

Result: Provides a tool to quantify physical realism, guiding development of physics-informed architectures and assessing operational reliability of MLWP models.

Conclusion: PhysMetrics.Weather helps bridge the gap between data-driven MLWP performance and physical law consistency for reliable operational use.

Abstract: Machine learning weather prediction (MLWP) models have achieved impressive forecasting performance at a small fraction of the computational costs required for traditional physics-based methods. However, they are primarily (1) data-driven and (2) evaluated using pixel-wide error metrics (e.g., RMSE), so there are no guarantees that their forecasts are consistent with known physical laws. We introduce PhysMetrics.Weather, an evaluation framework that assesses the physical realism of MLWP models across three types of metrics: conservation, spectral, and dynamical. By quantifying physical realism, this tool guides the development of physics-informed architectures and helps evaluate whether MLWP models are reliable for operational use. Our framework is available on Github at https://github.com/Emmakast/PhysMetrics.Weather.

</details>


### [302] [How Does Reasoning Flow? Tracing Attention-Induced Information Flow for Targeted RL in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10646)
*Zhichen Dong,Yang Li,Yuhan Sun,Weixun Wang,Yijia Luo,Zinian Peng,Taiheng Ye,Chao Yang,Wenbo Su,Yu Cheng,Bo Zheng,Junchi Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlowTracer is an RL framework for LLMs that assigns token-level credit by tracing information flow through attention-weighted DAGs, reweighting edges to isolate answer-reachable influence, and using flow throughput to score tokens for targeted reward shaping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Token-level credit assignment is a key obstacle in RL for LLMs—existing methods treat all tokens equally or use point-wise heuristics that ignore the global structure of information propagation.

Method: FlowTracer builds an attention-induced DAG where nodes are tokens and edge capacities come from aggregated attention weights. It reweights edges to retain only answer-reachable influence, enforces local flow conservation, extracts an information-flow backbone from question to answer, and scores tokens by flow throughput to derive token-level rewards.

Result: Consistent performance gains across a range of reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: FlowTracer's global, flow-based credit assignment enables learning signals to focus precisely on tokens that route information toward (or away from) correct answers, outperforming existing token-level RL methods.

Abstract: Token-level credit assignment remains a key obstacle for reinforcement learning (RL) in large language models (LLMs), where RL recipes typically treat all tokens equally, failing to distinguish decisive reasoning steps from routine formatting or fluent filler. Recent attempts leverage model-internal signals to assign finer-grained credit, but these are often point-wise heuristics that ignore the global structure of information propagation. We propose FlowTracer, an RL framework that traces answer-targeted reasoning flow on an attention-induced directed acyclic graph in which nodes correspond to tokens and edge capacities come from aggregated attention weights and derives token credit from this global structure. The edge capacities are reweighted to retain only the influence that can reach the answer region, while enforcing local flow conservation so intermediate tokens neither lose nor gain effective mass due to path length or irrelevant branches. On this graph, FlowTracer extracts an information-flow backbone connecting the question to the answer and scores tokens by flow throughput, revealing high-impact hubs and aggregation checkpoints that mediate long-range dependencies. These derived importances are used to shape token-level rewards, enabling learning signals to focus precisely on the tokens that route information toward (or away from) correct answers and delivering consistent performance gains across a range of reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [303] [In Defense of Information Leakage in Concept-based Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10669)
*Mateo Espinosa Zarlenga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper challenges the conventional view that concept leakage in concept-based models is always undesirable, arguing that in real-world settings with incomplete concepts, some leakage (termed "benign leakage") is actually necessary for accuracy and intervenability, and proposes a reframed training objective to exploit it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors identify that the traditional narrative against concept leakage in CMs is ill-posed (evidence of harm is inconclusive) and leads to impractical models under real-world constraints where concept incompleteness is common.

Method: The paper reframes the typical CM training objective to encourage and exploit benign leakage - a form of concept-irrelevant information that is actually beneficial for model accuracy and intervenability without sacrificing interpretability.

Result: Shows that by optimizing the reframed training objective, CMs can leverage benign leakage to maintain both accuracy and intervenability under real-world concept incompleteness.

Conclusion: The conventional view of leakage in concept-based models is too strict; benign leakage exists and should be exploited rather than eradicated to build practical, accurate, and intervenable models in real-world settings.

Abstract: Concept-based models (CMs), deep neural networks that ground their predictions on representations aligned with human-understandable concepts (e.g., "round", "stripes", etc.), have been shown to learn representations that leak concept-irrelevant information. As the traditional narrative goes, this leakage is undesirable and should be eradicated as it leads to uninterpretable models. In this paper, we posit that this conventional view of leakage in CMs is not only ill-posed, as the evidence of how leakage makes a model less interpretable is often inconclusive, but also bound to lead to impractical CMs under common real-world constraints. Specifically, we argue that in real-world settings where concept incompleteness is the norm, some leakage is often necessary for constructing accurate and intervenable CMs. To this end, we propose that there is such a thing as benign leakage and show that, by optimizing a reframing of the typical CM training objective, CMs can encourage and exploit this form of leakage without sacrificing accuracy or intervenability.

</details>


### [304] [One Step Closer to Ground Truth: A Multi-Scale Residual-Aware Representation Learning Pipeline for Predicting Time Series Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10678)
*Amrijit Biswas,Mustafa Kamal,Robin Krambroeckers,M. M. Lutfe Elahi,Sifat Momen,Nabeel Mohammed,Shafin Rahman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a two-stage, model-agnostic framework for time-series forecasting that decouples base prediction and residual learning. A base Transformer generates initial forecasts, then a meta-corrector models structured error patterns across multivariate channels to refine systematic biases, achieving state-of-the-art performance on eight benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-stage transformer forecasting models suffer from persistent systematic residual biases due to structural discrepancies, unmodeled stochastic components, and inadequate multi-scale temporal representations. Existing approaches treat residuals as irreducible noise, preventing adaptive correction of structured error patterns.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) a base Transformer generates initial predictions; (2) a dedicated meta-corrector dynamically models structured error patterns across multivariate channels, preserves cross-variable dependencies, and iteratively refines residual bias. The pipeline is formalized as a hypothesis space expansion, enabling end-to-end learning of complex error dynamics without restrictive assumptions.

Result: Evaluated on eight benchmark datasets with established protocols, the method achieves state-of-the-art performance with significant improvements in MSE and MAE metrics, demonstrating mitigation of systematic biases and enhanced robustness to complex temporal dynamics.

Conclusion: The two-stage framework effectively addresses approximation limitations of single-stage architectures, removes reliance on restrictive assumptions, and advances practical applicability of transformer-based forecasting models.

Abstract: Transformer-based models have emerged as leading paradigms in time-series forecasting in recent years, employing self-attention mechanisms to capture long-range dependencies. Despite their success, these single-stage forecasting architectures exhibit persistent systematic residual biases arising from structural discrepancies, unmodeled stochastic components, or inadequate multi-scale temporal representations. This limitation persists when residuals are treated as irreducible noise, precluding adaptive correction of structured error patterns. To address this limitation, we introduce a two-stage, model-agnostic framework that explicitly decouples forecasting and residual learning into distinct stages of representation learning. A base transformer first generates the initial predictions. Subsequently, a dedicated meta-corrector dynamically models structured error patterns across multivariate channels, preserves cross-variable dependencies, and iteratively refines the residual bias of the base transformer. By formalizing this pipeline as a hypothesis space expansion, our framework addresses approximation limitations inherent in single-stage architectures, removes reliance on restrictive assumptions, and enables end-to-end learning of complex error dynamics. Evaluated on eight popular benchmark datasets using established protocols, our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, with significant improvements in standard metrics (MSE, MAE). The results demonstrate the framework's ability to mitigate systematic biases and enhance robustness to complex temporal dynamics, advancing the practical applicability of transformer-based forecasting models.

</details>


### [305] [PL-KKT-hPINN: Enforcing Nonlinear Equality Constraints on Neural Networks via Piecewise-Linear Projection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10682)
*Fateme Mohammad Mohammadi,Hector Budman,Joshua L. Pulsipher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PL-KKT-hPINN extends KKT-hPINN to enforce nonlinear equality constraints via piecewise-linear projection, achieving accurate surrogate models with low constraint violations for chemical engineering systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PINNs only enforce physical equations as soft constraints during training, failing to guarantee constraint satisfaction at inference. The authors aim to strictly enforce nonlinear equality constraints in neural network outputs.

Method: The framework extends KKT-hPINN by using piecewise-linear projection to enforce nonlinear equality constraints via the KKT conditions, projecting neural network outputs onto the feasible region.

Result: PL-KKT-hPINN preserves predictive accuracy comparable to standard neural networks while achieving substantially lower constraint violations. It also shows improved robustness and lower RMSE in low-data regimes.

Conclusion: PL-KKT-hPINN provides a computationally efficient and physically consistent framework for surrogate modeling of nonlinear chemical engineering systems.

Abstract: While physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have shown strong potential for process modeling, physical equations are only enforced as soft constraints during training, and thus, they do not guarantee constraint satisfaction at inference. We propose a framework, called piecewise-linear Karush--Kuhn--Tucker hard-constrained PINNs (PL-KKT-hPINNs), that strictly enforces nonlinear equality constraints through piecewise-linear projection. This extends the KKT-hPINN framewor, which exactly enforces linear equalities through the Karush--Kuhn--Tucker (KKT) conditions associated with orthogonally projecting neural network outputs onto the constraint feasible region. The method is demonstrated on a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) case study for both one and two inputs. Results show that PL-KKT-hPINN preserves predictive accuracy comparable to that of a standard neural network while achieving substantially lower constraint violations. In addition, the proposed model shows improved robustness in low-data regimes, yielding lower RMSE than the unconstrained neural network for limited training sample sizes. These results demonstrate that PL-KKT-hPINN provides a computationally efficient and physically consistent framework for surrogate modeling of nonlinear chemical engineering systems.

</details>


### [306] [Divide and Cooperate: Role-Decomposed Multi-Agent LLM Training with Cross-Agent Learning Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10684)
*Jaewan Park,Solbee Cho,Jay-Yoon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DAC is a multi-agent framework that divides agentic search into two cooperative subtasks (search and generation), each handled by a dedicated agent with role-specific training signals, achieving strong QA performance without monolithic model fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing language agents couple evidence acquisition and answer generation in a single policy, causing conflicting roles, combinatorial explosion in policy space, and credit assignment problems where search actions may be penalized despite retrieving sufficient evidence.

Method: DAC (Divide and Cooperate) decouples agentic search into two specialized agents: a searcher for evidence retrieval and a generator for answer production. The generator also serves as an evidence sufficiency verifier, abstaining when evidence is insufficient. The abstention signal feeds back into the searcher's reward for structured credit assignment. The searcher uses hard-positive evidence augmentation to expose the generator to diverse evidence environments. Implemented via parameter-efficient LoRA modules on a shared backbone.

Result: DAC achieves strong performance on general and multi-hop QA benchmarks, outperforming prior baselines that rely on full fine-tuning of monolithic models.

Conclusion: Role decomposition with structured cross-agent learning signals improves credit assignment and robustness in multi-step reasoning for knowledge-intensive QA.

Abstract: Modern language agents which perform multi-step reasoning have shown strong performance in knowledge-intensive question answering. However, existing approaches typically couple evidence acquisition and answer generation within a single policy. This forces a single model to play multiple potentially conflicting roles, inducing a combinatorial explosion in the policy space and hindering efficient exploration. It also introduces a credit assignment problem during training: a search action that retrieves sufficient evidence may still be penalized when generation fails, and vice versa. We propose DAC (Divide and Cooperate), a role-decomposed multi-agent training framework that divides agentic search into two cooperative subtasks, each handled by a dedicated agent trained with role-specific learning signals. The generator serves a dual role as both an answer producer and an evidence sufficiency verifier, abstaining when retrieved evidence is insufficient. This abstention signal is incorporated into the search agent's reward, providing structured cross-agent learning signals that improve credit assignment. Conversely, the searcher exposes the generator to diverse and challenging evidence environments by hard-positive evidence augmentation, improving its robustness. Experiments on general and multi-hop QA benchmarks show that DAC, implemented via parameter-efficient LoRA modules over a shared backbone, achieves strong performance against prior baselines that rely on full fine-tuning of monolithic models.

</details>


### [307] [From Observation to Intervention: A Causal Audit of Expert Importance in Mixture-of-Experts Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10703)
*Leonard Engmann,Christian Medeiros Adriano,Holger Giese*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that population-level observational metrics (like routing statistics in MoE models) fail to predict causal expert importance at the token level, with interventional audits across three architectures finding no significant correlation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability methods often conflate associational (rung-1) evidence with interventional (rung-2) conclusions without validation. The paper aims to test whether this inferential leap holds in the specific case of MoE pruning.

Method: The authors conduct token-level interventional audits across three MoE architectures (OLMoE-1B-7B-0924, Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite), comparing observational routing statistics against causal expert importance after multiple-comparison correction, with per-token routing weight control.

Result: No observational metric predicts causal expert importance after Bonferroni correction across 60 metric-layer combinations (effect sizes below Cohen's d=0.17). Only one signal emerged under per-token routing weight control at OLMoE's final layer (d=+0.231, p=0.0013).

Conclusion: Existing MoE pruning methods succeed due to early-layer redundancy making selection criteria interchangeable, not because metrics identify truly dispensable experts. The findings serve as a counterexample to the invalid inference from rung-1 observations to rung-2 interventional claims.

Abstract: Interpretability methods routinely use population-level summary statistics over observed model behaviour to license claims about the effects of targeted interventions on specific computations; in Pearl's terms, they treat rung-1 associational evidence as if it supported rung-2 interventional conclusions, a move whose validity is rarely tested. We examine one concrete instance: the use of routing statistics in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) pruning, where utilization rates, activation norms, and routing weight distributions are treated as predictors of which experts can be removed without functional cost. A token-level interventional audit across three high-redundancy MoE architectures (OLMoE-1B-7B-0924, Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite) finds no observational metric predicts causal expert importance after multiple-comparison correction in any model, with effect sizes below Cohen's $d = 0.17$ across all 60 metric-layer combinations. A per-token routing weight control rules out insufficient power, recovering a single Bonferroni-significant signal at OLMoE's final MoE layer ($d = +0.231$, $p = 0.0013$). Existing pruning methods succeed in this regime not by identifying dispensable experts but because early-layer redundancy renders most selection criteria interchangeable. Our results provide an explicit counterexample to the common inferential step from population-level observational summaries to token-level interventional claims about expert importance, and illustrate how interventional audits can calibrate the evidential standards for interpretability claims.

</details>


### [308] [Event-Driven Reinforcement Learning Enables Long-Horizon Control in Semiconductor Fabrication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10705)
*Yavar Yeganeh,Mahsa Shekari,Nicla Frigerio,Daniele Pagano,Andrea Matta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning framework for multi-objective policy optimization in semiconductor manufacturing, using a centralized-agent approach with event-driven temporal-difference learning. Experiments across offline and online settings show significant improvements in throughput and utilization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Semiconductor manufacturing systems are stochastic, highly constrained environments where heterogeneous wafers traverse hundreds of processing steps across extensive equipment networks, creating complex, high-dimensional decision problems with delayed feedback and long-horizon requirements that complicate production planning and control.

Method: The authors formulate control as a centralized-agent problem with a core policy coordinating system-wide decisions, represent system evolution as an interconnected temporal process driven by discrete events, and develop a tailored event-driven temporal-difference formulation that integrates with various policy optimization methods under different training settings.

Result: In high-fidelity simulations of diverse industry-real operating scenarios, agents trained in both offline and online settings show significant and consistent gains in throughput and utilization across extensive validation experiments.

Conclusion: The results support the scalability, generality, and transferability of the proposed framework for controlling event-driven complex adaptive systems in semiconductor manufacturing.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning promises to optimize sequential decisions in large-scale systems. Semiconductor manufacturing systems are stochastic and highly constrained environments where heterogeneous wafers traverse hundreds of processing steps across extensive equipment networks. These characteristics yield complex, high-dimensional decision problems with delayed feedback and long-horizon requirements, complicating production planning and control. We propose a deep reinforcement learning framework for multi-objective policy optimization at this scale. Specifically, we formulate control as a centralized-agent problem, where a core policy coordinates system-wide decisions, while system evolution is represented as an interconnected temporal process driven by discrete events. Accordingly, we develop a tailored event-driven temporal-difference formulation that remains general and can be integrated with various policy optimization methods under relevant training settings. We investigate several core model-free algorithms incorporated into this framework and evaluate their effectiveness using high-fidelity simulations of diverse, industry-real operating scenarios. Across extensive validation experiments, agents trained in both offline and online settings show significant and consistent gains in throughput and utilization. We further evaluate performance and generalization across training phases, clarifying the relative strengths of alternative reinforcement learning formulations and algorithms. Overall, the results support the scalability, generality, and transferability of the proposed framework for controlling event-driven complex adaptive systems.

</details>


### [309] [Unifying Data, Memory, and Compute Efficiency in LLM training: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10706)
*Vanessa Schmidt,Huy Hoang Nguyen,Cédric Jung,Shirin Salehi,Anke Schmeink*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This survey systematically reviews LLM efficiency through three coupled bottlenecks—data efficiency, memory efficiency, and compute budget awareness—arguing that optimal strategies depend on specific resource constraints rather than universal solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resource constraints increasingly limit what can be trained, fine-tuned, and deployed in LLMs, yet efficiency is typically studied through isolated techniques rather than as an interacting system of constraints. A unified, constraint-centric perspective is needed.

Method: The survey organizes recent progress around three coupled bottlenecks: (1) data efficiency (selection and pruning methods maximizing learning per token), (2) memory efficiency (reducing weight storage, optimizer states, and activation memory jointly), and (3) compute budget awareness (optimal allocation, stopping rules, and adaptive inference under finite FLOP budgets).

Result: Evidence shows that optimal data subsets depend on task objective and resource budget, not universal quality. GPU memory is often the dominant bottleneck in fine-tuning, not raw compute. Compute-optimal stopping rules can halt or reallocate computation when marginal gains fall below a budget-dependent threshold.

Conclusion: The findings unify compute-aware data selection, scaling laws, and adaptive inference under a common principle of resource-conditioned decision-making, advocating for joint optimization across all three bottlenecks.

Abstract: Resource constraints increasingly determine what can be trained, fine-tuned, and deployed in large language models (LLMs), yet efficiency is often studied through isolated techniques rather than as an interacting system of limits. This survey adopts a constraint-centric perspective and organizes recent progress around three coupled bottlenecks: data efficiency (what to train on), memory efficiency (how to fit training), and compute budget awareness (when and where to spend FLOPs). On the data axis, we review selection and pruning methods that maximize learning per token, ranging from scalable proxy signals based on learning dynamics to gradient- and influence-based scoring, as well as difficulty-aware and curriculum-style strategies. We highlight emerging evidence that different notions of good data dominate in different regimes, implying that optimal subsets depend on the task objective and resource budget rather than being universal. On the systems side, we show that GPU memory, not raw compute, is often the dominant bottleneck in fine-tuning, and that effective scaling requires jointly reducing weight storage, optimizer states, and activation memory rather than optimizing any single component in isolation. Beyond memory, we frame training and inference as compute-governed processes in which optimization, data selection, and decoding must explicitly account for finite FLOP budgets. We review evidence for compute-optimal allocation and stopping rules, where computation should be halted or reallocated once marginal performance gains fall below a budget-dependent threshold. Together, these results unify compute-aware data selection, scaling laws, and adaptive inference under a common principle of resource-conditioned decision-making.

</details>


### [310] [Transformer Based Model for Spatiotemporal Feature Learning in EEG Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10718)
*Xinglong Cui,Dian Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EEG-TransNet is a deep learning architecture for EEG classification that combines ResNet preprocessing with wavelet denoising, a Local Self-Attention Block for regional features, and a Fuzzy-Attention Synchronous Transformer (FAST) for spatiotemporal modeling, achieving state-of-the-art results across three EEG datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance analysis of complex EEG data by capturing temporal, regional, and synchronous features simultaneously, addressing limitations of existing methods that fail to model all three aspects effectively.

Method: Three-module architecture: (1) ResNet + wavelet denoising for preprocessing/feature extraction, (2) Local Self-Attention Block for regional feature learning, (3) Fuzzy-Attention Synchronous Transformer (FAST) for spatiotemporal dependencies. Decoder uses depthwise separable convolutions for efficiency.

Result: Outperforms baselines on BETA, SEED, and DepEEG datasets in classification accuracy and robustness. Ablation studies confirm Local Self-Attention Block's contribution. Depthwise separable convolutions reduce complexity without sacrificing accuracy. Generalizes well across subjects with minimal performance variation.

Conclusion: EEG-TransNet is a robust and generalizable tool for EEG-based brain activity classification and emotion recognition, effectively capturing multi-dimensional EEG features with computational efficiency.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a widely adopted technique for monitoring brain activity, offering valuable insights into neurological states due to its high temporal resolution and cost-effectiveness. To enhance the analysis of complex EEG data, we propose EEG-TransNet, an architecture designed to capture temporal, regional, and synchronous features of EEG signals. EEG-TransNet introduces three key modules: 1) a preprocessing and feature extraction module leveraging ResNet and wavelet-based denoising, 2) a Local Self-Attention Block for regional feature learning, and 3) a Fuzzy-Attention Synchronous Transformer (FAST) to model spatiotemporal dependencies. Through extensive experiments on three EEG datasets (BETA, SEED, and DepEEG), the proposed model consistently outperforms other methods in terms of classification accuracy and robustness across varying signal lengths. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of the Local Self-Attention Block in improving performance, and the inclusion of depthwise separable convolutions in the decoder reduces computational complexity while maintaining high accuracy. EEG-TransNet's ability to generalize across subjects with minimal performance variation highlights its potential as a robust tool for EEG-based brain activity classification and emotion recognition tasks.

</details>


### [311] [Pre-AF 13: An Interpretable Atrial Fibrillation Risk Score Mined from Discharge Reports](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10725)
*Olga Shakhmatova,Dmitrii Kriukov,Daniil Larionov,Nikita Khromov,Iaroslav Bespalov,Alexander Zolotarev,Kirill Grishchenkov,Ekaterina Ivanova,Miron Kuznetsov,Ilya Sochenkov,Elizaveta Panchenko,Artem Shelmanov,Dmitry V. Dylov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML models using EHR data (Pre-AF 13 and Pre-AF 9) predict 24-month AF risk in CVD patients, outperforming established clinical scores (CHARGE-AF, C2HEST, MHS, HAVOC) with ROC AUC up to 0.735.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AF risk scores rely on ubiquitous CVD risk factors (age, hypertension) and focus on long-term (5-10 year) prediction, offering limited stratification for high-risk CVD patients. Medium-term prediction tools are needed.

Method: Single-center retrospective study of 17,562 CVD patients without pre-existing AF. Custom NLP pipeline extracted 73 features from unstructured discharge reports (rule-based + transformer NER). Built interpretable ML models (LightAutoML) - full model, simple model (Pre-AF 13), linear risk score (Pre-AF 9). Evaluated by ROC AUC, compared with 4 clinical risk scores, interpreted via SHAP.

Result: Full model: ROC AUC 0.735 (24-month), 0.696 (entire follow-up). Simple model nearly identical (0.725, 0.696). All non-linear models outperformed clinical scores (ROC AUC 0.53-0.64). Pre-AF 9 stratified AF incidence from ~7% to 36%.

Conclusion: Interpretable ML models built from routinely collected EHR data identify high-AF-risk CVD patients, outperforming established clinical risk scores for medium-term prediction.

Abstract: Background. Atrial fibrillation (AF) is the most prevalent cardiac arrhythmia and a major determinant of prognosis. Established AF risk scores rely on factors (older age, hypertension) nearly ubiquitous among patients with cardiovascular disease (CVD), offering limited stratification in this high-risk group. Most target long-term (5-10 year) rather than medium-term prediction. We developed interpretable ML models predicting AF risk over a 24-month and entire follow-up horizon in CVD patients using routinely collected hospital data.
  Methods. Single-center retrospective study of electronic health records from the National Research Cardiology Center (Russia) for patients aged >=18 with CVD but without pre-existing AF, hospitalized more than once between January 2012 and May 2019. A custom NLP pipeline transformed unstructured discharge reports into 73 structured features, combining a rule-based parser with transformer-based NER. Using LightAutoML we built a full model (73 features), a simple model (reduced subset), and a linear model for a bedside risk score. Performance was assessed by ROC AUC, compared with CHARGE-AF, C2HEST, MHS, and HAVOC, and interpreted via SHAP.
  Results. Of 80,576 records from 45,000 patients, 17,562 met inclusion criteria; 1,438 (8.19%) developed AF. The full model reached ROC AUC 0.735 (24-month) and 0.696 (entire follow-up); the simple model was nearly identical (0.725, 0.696). All non-linear models outperformed the four clinical risk scores (ROC AUC 0.53-0.64). The simple model uses 13 features and is named Pre-AF 13. SHAP identified age and left atrial volume as dominant predictors. A linear risk score (Pre-AF 9) stratified observed 24-month AF incidence from ~7% to 36%.
  Conclusion. Interpretable ML models built from routinely collected EHR data identify high-AF-risk CVD patients, outperforming established clinical risk scores.

</details>


### [312] [SPACR: Single-Pass Adaptive Training of Uncertainty-Aware Conformal Regressors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10734)
*Soundouss Messoudi,Sylvain Rousseau,Sébastien Destercke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPACR is a new method that trains uncertainty-aware regressors end-to-end to directly produce efficient and valid prediction intervals at multiple confidence levels in a single pass, outperforming standard CP and DOICR in both efficiency and computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Conformal Prediction is applied post hoc, misaligning model training with the goal of producing efficient (narrow) intervals. Existing methods like DOICR require batch-splitting or predefined confidence levels, leading to costly retraining.

Method: SPACR (Single-Pass Adaptive Conformal Regressor) integrates a differentiable loss that jointly optimizes efficiency and validity during training, without needing batch-splitting or predefined confidence levels. A single model can output valid prediction intervals at multiple confidence levels during inference.

Result: Experiments on diverse datasets show SPACR consistently produces tighter intervals and better coverage-efficiency trade-offs than standard CP and DOICR, while significantly reducing computational costs.

Conclusion: SPACR effectively addresses the misalignment between CP and model training by enabling end-to-end learning of uncertainty-aware regressors that are both efficient and valid across multiple confidence levels with lower computational overhead.

Abstract: Conformal Prediction (CP) provides robust uncertainty guarantees for predictive models, but is typically applied post hoc, which misaligns model training with the conformal goal of producing efficient (i.e, narrow) intervals. We propose SPACR (Single-Pass Adaptive Conformal Regressor), a novel method for directly training uncertainty-aware regressors within a differentiable loss. SPACR jointly optimizes efficiency and validity without batch-splitting or a predefined confidence levels during training. As a result, a single SPACR model yields valid prediction intervals at multiple confidence levels during inference, avoiding the costly retraining required by methods like DOICR. Experiments on diverse datasets show that SPACR consistently gives tighter intervals and better coverage-efficiency trade-offs compared to standard CP and DOICR, while significantly reducing computational costs.

</details>


### [313] [N-GRPO: Embedding-Level Neighbor Mixing for Enhanced Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10768)
*Xukun Zhu,Hang Yu,Peng Di,Linchao Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: N-GRPO introduces Semantic Neighbor Mixing into the GRPO framework to generate diverse yet semantically consistent solution paths for mathematical reasoning, outperforming token-level and embedding-level methods on math benchmarks and showing robust generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current rollout techniques in LLM mathematical reasoning face a trade-off: token-level sampling produces redundant trajectories (only rephrasing differences), while embedding-level random noise disrupts semantic consistency. A method is needed that injects diversity without breaking semantic validity.

Method: N-GRPO integrates Semantic Neighbor Mixing into the GRPO framework. Instead of token-level sampling or native noise injection, it mixes embeddings of an anchor token with its nearest semantic neighbors to construct diverse yet semantically faithful input representations during rollout.

Result: Evaluations on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen models (various sizes) show consistent improvements over strong baselines on math reasoning benchmarks and robust generalization on out-of-distribution tasks.

Conclusion: N-GRPO effectively resolves the diversity-consistency trade-off in rollout strategies, providing a principled approach for enhancing mathematical reasoning in LLMs without sacrificing semantic integrity.

Abstract: The success of Large Language Models in mathematical reasoning relies heavily on the generation of diverse and valid solution paths during the rollout phase. However, current rollout techniques face a fundamental trade-off: token-level sampling often yields redundant trajectories that differ only in rephrasing, while embedding-level methods utilizing random noise frequently disrupt semantic consistency. To resolve this, we introduce N-GRPO, a novel exploration strategy integrated into the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework. Rather than relying on token-level sampling or native embedding-level noise, our approach leverages Semantic Neighbor Mixing. This mechanism dynamically constructs input representations by mixing the embeddings of an anchor token and its nearest semantic neighbors, thereby injecting diversity while strictly adhering to the local semantic manifold. Experimental evaluations on the DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen models across different sizes show that N-GRPO not only achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines on math reasoning benchmarks but also exhibits robust generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks.

</details>


### [314] [Inverse Probability Weighting and Age-of-Information Aggregation for Decentralized Federated Learning under Partial Reception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10774)
*Chanuka A. S. Hewa Kaluannakkage,Rajkumar Buyya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DFL-AA proposes a decentralized federated learning method that corrects selection bias and mitigates update staleness over lossy wireless networks using Adaptive AoI-weighted Aggregation with Inverse Probability Weighting and EWMA-based channel estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decentralized Federated Learning over lossy wireless networks suffers from selection bias (underrepresentation of updates from poor-quality links) and update staleness (outdated information from asynchronous nodes), which degrade model performance.

Method: DFL-AA combines Inverse Probability Weighting with online EWMA-based channel estimation to correct selection bias, and uses Age-of-Information-based weighting to mitigate staleness without requiring global synchronization.

Result: Theoretically proves removal of link-quality distortion in expectation; experimentally demonstrates consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines across varying loss rates, network sizes, and heterogeneous wireless conditions.

Conclusion: DFL-AA effectively addresses both selection bias and update staleness in decentralized federated learning over lossy wireless networks, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) over lossy wireless networks faces two key challenges: selection bias, where updates from poor-quality links are systematically underrepresented due to partial model reception, and update staleness, where asynchronous nodes contribute outdated information. We show that uniform gossip aggregation with local-fill reconstruction introduces persistent link-quality-induced bias, while completeness-based weighting further amplifies this effect. To address these challenges, we propose DFL-AA (Decentralized Federated Learning with Adaptive AoI-weighted Aggregation), which combines Inverse Probability Weighting with online EWMA-based channel estimation to correct selection bias and Age-of-Information-based weighting to mitigate staleness without requiring global synchronization. We theoretically show that DFL-AA removes link-quality distortion in expectation and experimentally demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines across varying loss rates, network sizes, and heterogeneous wireless conditions.

</details>


### [315] [Can we trust our models? Epistemic calibration in second-order classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10777)
*Arthur Hoarau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces epistemic calibration, a new criterion for evaluating whether epistemic uncertainty estimates in second-order classification models are trustworthy, which is strictly stronger than classical calibration. They propose the Expected Epistemic Calibration Error (EECE) as a consistent estimator and demonstrate through experiments that this metric reveals meaningful differences across uncertainty quantification methods that classical calibration misses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical calibration only assesses reliability of predicted probabilities but fails to evaluate whether epistemic uncertainty estimates themselves are trustworthy, which is critical for high-stakes deployments of machine learning models.

Method: The authors define epistemic calibration as a criterion measuring whether reported epistemic uncertainty reflects the dispersion of model predictions around ground truth. They propose EECE as a consistent estimator of True Epistemic Calibration Error (TECE) and relate their work to an impossibility theorem under the epistemic calibration hypothesis.

Result: Epistemic calibration is a strictly stronger notion than classical calibration and captures failure modes invisible to standard metrics. Experiments across various UQ methods show substantial differences in epistemic calibration despite similar predictive performance.

Conclusion: Epistemic calibration is a coherent and meaningful criterion for evaluating uncertainty quantification methods, revealing insights that classical calibration cannot provide.

Abstract: Uncertainty estimation is critical for deploying machine learning models in high-stakes settings. However, classical calibration only assesses the reliability of predicted probabilities and does not evaluate whether epistemic uncertainty estimates are themselves trustworthy. This limitation is particularly relevant for second-order classification models. We introduce epistemic calibration, a principled criterion that measures whether reported epistemic uncertainty faithfully reflects the dispersion of model predictions around the ground truth. We show that epistemic calibration is a strictly stronger notion than classical calibration and captures failure modes invisible to standard metrics. We relate this work to the existing literature through an impossibility theorem that holds under the epistemic calibration hypothesis. To operationalize this concept, we propose the Expected Epistemic Calibration Error (EECE), which we prove to be a consistent estimator of a True Epistemic Calibration Error (TECE). Experiments across a broad range of uncertainty quantification methods show that epistemic calibration is a coherent and meaningful criterion and reveal substantial differences across methods, despite similar predictive performance.

</details>


### [316] [Closing the Modality Gap in Zero-Shot HAR: Contrastive Training and Separability-Optimized Prototypes on IMU Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10789)
*Anik Ghosh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper systematically evaluates zero-shot learning for IMU-based human activity recognition, finding that the modality gap is a training-time phenomenon. Using richer text descriptions (discriminative activity descriptions) instead of simple label names substantially improves sensor-to-text alignment (from 0.30 to 0.69 cosine similarity). The best configuration achieves 73.2% accuracy and 0.583 macro F1 on unseen classes. The paper also cautions that richer text can reduce prototype separability and recommends macro F1 over accuracy for imbalanced ZSL-HAR benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the central challenge of bridging the modality gap between sensor embeddings (from IMU data) and semantic class representations (text prototypes) in zero-shot human activity recognition.

Method: Systematically evaluated 7 configurations combining 3 inference methods with 2 training pipelines on PAMAP2 dataset (14 seen, 4 unseen classes). Used TCN encoder with cross-entropy or contrastive training, comparing label-name Sentence-BERT prototypes vs. discriminative activity descriptions. Evaluated with cosine similarity alignment, accuracy, and macro F1.

Result: Discriminative descriptions improved sensor-to-text alignment from 0.30 to 0.69 cosine similarity. Best result: contrastive training + inverted softmax correction achieved 73.2% accuracy and 0.583 macro F1 on unseen classes, vs. 58.3%/0.34 for label-name baseline. Richer text descriptions reduce inter-prototype separability in Sentence-BERT space.

Conclusion: The modality gap is a training-time phenomenon determined by the encoder objective. Richer semantic descriptions improve alignment but can compress prototype space. Macro F1 is recommended over accuracy as the primary metric for imbalanced ZSL-HAR benchmarks.

Abstract: Zero-shot learning (ZSL) for inertial measurement unit (IMU)-based human activity recognition (HAR) faces a central challenge: bridging the gap between sensor embeddings and semantic class representations. We systematically evaluate seven configurations combining three inference methods with two training pipelines on the PAMAP2 dataset, using 14 seen and 4 unseen activity classes with subjects 108 and 109 held out for testing. We find that the modality gap is a training-time phenomenon governed by the encoder objective. A temporal convolutional network (TCN) trained with cross-entropy over label-name Sentence- BERT prototypes yields sensor embeddings with a mean cosine similarity of 0.30 to the corresponding text prototypes, while replacing the label-name prototype targets with discriminative activity descriptions raises this to 0.69. This alignment improvement transfers consistently across all three inference methods. The strongest result combines contrastive training with inverted softmax correction, achieving 73.2% accuracy and 0.583 macro F1 on unseen classes, compared to 58.3% accuracy and 0.34 macro F1 for the label-name baseline. A secondary finding is that richer text descriptions reduce inter-prototype separability in Sentence-BERT space, because shared biomechanical vocabulary causes the language model to compress the prototype cloud. This effect does not negate the benefits of contrastive alignment provided prototype descriptions retain sufficient discriminative vocabulary. We also demonstrate that overall accuracy is a misleading primary metric when test-set class distributions are imbalanced, and recommend macro-averaged F1 as the standard reporting metric for ZSL-HAR benchmarks.

</details>


### [317] [CITRAS-FM: Tiny Time Series Foundation Model for Covariate-Informed Zero-Shot Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10798)
*Yosuke Yamaguchi,Issei Suemitsu,Yuki Kajihara,Wenpeng Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CITRAS-FM is a tiny 7M-parameter time series foundation model that supports univariate, multivariate, and covariate-informed zero-shot forecasting with real-time CPU inference, using Shifted Attention and CovSynth for covariate-aware pretraining, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy among sub-10M models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TSFMs have high computational costs and limited support for diverse variable types, often failing to account for covariates that exogenously influence target variability.

Method: CITRAS-FM is a patch-based, decoder-only Transformer with Shifted Attention in the cross-variate module for leveraging known covariates, plus CovSynth which synthesizes realistic covariates from decomposed target series components for pretraining.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot accuracy among sub-10M parameter TSFMs on fev-bench (100 tasks), with sub-0.1-second CPU inference.

Conclusion: CITRAS-FM offers a strong balance between forecasting accuracy and real-time deployability, making it suitable for practical CPU-based applications.

Abstract: Pretrained time series foundation models (TSFMs) have enabled zero-shot forecasting on unseen target series. However, existing TSFMs often incur high computational cost and provide limited support for diverse variable types, often failing to account for covariates that exogenously influence target variability. To address these challenges, we propose CITRAS-FM, a tiny 7M-parameter TSFM that supports univariate, multivariate, and covariate-informed zero-shot forecasting with real-time CPU inference. Built on a patch-based, decoder-only Transformer, CITRAS-FM introduces Shifted Attention into the cross-variate module to effectively exploit known covariates accessible throughout the forecast horizon. Moreover, to enable covariate-aware pretraining despite the scarcity of covariate-rich corpora, we propose CovSynth, which synthesizes realistic covariates from decomposed components of target series. Experiments on fev-bench, spanning 100 tasks across various settings, demonstrate that CITRAS-FM achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot accuracy among sub-10M TSFMs while delivering sub-0.1-second CPU inference, offering a strong balance between forecasting accuracy and real-time deployability.

</details>


### [318] [Boosting ECG Classification Performance by Pre-training with Synthesized Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10802)
*Naoki Nonaka,Jun Seita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a knowledge-driven Gaussian-composition synthesis method to generate synthetic ECG data for training DNNs, demonstrating improved classification performance on four abnormal ECG classes, especially when real data is limited.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep Neural Networks require large datasets for effective training, but in the medical domain, acquiring large-scale data is challenging due to privacy concerns and rarity of diseases. The authors aim to address this data scarcity issue.

Method: A knowledge-driven Gaussian-composition synthesis algorithm is developed for single-lead II ECGs, where each heartbeat is represented by Gaussian-shaped P, Q, R, S, and T wave components. Synthetic data is generated for four abnormal ECG classes (AF, AFLT, PVC, WPW). Ten different DNN architectures are used for abnormal ECG classification.

Result: Synthetic-to-real training improves classification performance for three of the four target abnormalities, with the largest architecture-averaged gain of 33.2% for AFLT. The performance enhancement from synthetic data is more pronounced with smaller real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Domain-knowledge-based synthetic ECGs can serve as a useful pre-training resource, particularly in scenarios where real-world data are limited or difficult to obtain.

Abstract: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) typically require extensive datasets for effective training. In the medical domain, acquiring large-scale data is often challenging due to privacy concerns and the rarity of certain diseases. To address this data scarcity, we investigate the efficacy of training DNN models using synthetic data, generated based on domain-specific medical knowledge. Specifically, we develop a knowledge-driven Gaussian-composition synthesis algorithm for single-lead II ECGs, in which each heartbeat is represented by Gaussian-shaped P, Q, R, S, and T wave components. Using this simulator, we generate synthetic data for four abnormal electrocardiogram (ECG) classes: atrial fibrillation (AF), atrial flutter (AFLT), premature ventricular complex (PVC), and Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome (WPW). We evaluate the utility of this synthetic data by conducting abnormal ECG classification using ten different DNN architectures. Our results demonstrate that synthetic-to-real training improves classification performance for three of the four target abnormalities, with the largest architecture-averaged gain of $33.2\%$ observed for AFLT. Further analysis reveals that the performance enhancement from synthetic data is more pronounced with smaller real-world datasets. These findings suggest that domain-knowledge-based synthetic ECGs can serve as a useful pre-training resource, particularly in scenarios where real-world data are limited or difficult to obtain.

</details>


### [319] [K-Forcing: Joint Next-K-Token Decoding via Push-Forward Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10820)
*Zhiwei Tang,Yuanyu He,Yizheng Han,Wangbo Zhao,Jiasheng Tang,Fan Wang,Bohan Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: K-Forcing proposes a push-forward language modeling paradigm that distills an autoregressive model into a joint next-k-token decoder, enabling single-forward-pass generation of multiple tokens and achieving 2.4-3.5x speedup under high-load batch serving with modest quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive language models suffer from sequential token-by-token decoding that is memory-bound and inefficient, especially under high-load batch serving for industrial-scale deployment. Existing acceleration methods like speculative decoding or diffusion models don't directly address this critical scenario.

Method: K-Forcing distills an existing AR teacher model into a conditional push-forward mapping that transforms independent uniform noise variables into a joint sample of multiple future tokens in a single forward pass. It uses progressive self-forcing distillation to gradually expand the prediction window while preserving fixed-length outputs and compatibility with standard AR serving infrastructure.

Result: When configured to generate k=4 tokens per forward pass, K-Forcing achieves approximately 2.4-3.5x speedup across different batch sizes on LM1B and OpenWebText, with modest quality degradation relative to the AR teacher.

Conclusion: K-Forcing offers a promising route toward accelerating AR generation under real-world high-load deployment, addressing the critical bottleneck of inference-dominated compute costs in modern LLMs.

Abstract: Autoregressive (AR) language modeling is the dominant paradigm for text generation, yet its sequential token-by-token decoding makes inference memory-bound and inefficient. Existing acceleration approaches, such as speculative decoding and diffusion language models, can yield speedups under certain conditions but do not directly address high-load batch serving--the scenario most critical for industrial-scale deployment. We introduce K-Forcing, a push-forward language modeling paradigm for joint next-k-token decoding. K-Forcing distills an existing AR model into a conditional push-forward mapping--one that transforms independent uniform noise variables into a joint sample of multiple future tokens in a single forward pass. This design preserves fixed-length outputs, reuses the AR teacher backbone, and remains compatible with standard AR serving infrastructure. We train this mapping via progressive self-forcing distillation, which gradually expands the prediction window while enabling the student to closely match the sequence distribution of the AR teacher. We evaluate K-Forcing on LM1B and OpenWebText using a standard causal Transformer backbone. When aggressively configured to generate k = 4 tokens per forward pass, K-Forcing delivers approximately 2.4-3.5x speedup across different batch sizes, while incurring modest quality degradation relative to its AR teacher. As inference increasingly dominates the lifetime compute cost of modern LLMs, K-Forcing offers a promising route toward accelerating AR generation under real-world high-load deployment.

</details>


### [320] [Encoding the Euler Characteristic Transform](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10824)
*Nello Blaser,Odin Hoff Gardaa,Lars M. Salbu,Elena Xinyi Wang,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a continuous encoding of the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT) using per-vertex tokenization and a transformer, improving classification accuracy on 5/6 benchmarks over traditional discretization, with the encoding choice mattering more than the aggregation architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ECT encoding discretizes each Euler Characteristic Curve (ECC), imposing a fixed inductive bias. The authors aim to replace this with a more flexible, learnable continuous encoding that better preserves geometric information and improves downstream task performance.

Method: A continuous encoding records per-vertex net Euler characteristic change per direction, producing token sequences processed by a small transformer (ECC encoder). Six ECT representation architectures aggregate per-direction vectors, including feedforward, convolutional, and complex-valued models preserving planar rotation equivariance.

Result: On six classification benchmarks (point clouds, graphs, cubical complexes, meshes), the continuous encoding improves accuracy on 5/6 datasets. Control experiments confirm gains come from tokenization, not added capacity. Feedforward networks excel with continuous encoding but underperform with discretization.

Conclusion: The encoding choice dominates performance over representation architecture. Continuous encoding and transformer tokenization provide a superior inductive bias compared to fixed discretization for topological shape analysis with ECT.

Abstract: The Euler Characteristic Curve (ECC) records the Euler characteristic of a linearly embedded cell complex as a function of filtration height in a given direction, and the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT) is the injective shape descriptor obtained by collecting ECCs over many directions. How the ECT is encoded for a neural network is itself an inductive bias, conventionally fixed by discretizing each ECC. We introduce a continuous encoding: for each direction and each vertex it records the net Euler-characteristic change attributed to that vertex, producing a per-direction token sequence that a small transformer maps to a feature vector. We separate the resulting pipeline into two stages on orthogonal axes: an ECC encoder that acts within each direction, mapping its curve to a fixed-length vector, and an ECT representation that acts across directions, aggregating the per-direction vectors into one. We study six ECT representation architectures spanning a range of inductive biases, from a structure-agnostic feedforward baseline to convolutional and complex-valued models that preserve equivariance under planar rotations. Across six classification benchmarks covering point clouds, graphs, cubical complexes, and meshes, the continuous encoding improves accuracy on five of six datasets, and control experiments attribute the gain to the tokenization itself rather than to the added transformer capacity. The representation architecture matters less than the encoding, and the payoff from its inductive biases depends on the encoding: a feedforward network performs best under continuous encoding but is less robust under discretization than convolutional architectures.

</details>


### [321] [MODIP: Efficient Model-Based Optimization for Diffusion Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10825)
*Zakariae El Asri,Philippe Gratias-Quiquandon,Nicolas Thome,Olivier Sigaud*

Main category: cs.LG
