Conclusion: The contribution is a clearer mathematical notation rather than a new capability, making matrix Lie group operations more accessible for estimation problems.

Abstract: Several recent papers have demonstrated the utility of using Lie groups within estimation problems, yielding improved accuracy and consistency. This paper introduces a new tool for describing operations with matrix Lie groups: tensors and the Einstein summation notation. While tensors and Einstein notation are well-known in other research fields, applying this mathematical notation to represent and compute matrix Lie derivatives is novel. More importantly, this new notation greatly clarifies the derivatives and operations necessary to work with matrix Lie Groups in (gradient-based) estimation frameworks. Therefore, the main contribution of this paper is not a new capability, but a more perspicuous mathematical notation for working with matrix Lie groups.

</details>


### [376] [SARM2: Multi-Task Stage Aware Reward Modeling for Self Improving Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10305)
*Qianzhong Chen,Hau Zheng,Justin Yu,Suning Huang,Jiankai Sun,Ken Goldberg,Chuan Wen,Pieter Abbeel,Yide Shentu,Philipp Wu,Mac Schwager*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces RM, a multi-task stage-aware reward model combining action-primitive-based stage estimation with MMoE value head for dense per-step rewards, and SPIRAL, an on-policy reward-guided framework that improves VLA policies from cheap autonomous rollouts, achieving near-perfect performance on long-horizon manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning VLA policies for long-horizon manipulation relies on behavior cloning requiring costly high-quality demonstrations, while existing reward models are either task-specific (accurate but annotation-heavy) or general VLM-based (broad but too coarse for fine-grained progress).

Method: RM combines an action-primitive-based stage estimator with a multi-gate Mixture-of-Experts (MMoE) value head to produce dense per-step rewards. SPIRAL uses RM as a reward signal for on-policy reward-guided learning, improving VLA policies from cheap autonomous rollouts.

Result: RM reduces value-estimation MSE by 80% over strongest baselines. SPIRAL improves Folding Shorts from 58% to 100% and Cleaning Whiteboard from 50% to 90% success rates.

Conclusion: High-quality dense rewards are key to a stable robot data flywheel, enabling near-perfect performance from cheap autonomous rollouts without costly human demonstrations.

Abstract: Fine-tuning vision-language-action (VLA) policies for long-horizon manipulation still relies heavily on behavior cloning, which requires costly high-quality demonstrations and keeps policies near the demonstration distribution. Reward models can reduce this dependence by reweighting demonstrations and providing dense supervision for on-robot reinforcement learning (RL), but they must be dense, accurate, and general. Existing methods fall short: task-specific stage-aware models are accurate but require per-task annotations, while general vision-language-model (VLM) reward models are broadly applicable but too coarse for fine-grained long-horizon progress. We introduce RM, a multi-task stage-aware reward model that combines an action-primitive-based stage estimator with a multi-gate Mixture-of-Experts (MMoE) value head to produce dense per-step rewards across manipulation tasks. Building on RM, we further propose SPIRAL (Self-Policy Improvement via Reward-Aligned Learning), an on-policy reward-guided framework that improves VLA policies from cheap autonomous rollouts. On a 10-task benchmark, RM reduces value-estimation MSE by 80% over the strongest baselines; when used in SPIRAL, it improves task success from around 50% to near-perfect performance on Folding Shorts (58% to 100%) and Cleaning Whiteboard (50% to 90%), showing that high-quality dense rewards are key to a stable robot data flywheel. Project website: https://qianzhong-chen.github.io/sarm2.github.io/.

</details>


### [377] [OMG: Omni-Modal Motion Generation for Generalist Humanoid Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10340)
*Siqiao Huang,Kun-Ying Lee,Dongming Qiao,Guanqi He,Zhenyu Wang,Yitang Li,Shaoting Zhu,Hang Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OMG is an omni-modal whole-body controller for humanoid robots that uses a hierarchical architecture (scalable brain + reactive cerebellum), a data curation pipeline, and diffusion-based motion generation conditioned on language, audio, and reference motions, achieving state-of-the-art performance and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing humanoid control approaches are limited to few-skill policies with heavy reward engineering or motion trackers that are hard to extend to new input modalities. The authors aim to build a general-purpose humanoid control system by mirroring biological motor systems with a hierarchical structure.

Method: The method includes: (1) a meticulous data curation, filtering and labeling pipeline to acquire high-quality training data; (2) a diffusion-based motion generation backbone that conditions on compositional, extensible multi-modal inputs (language, audio, human reference motions); (3) a hierarchical architecture with a scalable reasoning "brain" and a reactive motion tracking "cerebellum".

Result: OMG demonstrates state-of-the-art whole-body control performance, exhibits effective model scaling behavior, and efficiently adapts to new distributions and modalities.

Conclusion: The work represents a concrete step toward foundation models for humanoid robots, showing that multi-modal conditioning and scalable data pipelines enable general-purpose whole-body control.

Abstract: Humanoid whole-body control has made significant progress in recent years, yet existing approaches remain limited to few-skill policies with heavy reward engineering, or motion trackers that are difficult to extend to new input modalities. We argue that the key to general-purpose humanoid control is to build a scalable brain, a module capable of reasoning with diverse conditioning modalities, atop a reactive motion tracking cerebellum, mirroring the hierarchical structure of biological motor systems. Two challenges arise in realizing this vision: acquiring a vast amount of high-quality data to achieve general purpose control, and equipping the generator with the capability to condition on compositional, extensible multi-modal inputs. We present OMG, which addresses these challenges with a meticulous data curation, filtering and labeling pipeline, as well as a diffusion-based motion generation backbone that conditions on language, audio, and human reference motions. Extensive experiments validate OMG as an omni-modal whole-body controller exhibiting state-of-the-art performance, model scaling behavior and efficient adaptation to new distributions and modalities, marking a concrete step toward foundation models for humanoid robots.

</details>


### [378] [Rethinking Embodied Navigation via Relational Inductive Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10348)
*Weitao An,Chenghao Xu,Xu Yang,Cheng Deng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DB-Nav improves object navigation by introducing dual relational biases (Activation and Inhibition) that suppress unreliable semantic cues, outperforming existing methods on success rate and SPL without costly online VLM reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing object navigation methods rely on open-vocabulary detectors or VLMs but overlook unreliable semantic cues (false positives, outdated priors, failed exploration), leading to contaminated mapping and decision-making.

Method: DB-Nav proposes dual relational biases: Activation Bias (propagates contextual evidence) and Inhibition Bias (suppresses unreliable regions via perceptual confusion and action-level falsification), unified into a Relational Activation-Inhibition Exploration Graph that modulates frontier exploration values.

Result: DB-Nav significantly outperforms existing methods in Success Rate (SR) and Success weighted by Path Length (SPL) on ObjectNav benchmarks, while remaining lightweight, interpretable, and robust without costly online VLM reasoning.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that explicitly handling unreliable semantic cues through relational biases effectively reshapes the search space and improves navigation performance.

Abstract: Object navigation requires an agent to locate a target in an unknown environment through visual observations. Existing methods typically rely on open-vocabulary detectors or vision-language models (VLMs) to answer where to search, but often overlook what not to trust - which semantic cues are unreliable. Open-vocabulary perception is prone to systematic misleading evidence: false positives, outdated static priors, and repeated failed exploration due to lack of embodied verification, which contaminates mapping and decision-making. Such errors are rooted in structured object relations in real-world scenes. To address this, we propose DB-Nav, a framework that reshapes the search space via dual relational biases. It factorizes target-centric relations into an Activation Bias (propagates contextual evidence) and an Inhibition Bias (suppresses unreliable regions via perceptual confusion and action-level falsification). These biases are unified into a Relational Activation-Inhibition Exploration Graph that modulates frontier exploration values using online observations and failed accesses. Experiments on ObjectNav benchmarks show that DB-Nav significantly outperforms existing methods in success rate (SR) and Success weighted by Path Length (SPL), offering a lightweight, interpretable, and robust navigation framework without costly online VLM reasoning.

</details>


### [379] [HiMem-WAM: Hierarchical Memory-Gated World Action Models for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10363)
*Xiaoquan Sun,Ruijian Zhang,Chen Cao,Yihan Sun,Jiahui Chen,Zetian Xu,Bo Chen,Haijier Chen,Zhen Yang,Jiarun Zhu,Yijun Hong,JingZhe Xu,Jingrui Pang,Mingqi Yuan,Jiayu Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HiMem-WAM introduces a hierarchical memory-gated World Action Model that improves long-horizon robotic manipulation by jointly learning low-level motion and high-level skill latents with boundary-triggered memory updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing World Action Models struggle with task-relevant memory in long-horizon robotic manipulation tasks.

Method: HiMem-WAM uses a hierarchical latent action framework (low-level motion + high-level skill latents) combined with a boundary-aware memory gate that writes compact task states at predicted skill transitions for causal inference.

Result: Evaluated on LIBERO, LIBERO-PLUS, RMBench, and real-world tasks, showing improved robustness under perturbations and better performance on memory-dependent long-horizon tasks.

Conclusion: Hierarchical latents enhance robustness, and the memory module significantly benefits long-horizon manipulation.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) have emerged as a new powerful paradigm for embodied intelligence, learning action-relevant visual dynamics that significantly enhance generalization and robustness. However, existing WAMs still struggle with task-relevant memory in long-horizon robotic manipulation. To address this, we present HiMem-WAM, a Hierarchical Memory-Gated WAM that integrates motion-centric latent actions, high-level skill latents, and boundary-triggered memory updates. Specifically, we develop a hierarchical latent action framework that jointly learns low-level motion and high-level skill latents, providing structured temporal abstraction. Meanwhile, a boundary-aware memory gate writes compact task states at predicted skill transitions, enabling causal inference without test-time generation of future video or optical flow estimation. Evaluated on LIBERO, LIBERO-PLUS, RMBench and real-world tasks, HiMem-WAM shows that hierarchical latents improve robustness under deployment perturbations, and the memory module substantially benefits memory-dependent long-horizon manipulation.

</details>


### [380] [A Practical Recipe Towards Improving Sim-and-Real Correlation for VLA Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10366)
*Shuo Wang,Hanyuan Xu,Yingdong Hu,Fanqi Lin,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper systematically studies sim-and-real correlation in vision-language-action (VLA) policies, measuring whether simulation preserves real-world policy rankings, performance correlations, and failure patterns. It provides a framework for improving simulation usefulness for VLA policy evaluation and development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simulation is widely used for robot policy evaluation, but existing simulation benchmarks have not been reliably adopted as proxies for real-world policy evaluation. The authors aim to understand why simulation fails to correlate with real-world performance and how to improve this alignment.

Method: Systematic study across multiple simulation platforms, VLA policies, tasks, and perturbation factors, measuring policy ranking consistency, performance correlation, and perturbation-wise failure patterns between simulation and real-world deployment.

Result: Characterizes limitations of existing simulators, identifies simulation signals more aligned with real-world deployment, and establishes when simulator-based finetuning is beneficial and how post-training data quantity affects sim-and-real alignment.

Conclusion: Provides a unified framework for measuring, interpreting, and improving simulation usefulness for VLA policies, offering guidance for simulator designers and practitioners using simulation in the policy development pipeline.

Abstract: Simulation has become an essential tool for evaluating and improving vision-language-action (VLA) policies, offering scalable, reproducible, and controllable alternatives to costly real-world robot evaluation. Recent simulation benchmarks have made substantial progress on realism and diversity, yet these platforms have not been widely adopted as reliable proxies for real-world policy evaluation. In this work, we investigate this issue through the lens of sim-and-real correlation. We conduct a systematic study across multiple simulation platforms, VLA policies, tasks, and perturbation factors, measuring whether simulated evaluation preserves real-world conclusions in terms of policy ranking consistency, performance correlation, and perturbation-wise failure patterns. This analysis allows us to characterize the limitations of existing simulators and identify what kinds of simulation signals are more aligned with real-world deployment. We further examine how users should exploit simulation for policy improvement, including when simulator-based finetuning is beneficial and how the amount of post-training data affects sim-and-real alignment. Overall, our work provides a unified framework for measuring, interpreting, and improving the usefulness of simulation for VLA policies, offering guidance both for simulator designers and for practitioners who use simulation as part of the policy development pipeline.

</details>


### [381] [Test-time Adversarial Takeover: A Real-time Hijacking Interface against Robotic Diffusion Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10371)
*Zi Yin,Peilin Chai,Siyuan Huang,Zhanhao Hu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces TAKO, a test-time adversarial attack that inserts universal visual patches into a robot's camera stream to completely take over diffusion-based action generation policies, achieving 100% takeover success across multiple tasks, encoders, and generative inference methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adversarial attacks on visuomotor policies focus on disruption (reducing task success or causing erratic behavior), but the authors study a stronger threat where an attacker can remotely steer a frozen robot policy like a joystick, highlighting a more severe security vulnerability.

Method: TAKO learns a small set of reusable universal patches via differentiable diffusion inference. At test time, the attacker switches among these patches in the camera stream to compose arbitrary trajectories, exploiting the visual conditioning pathway where induced bias persists through iterative generative inference.

Result: Across 4 tasks, 2 visual encoders, and 3 generative inference families, human operators achieve 100% takeover success on attacker-defined objectives in every evaluated setting. The natural targeted baseline (target-policy matching) fails due to victim policy's inability to self-supervise on out-of-distribution shifts.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based action generation policies are severely vulnerable to visual adversarial takeover, as the perturbation on the visual conditioning pathway persists through iterative inference. This poses a critical security concern for embodied AI systems relying on diffusion-based visuomotor policies.

Abstract: Diffusion-based action generation has become a foundational component of embodied AI, but its reliance on visual conditioning leaves deployed visuomotor policies vulnerable to adversarial manipulation. Most prior attacks focus on disruption: they perturb the observation stream to reduce task success or induce erratic behavior. We study a stronger threat, Test-time Adversarial Takeover (TAKO), in which an attacker obtains a real-time steering interface over a frozen robot policy and turns it into a remotely piloted instrument. TAKO learns a small vocabulary of reusable universal patches through differentiable diffusion inference; at test time, the attacker switches among these patches in the camera stream to compose attacker-chosen trajectories. This works because the perturbation acts on the visual conditioning pathway, where the induced bias can persist through iterative generative inference. We further show that the natural targeted baseline, target-policy matching, fails because the victim policy cannot reliably supervise itself on out-of-distribution target shifts. Across four tasks (2D manipulation, simulated aerial delivery, simulated ground navigation, and physical-world ground navigation), two visual encoders (ResNet-18 and EfficientNet-B0 + Transformer), and three generative inference families (DDPM, DDIM, and flow matching), human operators achieve 100\% takeover success on attacker-defined objectives in every evaluated setting. The project page is available at https://tako-attack.github.io.

</details>


### [382] [UMI-Bench 1.0: An Open and Reproducible Real-World Benchmark for Tabletop Robotic Manipulation with UMI Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10382)
*Shi Jin,Yuntian Wang,Yuhui Duan,Di Wu,Gaoqi Dong,Xiaohang Liu,Xiaotong Li,Hongfei Jia,Zehao Zhang,Tianyu Wang,Zhongjie Jia,Yuanqi Yao,Chenjia Bai,Zhaxizhuoma,Siao Liu,Nieqing Cao,Jin Wang,Chao Yu,Yan Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: UMI-Bench 1.0 is the first dedicated real-robot benchmark for standardized evaluation of UMI-style manipulation policies, unifying data collection, scene reset, policy execution, logging, and task-factor analysis in a reproducible protocol.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-robot evaluation is critical for understanding learned manipulation policy reliability, especially for UMI-style policies where performance depends on complex couplings between wrist-view observations, action representation, data collection, and physical deployment. Existing benchmarks are not designed for this specific data-to-deployment setting.

Method: UMI-Bench provides a local-first real-robot benchmark that aligns data collection, scene reset, policy execution, result logging, and task-factor analysis within a unified, reproducible, and auditable protocol for evaluating UMI-based manipulation policies.

Result: This is the first benchmark dedicated to real-world evaluation of UMI-based manipulation models, enabling reproducible measurement of how UMI-trained policies generalize to real physical manipulation.

Conclusion: UMI-Bench offers a practical testbed for standardized evaluation, making the full evaluation process reproducible and auditable for UMI-style manipulation policies.

Abstract: Real-robot evaluation is essential for understanding whether learned manipulation policies can operate reliably outside curated demonstrations. This need is particularly pressing for Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI)-style policies, whose performance depends on the coupling between wrist-view observations, action representation, data collection, and physical deployment. Existing real-world benchmarks have made important progress, but they are not designed around this UMI data-to-deployment setting. We present UMI-Bench 1.0, a local-first real-robot benchmark for standardized evaluation of UMI-style manipulation policies. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first benchmark dedicated to real-world evaluation of UMI-based manipulation models. UMI-Bench aligns data collection, scene reset, policy execution, result logging, and task-factor analysis within a unified protocol. By making the full evaluation process reproducible and auditable, UMI-Bench provides a practical testbed for measuring how UMI-trained policies generalize to real physical manipulation.

</details>


### [383] [Information-Preserving Continuous Occupancy Mapping with Variance-Weighted Submap Joining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10442)
*Zhuhua Bai,Yingyu Wang,Liang Zhao,Shoudong Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes the first continuous probabilistic submap joining framework that jointly optimizes submap poses and a global occupancy field using a sparse Bayesian formulation with closed-form log-odds tuples, achieving higher accuracy and better uncertainty calibration than grid-based and existing continuous methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale SLAM suffers from trajectory drift and high computational cost; existing submap joining methods rely on discrete grids, causing non-smooth gradients and neglecting occupancy uncertainty.

Method: A continuous probabilistic submap joining framework using an information-preserving sparse Bayesian formulation that compresses raw observations into sufficient-statistic log-odds tuples, enabling closed-form predictive mean/variance and analytical Jacobians for joint optimization.

Result: Higher pose accuracy and improved global consistency than state-of-the-art grid-based submap joining; more compact maps and better-calibrated uncertainty estimates than existing continuous occupancy mapping methods.

Conclusion: The continuous probabilistic framework effectively addresses key limitations of grid-based submap joining, achieving superior accuracy and uncertainty quantification.

Abstract: Large-scale SLAM remains challenging due to accumulated trajectory drift and the increasing computational cost of maintaining global consistency. Submap joining alleviates these issues by constructing locally consistent submaps and subsequently fusing them into a global map. However, existing occupancy-based submap joining methods operate on discrete grids, resulting in non-smooth gradients during optimization and neglecting the uncertainty associated with occupancy estimates. We propose the first continuous probabilistic submap joining framework that jointly optimizes submap poses and a global occupancy field in the latent log-odds space. The framework employs an information-preserving sparse Bayesian formulation that compresses raw occupancy observations into sufficient-statistic log-odds tuples while retaining the posterior information of the original observations. This yields closed-form predictive mean and variance estimates for occupancy mapping, which directly enable a submap joining formulation with analytical Jacobians, leading to more accurate submap joining and yielding a closed-form optimal global map upon pose convergence. Experiments on both simulated and large-scale real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves higher pose accuracy and improved global consistency than state-of-the-art grid-based submap joining approaches, while producing more compact map representations and better-calibrated uncertainty estimates than existing continuous occupancy mapping methods.

</details>


### [384] [GuideWalk: Learning Unified Autonomous Navigation and Locomotion for Humanoid Robots across Versatile Terrains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10449)
*Haoxuan Han,Chen Chen,Linao Gong,Xin Yang,Hao Hu,Junhong Guo,Zhicheng He,Yao Su,Fenghua He*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GuideWalk is a unified framework for humanoid robot navigation that combines traversability-aware navigation guidance with terrain-adaptive locomotion via teacher distillation and reinforcement learning, achieving stable navigation and locomotion on versatile terrains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humanoid robots lack reliable navigation on versatile terrains because obstacle avoidance must be coordinated with dynamically feasible motion, which remains challenging despite strong locomotion capabilities.

Method: A navigation module decouples obstacle avoidance from terrain conditions via explicit velocity guidance. A composite teacher distillation scheme aggregates goal-directed commands and dynamically consistent actions into a single policy, then refined with RL and behavior cloning.

Result: GuideWalk achieves stable and effective navigation while maintaining stable humanoid locomotion across diverse environments.

Conclusion: The unified end-to-end framework successfully integrates navigation and locomotion for humanoid robots, with the teacher distillation and RL refinement approach proving effective for robust terrain-adaptive navigation.

Abstract: Humanoid robots have achieved strong locomotion capabilities, but reliable navigation on versatile terrains remains challenging because obstacle avoidance must be coordinated with dynamically feasible motion. In this work, we present GuideWalk, a unified end-to-end framework that integrates traversability-aware navigation guidance with terrain-adaptive locomotion teacher for humanoid navigation. Specifically, we introduce a navigation module that provides explicit velocity guidance, decoupling obstacle avoidance from terrain conditions to enable robust planning across diverse environments. We propose a composite teacher distillation scheme, where goal-directed commands and dynamically consistent actions are aggregated and distilled into a single policy. To further improve robustness, the distilled policy is refined with reinforcement learning and an auxiliary behavior cloning objective, which promotes exploration while preserving desirable teacher behaviors. Experiments demonstrate that GuideWalk achieves stable and effective navigation while maintaining stable humanoid locomotion.

</details>


### [385] [Act on What You See: Unlocking Safe Social Navigation in Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10495)
*Qingzi Wang,Xiyang Wu,Guangyao Shi,Dianwei Chen,Xianfeng Yang,Dinesh Manocha*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SALSA is a two-stage training framework that improves social navigation safety by teaching Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to use their existing pedestrian-object and collision representations, reducing near-collisions by 86.4% on SCAND and real-world tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pretrained VLA models encode socially relevant signals (pedestrian-object distinctions, collision cues) internally, but behavior cloning fails to translate these into appropriate social actions, creating a representation-to-action mismatch.

Method: Two-stage annotation-free post-training: (1) Social behavioral alignment bridges intermediate social features to the action head via counterfactual human-object scene pairs; (2) Temporal safety alignment provides automatically generated future-risk supervision for anticipatory collision avoidance.

Result: SALSA reduces near-collisions by 86.4% and improves social counterfactual accuracy from 53% to 93% on SCAND and real-world deployment.

Conclusion: Safer social navigation can be achieved by teaching VLA policies to act on social representations they already possess, rather than learning new representations from scratch.

Abstract: Safe social navigation requires robots to distinguish people from ordinary obstacles and to react before danger becomes imminent. We show that pretrained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models already encode pedestrian-object distinctions and future collision signals in their internal representations, but behavior cloning fails to translate these signals into socially appropriate actions. To address this mismatch, we propose SALSA, a two-stage annotation-free post-training framework: (1) social behavioral alignment bridges intermediate-layer social features to the action head and trains on counterfactual human-object scene pairs to break visual saliency shortcuts; (2) temporal safety alignment provides automatically generated future-risk supervision to enable anticipatory collision avoidance. On SCAND and real-world deployment, SALSA reduces near-collisions by 86.4% and improves social counterfactual accuracy from 53% to 93%, demonstrating that safer social navigation can be achieved by teaching VLA policies to act on representations they already possess. These results show that pretrained VLA policies can be adapted for safer social navigation by better aligning their latent representations with action generation.

</details>


### [386] [Uncovering Vulnerability of Vision-Language-Action Models under Joint-Level Physical Faults](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10501)
*Minsoo Jo,Taeju Kwon,Junha Chun,Youngjoon Jeong,Taesup Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper shows that VLA models are vulnerable to joint-level physical faults (e.g., actuator degradation, friction) in real robots, and proposes J-PARC—a lightweight residual calibration framework that infers fault regimes from joint dynamics and adaptively corrects actions for a frozen VLA policy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real robots experience joint-level faults (actuator degradation, hardware faults, collision damage, friction) that alter the action-to-motion interface, yet VLA model robustness under such embodiment-side faults is under-explored.

Method: Proposes J-PARC (Joint-level Physical-fault Aware Residual Calibrator): a lightweight residual calibration framework that (1) infers a latent joint-fault regime from recent joint dynamics, and (2) conditions a shared residual calibrator on this regime to adaptively correct actions, while keeping the base VLA policy frozen.

Result: VLA models are vulnerable to joint-level faults with heterogeneous degradation across joints; performance drops are not solely due to physical infeasibility (e.g., friction still hurts). J-PARC improves robustness under faults while preserving fault-free performance.

Conclusion: Joint-level physical faults critically disrupt VLA policies in real robotics, and J-PARC offers an effective, lightweight calibration solution without modifying the base policy.

Abstract: Deploying Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in real robotic systems requires robustness not only to semantic and perceptual variations, but also to embodiment-side faults that change how actions are physically realized. Real robots can experience joint-level changes caused by actuator degradation, hardware faults, safety limits, collision damage, or wear-induced friction. These faults are critical because they alter the action-to-motion interface of a policy, disrupting the learned closed-loop relationship between commanded actions, realized motion, and subsequent observations. In this work, we study realistic joint-level physical faults and show that VLA models are vulnerable when predicted actions are executed through a perturbed robot body. Our analysis reveals joint-dependent effects, with heterogeneous degradation in task success across affected joints. We also show that performance drops cannot be attributed solely to physical infeasibility, since feasible faults such as increased joint friction can still substantially reduce success rates and induce closed-loop execution mismatch. Motivated by these findings, we propose Joint-level Physical-fault Aware Residual Calibrator (J-PARC), a lightweight residual calibration framework built on top of a frozen VLA policy. J-PARC infers a latent joint-fault regime from recent joint dynamics and conditions a shared residual calibrator on this regime, enabling adaptive action correction across faulty joints. Experiments show that J-PARC improves robustness under joint-level faults while preserving fault-free environment performance.

</details>


### [387] [VeriSpace: Spatially Grounded Action Verification for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10568)
*Guiyu Zhao,Longteng Guo,Junyou Zhu,Jun Fu,Yanghong Mei,Bin Cao,Jie Jiang,Xingjian He,Jing Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VeriSpace is a 3D-aware action verifier that improves VLA robot manipulation by evaluating multiple candidate actions at test time, using dual-path 3D scene encoding and spatially-grounded reasoning to select better actions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models rely on one-shot action prediction, where small action errors can cause failures. Test-time verification is needed to evaluate multiple candidate actions before execution.

Method: VeriSpace has two key components: (1) Dual-Path 3D-Injected Scene Encoding — constructs scene representations preserving both visual semantics and explicit 3D geometry; (2) Spatially-Grounded Action Reasoning — evaluates actions via task-relevant spatial relations, geometric validity, and goal progress.

Result: Improves decision reliability over underlying VLA policies and prior verification methods, with substantial gains in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings on public benchmarks and real-world tasks.

Conclusion: 3D-aware test-time verification effectively enhances VLA policy reliability, suggesting that integrating spatial reasoning into action selection is a promising direction for robust robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown strong promise for robotic manipulation, but their reliability at test time remains limited by one-shot action prediction, where even small action errors can cause grasp failure, collision, or incorrect task progression. A natural alternative is to equip VLA systems with test-time verification, allowing multiple candidate actions to be proposed and evaluated before execution. However, reliable action verification is challenging because it requires not only distinguishing subtle geometric differences between candidate actions, but also assessing whether an action makes meaningful progress toward the task goal. We present VeriSpace, a 3D-aware action verifier for test-time action selection in VLA systems. VeriSpace evaluates candidate actions through two key components: Dual-Path 3D-Injected Scene Encoding, which constructs a scene representation that jointly preserves visual semantics and explicit 3D geometry, and Spatially-Grounded Action Reasoning, which evaluates each action by reasoning over task-relevant spatial relations, geometric validity, and expected goal progress. Together, these components enable more reliable discrimination between subtle yet outcome-critical action candidates while remaining fully compatible with existing VLA policies. Experiments on public benchmarks and real-world robotic manipulation tasks show that VeriSpace consistently improves decision reliability over both underlying VLA policies and prior verification-based methods, yielding substantial gains in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.

</details>


### [388] [AgenticNav: Zero-Shot Vision-and-Language Navigation as a Tool-Calling Harness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10577)
*Yijian Li,Changze Li,Hantian Shi,Jiaying Luo,Jiyuan Cai,Ming Yang,Tong Qin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AgenticNav is a zero-shot VLN-CE method that reframes navigation as an agentic interface where the VLM directly controls action (pixel selection), depth (on-demand metric queries), and memory (compact map + recall tool), achieving SOTA on R2R-CE and demonstrating real-world generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing zero-shot VLN-CE methods rely on learned waypoint predictors (limiting action space and depth usage) and handle memory via long histories or cross-episode retrieval (weakening the zero-shot setting). The authors aim to overcome these limitations by exposing navigation primitives directly as callable tools for the VLM.

Method: AgenticNav provides three tools: (1) Action tool - VLM selects a target pixel in RGB, converted to executable motion; (2) Depth tool - on-demand pixel-depth queries for precise metric distances; (3) Memory tool - compact map image summarizing trajectory + recall tool to revisit past observations without overwhelming context. This turns VLN-CE into an agentic interface between VLM and environment.

Result: Achieves new state-of-the-art among zero-shot methods on R2R-CE benchmark with the same VLM backbone. Real-world validation shows zero-shot generalization. Ablations confirm action tool outperforms waypoint predictors, and depth + memory tools further improve performance.

Conclusion: Rethinking zero-shot VLN-CE as an agentic tool interface is effective, eliminating the need for learned waypoint predictors and specialized memory modules while achieving superior performance and generalization.

Abstract: Zero-shot vision-and-language navigation in continuous environments (VLN-CE) has recently become feasible with large vision-language models (VLMs). However, existing methods typically rely on learned waypoint predictors to propose navigable actions. This severely limits the model's action space and fails to leverage depth inputs effectively. Moreover, memory is commonly handled by accumulating long textual or visual histories with substantial irrelevant context, or by retrieving cross-episode experiences, which weakens the zero-shot setting. In this paper, we rethink zero-shot VLN-CE as an agentic interface between the VLM and the environment, and present AgenticNav, a lightweight navigation harness that exposes action, depth, and memory as callable tools. Instead of choosing from predicted waypoints, the action tool allows the VLM to directly select a target pixel in RGB observations, converting it into executable motion. Depth is exposed through an on-demand pixel-depth tool, enabling the VLM to request precise metric distances only where they matter. For memory, AgenticNav provides a compact map image summarizing the historical trajectory, paired with a recall tool that allows the VLM to selectively revisit past visual observations without overwhelming the prompt context. On the R2R-CE benchmark, AgenticNav establishes new state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance among zero-shot methods given the same VLM backbone. Real-world validation further highlights its zero-shot generalization compared to prior methods. Ablations show that our action tool design outperforms traditional waypoint predictors, and that depth tool and agentic memory further contribute to navigation performance.

</details>


### [389] [LieIPM: Lie Group Interior Point Method for Direct Trajectory Optimization of Rigid Bodies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10579)
*Sangli Teng,Ruiqi Zhang,Tzu-Yuan Lin,William A Clark,Mark Mueller,Ram Vasudevan,Maani Ghaffari,Koushil Sreenath*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes LieIPM, a structure-aware constrained trajectory optimization framework on matrix Lie groups for rigid body motion planning, which preserves geometric structure and avoids singularities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing constrained optimizers operate in Euclidean space and ignore the manifold structure of rigid body motions, introducing singularities and poorly conditioned optimization problems.

Method: Develops a second-order rigid body model on matrix Lie groups with Newton-type updates, combined with a line-search Lie Group Interior Point Method (LieIPM) that uses Lie group variational integrators and closed-form intrinsic derivatives.

Result: LieIPM preserves rotation topology, avoids singularities, and achieves superior robustness and faster convergence compared to general-purpose solvers and structure-exploiting optimal control methods.

Conclusion: The geometry-aware LieIPM framework effectively bridges the gap between manifold structure and trajectory optimization for rigid body motion planning.

Abstract: Designing dynamically feasible trajectories for rigid bodies is a fundamental problem in robotics. While direct methods are widely used, the existing constrained optimizers typically operate in Euclidean space and ignore the manifold structure of rigid body motions. This mismatch may introduce singularities or lead to poorly conditioned optimization problems. To bridge this gap, we develop a structure-aware framework for constrained trajectory optimization directly on matrix Lie groups. Our approach is based on the second-order rigid body models utilizing Lie group structures, which enables efficient Newton-type updates while preserving the underlying geometry. Building on this model, we propose a line-search Lie Group Interior Point Method (LieIPM) to handle constraints on the manifolds. We instantiate the framework for rigid body motion planning using Lie group variational integrators and derive closed-form intrinsic derivatives that exploit group symmetries. The LieIPM preserves the topology of rotation motions by construction and avoids singularities. Numerical results demonstrate superior robustness and faster convergence compared to general-purpose solvers and structure-exploiting optimal control methods.

</details>


### [390] [Dexterous Point Policy: Learning Point-based Dexterous Hand Policies from Human Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10614)
*Beomjun Kim,Seong Hyeon Park,Seunghoon Sim,Seungjun Moon,Sanghyeok Lee,Jinwoo Shin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Dexterous Point Policy (DPP) learns dexterous manipulation policies from human videos without any robot demonstrations, using unified 3D keypoint representations to bridge the embodiment gap between humans and robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot data collection for dexterous manipulation is extremely expensive and time-consuming (e.g., teleoperating a multi-fingered hand for one atomic task can take days), yet current robotic foundation models pre-trained on human videos require fine-tuning on robot-specific demonstrations to bridge the embodiment gap.

Method: DPP extracts 3D keypoints of task-relevant objects and human hands from raw videos, then trains an autoregressive transformer over these keypoints. At the keypoint level (wrist and fingertips), human and robot behaviors align, enabling direct policy transfer without robot demonstrations.

Result: DPP achieves 75.0% success on real-robot pick-and-place and tool-use tasks, compared to only 1.0% for a state-of-the-art VLA baseline. It also generalizes strongly to unseen multi-object environments and novel object categories.

Conclusion: Unified 3D keypoint representations can effectively bridge human and robot embodiments for dexterous manipulation, enabling zero-shot policy transfer from human videos to real robots without any robot-specific demonstration data.

Abstract: Robotic foundation models pre-trained on human demonstration videos have shown promise, but a significant embodiment gap remains when the resulting policies are deployed on real robots. A common remedy is to fine-tune these models on robot-specific demonstrations. However, robot data collection can be prohibitively expensive and time-consuming, which is particularly acute in dexterous manipulation, e.g., teleoperating a multi-fingered hand for even a single atomic task can take days. To address this, we introduce Dexterous Point Policy, a framework that learns dexterous manipulation policies directly from human videos and requires no robot demonstrations. Our core insight is that a unified 3D keypoint representation can bridge human and robot embodiments when used for both observations and actions. Specifically, we extract 3D keypoints of task-relevant objects and human hands from raw videos, and train an autoregressive transformer over these keypoints. We observe that at the keypoint level, specifically the wrist and fingertips, human and robot behaviors closely align, enabling direct policy transfer. On a suite of real-robot tasks spanning pick-and-place and tool use, Dexterous Point Policy attains 75.0% success, whereas a state-of-the-art VLA baseline reaches only 1.0%. Furthermore, our method generalizes strongly to unseen scenarios, including multi-object environments and novel object categories.

</details>


### [391] [Planar-Sector LOS Guidance for Interception of Agile Targets with Lifting-Wing Quadcopters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10639)
*Linkai Liu,Kun Yang,Han Zou,Chen Min,Shuli Lv,Shuai Wang,Quan Quan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Planar-Sector Line-of-Sight (PS-LOS) guidance framework for autonomous visual interception of agile aerial targets using a lifting-wing quadcopter with only a strapdown monocular camera, achieving ~50% more usable thrust compared to conventional conic LOS constraints while maintaining visibility, with successful outdoor interceptions up to 138 m range.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing conic LOS constraints for strapdown-camera interception are too conservative—they unnecessarily restrict maneuverability by keeping the target symmetrically near the image center, reducing usable thrust for pursuit. The authors are motivated by the observation that aggressive FPV pilots do not maintain equal visibility margins in all image directions.

Method: PS-LOS tightly constrains lateral image error while relaxing longitudinal image error within a safe FOV margin; combined with a delay-compensated state-estimation framework and nonlinear guidance-and-control architecture for lifting-wing quadcopters without direct depth measurements.

Result: Nearly 50% more available thrust near the LOS direction than conic LOS constraints; successful long-range interception up to 138 m with continuous visual tracking under real wind disturbances against agile, unpredictable targets.

Conclusion: PS-LOS is validated as a visibility-preserving, maneuverability-aware guidance framework for long-range visual interception of agile aerial targets.

Abstract: Autonomous visual interception of agile aerial targets is challenging due to unpredictable target motion, limited sensing, and the strong coupling between target visibility and interceptor maneuverability. Most existing strapdown-camera interception methods preserve visibility using conic line-of-sight (LOS) constraints that keep the target near the image center. While safe, such symmetric constraints unnecessarily restrict maneuverability and can significantly reduce the usable thrust for pursuit. Motivated by the observation that aggressive FPV pilots do not maintain equal visibility margins in all image directions, this paper proposes a Planar-Sector Line-of-Sight (PS-LOS) guidance framework for autonomous interception using a lifting-wing quadcopter equipped with only a strapdown monocular camera. PS-LOS tightly constrains lateral image error while relaxing longitudinal image error within a safe field-of-view margin, preserving visibility while releasing maneuverability for acceleration-intensive pursuit. Under the lifting-wing quadcopter model, PS-LOS provides nearly 50% more available thrust near the LOS direction than conventional conic LOS constraints. To realize LOS-only interception without direct depth measurements, a delay-compensated state-estimation framework and a nonlinear guidance-and-control architecture are developed for lifting-wing quadcopters. Extensive outdoor flight experiments demonstrate autonomous interception of agile targets exhibiting large-amplitude, high-frequency, and unpredictable motion under real wind disturbances. The proposed system achieves successful interceptions at ranges up to 138 m while maintaining continuous visual tracking throughout the engagement. The results validate PS-LOS as a visibility-preserving, maneuverability-aware guidance framework for long-range visual interception of agile aerial targets.

</details>


### [392] [UniDexTok: A Unified Dexterous Hand Tokenizer from Real Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10683)
*Dong Fang,Youjun Wu,Yuanxin Zhong,Rui Zhang,Yunlong Wang,Xiaosong Jia,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: UniDexTok is a unified tokenizer for dexterous hands that maps diverse robot/hand states into a shared 22-DoF representation, achieving sub-millimeter reconstruction accuracy and enabling cross-embodiment learning without retargeting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dexterous hands vary significantly in kinematics, joint definitions, and DoF, making it difficult to define a shared state representation. This fragmentation prevents joint training across different hand embodiments.

Method: Proposes UDHM (Unified Dexterous Hand Model) as a shared 22-DoF semantic interface, and UniDexTok as a retargeting-free state tokenizer that learns embodiment-conditioned discrete tokens from standardized real joint states.

Result: UniDexTok reduces MPJAE from 15.63° to 0.16° (98.98% error reduction) and MPJPE from 18.51mm to 0.18mm (99.03% error reduction) compared to UniHM, improving from centimeter-scale to sub-millimeter accuracy. Cross-embodiment data improves reconstruction, and zero/few-shot ability is demonstrated.

Conclusion: UniDexTok provides a unified representation for heterogeneous dexterous hands without retargeting or simulation, achieving dramatic accuracy improvements and enabling cross-embodiment learning benefits.

Abstract: Dexterous hands are essential for fine-grained manipulation, but their hardware designs vary substantially across embodiments. Differences in kinematics, joint definitions, and degrees of freedom make it difficult to define a shared state representation compared with parallel grippers. As a result, dexterous-hand data remains fragmented and difficult to use for joint training. In this work, we propose the Unified Dexterous Hand Model (UDHM), which maps human and robot hand states into a shared 22-DoF semantic interface. Based on UDHM, we introduce UniDexTok, a retargeting-free state tokenizer that learns embodiment-conditioned discrete tokens from standardized real joint states. UniDexTok provides a unified representation for heterogeneous dexterous hands without relying on retargeting or simulation data. Compared with the recent baseline UniHM, UniDexTok reduces MPJAE from 15.63 degrees to 0.16 degrees and MPJPE from 18.51 mm to 0.18 mm, corresponding to error reductions of 98.98% and 99.03%, respectively. These results improve reconstruction from centimeter-scale to sub-millimeter accuracy. Experiments further show that data from other embodiments improves target-embodiment reconstruction accuracy, demonstrating the benefit of cross-embodiment tokenization. UniDexTok also shows strong zero-shot and few-shot reconstruction ability when new dexterous hands are introduced.

</details>


### [393] [Self-Supervised Relevance Modelling in Autonomous Driving via Counterfactual Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10688)
*Luca Lusvarghi,Javier Gozalvez,Pablo Urbano Hidalgo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a self-supervised counterfactual approach to train a relevance model that quantifies which objects matter for autonomous driving decisions, achieving real-time performance and enabling heatmap-based policy insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable efficient resource usage and reduce latency in autonomous driving by focusing computational efforts on relevant objects while ignoring irrelevant ones, and to limit perception noise propagation.

Method: A self-supervised approach based on counterfactual analysis to train a relevance model on a synthetic causal dataset generated in urban scenarios.

Result: The relevance model accurately estimates object relevance with millisecond-level latency, enabling real-time performance even in high-density scenes. It also produces interpretable relevance heatmaps.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively identifies object relevance in real-time and provides actionable insights into driving policies, with the model and dataset publicly released.

Abstract: Autonomous driving relies on computationally intensive perception pipelines to continuously detect and track objects in the surrounding environment. While some objects are key to plan safe and effective maneuvers, others may not be relevant and have no impact on the autonomous vehicle's driving decisions. Focusing on relevant objects allows a more efficient usage of available computational resources, reduces processing latencies, and limits the downstream propagation of perception noise. In this work, we propose a novel self-supervised approach based on counterfactual analysis to develop a relevance model - an AI-based tool that quantifies the relevance of objects for an autonomous vehicle. To demonstrate the potential of the proposed approach, we train a relevance model on a synthetic causal dataset generated in a selected urban scenario. Results show that the relevance model is able to accurately estimate the objects' relevance with millisecond-level latency, enabling real-time relevance estimation also in high-density scenarios. We also show that the relevance model can be used to build relevance heatmaps that offer valuable insights into the autonomous vehicle's driving policy and can be used to proactively inform perception and planning tasks. We openly release both the relevance model and the causal dataset.

</details>


### [394] [Vehicle Prediction Model for Enhanced MPC Path Tracking in Formula Student Driverless](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10732)
*Sebastian Baader,Tamara Bergerhoff,Pascal Meißner,Frank Deinzer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a novel real-time prediction model for autonomous race cars that combines a kinematic bicycle model, offline Bayesian Linear Regression, and online Sparse Gaussian Process Regression to improve path tracking accuracy by up to 57% while providing uncertainty assessment, demonstrated on a real Formula Student car.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous race cars operate near physical handling limits, causing highly nonlinear vehicle behavior that increases path tracking complexity. Existing prediction models lack adaptability to changing conditions and real-time capability.

Method: A three-part cascaded prediction model: (1) nominal Kinematic Bicycle Model, (2) offline Bayesian Linear Regression (BLR) model, (3) online Sparse Gaussian Process Regression (SGPR) model, combining past run data with current driving context.

Result: Up to 57% improvement in prediction accuracy compared to existing approaches, with demonstrated practical applicability in an MPC-based path tracking controller on a real Formula Student race car.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid model efficiently integrates all available data without significant computational overhead, achieves high prediction accuracy from the start, and provides quantitative uncertainty assessment, making it suitable for real-time autonomous racing.

Abstract: Autonomous race cars, such as in Formula Student Driverless, operate close to their physical handling limits. The resulting highly nonlinear vehicle behavior increases the path tracking complexity, especially on narrow tracks. Model Predictive Control (MPC) is commonly used to address this issue, a method whose performance is closely tied to the accuracy of the underlying prediction model. This paper presents a novel, real-time capable prediction model for autonomous race cars that adjusts to changing conditions by combining information from past runs and the current driving situation. Our model is divided into three consecutive submodels: a nominal Kinematic Bicycle Model, an offline Bayesian Linear Regression (BLR) model, and an online Sparse Gaussian Process Regression (SGPR) model. The proposed approach enables efficient integration of all available data without significantly increasing computational cost, ensuring high prediction accuracy and a quantitative uncertainty assessment right from the start of the run. Compared to existing approaches, an improvement in prediction accuracy of up to 57% was achieved. Further, we successfully demonstrated the practical applicability of the model within an MPC-based path tracking controller on a real Formula Student race car.

</details>


### [395] [Pushing the Performance Limits in Autonomous Racing: Continuous Stability-Aware Adaptive Velocity Planning in Formula Student Driverless](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10733)
*Tamara Bergerhoff,Sebastian Baader,Pascal Meißner,Frank Deinzer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel adaptive velocity planning approach for autonomous racing that dynamically adjusts target speeds using a continuous scaling factor inferred from vehicle stability, rather than directly estimating tire-road friction, achieving 35% lap time improvement over ten laps and 8% average improvement over non-adaptive methods on a real Formula Student car.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Velocity planning in autonomous racing is challenging due to the need for real-time adaptation to track layouts, environmental changes, mechanical tolerances, and control inaccuracies, especially at high speeds. Existing approaches struggle to dynamically handle these varying conditions.

Method: Instead of directly estimating the tire-road friction coefficient, the approach infers a continuous scaling factor from vehicle stability, which captures both effective tire-road interaction and control inaccuracies. This generates a continuous friction map used to compute optimal target speeds.

Result: Evaluated on a real Formula Student race car, showing 35% lap time improvement over ten laps and 8% average improvement compared to a non-adaptive approach.

Conclusion: The adaptive velocity planning approach significantly improves lap times and driving stability by dynamically adjusting to changing conditions without explicitly estimating tire-road friction.

Abstract: In autonomous racing, especially in competitions such as Formula Student Driverless, precise planning of the target velocity of a race car is crucial for competitive lap times and stable driving behavior. Especially at high speeds, Velocity Planning (VP) is a significant challenge as it has to be performed in real time, taking into account track layouts, environmental influences, mechanical tolerances, and the resulting control inaccuracies. In this paper, we present a novel approach to VP that dynamically adapts to such changing conditions. Instead of estimating the physical Tire-Road Friction Coefficient (TRFC), a continuous scaling factor is inferred indirectly from vehicle stability. This factor not only reflects the effective tire-road interaction but also captures effects of control inaccuracies. From this, we generate a continuous friction map, which serves as a robust, adaptive basis for computing the optimal target speed, accounting for both vehicle and environmental limits. Our proposed approach was evaluated on a real Formula Student race car, showing a lap time improvement of 35 % over ten laps and an average increase of 8 % compared to a non-adaptive approach.

</details>


### [396] [Hand-centric Human-to-Robot Trajectory Transfer from Video Demonstrations via Open-World Contact Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10743)
*Yitian Shi,Di Wen,Zhengqi Han,Zicheng Guo,Yu Hu,Edgar Welte,Kunyu Peng,Rainer Stiefelhagen,Rania Rayyes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HOWTransfer is a hand-centric framework that distills human video demonstrations into robot-executable trajectories by recovering 3D hand motion, localizing contact intervals, retargeting grasps to parallel-jaw grippers, and editing trajectories, achieving 86% success in diverse manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning from human video demonstrations is difficult due to noisy hand-object interactions, unseen objects with partial observation, and cross-embodiment discrepancy between human hands and robot grippers.

Method: (1) Recovers temporally consistent 3D hand motion and localizes temporal contact intervals from hand-object interaction cues. (2) Retargets human grasp intent into multi-modal parallel-jaw grasp hypotheses using contact onsets. (3) Propagates grasps along the recovered wrist trajectory. (4) Refines contact alignment via trajectory editing to generate diverse executable variants from a single demonstration.

Result: Achieves 86% success rate in accurate contact localization and high-quality robot motion retargeting across diverse manipulation tasks. Preferred over teleoperated trajectories in a blinded preference study.

Conclusion: HOWTransfer effectively addresses cross-embodiment and partial observation challenges by leveraging contact-aware, taxonomy-informed retargeting without relying on object-specific descriptions or explicit object-state tracking.

Abstract: Learning from human video demonstrations remains challenging due to noisy hand-object interactions, unseen objects with partial observation, and cross-embodiment discrepancy. To address these challenges, we present \textit{HOWTransfer} (\emph{H}and-\emph{O}bject \emph{O}pen-\emph{W}orld Transfer), a hand-centric framework that distills human demonstrations into contact-aware, taxonomy-informed, and diverse robotic trajectories. Instead of relying on object-specific descriptions, vision-language queries, or explicit object-state tracking, \emph{HOWTransfer} recovers temporally consistent 3D hand motion and localizes temporal contact intervals by reasoning over observed hand-object interaction cues. The localized contact onsets are then used to retarget human grasp intent into multi-modal parallel-jaw grasp hypotheses, which are propagated along the recovered wrist trajectory to generate robot-executable motions. Finally, a trajectory editing stage refines contact alignment and produces diverse executable variants from a single demonstration. Experiments across diverse manipulation tasks show that \emph{HOWTransfer} enables accurate contact localization and high-quality robot motion retargeting with $86\%$ success, which is preferred over teleoperated trajectories in a blinded preference study.

</details>


### [397] [ros2probe: Non-intrusive, Kernel-selective Observability for Robot Operating System 2 Middleware](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10746)
*Jisang Yu,Sanghoon Lee,Yeonwoo Choi,Kyung-Joon Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ros2probe enables non-intrusive ROS 2 communication observation by reconstructing state from discovery packets and using in-kernel filtering, avoiding the probe effect that plagues domain-joining tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ROS 2 observation tools must join the DDS domain as subscribers, which perturbs the system (probe effect)—inflating discovery traffic, adding overhead, and displacing real subscriber messages especially near saturation. Passive wire capture loses ROS 2 semantics and doesn't scale.

Method: ros2probe reconstructs full ROS 2 communication state from domain discovery packets at no bandwidth cost, then drives an in-kernel filter that captures only user-requested topics, lifting those packets at minimal cost while observing exactly what the real subscriber receives.

Result: Across 3 platforms (laptop, Jetson, Raspberry Pi), 2 DDS implementations, and 7 workloads: discovery graph within 0.5% of unobserved (vs up to 2.6× inflation for domain-joining tools); zero subscriber message drops at saturation (vs 38.5% drop); loss recall of 1.0; CPU and memory reductions up to 7× and 28×.

Conclusion: ros2probe effectively eliminates the observer's probe effect in ROS 2, providing accurate, non-intrusive observation that is practical even on resource-constrained embedded robots where existing tools fail.

Abstract: Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2), the de facto standard middleware framework for robots, runs each robot as a graph of nodes communicating over the Data Distribution Service (DDS), a publish/subscribe substrate. Observing this inter-node communication in real time is essential to robot development, yet it has a price. A tool can receive data only by joining the DDS domain as a subscriber that discovery has matched to the publisher, so observing folds the tool into the system it measures and perturbs it. We define this protocol-inherent perturbation as the observer's probe effect. It inflates the discovery plane, adds deserialization cost on the observer, makes the loss it reports diverge from what the subscriber actually received, and near saturation displaces the subscriber's messages. The only escape, capturing all wire traffic passively, discards ROS 2 message semantics and scales with total traffic, not what is observed. We present ros2probe, a non-intrusive observation framework that removes the probe effect. It reconstructs the full ROS 2 communication state from the domain's discovery packets at no bandwidth cost, then drives an in-kernel filter restricted to the topics the user asks for, lifting only those packets at minimal cost and observing what the real subscriber receives. Its interfaces and recordings match the standard ROS 2 tools. Across three hardware platforms (laptop, Jetson, and Raspberry Pi), two DDS implementations, and seven robot-operation workloads, ros2probe holds the discovery graph within 0.5% of an unobserved system, whereas domain-joining tools inflate discovery up to 2.6$\times$ and drop 38.5% of the subscriber's messages at saturation while ros2probe drops none. It reports loss with a recall of 1.0, cuts observer CPU and memory by up to 7$\times$ and 28$\times$, and stays practical on the embedded robots where existing tools overload the system.

</details>


### [398] [Bridging Semantics and Physical Execution: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Multi-Pair Robotic Assembly](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10808)
*Xinyi Li,Aiguo Song,Linhu Wei,Huijun Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A neuro-symbolic framework for multi-pair robotic assembly that uses LLMs to generate optimal subgraphs, resolves cross-pair interference via divergence-driven coordination, and achieves 97% executability offline and 90% real-robot success with 0.5mm tolerance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for multi-pair robotic assembly suffer from either state-space explosion and knowledge bottlenecks (classical) or logical hallucinations and topological conflicts (learning-based), lacking a bridge between cognitive decision-making and physical execution in unstructured environments with spatial interference and contact uncertainties.

Method: An end-to-end neuro-symbolic framework that: (1) extracts semantic identity/state and quantifies scene divergence from RGB-D input; (2) generates optimal subgraphs per pair via LLM with basic actions to avoid hallucinations; (3) reasons and inserts supportive actions for edge cases using a lightweight discriminator; (4) topologically coordinates augmented subgraphs into global sequences; (5) uses dynamic behavior trees with atomic skills for force-aware execution.

Result: Offline evaluation on 100 real-world scenes achieved 97.00% global executability, outperforming classical and state-of-the-art planners. Real-robot deployment on a UR3 arm attained 90% success rate with 0.5 mm tolerance under strong interference.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a unified, verifiable, and easily extensible solution for complex autonomous multi-pair robotic assembly, effectively bridging cognitive decision-making and physical execution in unstructured environments.

Abstract: Multi-pair robotic assembly in unstructured environments faces spatial interference and contact uncertainties. Existing paradigms fail to bridge cognitive decision-making and physical execution, as they either encounter state-space explosion and knowledge bottlenecks or suffer from logical hallucinations and topological conflicts. We propose an end-to-end neuro-symbolic framework that solves the challenge hierarchically: generating optimal subgraphs for each pair, decoupling generality from edge cases, and then resolving cross-pair interferences. Given an eye-on-hand RGB-D assembly scene, the framework extracts semantic instance identity and state while quantifying the scene for divergence calculation. For each pair, optimal subgraph is generated via LLM using barely basic actions to mitigate hallucinations. Supportive actions for edge cases are reasoned and inserted with a lightweight discriminator. Driven by the divergence between the quantified baseline and current scene, it is easily extensible at low cost. Augmented subgraphs are topologically coordinated into global sequences while preserving internal behavioral coherence. Dynamic behavior trees embedding atomic skills close the force-aware execution loop. Offline evaluation on 100 real-world scenes achieves 97.00% global executability, outperforming classical and state-of-the-art planners. Real-robot deployment on a UR3 arm attains 90% success rate with 0.5 mm tolerance under strong interference, demonstrating a unified and verifiable solution for complex autonomous assembly.

</details>


### [399] [IMPACT: Learning Internal-Model Predictive Control for Forceful Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10818)
*Jiawei Gao,Chaoqi Liu,Peilin Wu,Haonan Chen,Yilun Du*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: IMPACT decouples forceful robotic manipulation tasks into task-planning and internal-model-based predictive control, outperforming implicit/explicit methods in success rate, weight generalization, safety, and energy efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for forceful robotic manipulation (implicit via impedance control or explicit via force/tactile sensors) suffer from poor generalization across object weights or require specialized hardware, increasing complexity.

Method: Proposes IMPACT, a framework that decouples forceful tasks into (1) task-planning and (2) internal-model-based predictive control, avoiding reliance on steady-state tracking errors or extra sensors.

Result: Achieves higher success rates, better generalization to unseen object weights, improved safety, and greater energy efficiency in both simulation and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: Decoupling task-planning from internal-model-based predictive control effectively addresses forceful manipulation challenges without requiring specialized hardware, offering a practical and generalizable solution.

Abstract: Real-world robotic manipulation tasks often involve forceful interactions with the environment, such as using tools of varying weights, transporting objects with different masses, and performing contact-rich tasks like table wiping. Previous learning-based approaches typically employ imitation learning policies that output target end-effector poses tracked by low-level impedance controllers. In these systems, forceful interactions are either implicitly realized through steady-state tracking errors or explicitly commanded using wrist force/torque or tactile sensors. However, implicit approaches generalize poorly across object weights, while explicit approaches require specialized hardware and increase system complexity. In this work, we propose IMPACT, a framework that decouples these forceful tasks into task-planning and internal-model-based predictive control. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves higher success rates and improved generalization to unseen object weights, as well as better safety and energy efficiency.

</details>


### [400] [GUIDE: Goal-Initialized Directional Understanding for End-to-End Visual Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10832)
*Liang Wang,Jin Jin,KanZhong Yao,YiBin Wu,Fangqiang Ding,Jin Wang,Jun Wu,Zhe Sun,Qiuguo Zhu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GUIDE is a fully end-to-end RL framework for legged robot navigation that uses spatial anchor prediction from proprioceptive history to maintain directional awareness, requiring only a single initial goal without continuous updates or external maps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing learning-based visual navigation for legged robots relies on continuous goal updates from hierarchical state estimation, causing sensory/computational overhead and myopic behaviors under partial observability.

Method: GUIDE: a fully end-to-end RL framework with a spatial anchor predictor that uses multi-frequency proprioceptive history for egomotion representations (long-horizon spatial context) and raw depth streams for local geometry perception.

Result: GUIDE learns reliable egomotion and directional awareness, enabling safe navigation through dense clutter and structured mazes in both simulation and real-world on a quadruped robot.

Conclusion: A fully end-to-end deployed policy can navigate without subsequent goal guidance or prior maps by leveraging intrinsic spatial memory from proprioceptive history.

Abstract: Learning-based visual navigation for legged robots typically relies on continuous goal updates from hierarchical state estimation to provide a persistent directional reference. This reliance incurs additional sensory and computational overhead and deviates from fully end-to-end mobile autonomy. Furthermore, under partial observability, policies are prone to learn myopic behaviors, easily becoming trapped in dead ends and complex structural layouts. To address these limitations, we investigate a goal-initialized navigation setting, where the target is provided only once at the beginning of an episode, requiring the robot to operate based on intrinsic spatial memory without subsequent goal updates from external modules. In this work, we propose GUIDE, a fully end-to-end reinforcement learning framework designed to cultivate internal directional awareness. Specifically, GUIDE incorporates a spatial anchor predictor that leverages multi-frequency proprioceptive history to extract egomotion representations, thereby maintaining a persistent long-horizon spatial context for navigation. Concurrently, it utilizes raw depth streams to perceive local environmental geometry. We evaluate the proposed framework across both simulation and real-world scenarios on a quadruped robot. Experiments show that GUIDE learns reliable egomotion and directional awareness, enabling a fully end-to-end deployed policy to safely navigate through dense clutter and structured mazes without subsequent goal guidance or prior maps.

</details>


### [401] [Gradient based Bilevel for Inverse Optimal Control, a Riemannian approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10841)
*Ahmed-Manaf Dahmani,Vincent Bonnet,David Daney,François Charpillet*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper reformulates Inverse Optimal Control (IOC) as an optimization problem on a Riemannian manifold, proposing RIOC that projects observed trajectories onto the manifold of optimal solutions. It achieves comparable accuracy to classical bilevel IOC while reducing computation time by about 4x on real human arm trajectory data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical IOC using bilevel optimization is computationally prohibitive for realistic systems, and recent projection-based approaches suffer from numerical instability due to violations of constraint qualifications when solved with gradient-based methods.

Method: The authors identify that the set of trajectories satisfying optimality conditions forms a manifold. They reformulate IOC as an optimization problem on this manifold and propose Riemannian Inverse Optimal Control (RIOC), which projects observed trajectories onto the manifold of optimal solutions while preserving feasibility.

Result: On real human arm trajectories, RIOC achieves comparable or better reconstruction accuracy than classical bilevel IOC while reducing computation time by about a factor of four.

Conclusion: Geometric optimization methods can improve the scalability and reliability of IOC, making it more practical for robotics and human motion analysis.

Abstract: Inverse Optimal Control (IOC) aims to recover the cost function that explains observed trajectories as solutions of an optimal control problem. Classical IOC formulations rely on bilevel optimization, which repeatedly solves a nested optimal control problem and quickly becomes computationally prohibitive for realistic systems. Recent projection-based approaches offer a promising alternative but suffer from numerical instability when solved with gradient-based methods due to violations of standard constraint qualifications.
  In this paper, we show that these difficulties stem from the geometric structure of the IOC feasible set. We demonstrate that the set of trajectories satisfying the optimality conditions naturally forms a manifold and reformulate IOC as an optimization problem on this manifold. Based on this insight, we propose a Riemannian Inverse Optimal Control (RIOC) method that projects observed trajectories onto the manifold of optimal solutions while preserving feasibility by construction. Experiments on real human arm trajectories show that the proposed method achieves comparable or better reconstruction accuracy than classical bilevel IOC while reducing computation time by about a factor of four. These results highlight the potential of geometric optimization methods to improve the scalability and reliability of IOC for robotics and human motion analysis.

</details>


### [402] [An Exposure-Time-Aligned Primary-Path Architecture for Autonomous-Driving ECUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10856)
*Toru Saito,Yuki Hagura,Tatsuya Konishi,Satoru Mizusawa,Takumi Yajima*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes three design principles (Primary-Path, Exposure-Time-Aligned, Co-Path Coexistence) for supporting both modular pipeline and end-to-end autonomous driving on production SoCs, enabling staged migration from modular to E2E systems while meeting latency budgets (mean 296ms within 350ms budget).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Production vehicles still rely on modular multi-NN pipelines despite E2E autonomous driving being the dominant research direction. There is a need for an architecture that supports both paradigms during this transitional period, with a path for staged migration from modular to E2E.
